blocks.html: Quick Rules
Quick Rules
Basics
Action Rolls
You make an action roll when any action can fail or is contested.
An Action Roll is 1d20 + Bonus Dice
Victory Sum - VS
The purpose of an Action Roll is to meet or exceed a Victory Sum. Example VS
Feeble - 5
Poor - 10
Ordinary - 15
Heroic - 30
Epic - 70
Legendary - 90
Mythic - 110
Ascendant - 150
Bonus Dice
Bonus Dice are d10s. Your ability scores and skills will add bonus dice to action rolls.
Dice Ranks
Charges and Challenges increase or decrease the rank of Bonus Dice.
Feeble - d4
Poor - d6
Ordinary - d8
Heroic - d10
Epic - d12
Legendary - d20
Mythic - d100
Chances

Add +15 to the target VS, but add 1 Charge to the effects, such as damage dice or dice recovered. You may take up to 3 chances on any roll, and you cannot charge an outcome beyond Legendary through taking chances.
Challenges
Reduce an Action Roll’s Bonus Dice by 1 Rank. i.e. d10 become d8.
Charges
Increase the effect or the effect’s bonus dice by 1 rank, such as Bonus Dice to damage, or quality of a crafted item.
Combat Rules
Initiative
Initiative is 1d20 and rolled at the beginning of the encounter. Turns progress in order of highest roll first. Ties go to the combatant with the highest number of Speed Bonus Dice.
Actions
All creatures begin with two actions. On your turn you may use actions to move, attack, or use abilities.
Attack Rolls
When attacking with a weapon you are making a skill check VS the target’s Guard. Guard is 15.
Casting Rolls
When targeting an opponent with a spell or magical ability you are making a skill check VS the target’s Ward. Ward is 15.
Attack rolls:
1d20 + Skill Bonus Dice + Agility Bonus Dice
Casting rolls:
1d20 + Skill Bonus Dice + Soul Bonus Dice
Defending
You can attempt to evade attacks made against you. Defending uses up 5 Vigor.
Resisting
You may attempt to overcome mental, physical, and spiritual assaults that affect you with a Resistance Check. Resisting consumes 5 Mana.
Defense Rolls:
1d20 + Defense Bonus Dice + Speed Bonus Dice
Resisting:
1d20 + Resistance Bonus Dice + Target Ability Bonus Dice
Movement
You can move 10 Feet +5 feet for each of your Speed Bonus Dice as an action.
Recovery Phase
At the end of your turn you recover 1 Vigor for each of your Stamina Bonus Dice. If you have active effects, you can choose to spend 5 Mana to resist one of them.

The Realms of Ouroboros are old. Older than the kingdoms that map them, older than the sects whose disciplines still shape their fighters and scholars, older than the gods whose names are spoken in the high temples. Into this old, layered world steps a new generation of cultivators — yours among them. Their story begins now.

This game uses a simple set of rules known as the Promethium System. You'll take on the role of a hero of your own creation to explore a world of danger and adventure, and these rules will help you resolve contests and conflicts and advance your character. One player, the Game Master, will adjudicate rules. The Game Master will describe your encounters and adopt the role of creatures, characters, and adversaries you face.
Together you will craft a freeform narrative and shape a story through your actions and the consequences of them.

To play Radiant Age you need: this book, a set of polyhedral dice (one each of d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20, and ideally a d100), pencils, character sheets, and one to six friends willing to imagine the Realms of Ouroboros with you.

As a Player, you bring a single character to life. You decide who they are, what they cultivate, what they value, and how they react to the world the Game Master describes. You speak for your character, roll for their actions, and shape the unfolding story through the choices you make at the table.

The Game Master describes the world, runs the cast of supporting characters and adversaries, sets the stakes of every scene, and adjudicates the rules when situations arise that the text does not cover. The GM is not the adversary of the players; the GM is the world the players are exploring.

Radiant Age uses a set of polyhedral dice: d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20, and occasionally d100. Each player and the GM should have at least one full set, though having multiple sets of each is helpful — Action Rolls use 1d20, and most situations involve adding multiple Bonus Dice in varying sizes. You will roll often. Pick dice that feel good in your hand; you will be living with them for a long time.

Each player keeps a Character Record — a sheet (paper or digital) that tracks their character's ability scores, skills, talents, cultivated engines, resources, equipment, Titles, Deeds, and other accumulated details. The Character Record is the player's living tally of who their character is and what they have done. Keep it updated; refer to it often. As your character advances through the long path of cultivation, their record will become a substantial document — and rightly so.

Many tables use digital tools to support play: virtual tabletops for online sessions, digital character sheets for organization, dice-rolling apps for convenience, note-taking software for shared records, and audio applications for ambient soundscapes. None of these are required, but most groups eventually adopt at least one. Whatever tools you use, the heart of the game remains the same — friends gathered around a story, imagining the Realms of Ouroboros together, with rules and dice to ground their decisions.

As a player, you will take on the persona of a character of your own devising. Hopefully, this character will be interesting, unique, and more than just the numbers and rules used to describe them. Your fellow players, each with their own character, form the cast. You must use your imagination to construct the scene in your mind's eye and play the role of this persona you have crafted. This is the heart of any role-playing game.

You will imagine the scene that the GM has described and picture your character in it. You will see and act upon this scene through your character, in the guise of your character's persona. The GM will tell you where you are, what you see, and what the situation is like. It will be your job to act upon this information in character, thus, as a Player Character.

The hardest part is deciding what actions you, in the role of your character, take. Do you leap down from the cliff and attack the guards head-on? Or do you wait until you have an opportunity to attack from the shadows? The best thing about an RPG is that the character you are playing can do almost anything. There are no limits on what choices or actions you can take, although there are limits on what you can know and do based on the abilities of the character you are playing.

As you choose the actions your character takes, remember that you are experiencing the events through your character's eyes, not your own. Your character, and therefore you, should act in a manner consistent with their outlook, knowledge, and personality.

A session of Radiant Age moves in cycles between three modes. In Exploration, the party traverses the world — through cities, sects, wilderness, and stranger places — gathering information, seeking opportunities, and answering the questions the GM raises. In Interaction, the party engages with NPCs through conversation, negotiation, persuasion, and intrigue, often the most important work a cultivator does. In Combat, the party faces direct conflict — bandits, beasts, rival cultivators, eldritch incursions — and dice resolve the contest in structured turns and rounds.

Most sessions blend all three modes. A session might begin with travel through a difficult mountain pass (Exploration), find the party in a tense audience with a sect master who wants to test them (Interaction), and conclude in a brutal contest against the sect's apex disciple (Combat). The GM moves the table between modes as the situation requires; the rules support each mode with the structure it needs.

Radiant Age is built on four cardinal rules — the pillars that hold every session together. These are not optional, and they are not negotiable. When in doubt, return to them; they will help you find your way.

Action. Characters take actions, the world responds, consequences accumulate. Every session needs forward motion — moments where a player declares an intent, rolls dice, and reshapes the situation. Without action, the game stalls. The GM creates the conditions for action; the players take the actions. This is the engine that drives every story forward.

Adventure. The Realms of Ouroboros are vast and strange, and characters are not bound to any single location, sect, or court. The game thrives on movement — into new places, new political situations, new mysteries, new dangers. The party's path should always have a direction forward, even if that direction is uncertain. Sessions where nothing new is encountered, no new people are met, and no new questions are raised tend to slow the table; build adventure into your campaign's bones.

Interaction. The world is full of people who want things, fear things, hope for things, and resist things. Characters must engage with them — to learn from them, to oppose them, to ally with them, to deceive them, to love them. Combat is a small part of the game; the long work of a cultivator is conversation. Treat the NPCs at your table with the same seriousness you treat enemies in combat.

Fun Is the Most Important Rule. Above all four pillars, above any rule, above any campaign principle: the game must be fun for the people at the table. If a rule is producing frustration without producing enjoyment, set it aside. If a campaign direction is making players unhappy, change direction. If something is working — even if it bends a rule — let it work. The system serves the table, not the other way around.

At the nucleus of the Promethium System are a few simple concepts: Action Rolls for how you resolve contests, such as hitting or defending in combat; Effect Rolls for determining outcomes, including how much damage you deal should you strike an enemy with a weapon; and Dice Rank for establishing what dice you use when you make an action or effect roll.

Throughout the text, you'll see dice types abbreviated as d20, d12, d10, d8, d6, and d4. The number on the right is the number of sides the dice has. When you see a number such as 3d8 or 1d20, this is the number and type of dice you roll. For example, when you see 3d8, this means three eight-sided dice.
An Action Roll is:
1d20 + Ability Score Bonus Dice + Skill Bonus Dice against a Victory Sum

When you want to take an action, such as make an Attack or disarm a bomb, you make an action roll, 1d20 plus any Bonus Dice. Your objective is to roll higher than the Victory Sum (VS) set by the GM. If you take an action that affects another creature, it is usually entitled to a Reaction, such as using Defense to Dodge or Deflect an attack or using Resistance or Resilience to overcome a Spell or effect.

The difficulty of an Action Roll is determined by the nature of the action and the stakes involved. VS values are fixed for many actions to reduce ambiguity, but your GM or a specific rule might decide or impact the VS you are trying to beat. As a player you also have the opportunity to gamble with your success by taking Chances.
Generally, Victory Sums are clearly defined by narrative conditions (stakes and action type) and use the same ranks as dice and qualities: feeble through ascendant.

rows: Routine action, negligible stakes; trivial tasks.
Routine action, minor stakes; simple, everyday tasks.
Routine action, risky stakes; common actions under pressure. Heroic action, minor stakes; ambitious but manageable feats.
Routine action, life-or-death stakes; crucial tasks under extreme pressure. Heroic action, risky stakes; difficult feats that push capabilities.
Heroic action, life-or-death stakes; extraordinary feats with significant risk or impact.
Exceptional heroic acts involving near-impossible conditions or overwhelming stakes.
Transcendent actions; tasks beyond normal mortal limits, defining moments of extraordinary heroism or desperation.
Near-miraculous feats, reality-altering events, or legendary achievements that permanently impact the story or world.

blocks.html: Most actions produce an effect, such as damage or the outcome of a skill. In cases where this is variable, you'll make an effect roll.

Any time you receive a bonus to a roll, whether it is an action or effect roll, you receive additional dice to roll. Your Ability Scores, Skills, and Talents will give bonus dice on various action and effect rolls, and situations might provide you bonus dice. Bonus dice are usually Heroic dice, but wounds, injuries, and other conditions can reduce their Rank.
All dice have a dice rank. The Action Dice that you get to roll for any action are Legendary, a d20. Bonus dice are heroic, a d10.
When you roll the maximum number on any bonus dice, the dice Burst, and you roll one of those dice again and add it to the total. This occurs with both actions and effects.

You'll often find you are rolling your action dice plus 2 or 3 Bonus Dice. In a situation like this a VS 20 can seem trivial. As the player, you have the option to gamble with your success, playing a game of all or nothing by taking up to three Chances on an action. For each Chance you take, you increase the VS of the action by 15 but gain 1 Charge, which increases the Rank of the outcome or effect roll.

For example, while attacking an enemy, you could attempt to decapitate them in one blow, taking 2 Chances to increase your Bonus Dice on damage from heroic to legendary. But that task is much more difficult, and the VS is increased by 30.
Three is the Limit. Regardless of how many dice you have to throw around, you cannot take more than three (3) chances on any single action roll, increasing the VS by +45 but adding 3 Charges.

Although each core skill tree includes a list of possible chances, it's often up to the GM to dictate what effect a Charge has on an outcome. In combat, it's easy to understand that taking chances can increase your rank of your damage bonus dice, allow you to deal non-lethal damage, or make called shots, but with more narrative actions, such as Awareness, it will be the GM's responsibility to give you additional or higher quality information.

Chances are something that happens on your turn under your direction, as such you cannot take chances on reactions or effect rolls. You take chances when you choose to undertake an action and risk everything for a chance at something greater.
Legendary Chances. No matter how many chances you take on an action roll, you cannot push an effect beyond legendary (d20) rank. This includes damage dice, item quality, and any other effect.
There are several instances where the situation changes the outcome of an action, as reflected by Charges, and Challenges.

Charges. In the event that you receive an increase to the rank of your Bonus Dice on an action or effect roll, such as damage or amount healed, this is a Charge. For example, if you took 1 Chance on your attack roll, increasing the VS you need to hit by +10, you would gain 1 Charge to your damage bonus dice, increasing them one rank, from Heroic (d10) to Epic (d12). Charges can also impact the Rank of items you create or information you can obtain, ultimately increasing their quality by one rank.

Challenges. When you are faced with a situation that hampers your ability, you may receive Challenges. Each Challenge reduces the Rank of your Bonus Dice on an action by one rank. The most common form of Challenge is being Wounded, which affects all actions you take. Normally, a Challenge is specific and situational. The GM might rule that attacking with a melee weapon underwater provokes 2 Challenges to your attack and damage rolls but firing a bow does not.
There are many instances where a value, effect, or number is multiplied or divided. In the case of odd numbers, round down as a rule of thumb.

Even the immortal have moments of glory, defiance, and conviction. When a character rises above all odds, they may become Destined, entering a state of heightened clarity and resolve. Whether through sheer skill or a twist of fate, becoming Destined means your character stands on the cusp of greatness.
You become Destined when one of the following occurs:

items: You roll a Natural 20 on an Action roll.
You succeed on a roll after taking 3 Chances.
You Burst 3 Bonus Dice on a single roll.
You perform an action the GM deems narratively powerful, iconic, or heroic.

blocks.html: When you become Destined, you may hold this state until it is spent. A character can only be Destined once at a time; the condition does not stack. Finally, you cannot become Destined on the same turn that you unfold your Destiny — i.e., you cannot become Destined on an action, reaction, or interrupt action that unfolded your Destiny.
When you are Destined, you may choose to unfold your destiny, spending the state to fuel a moment of extraordinary effort. You may do this at any time under the listed conditions.
You may unfold your destiny to do one of the following:

items: Activate limit break abilities.
Add +3 Bonus Dice to an Action or Reaction roll.
Charge the outcome of an action roll beyond Legendary by taking Chances.
Recover ¼ of your maximum Durability, Vigor, or Mana as an Action on your turn.
Immediately take 1 Action out of turn, as long as it is not another player's active turn.

blocks.html: Destined is not just a reward: it's your character's chance to shape their fate in a pivotal moment. Whether it's a perfectly timed attack, a heroic surge of strength, or the unleashing of a legendary technique, becoming Destined represents your rise to legend. When your moment comes, make it count.

When in doubt, the best precedent to set is that situational rules take priority over global standards. Every rule is defined by the situation in which the rule is applied. A long sword is an ordinary weapon that deals Ordinary damage dice, save for when it is a heroic weapon and deals heroic dice.

In each situation, the rules may have exceptions or modifiers, and that's ok. The rules are designed to allow these situations to exist, not to prevent them or eliminate any exceptional circumstances.

To adventure in the Realms of Ouroboros is to step willingly into the unfinished business of an old world. The kingdoms have borders, the sects have territories, the divine and forbidden powers have established their courts — and within all of this carefully arranged order, things have begun to come loose. Borders are contested. Old sects are dying or reforming. The unseen world presses against the seen. Cultivators are needed, and characters of skill, will, and dedicated cultivation may find themselves at the center of events that will shape the next century. The roads are long. The wilderness is older than the kingdoms. The cities have unfinished business that has waited for decades for someone with the cultivated capacity to address it. Adventure waits.

As you play, you may find that minutes of role-playing only amount to seconds through the eyes of your characters. Therefore, abstract units of time like turns and rounds exist for the sake of the continuity of the game.

There are points when it doesn't matter whether an event takes ten minutes or ten seconds; in other situations, every second counts. In combat each turn and round are important, and while they last seconds, many effects and situations are only applicable during your turn or after a full round. Sometimes what you want to do may take multiple actions or turns to complete, such as reloading a weapon or chanting a spell.

As players act and describe their behavior, react to events, and generally play in character, they are in uptime. It can be important to measure in-game time in these situations, such as a character trying to maintain a guard's attention for five minutes so that a comrade can compromise a security system or when another character who has been poisoned has mere seconds to find an antidote.

Conversely, downtime describes events covered in little detail, where the GM has "fast forwarded." The weeks spent in training, the months a master smith spends on a perfect sword, or the long nights a scholar spends transcribing an ancient text are good examples of downtime.

Whether you are running full tilt down an exploding hallway or leaping across a gaping chasm, how you move about the environment means the difference between life and death. Furthermore, many creatures have exceptional modes of movement. Exceptional modes of movement, such as being a natural swimmer or having wings and the ability to fly, will generally be based on your Speed Score, just like your land movement.
As an action you can move 10 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Speed bonus dice. This movement speed is modified by a number of effects.

All creatures can run faster for a short period. A normal sprint increases your movement distance based on Speed by +½ for 1 minute and consumes 10 Vigor at the end of that period. The talent Athletics (Sprinting) can make you even faster, and allow you to sprint in combat.
When forced to move through obstacles, be it thick underbrush or boggy swamp, your movement is reduced by ½. This holds true for both overland travel and movement in combat.
While you are winded or engaged in other activities (such as sneaking) while moving, you can only move at a slow pace, ½ your normal movement based on Speed.

From a standing position, you could jump vertically up to 1 foot up for every 10 points of Strength. On a VS 20 Athletics check, you can jump vertically up to 2 feet up for every 10 points of Strength.

A medium-sized creature can leap horizontally up to 5 feet, plus 5 feet for each Strength Bonus Dice. With a minimum 20-foot running start and a VS 20 Athletics check, you can add your Speed and Strength bonus dice together to determine your maximum long jump distance.

For most creatures, swimming and climbing qualify as Challenging terrain. In some cases, creatures may be required to make an appropriate skill check while engaged in this movement or else they might fall or drown.
Your overall movement speed is affected by your size. Small creatures are somewhat slower than large creatures overland, and how far a creature can leap is drastically impacted by its size category.

The distance you can travel overland on foot or on a mount (or by wing) is based on your Speed Score or the Speed Score of your mount. Each hour you can cover 2 miles, plus 1 mile for each of your Speed bonus dice. A party traveling together uses the slowest Speed Score of the group members. Traveling by vehicle, you would use that vehicle's cruising speed in miles per hour.

Long periods of overland travel should be considered downtime. While traveling, there is no reason you cannot forage for food and water, investigate the area, map or chart your course, or even engage in some light training.

While traveling on long journeys, your GM might enact random encounters with monsters, brigands, or bystanders in need of aid as well. Such situations can delay you or put you in danger, or they can provide you with an opportunity to pursue some kind of reward.

It is possible to have multiple stacking movement penalties, such as sneaking through challenging terrain, or moving through challenging terrain during a storm. In most instances, these penalties do not stack; you use the highest penalty.

Falling from a dangerous height or having something large and heavy fall on you can cause serious damage. At the end of a fall, a creature takes a dice of bludgeoning damage for every 10 feet it fell, based on its size. The creature lands prone unless it avoids taking damage from the fall. When an object falls on a creature (or another object), both take this damage.
In many cases the temperature, be it extremely hot or extremely cold, can change how you explore and adventure.

When you attempt to travel overland during storms, blizzards, or dust storms, your overland distance is halved unless specified otherwise. Storms can also severely reduce visibility and create dangerous situations, such as unseen falls or Challenging terrain.
Severe temperatures can sap your Strength or even kill you. Generally, there are five categories of temperature to consider while adventuring.

Severe cold, anything below -10°F/-23°C, is considered frigid. Most creatures need twice as much food per day in this climate. In addition to the effects of hypothermia described below, going without proper protection causes 1d4 cold damage each minute directly to Health.

Winter chills of around 0°F/-18°C can cause hypothermia to those who go without adequate protection. Each hour without protection, you must make a Resistance (Physical, Stamina or Resolve) check VS 20 or become hypothermic. Once you become hypothermic you become Dazed, and you lose 1d8 Vigor each hour until you are warmed back up. Once your Vigor reaches 0, you begin losing Health.
This is a wide range from 32°F/0°C to 85°F/30°C. There are no modifiers for most creatures in this temperature range.

Anything higher than 90°F/32°C is considered hot. When you expend Vigor while hot, you consume twice as much Vigor and run the risk of becoming winded very easily. If you become winded, you incur 1 rank of Fatigue while it is hot.

At around 110°F/43°C the temperature is scorching and extremely dangerous. Most creatures require twice as much water to remain hydrated. In addition to the increased Vigor cost, each time you use Vigor you must make a Resistance (Physical, Stamina or Resolve) check VS 25 or suffer heatstroke, suffering from 1 rank of Fatigue.
Lighting can affect everything a character is trying to do, from fix a machine to shoot long distance, but only when it is very dark or very, very bright.
Low lighting and darkness can have a significant impact on your performance.

Total darkness. In complete darkness or being blind, a character cannot normally see and must rely on other senses. Manual skills such as writing or crafting or anything that is deeply reliant on dexterity and visual/spatial senses, such as piloting, have their VS increased by +15.
Partial darkness. This is darkness without any light source brighter than a candle within 5 feet. A character suffers a +10 penalty to all manual specialty VS.
Dim lighting. Such as only having a candle or partial light, characters in dim lighting suffer a +5 VS penalty.

In constant extremely bright and dazzling light, characters are visually impaired and suffer a +10 penalty to all VS requiring sight while exposed to the dazzling light and for 1d10 rounds afterwards. Exposure to a blinding flash or blinding light causes the Blinded condition for 1d10 rounds unless you can make a Resistance check VS 25.

Creatures forced to endure without enough food and water will eventually succumb to starvation and dehydration. A character who is starving or dehydrated cannot recover Vigor or Durability until they eat and drink the full required amount and complete a break.

At regular exercise and exertion levels, a medium-sized creature needs 1 lb. of food per day. A character can go without food for 1 day for every 10 points of Stamina while active and using energy. A character can last longer by spending at least half their day resting or eating only half rations. Each day at half rations counts as half a day without food, and each day spent fully resting requires only half as much food.

At the end of the first full day beyond this period without food, and for each full day after, a starving character suffers 1 rank of Fatigue. They can attempt to overcome that fatigue through normal rest.

A character needs one liter of water per day to survive, or two liters per day if they are very active or the average temperature is hot or scorching. A character can last 1 day for every 15 points of Stamina before serious dehydration sets in. Beyond this, each day without enough water, a character suffers 1 rank of Fatigue.

Virtually all characters will need to breathe, and underwater or in a vacuum may suffocate without air. Most creatures can hold their breath for 1 minute, plus 1 minute per Stamina Bonus dice they have. Each minute beyond this consumes 30 Vigor. Once Vigor is depleted and there is no air, creatures lose consciousness and go into shock. If the creature is not recovered in a number of turns equal to its Stamina score, it dies. A creature that is recovered in time can be resuscitated with a Medicine check, VS 20.
Even the mightiest beings require respite, and Durability, Health, Vigor, and Mana take time to recover.

Break. This is a rest period of at least 2 hours. When you take a break, you engage in light activity, such as walking, cooking, maintenance, reading, meditation, or resting. A break will recover all your Vigor and 1 Durability per point of Stamina. If you have a Stamina bonus, you can roll your bonus dice once per day and recover that much Durability during a break. You will recover 5 points of Mana during a break unless you have Meditation. A break will only recover 2 points of Health without medical treatment.

Sleep. A good night's rest of at least eight hours will recover all your Vigor and Durability and 10 points of Health. If you have a Stamina bonus, you may roll those dice and recover that much additional Health. While you sleep, you will recover 20 points of Mana. If you have any bonus dice from your Soul Score, you may roll those dice and recover that much Mana while you sleep. Even if a creature were to sleep for days, you can only gain these benefits once per day.
Given this rate, extremely powerful creatures often must rest for weeks or months to fully recover after a serious battle.

You won't spend every moment of every day battling evil, causing trouble, and hunting loot. There will be quiet moments of downtime between adventures where you may want to engage in other activities. These fleeting moments might be described by the GM in some detail, narrating key events and interactions that occur, or they may simply be quickly synopsized as the prologue or postlude to your adventures.

Food, drink, and lodging all cost something. Unless you own property or live aboard your transportation, you will need to pay for a place to stay between adventures. This represents your cost of living. During downtime of any significant length, you'll need to spend enough to support yourself at a lifestyle of your choosing.

There are several ways you can employ the time between adventures. If you are injured, you might spend the time in recuperation, but otherwise you may want to engage in practical research, work in a mundane occupation, exercise one of your skills, or train your body and mind.

Whether as an artist or an engineer, it should be no surprise that adventurers might have the skill to create any number of things, be it practical works or the arcane or scientific. You can use your downtime to make or modify your equipment, create goods to earn income, or simply make something for the joy of it.

As a rule, creating any kind of tool or equipment requires at least +1 rank in the appropriate academic or practical skill, or the appropriate talent. Unless specified by your talents, you create feeble quality goods. It costs 30% the value of what you are trying to make in raw materials, though you are not required to spend money to acquire the materials; you can gather them from raw sources or salvage the components from damaged equipment.
It requires 1 hour of work for each point of the VS needed to create the work in question. A failed roll indicates the time is wasted, but the next attempt receives +1 bonus dice.
Large and complicated constructs, such as vehicles, require multiple skill rolls for each of the sub-components (engines, controls, chassis).
Specific formulas, blueprints, or other objects will have their own VS specified. Otherwise, the VS to craft or build something is largely dependent on the object:

With access to a suitable source of information, it is possible to spend the time to learn important information, designs for new equipment, and new techniques or spells through research and investigation. This kind of self-education assumes that you don't have a teacher, and that you are learning material on your own. If you are being trained, see Training below.

Attempting to learn about an academic or practical subject, such as learning a language, recipe, or design, requires 20 total hours of study in a period no less than one full week. At the end of this time, you are entitled to a Studies (Memory) check against a VS 20 to learn this subject competently. A failed roll requires an additional 10 hours of study, and another roll.

Attempting to learn a spell, technique, or formula for which you meet all the requirements requires the same amount of time, but you must succeed a Memory skill check under your relevant skill, such as Occult for spells or Unarmed for martial arts. The VS will be set by the rank of the ability you are attempting to learn. A failed roll means you need another 20 hours of study and practice to master the knowledge. Learning a spell or technique from another school requires 40 total hours spent over no less than one month, and no more than six, and is much more difficult, imposing 2 Challenges on your Memory skill check.

While you are between adventures, you can use your time to train and improve. There are two types of training available: the first is to practice a skill or ability in order to gain experience, and the second is to learn a spell or technique from a master. Training with a master can provide additional bonus dice based on your master.

Personal improvement requires a place to practice your skill and train. It takes at least 20 hours over no less than one week and no more than a month to gain the benefits of practice. At the end of this time, you gain 10 Exp per level of experience. You can gain the benefits of practice and training up to twice each month.

Learning an ability requires 10 hours to be spent with the master over no less than one week. During this time, you learn and practice the language, technique, or spell, and at the end make a Memory skill check under your relevant skill, such as your Studies, Light Weapons, or Occult. The VS will be set by the rank of the ability you are attempting to learn.

If you are trying to learn a spell or technique from a school that you do not have access to, you will need to spend 20 hours with your master over a period of no less than one week, and succeed your check with 2 Challenges.

Provided you have the time, you can also work to earn money. How much you can earn per day in a general service industry or as a laborer is up to your GM. As a general rule, you should be able to make enough to cover a poor cost of living for a week with two days of labor, maybe more if you're well-versed in a highly sought-after skill, such as Medicine or Crafting.

In the Realms of Ouroboros, combat is the test that cultivation prepares you for. Every Aura Star you ignite, every Mana Circle you inscribe, every Edge you sharpen, every Mystery you carry — all of them have a moment in the small, dangerous space of a fight where their worth is decided. Whether the battle is a sect-master's duel under formal witness, a bandit ambush on a forest road, a hunt for an eldritch incursion in a fog-shrouded marsh, or the heat-cracked clash of warriors on a battlefield where banners are still being raised, the same rules apply. Skill, cultivation, and a willingness to risk everything in the right moment are what carry a cultivator through. Read carefully. The fight begins on the next page.

Combat in the Promethium System is designed to be tactical, thrilling, and deeply engaging, drawing on the intense and dynamic battles of the cultivation tradition that shapes Radiant Age. Here, every action counts, and your choices will shape the outcome of every encounter.

At its core, combat focuses on teamwork, positioning, and leveraging your unique abilities to outsmart and outmaneuver your enemies. Each turn, you'll make important decisions: will you attack, support an ally, reposition to gain a better angle, or use one of your signature Techniques or Spells? Your Disciplines are the heart of your arsenal, fueled by Vigor or Mana, and enhanced by the cultivated engines you have developed — Aura Stars, Mana Circles, Edge Dice, Mysteries, Vyana, or Chalices, depending on the path you walk.

Actions in combat are resolved using a d20 roll, combined with any Bonus Dice from your skills, ability scores, and other sources. Your goal is to meet or exceed the Victory Sum (VS) set by the GM. Success means your action works as planned, while Bursting Bonus Dice (rolling maximum values) amplifies the effects.
Conflict isn't just physical. Whether it's breaking an enemy's morale or persuading a stubborn rival, social and mental challenges use the same mechanics to let all characters shine.
The following is a sample of combat play, illustrating how Actions, Reactions, Bonus Dice, Chances, and the small mechanical moments combine into a moving, tactical scene at the table.

Three companions have been ambushed on the temple road by half a dozen bandits. The party consists of Khaeli (a Knight with 2 Aura Stars and Aura Blade active), Toren (a Mage with 2 Mana Circles, casting through Incantation), and Mira (a Rogue with 3 Edge Dice and Awakened Edge Ranged Specialization). The bandits have taken cover behind overturned wagons; their leader stands on the lead wagon, weapon raised, shouting orders.
The GM rolls initiative. Khaeli rolls a 17 — first to act. Toren rolls 12, then the bandit leader 11, then Mira 9. The bandits go on shared lower initiatives.

Khaeli acts. She charges the lead wagon — using her first Action to move 10 feet (her base Speed) plus 15 feet (3 Speed Bonus Dice) into adjacency with the bandit leader. Her second Action: a basic Aura Blade attack with her longsword. She rolls 1d20 + 2 Bonus Dice (Heavy Weapons skill, 1 BD) + Strength BD (1 BD) = 14+5+8 = 27, against the bandit leader's Guard of 15. She hits. Damage: 1d10 weapon + 2 Aura Star BD (heroic d10) + 1 Strength BD = 8+6+9+3 = 26. One of her Aura BD Bursts (rolled a 10), so she rolls another d10: an 8. Total damage: 26 + 8 = 34. The bandit leader's Durability drops fast.

Toren is next. He decides to support Mira. His first Action: cast Mana Mark (a Basic-rank Incantare spell) on the bandit leader to mark them as a primary target — he rolls 1d20 + 1 BD (Occult) + 1 BD (Soul) = 13+8+10 = 31, against the bandit's Ward of 15. Success. The leader is now marked; any ally targeting them gains +1 BD. His second Action: cast Flame Bolt at a bandit hiding behind the lower wagon. Roll 14 + 7 + 8 = 29 against Guard 15. Hit. Damage 2d8 + 2 Circle BD (d10) = 6+5+8+4 = 23.
The bandit leader acts. He takes the Defense reaction in anticipation of further attacks, entering Guard Stance (5 Vigor). His second Action: he commands his nearest bandit to fire at Khaeli.

Mira acts. She has a clean shot at the marked bandit leader. She decides to take 2 Chances on the attack — increasing the VS by 30 but gaining 2 Charges (her Bonus Dice and damage scale up two ranks each). She rolls 1d20 + 2 BD (Ranged Weapons, Aim) + 1 BD (Toren's mark) = 16+8+9+8 = 41, against an increased Guard of 45 (15 base + 30 from Chances). She misses by 4 — the bandit leader still has Khaeli's Aura cuts to contend with, but Mira's shot whistles past. Toren and Khaeli exchange a look across the smoke.
Then the bandits act, and the round continues...
Victory is about more than just rolling dice. Plan ahead, work with your team, and use your character's strengths to turn every challenge into triumph.

Every action that requires a roll follows the same formula: 1d20 (the action dice) + any bonus dice against a Victory Sum. VS Bonus Dice will be d10 unless you are wounded or suffering Challenges to the roll, in which case they will drop a step, from d10 to d8 and from d8 to d6, and if you are particularly hindered, from d6 to d4. Bonus dice cannot be reduced below d4, and nothing can ever reduce your action dice, only your bonus dice.

Bonus dice can be added from having skill bonuses, talents, or high ability scores. When something increases your bonus dice rank, such as having a Charge on an action roll or having a special ability, all bonus dice from all sources are increased one rank — from d10 to d12 and from d12 to d20. Charges will counter Challenges on a one-for-one basis, negating each other.

When you add bonus dice to an effect roll such as damage, these bonus dice will only benefit from the effects of a Charge and only suffer from a Challenge if explicitly stated so. For example, being wounded reduces the rank of all bonus dice to action and effect rolls, but attempting to hit a distant enemy with a ranged weapon only Challenges the bonus dice added to the attack roll.

Adventuring is dangerous business. You will find that often situations can only be solved with conflict or violence. When this happens, the situation is called an Encounter. Encounters may be planned in advance by the GM or be a chaotic happening of chance. There will be occasions where the player character's own actions escalate a social situation into an encounter.

An encounter is divided into Turns and Rounds. A turn is one creature's series of actions on their initiative. A round ends once all creatures in an encounter have taken their turns. Each combat round is approximately 15 seconds in game, though it might take a couple minutes of real time to sort out.

Turn. In the heat of combat, a turn only lasts a few seconds, but in that time you will use all your actions. Once all your actions have been taken, it is the next character or creature's turn, depending on the order of initiative.

Round. Unlike a turn, a round is defined as the period it takes for a group of people to exhaust all their actions, around 15 seconds. Any effect that lists duration in rounds assumes the unit to be a 15-second block. If an act requires more actions than you have on your turn, you must use actions from the following turns to complete the act, such as powerful spells.

On your turn, you may take as many actions as you have. This can be any combination; you may use all your actions to attack, mix attack actions and movement, movement and skills, or any combination thereof. Only Casting spells and using techniques may require more than one action.

When you take an action that affects another creature, such as making an attack or attempting to persuade them, the target is entitled to a reaction, such as Defense. If they have no reactions available to them, the action continues unopposed, and effect is applied.
Once all creatures have taken their actions and reactions in order of initiative, recovery is applied, and the next round begins.

Recovery. At the end of your turn, you will recover a set amount of Vigor and potentially other resources such as Durability and Health. This is the recovery phase. During this time, resource regeneration is applied, as is your chance to overcome effects.
Destined. A destined character can unfold their destiny to take a single out-of-turn action during a non-player turn. However, as it is not their turn, some action types may be unavailable.

When combat begins, all participants who wish to act roll their action dice, 1d20. This is your initiative for the round. Talents or skills might add bonus dice, as might situational modifiers. Each round, all fighters act in order of their initiative roll from highest to lowest.

An ambush situation is when one set of combatants is intentionally ambushed by another set of combatants, such as lying in wait, attacking from a blind spot, or attacking from a hidden or invisible position. When you are ambushed, you may only take 1 Action at the beginning of the first round.

The opposite side of a surprised party; a combatant gains an ambush bonus when they have purposefully been waiting for an unsuspecting target. When the attack is launched, each ambusher gains 1 extra Action for the first round.

A combatant that is actively expecting an attack from a narrow vector is considered in Overwatch. In this state, they are a bit jumpy and gain +1 bonus dice to initiative on the first turn of combat so long as it initiated from the expected attack vector. Conversely, if an attack is initiated from another vector, the Overwatch combatant is considered ambushed.

An incapacitated character is not as much a combatant as a victim. Incapacitated can mean unconscious, bound or tied, or pinned in such a manner as to be unable to move or act. In such a situation, the character can take no actions that require physical movement, cannot Dodge or Defend, and if attacked will likely be killed outright.

When you make an attack against another creature, whether it is with a weapon, a technique, or a spell, you roll your action dice (1d20) plus any bonus dice you have from your skill bonuses and ability scores. This is usually Agility for weapons and hand-to-hand, and Soul for magical spells and workings. An attack must meet or exceed the target's Guard or Ward. Any roll less than this is a miss.
To land a physical attack with a weapon, martial technique, or weapon-like magic, you must roll higher than your target's Guard, which is 15 plus any bonuses from armor and abilities.
To land a magical spell or working, you must roll higher than your opponent's Ward, which is 15 plus any bonuses from talents and abilities.

Once an attack is rolled, the defender has the option to use a reaction, such as Defense or Resistance. Reactions consume Vigor or Mana but allow the defender to counter roll their action dice plus bonus dice. If the defender rolls higher than the attacker, they successfully defend and avoid the attack. If the attacker and defender tie, the contest goes to the attacker, and the attack hits.

You begin with 2 Actions. On your turn, you may take as many actions as you have. You gain additional actions through upgrading skill trees and learning talents. On your turn, you can take the following actions:
Attack. You make an attack with a weapon in hand or with your own natural weapons. An attack roll is your action dice plus any bonus dice against your target's Guard.

Off-hand Attack. When you take an attack action or use a light weapon technique on your turn, you may consume 8 Vigor to make an Off-Hand attack as part of the same action. The Off-Hand attack suffers a 2 Challenge penalty to your bonus dice to hit and damage. This may be a weapon attack if you are holding a weapon in your off-hand, or unarmed attack.

Combat Technique. If you are skilled in the weapon you are wielding, you can do more than simply attack. Skilled fighters can employ combat techniques, such as power attacks, grapples, melee shots, and feints.

Defend. When you defend, you enter the Guard Stance, and position yourself to react easily to an attack you suspect is coming, reducing the Vigor cost of your first defensive reaction until the beginning of your next turn by 2.
Escape. You flee from combat. Adjacent attackers may react to your attempt to escape as you create an opening when you flee. Otherwise, you are removed from the encounter and continue moving away.

Move. You move your combat movement distance; this is 10 feet + 5 feet for each of your Speed Bonus Dice (2 hexes plus 1 hex for each Speed Bonus Dice). You can take as many Move actions as you choose. For example, if you have three actions, you could use them to move up to an enemy (first action), attack that enemy with your weapon (second action), and then move away (third action). Regardless of how many move actions you take, each action can only take you 10 feet + 5 feet for each of your Speed Bonus Dice.

Ready. In a way, defend is a kind of Ready action. When you ready an action, you set a singular condition you are waiting for and what you want to do when and if that condition occurs before the beginning of your next turn. If that condition occurs, you may use a reaction to respond to that event with the action you readied. You can only prepare 1 action with Ready, abilities that call for multiple actions cannot be readied, and you may only have 1 readied action prepared on any given turn.
Use Ability. If you have techniques, spells, or talents you can activate, you can use them on your turn. Some abilities require more than one action to use, and most consume Vigor or Mana.
Use Item. You use an item that is located on your person, such as a potion, an inscribed talisman, or a magic item.
Use Skill. You use one of your skills. You could attempt to use Social to charm an enemy, use Awareness to find a weakness, or use Entertain to bolster your allies.
All creatures have two defensive values that both start at 15: Guard and Ward.
Guard represents your passive defensive ability, and generally how hard it is to hit you with a weapon or weapon-like attacks. Most attacks are resolved against a target's Guard.
Ward represents your willpower and ability to overcome assaults that affect a target without needing to hit them. This includes many spells and magical attacks, poisons, and even pain.

When a creature takes an action against you, in many cases you are entitled to a reaction. However, reactions consume Vigor, which limits the number of reactions you may take in an encounter. You may only take one reaction to any trigger. If you are attacked, you may only take one reaction against that attack. If an opponent creates an opening, you only take that opening once.

Defense (Dodge, Deflect, or Block). All creatures may take this reaction when they are subject to an attack they are aware of. Roll your action dice (1d20) plus any bonus dice you have from Speed. If you score higher than your attacker's roll, you successfully evade the attack. Using the Defense reaction consumes 5 Vigor.

Resistance (Mental, Physical, and Spiritual). All creatures can make a Resistance reaction check when they are subject to non-physical magic. Roll your action dice (1d20) plus any bonuses from the target ability score, such as Resolve or Stamina. If you roll higher than your attacker's casting roll, you successfully overcome the magic. Using a Resistance reaction consumes 5 Mana.

Take Opening. When an opponent creates an Opening, if you are adjacent or within range, you may consume 5 Vigor to make an attack with a weapon or unarmed strike. When you use some combat techniques, attempt to flee, and some other situations, you create an opening for others to attack.

Readied Action. When you ready an action on your turn, you may use your reaction to an event to perform your readied action. You may only ready 1 action at any given time, and you may only take 1 reaction to any event.
Special Reactions. Many skill trees and talents might give you additional reactions or change how you roll reactions, such as Deflect and Block.
In combat, being Destined is a powerful edge. While Destined you can unfold your destiny at any time to:

items: Enhance an Action with +3 Bonus Dice. This is great for critical hits, resistance rolls, or defense.
Take an Action immediately, such as attacking, moving, or reacting, even outside your turn.
Restore ¼ of a core resource: Durability, Vigor, or Mana, keeping you in the fight.
Trigger your limit breaks, unleashing your character's ultimate ability.

blocks.html: Destined is meant to reflect turning points; use it in dramatic moments, when your character's will allows them to break through the limits of skill or luck.
You cannot become Destined from unfolding your destiny or on the same turn that you do, but taking Chances and being daring can ensure you are Destined.

Beyond simply attacking and defending, combat techniques are maneuvers that you may employ if you are skilled in the weapon you are using. Techniques may attack, defend, change your stance, or change your position. When you use a technique, it will consume some amount of your Vigor. If you have insufficient Vigor to pay the cost of an ability, you are unable to use that technique.
These are fundamental defensive reactions that anyone with Novice Defense may make use of.
Vigor: 8
You evade an incoming attack by repositioning. On success, the attack misses, and you move 5 feet in any direction.
Vigor: 8

You use your weapon or shield to deflect an attack. Roll Defense, and add your equipment bonus (e.g., shield, parrying weapon) to the check. On success, the attack is nullified; on a tie, damage is reduced by half.
Vigor: 5

You take the hit head-on, bracing against it. Roll Defense with +1 Bonus Die. On success, reduce the incoming damage by 1d8, regardless of weapon traits. On failure, you still reduce incoming damage by your Armor Rank.
These are the basic reactions that anyone with the Novice Resistance talent can employ.
Mana: 8

You resist physical non-weapon assaults (grapples, stuns, knockdowns, poison gas) through sheer brawn. Even on a failure you reduce the duration of the condition by 1 turn, or impose 1 Challenge on the attacker to maintain the assault.
Mana: 8

You steel your mind against fear, charms, illusions, or psychic intrusion. On a success you not only nullify the assault, you become immune to assaults that call for Memory, Empathy, Reason, or Resolve until the end of your next turn.
Mana: Special

As a reaction to an opponent in sight casting a spell or ritual with a discernible casting window that targets you, you may attempt a Counterspell Resistance Reaction. Unlike a normal resistance reaction, before you roll you must declare your Mana spent. The caster resolves their casting roll (Occult) as normal. You then compare your Mana spent: if it is at least ¼ or more of the casting cost, you may roll your Counterspell Resistance, and if you beat the casting roll the spell is completely nullified. If your spend is at least 2× the casting cost, the magic rebounds onto the caster on a success.
These fundamental combat maneuvers are available to any character with the Novice Light Weapons, Heavy Weapons, or Hand-to-Hand talents.
Action Cost. Unless stated otherwise, a technique takes 1 action on your turn.
Vigor: 6

You launch a brutal all-out attack on an opponent. By consuming 6 Vigor, you can add +1 bonus dice to the damage of your attack. If you chain a Rush followed by a Power Attack, you add an additional +1 bonus dice of damage.
Vigor: 3+

A Feint is a tactical bluff; you give all appearance of one kind of attack while positioning for another. When you use this maneuver, you may inflict 1 to 3 Challenges on your opponent's defense reaction roll against the attack. Each Challenge you inflict consumes 3 Vigor. If the target chooses not to defend, the feint becomes increasingly lethal. For each Challenge you chose to inflict toward defense, you add 1 weapon dice of damage to your attack against a target that does not defend.
Vigor: 6

The Grapple technique is a setup and does no damage; however, it changes the stance of both you and your target. Make an attack roll with your weapon or unarmed skill. On a hit, you have grappled your opponent. Following a successful grapple, you may make one off-hand or secondary weapon attack that cannot be dodged or use a follow-up technique. Grapple is required to perform any kind of hold, casting throw, or pin. After the first turn, you may choose to maintain the grapple for 2 Vigor. No roll is required unless the target attempts to break the hold with a Resistance (Strength) check VS your Strength Score.
Vigor: 5

Unarmed versus Armed: While both hands are empty, you may attempt to take an enemy's weapon with a melee attack. You make your Hand-to-Hand attack roll normally. If the attack fails or the target successfully defends, you create an opening for that opponent, and the target may make an attack against you. Otherwise, you disarm them and now have their main-hand weapon.

Armed versus Armed: When two armed opponents face off, the Disarm functions similarly: you make your attack roll, and the target defends. On a success, your opponent's weapon is knocked from their hand, flying 5 feet (1 hex) in a random direction. However, the defender is not entitled to take an opening in the case that you fail to disarm them.
Vigor: 6

Usually performed with a kick or a weapon such as a staff or spear, but any weapon can trip. With a Trip technique you strike, sweep, or tangle the opponent's legs to remove their support, causing them to fall. The advantage of a trip maneuver over a tackle is that the attacker maintains distance and balance. Trip is performed like a regular attack: if successful, the target is knocked down. Targets may attempt to defend themselves normally.
Vigor: 9

You blitz a target with a full sprint, moving at least half your total Speed, either slamming into the target or using another technique as you hit them. If you only spend 1 action to Rush, you bodyslam the target, dealing unarmed damage and knocking them down. If you have the actions, you can spend 1 action as a follow-up to your Rush to attack with a weapon or use another technique. If you hit with your follow-up, the target is knocked down. If you Rush an enemy and follow up with a Power Attack, you deal +1 bonus dice of damage to your Power Attack.
These basic ranged techniques are available to anyone with a +1 rank in an Aim skill. These focus not only on aiming ranged weapons but the realities of fighting in mixed combat situations.
Vigor: 9

You strike an adjacent enemy with your ranged weapon and then shoot them. This attack requires a melee attack roll using your ranged weapon skill and Agility. If your target makes a successful Deflect of the melee attack (but not other defense reactions), the shot is wasted. Otherwise, on a hit you deal 1d6 (or bayonet) damage from the melee strike, and unless they deflect the target takes normal ranged weapon damage, and receives 1 Charge to your weapon damage Bonus Dice.
If you follow a Rush with a Body Shot you may add your Strength Bonus Dice to the strike damage.
Vigor: 15

With this technique you make a ranged attack in melee while using your ranged weapon, and immediately disengage. Your attack receives 1 Challenge to hit. However, at the end of this action you have backed away 10 ft. At this point you could flee and you will not create an opening.
Vigor: 15

This melee shot is used to trip then finish an enemy. You make a swift feint, then trip your opponent with your weapon. This is a Ranged Weapon (Strength) check to hit. Unless the opponent defends, creatures of your size or smaller are knocked down and become prone, and you immediately fire a prepared shot to which you receive 1 Charge to your weapon damage Bonus Dice. If you miss the initial trip, the technique is wasted.
Vigor: 10

This is practically a trick shot, as you fire your weapon to cause the projectile's trajectory to curve laterally. This allows you to "fire around corners," ignoring the protection of targets with Partial Cover, so long as the target is within long range.

The following basic martial arts techniques are available to anyone with the Novice Hand-to-Hand talent. These techniques use weight, leverage, and spin to add power to unarmed attacks, such as punches, elbow strikes, kicks, knee strikes, and throws.
Vigor: 3

A fast hard punch with your leading hand (the hand closest to the opponent). While it does no more damage than an ordinary unarmed strike (not counting modifiers), the key to a jab is that it does not telegraph — give away the attack — making it harder to defend against. Opponents attempting to defend against your Invisible Jab do so with 1 Challenge.
Vigor: 6

Usually reserved for close quarters, this strike is an effective way to deliver force into an opponent, as well as through protective covering. A Shattering Palm blow ignores Armor Rank against creatures, and deals double damage to objects and constructs.
Vigor: 6

The Launch Kick has two uses: as an attack to off-balance or repel a standing opponent, or as a devastating blow against stationary targets and objects. The launch kick is performed by raising the knee to the chest and then launching the foot out, sole first, into the center of an opponent. Against standing opponents, the kick does 1 point of damage for each of your Strength bonus dice on a hit, and a target that is your size or smaller is knocked down and pushed back 5 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Strength Bonus Dice. Against stationary or restrained targets, such as an opponent tied to a tree or a locked door, you deal full unarmed damage +1 bonus dice of damage.
Vigor: 5

Much like the Launch Kick, the attack is performed by raising the knee to the chest and launching the foot out. However, the power comes from the speed and legs in the Snap Kick, not the hips and weight. Further, the Snap Kick usually uses only the ball of the foot to make contact. A Snap Kick does not add Strength Bonus Dice to damage, but it is fast and recovers quickly, allowing you to make two kicks for 1 Action.
Vigor: 9

Named so because in this maneuver the martial artist makes a full rotation of the body, drawing the leg up and spinning the whole body on the ball of the planted foot. The spin ends with the shin used as the contact point for the attack. In a Heel Kick, the maneuver is performed by rotating in the opposite direction, making contact with the heel. Slow and easy to dodge, the attack is nonetheless devastating, dealing +2 bonus dice damage. Defenders receive a Charge on their defense rolls against a roundhouse kick.
Vigor: 6

This maneuver begins exactly like a side kick; however, rather than launch the foot directly at the opponent, you kick to the side and then whip the foot back, heel first, into the defender. This attack is difficult to defend against, resulting in a 1 Challenge penalty to the target's Defense roll.
Vigor: 5

Often considered the throw of choice for grappling martial arts, this difficult throw involves using an incoming opponent's inertia and force to throw them over the shoulder. Unlike most techniques, you may use the Shoulder Throw only as a reaction; the character attempting this throw must roll Defense (Block) check against an incoming melee attack with a 1 Challenge penalty. If successful, the attack is blocked, and the opponent is lifted off the ground, over your shoulder, and tossed to the ground. The attacker takes a flat 1d10 impact damage from the fall as well as being rendered prone.
Vigor: 6

This throw involves using the manipulation of the target's center of gravity to spin and toss them to the ground. Usually this involves lowering one's own center and using the hips for leverage or as a fulcrum. On a hit, you throw the target to the ground for a flat 1d10 impact damage, and they are rendered prone.
Vigor: 7

This technique uses pure strength to lift and hurl a grappled opponent. To perform this technique, you must grapple the target, and they must be within your maximum lift value. You have two options at this point:
Head throw: You toss the target 5 feet away. The target takes 2d10 impact damage (no bonus dice, +1d10 per size above medium) and is prone.

Targeted throw: You use the target as a weapon and throw them at another target, requiring a normal ranged weapon attack roll. As long as the target of the thrown victim is the same size or smaller, the target and thrown victim take 1d10 damage, and both are rendered prone. If the target of the thrown victim is larger, the thrown creature takes 1d10 damage and is rendered prone, while the target is unaffected. The range of the thrown victim is 5 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Strength Bonus Dice.

Throughout the round, you and your opponents may take actions to move and change their position or suffer from attacks that knock them back or knock them down. This can change whom you can hit with a melee weapon or attack, how you resolve an attack, or how you move.

In a combat encounter, a creature's Engagement Zone is a 5-foot (1-hex radius) area around them. Moving out of this zone without disengaging can create an Opening, and provoke a Reaction. Creatures with Reach increase this zone to 10 feet (2 hexes).

Adjacent. Creatures are considered adjacent when they are positioned within 5 feet of another creature and have a clear path between them. On a hexagonal grid a creature can be adjacent to up to 6 other creatures. Cover, walls, and obstacles prevent creatures from being considered adjacent.

Behind. Attacking and defending from the front, sides, or behind carries no special rules. However, being attacked from behind while you are unaware and attacking an opponent from behind who is unaware impacts Guard. When you are attacked from behind while unaware of your enemy, your Guard is reduced by half, rounding up. When you are attacking an opponent from behind, your opponent's Guard is reduced by half, rounding up. Generally, this means that a rear attack must beat a Guard of 8, rather than 15.

Small, medium, and large creatures have a reach of 5 feet (1 hex); this is their engagement zone. This is how close they need to be to initiate a melee attack with a weapon or hand-to-hand without using an action to move on your turn. This is also the distance at which an enemy attempting to flee, or who otherwise exposes themselves, creates an opening. Weapons such as polearms and chains might change this.

A character who is hit hard enough or has the ground taken from them is knocked down. This event negates the character's action if it happens on their turn, and they must spend an action getting up from their prone position.
While you are knocked down and prone, your Speed is reduced to ¼ for calculating your movement and your Guard is reduced by 5. However, ranged attacks against you are made with a Challenge.

Combat actions that change your position, or your opponent's position, change your stance. Stances rarely have an immediate effect; rather, they prepare for follow-up attacks. For example, the Guard Stance will reduce the cost of Defending, while using Grapple changes both your and your opponent's stance, and allows you to follow up with powerful attacks, throws, or incapacitate your opponents. Some abilities may only be available once you have taken a specific stance.

If your group uses miniatures, tokens, or virtual tabletop software, then using a hex or square grid provides clear spatial rules for movement, positioning, reach, and area effects. Each hex or square is 5 feet across. A character's movement is expressed in feet and hexes, making it easy to plan routes, control space, and execute strategies during combat.

Throughout each round, you will consume Vigor and Mana to use abilities you have, and as you are attacked, you will lose Durability and potentially Health. But after each round, there is a moment where you will recover some energy needed to continue the battle.
Many actions may require you to consume Vigor. If you exhaust your Vigor, you become Winded, and you cannot take any actions. This condition remains until you recover at least 1 point of Vigor.
At the end of their turn, all combatants gain the benefits of their recovery.
Vigor. At the end of your turn you recover 1 Vigor for each of your Stamina Bonus Dice.
Durability. If you have a special ability, you may recover some Durability.
Mana. You may recover some depleted Mana if you have a special ability.

Weapons and offensive abilities that deal damage have a Damage Value, usually just called damage. The damage value is usually just a flat value, such as 1 or 3. This is the number of dice rolled for damage with that weapon or ability. The dice rolled depends on the quality rank of the attack; an ordinary sword deals d8, a heroic axe deals d10, and so on.
When you score a hit with an unarmed attack, a melee weapon — or some ranged weapons if you have the appropriate talent — you add your Strength Bonus Dice to damage.
Provided you are sufficiently skilled and have the requisite talents, when you score a hit with a crossbow, modern weapon, or magic projectile you add your Skill Bonus Dice to damage.

When you are injured, you take damage. Living creatures can sustain an amount of damage before they are wounded and before they go into shock. Damage is first subtracted from your Durability. When your Durability is reduced to 0, you are wounded, and you begin to subtract damage from your Health.

Durability. Damage to Durability is generally not life-threatening. This represents your ability to shrug off superficial attacks as well as power through injuries. Durability recovers quickly unless you are wounded.

Wounded. Once you have taken enough damage to reduce your Durability to 0, you become wounded. In the wounded state, some skills or talents may come into effect, and you become vulnerable to some techniques and spells. The most important effect of becoming wounded, however, is that you no longer recover Durability quickly, and your bonus dice move down one step (heroic d10 become ordinary d8, etc.). Any damage you take once you are wounded is potentially life-threatening damage to your Health.

Health. Once you are wounded, or if you are subject to some special attacks like poisons, any damage you take is done to your Health. Your Health recovers very slowly. When your Health is reduced to 0, you lose consciousness and will die unless you receive immediate medical attention. Any damage while you are at 0 Health will kill you instantly.

Roll-Over Damage. If you suffer an attack that would take you to negative Durability, that damage is carried on to Health once your Durability reaches 0. However, if you have any Health and suffer an attack that would reduce you to negative Health, you instead are reduced to 0 Health and are in shock.
All bonus dice Burst when you roll maximum value on the dice, including bonus dice to damage. When one or more of your bonus dice to damage Burst, it is called a critical hit.

Protective armor provides a reduction to damage known as Armor Rank. You reduce your incoming damage by an amount equal to your Armor Rank. If you have two or more Armor Ranks from separate sources, you can combine them. For example, if you have a magical effect granting you an Armor Rank of 6, and you are also wearing light armor with an Armor Rank of 4, your total Armor Rank is 10. However, you normally cannot layer armor of the same type or the same magic that grants Armor Rank.

Weapons and armor both have a quality. When an attack from a superior weapon hits inferior armor, it ignores that armor. Conversely, when an inferior weapon strikes superior armor, the armor will take no damage. Beginning at Heroic, weapons ignore armor of three steps lower than the weapon's quality, and armor becomes immune to damage from weapons of three steps or more ranks lower quality.

Likewise, exceptionally dangerous adversaries and powerful abilities may grant superior Durability, such as Heroic, Epic or even Legendary Durability. Anything with this trait is immune to damage from weapons that are not of the same or higher rank. To harm a creature with Heroic Durability requires a weapon of Heroic or higher quality; to harm a creature with Epic Durability requires a weapon of Epic or higher quality. Generally, energy damage will bypass superior Durability, but not always.

All attacks, spells, and effects in Radiant Age fall into one of several elemental or kinetic types. Understanding these properties can be crucial for exploiting enemy weaknesses or bolstering your own defenses. Damage types for weapons and attacks come into play when protection functions better or worse against specific types of attacks.

For example: normal armor might protect against fire damage just like it will a weapon. Conversely, lightning damage sometimes passes through regular armor, but rubberized or insulated armor may have a high Armor Rank against lightning attacks. Furthermore, enemies may have an Armor Rank only against a specific damage type, or complete immunity.

The damage type of pure magic, or the magical energy of the existential realm, aetheric or astral damage comes from attacks that originate from pure eldritch energy. Unlike other elemental damage, aetheric damage is its own opposite.
When a target is flash-cooled, such as through exposure to a super-frozen liquid or by magic that strips away heat, it suffers cold damage. Freezing can make material brittle, but not always.
This type of damage comes from powerful acids or bases that quickly burn and erode a material or flesh.
This is the damage type of order and time. Sometimes called time damage, crystal damage comes from sources of eldritch law, space, and time. As such, it is opposite to entropic damage.

Associated with chaos and decay, entropic damage is the damage of eldritch disorder and fear. Fluid and free, entropic damage is not necessarily negative, but chaotic and dynamic. This makes entropic damage opposite to crystal damage.
More than just fire, thermal damage can include magic energies, solar rays, and plasma. Fire damage can weaken materials or ignite fires.

Considered the default types of damage for mundane weapons, virtually nothing is immune specifically to kinetic damage. However, there are specific types of kinetic damage: slashing for blades and axes; piercing for some blades, arrows, and sling bullets; and impact for hammers, clubs, and fists.
Sometimes called electric damage; exposure to massive electrical direct current discharges is extremely dangerous. This type of damage is found in lightning strikes and plasma.

Often associated with light, life, and the animating force, lucent damage can also be devastating and destructive, even if its nature most often stuns and dazes. Lucent damage is opposed to umbral damage.
Sonic or vibration-frequency damage caused by cascading sound waves through a medium. This damage is normally impossible at range through a vacuum, and can stun and disorient creatures.
Any damage that is toxic is biological in nature — although its delivery mechanism may not be. Pure toxic damage may not necessarily damage armor and instead only harm living creatures.

The power of decay, death, and darkness, umbral damage is the power of death itself. While the dead have little to fear from it, living things are at risk. Umbral damage is diametric to lucent damage by nature.

Most humanoid creatures don't have natural weapons like claws and fangs. For creatures like humans and humanoids, unarmed attacks, such as punches and kicks, deal 1 feeble (d4) impact damage (plus Strength Bonus Dice) unless you are trained in martial arts. Exceptional martial prowess can increase this amount of damage substantially.

Everything has Durability, even objects. When you attack an object or construct, you deal damage to its Durability. Some objects composed of particularly hard material might have a natural Armor Rank or superior quality as well. In the case of objects, inferior quality weapons simply do no damage to the superior quality object. Especially complicated objects, such as animate constructs and piloted machines, may also have Integrity, in place of Health. Otherwise, when an inanimate object's Durability is depleted, it is destroyed beyond meaningful use.

Adventure is dangerous work. Monsters, men, and disasters can all threaten your life. When you are reduced to 0 Health, you do not die instantly, but you do go into shock and are in serious risk of death.
A creature in shock is on death's door and will die in a number of rounds equal to its Stamina Score. If you take any additional damage while you are at 0 Health, you are killed outright.

A creature in shock can be stabilized by making a successful Medicine check VS 20. If you are stabilized, you will not die in a set number of rounds; however, you remain unconscious until you recover at least 1 point of Health. Taking any damage while at 0 Health, even if stabilized, will instantly kill a creature.

There are several impairments and handicaps that can befall a character. When you are under the effects of one of these conditions, you will suffer some mechanically significant disadvantage in one or more circumstances.

Most conditions have a set duration; however, during the recovery phase of each turn you may choose to spend Mana to make a Resistance Check against an active condition you are afflicted with. Normally, you can only resist 1 condition during each recovery phase.

Burning is caused by continued fire damage or being doused in flammables. At the beginning of your turn, you take a number of d8 of fire damage unless you take a Resistance Reaction during the recovery phase at the end of the round. The number of d8 of damage is set by the attack that causes burning. The burning condition ends once you make your check. Burning ends if you are subject to cold damage, are doused in water, or subject to the Freeze condition.

You are restrained in ice (see Restrained below). At the beginning of your turn you take 1d8 cold damage. The Freeze condition ends if you take a Resistance Reaction during the recovery phase at the end of the round or if you take fire damage.

You are slowly encased in weakening stone or salt, and restrained. Only curatives and miraculous workings can reverse the condition. Your Guard is reduced to 0, attacks ignore your armor, and you cannot take most reactions.
For the duration you cannot access your Vigor, meaning you cannot use techniques or reactions.
For the duration, you cannot cast spells or access Mana, including Reactions.

When subject to this condition you take 1d4 damage directly to Health at the beginning of your turn for the duration unless you take an antidote as an action. You can end Poisoned if you take a Resistance reaction during the recovery phase at the end of the round.

Caused by spells and illusions, in this state you are disoriented and have 2 Challenges on Reason, Memory, Empathy, and Resolve checks. You have trouble identifying friends and foes and may attack your allies. You can end Confused if you succeed a Resistance reaction during the recovery phase at the end of the round.

This condition causes open lacerations that continue to bleed for the duration of the effect. A creature suffering from bleeding takes a number of d6 damage at the end of their turn based on the bleed effect. Things that inflict Bleed will list this number as Bleed (1) or Bleed (2) for the number of dice.
When you are blinded due to a bright enough light or an injury, you suffer from similar penalties to being in complete darkness. Any actions dependent on sight have their VS increased by +15.

When you are inflicted with severe disorientation or shell-shock, you are Dazed. Poisons, pain, and blows to the head can cause the Dazed condition. While you are Dazed, you only have 1 Action, and you have 1 Challenge on all bonus dice to Actions.

This condition occurs when you are bombarded by sensory noise. Dazzled penalties can only be overcome by removing, blocking, or neutralizing the source of the effect. Dazzling effects hamper focus and concentration, adding 1 Challenge to most Actions. In combat, dazzled characters have one less Action per round than they would have otherwise.
Being Deafened makes it impossible to make Awareness checks related to hearing and prevents communication except through gestures or sign language.

A medium creature can lift and carry 10 times their Strength Score in pounds without meaningful impact.
