Being overburdened — attempting to move while carrying any amount up to 1½ times your lift value — you suffer a 2 Challenge penalty to all action rolls based on Agility, Speed, and Stamina. Further, you can move only half your normal Speed and half distance in combat, running Speed is reduced by half, and you cannot manage a jump greater than half your normal jumping distance.

You can spend Vigor to deadlift and hold up any amount between one and a half to two times your lift value. You can lift this amount for 1 round at the cost of 10 Vigor. Each round, you consume 10 Vigor, if you have it, to hold the weight up. You cannot move while holding this amount.

Being Enthralled partially robs a creature of its free will. While you are Enthralled, you cannot take actions unless directed to by the being that has you enthralled. It's up to you how you carry out the directions you are given, so a more open-ended request could allow you to flee your captor. Any direction that is directly self-destructive or would cause you severe distress, such as being forced to murder your loved ones, entitles you to a Resistance reaction to resist. Ambiguous directions are up to the Game Master: "Stop those intruders!" might not elicit a resistance check, but "fight them to the death" might.

In extreme cases, a creature might suffer from fatigue, a condition of pronounced weakness. Fatigue can be caused by things like disease, life-draining magic, and starvation. Each rank adds a cumulative Challenge to all actions, just like being wounded.
At four ranks a character can no longer recover Vigor through rest or abilities, only magic or consumables.
A fifth rank of Fatigue kills.

A character with Fatigue ranks suffers from rather steep penalties. The easiest way to lose Fatigue is to rest. Each full night of restful sleep, make a Resistance check VS 20 to recover from one Fatigue rank. You can only recover from 1 rank per day in this way.
Few things are as distracting and debilitating as fear; it can make the strongest warrior weak, turn a squad of trained assassins into helpless fools, and debilitate even the mightiest heroes.

Anxiety. This type of fear is born from an unnerving and pervasive sense of danger or threat. While these are different emotions, the fundamental fearful effect is the same: reduced ability to concentrate, jumpiness, and increased susceptibility to emotional attacks. While you are suffering from anxiety, you suffer 1 Challenge on all mental action rolls. Further, you may not make Resistance checks against emotional attacks or surprises.

Terror. Things can produce terror: being attacked by a vicious creature or witnessing horrific torture. When presented with terror, you must make a Resistance reaction or be frozen in fear for one turn. The VS is determined by the source of the Terror; many horrific monsters have a specific terror VS, as do spells that invoke terror. However, the same stimuli can only produce terror once. After the initial fear, the character is immune (if they survive) to that specific source of terror.

Panicked. A creature inflicted with panic is driven to flee from the source of their fear at their maximum speed or through their fastest method of motion for 1 full round, or longer. A Resistance reaction check is required to stand and face the danger at hand. A creature that overcomes panic becomes immune to the effect for 1 round.

Being tied up or grappled can cause this condition. While you are Restrained, your movement speed is reduced to 5 feet (1 hex). Melee attacks against you receive 1 Charge, while you have 1 Challenge on Speed and Agility checks.

Suffering from a physical detriment, such as a poison or disease, causes the Sickened condition. While sickened, your physical actions suffer 1 Challenge, and you recover Vigor at half the normal rate.

While you are unconscious, you cannot take actions or reactions and cannot interface with the situation. Your Guard is reduced to 0, attacks ignore your armor rank unless it is magical, and you cannot actively resist or move.
You become Winded when you have reached your physical limits in a small amount of time by exhausting your Vigor to 0.
While you are winded, you cannot take any actions in combat. Out of an encounter, you can only move at slow pace and talk until you have recovered at least 5 points of Vigor.

When your Durability is reduced to 0, you become wounded. In the wounded state, some skills or talents may come into effect, and you may become vulnerable to some effects. While you are wounded you receive 1 Challenge on all action rolls.
Any damage you take once you are wounded is potentially life-threatening damage to your Health. While you are wounded, you cannot recover Durability if you have sustained any damage to your Health.
Many situations can modify how an encounter plays out.

Size matters: many derived attributes function differently at different sizes. There are seven size categories: tiny, small, medium, large, huge, massive, and colossal. Humans are medium-sized, a grizzly bear would be a large creature, and a blue whale would be a massive creature.

Size categories are generally defined by mass and volume. As a rule, volume trumps mass: a fifteen-foot spherical creature filled with hot air is a huge creature, even if it only weighs in at a few hundred pounds.
Tiny: Median mass of less than 2 lbs., total volume of 6 cubic inches.
Small: Median mass of less than 60 lbs., total volume of 2 cubic feet.
Medium: Median mass of less than 450 lbs., total volume of 5 cubic feet.
Large: Median mass of less than 3,000 lbs., total volume of 15 cubic feet.
Huge: Median mass of greater than 25,000 lbs., total volume of 35 cubic feet.
Massive: Median mass of greater than 200,000 lbs., total volume of 100 cubic feet.
Colossal: Median mass of greater than 1 million lbs., total volume of 400 cubic feet.

When a larger creature is attacking a smaller creature, the smaller creature's Guard increases by 5 for every size category of difference. For example, a medium-sized creature (like a human) attacking a tiny creature (like a mouse) has a more difficult time, as the mouse's Guard is +10 (2 sizes difference). Equally, a colossal creature (like a Behemoth) attacking a medium-sized creature (a human) will need to beat the human's Guard +20 (4 sizes difference). However, in both examples, one hit of the larger creature will probably kill the smaller opponent outright.

Conditions in the atmosphere that impair vision range cause penalties to ranged attacks and piloting skills. Visibility conditions can be impaired by any number of things but are usually the product of weather, smoke, or steam. Visibility conditions are ranked Normal, Reduced, Low, and Zero Visibility.
Normal Visibility. Under normal visibility there are no changes. Unobstructed line of sight is between 800 feet and 1200 feet.

Reduced Visibility. Range of vision is between 70 and 100 feet. Examples include moderate rain or snowfall, smoke from a small fire, light foliage, or light fog. Characters attempting to drive, navigate, or similar suffer a +5 VS penalty. Distance attacks are limited to a 300-feet range with a +5 VS penalty to hit the target.

Low Visibility. Range of vision is limited to between 30 and 50 feet in situations like heavy rainstorms, smoke from large fires, most fog, or thick foliage. Piloting and navigation suffer a +10 VS; ranged attack accuracy is limited to 150 feet, and all ranged attacks are made with a +10 VS penalty to strike.

Zero Visibility. Range of vision is reduced to between 5 and 10 feet; zero visibility is caused by dense smoke or fog, deep blizzards, or very dense foliage. Piloting by sight is basically impossible and suffers a +15 VS. Ranged attack accuracy is limited to 30 feet with a +15 VS penalty to strike, but suffer no penalties at point blank, 10 feet or under.

Obstacles and obstructions could be anything from a canopy of leaves to a cement wall. There are three categories of cover: light, partial, and full. Cover comes into play when an attacker at range is targeting an opponent that has something to obscure their location, act as a shield, or both.

Light Cover. Minor obstructions, such as being in a bush or foliage or being under a camouflage net. The attacker still generally knows where the target is, but not exactly, and the cover provides no stopping power. Light Cover provides the target with a +10 bonus to Guard from direct-ranged attacks. Area attacks are unaffected.

Partial Cover. Crouching behind a tree stump or stone barricade provides partial cover. You can be seen, but the cover can stop some attacks. When you have Partial Cover, you have a +3 bonus to Guard, and you gain a +5 Armor Rank bonus against attacks. This damage is done to your cover.

Full Cover. Hiding behind a stone pillar or wall with an exposed position. Full Cover obscures your position and provides you with a +5 bonus to your Guard. Assuming your cover is sturdy, you gain a +10 bonus to your Armor Rank from ranged attacks. This damage is done to your cover. While this limits your vulnerability to assaults that require line of sight, higher power weapons might be able to blast through your cover.

To create a character for Radiant Age is to choose how you will participate in the long age of cultivated power. Every hero of this world stands on the same foundation — heritage, history, the cultivation of one or more inner engines — and from that foundation grows the kind of person who could change a city, an empire, or the shape of the unseen world. The character creation process gives you the chance to design that person with intention, from their inner architecture outward.

Before you begin rolling ability scores or selecting skills, think of the type of character you want to create. Are you the disciplined Armsman whose Aura ignites with each breath? The scholar Mage whose heart has become a library of inscribed Circles? The survivalist Rogue whose Edge Dice mark the sharpened instinct of someone who has lived through things they probably should not have? The Spiritualist whose Mysteries open doors that other practitioners do not even know are doors? The personality of your character is what will set them apart. Radiant Age allows you to deeply customize every aspect of your hero — who they are, and who they will become.
All characters are composed of the same basic building blocks. These values and statistics will help you resolve actions and understand your character's capabilities.

Ability Scores. Your heritage, history, and rolls will determine the values for your nine ability scores — Strength, Agility, Stamina, Speed, Soul, Empathy, Memory, Reason, and Resolve. Each score governs different actions and skills.

Corruption. A tenth ability-like score, beginning at 0 for most characters. Corruption is the visible accumulation of contact with forbidden powers, eldritch beings, and dark cultivation. It rises through specific in-game events and provides certain benefits and severe penalties at thresholds. See Chapter Five for full details.
Heritage. Your ancestry and bloodline. Your heritage will influence your initial ability scores and grant innate abilities — physical traits, sensory advantages, and cultural starting capabilities.

History. This reflects your upbringing, education, and the trial that called you to the adventuring life. Your History grants two starting Novice skills and a Testament feature with a small starting kit.

Class. Your class represents your specialized training and cultivated potential. Your class grants you starting skills, your Foundation Talent (a permanent resource bonus), and access to your Elite Tree — the 17-talent path through your chosen cultivation engine.
Guard. Guard is the VS others must beat to hit you in combat. Guard begins at 15 for most creatures. Abilities, talents, and stance choices can modify your Guard.
Ward. Ward is the VS magical or spiritual effects must beat when targeting you. Ward begins at 15. Many supernatural abilities target your Ward rather than your Guard.

Armor Rank. If you wear armor, you have an Armor Rank. When you take damage from an attack, you ignore an amount equal to your Armor Rank, and that subtracted damage is dealt to your armor's Durability instead.
Durability. Superficial damage you can sustain before you begin taking life-threatening damage. Damage flows first to Durability, then to Health. Durability recovers more readily than Health.
Health. Once your Durability is exhausted, you can sustain this much damage before you die. Health recovers slowly and may require Medicine, Miracle, or extended rest to return.
Vigor. The fuel for physical combat abilities — techniques, sustained stances, special martial maneuvers. Vigor recovers between scenes with brief rest.

Mana (or Grace). The fuel for magical, spiritual, and supernatural abilities. Mana recovers slowly with rest. Priests and other divine practitioners replace Mana with Grace — a divine resource recovered through prayer rather than rest.

Reactions. When subject to an attack or effect, you may take a Reaction — typically Defense (Dodge), Fortitude (Resistance), or a class-specific reaction. Each round, you have one Reaction unless talents grant more.
Actions. The number of Actions you take on your turn. You begin with 2 Actions and gain more through specific talents and abilities.
Size. Your general size category — typically Medium, with some Heritages (Ogre, Hauflin) starting at different sizes. Size may impact Guard, movement speed, and other values.
Movement. As an Action you can move 10 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Speed Bonus Dice. Various effects modify this baseline. Sprinting allows extended movement both in and out of combat.

Skills. Action rolls against an area of knowledge. You begin with access to all Core Skills and may purchase Novice rank in specific skills through skill points. Bonus dice from skill ranks add to relevant action checks.

Talents. Abilities purchased with skill points from Skill Trees, or granted by your class, heritage, or origin. Talents grant new actions, ability score bonuses, derived value improvements, or signature abilities of your class.

Disciplines. Discrete learnable abilities — Weapon Techniques, Spells, Glamours, Miracles, Rites, and similar. While you typically need a Talent or class feature to unlock access to a Discipline, once unlocked you may continue to learn additional Disciplines through training and study.

Cultivation Engines. Most characters in Radiant Age cultivate one or more engines that produce magical effects: Aura Stars, Mana Circles, Edge Dice, Mysteries, Vyana, or Chalices. Each engine is granted by class progression and scales characters across the Ordinary, Heroic, Epic, and Legendary tiers. See Chapter Seven for class details.
Temperament. A descriptor of your personality and behavior. Are you more rational or more emotional? Methodical or zealous? Your Temperament is a tool for understanding how your character thinks.

Character creation can be broken down into seven discrete steps, with a final detail-and-story step to fill in the remaining elements. Roll your ability scores, build your origins (heritage and history), select your class, purchase your skills and talents, record your disciplines, calculate your final resources, and equip your starting gear. The first thing you should do, however, is sit down with your Game Master and discuss the game you are about to play.

As you advance by earning experience points, you will gain levels, accomplish Deeds, and earn additional skill points to spend deepening your cultivation engines, purchasing new talents, or expanding your reach into new domains.

While character creation is a structured process, it can take time given the number of options available to you. Heritage, History, Class, Foundation, and the choice of which engines to cultivate first all combine into a unique character whose path is genuinely your own. Take the time to plan; the work pays back across every session that follows.

Before you roll any dice or decide on heritage, history, or class, it's essential for you and the GM to have a session-zero conversation. Radiant Age is a world of cultivated power, ancient sects, divine and forbidden currents, and the long work of becoming more than you were born to be. The kind of hero you create — and the kind of campaign your party will run — should be agreed upon collaboratively.

Discuss the campaign's tone, scope, and themes. Will the game lean toward heroic political intrigue, monster-hunting and wilderness exploration, divine sect war, scholarly mystery, or some specific genre frame (cultivation epic, manhwa-style ascent, dark fantasy with eldritch undertones)? Clarify the key themes the GM intends to develop: loyalty, ambition, faith, corruption, the nature of cultivated power, the cost of legend. Your Heritage defines what you are. Your History defines where you came from. Your Class defines what you have become and what you might yet become. The campaign will shape how those choices play out at the table.

The GM should also set clear expectations for behavior, engagement, and the kinds of content the table will and will not include. Radiant Age can run dark — corruption, sacrifice, divine punishment, and lasting consequence are all in the system's design — and the table should agree on where the lines are before play begins. This ensures a cooperative, respectful environment for all participants.

The first step in character creation is generating your nine Ability Scores. The Promethium System uses Strength (STR), Agility (AGI), Stamina (STA), Speed (SPD), Soul (SOL), Empathy (EMP), Memory (MEM), Reason (RSN), and Resolve (RSL). Corruption (CRP) serves as a tenth ability score, beginning at 0 for most characters and accumulated through specific events in play.
There are three methods to generate your nine starting Ability Scores: Random Rolling (the preferred method), Point Buy, and Ability Array. Your entire party should use the same method.

Roll 3d8 nine times, once for each Ability Score, placing them in order or distributing them at your discretion (the GM determines which approach the table uses). This method allows for exceptional ability scores and produces the widest variety of characters.

When rolling your ability scores, if you roll the maximum value (8) on one or more of your dice, you are entitled to roll one more d8 of that ability score and add it to your total. This is called a Critical Ability Score. You may only gain one Critical Ability Score die per ability score, even if you roll multiple maximum values.

blocks.text: rolling 3d8 for Strength, you get 8, 4, 3. You roll one additional d8 (your Critical Ability Score die) and add it to your total. If your rolled result on the bonus d8 is 6, your final Strength is 8+4+3+6 = 21.

blocks.html: An ability score of 15 or higher grants Bonus Dice to action rolls and skills related to that score, as detailed in Chapter Five.

If your GM uses this rule, all Ability Scores begin at 8, and you have 60 points to distribute among them. You can raise any single score as high as 25 in this method. Each point spent increases the corresponding score by 1.

Another option is the Ability Array. You begin with a fixed set of nine ability scores to place wherever you see fit: 22, 19, 17, 15, 15, 13, 11, 10, 10. This method ensures balanced parties and removes the variance of random rolling.

Next you will build your character's origin from two elements: heritage and history. Heritage is your bloodline and ancestry — the body and soul you inhabit. History is the upbringing, training, and trial that called you to the cultivated path. Together they form the foundation upon which your class will build.

Radiant Age recognizes fifteen heritages across five families — Men, Ceith, Dwerin, Beastrals, and Exotic — and sixteen histories spanning the full breadth of mortal experience. Each is detailed in Chapter Six. The choices you make here shape both the mechanical and narrative texture of your character.

Your heritage is your bloodline and ancestry. It influences your starting ability scores through specific modifiers and grants innate abilities — physical traits, sensory advantages, racial talents, and cultural starting capabilities. Each heritage has a Physiology section describing its visible characteristics, a Disposition section describing its general temperament tendencies, and an At-the-Table note suggesting roleplay framing.

Heritages also have Heritage Features — the specific mechanical bonuses your character gains at the start of play. These include Ability Score Modifiers (typically +1d4 to +2d8 distributions), innate abilities (such as Human Ingenuity, Oni Blood, or Saurian Endurance), and any starting Durability, Health, Vigor, or Mana bonuses appropriate to your heritage.
See Chapter Six for full details on each of the fifteen heritages. Your choice here is permanent and shapes your physical and innate identity for the duration of your character's career.

Your History represents your upbringing, training, and the trial that called you to the cultivated life. Each History has three components: a Trial (rolled on 1d6, or chosen, describing the formative event of your early years), Training (two Novice skills), and a Testament (a starting kit and a small Heritage-style starting feature).

The sixteen histories of Radiant Age are: Acolyte, Artisan, Charlatan, Criminal, Entertainer, Farmer, Guard, Guide, Hermit, Merchant, Noble, Sage, Sailor, Scribe, Soldier, and Wayfarer. Each is detailed in Chapter Six. Your History determines both your starting skill kit and the narrative seed from which your adventuring career emerges.

Radiant Age features four Ordinary Classes — the broad starting paths for any cultivator. Each grants access to one cultivation engine and a 17-talent Elite Tree that develops that engine across five ranks. Your class also grants a Foundation Talent (a permanent resource scaling) and two starting Novice skills.

Choose carefully — your class shapes your cultivation engine, your starting Mana and Vigor, your role in combat, and the Heroic, Epic, and Legendary classes you may pursue later. While Radiant Age uses class-agnostic Heroic-tier prerequisites (any character meeting the requirements may take any Heroic class), your Ordinary class establishes the foundation from which all later cultivation grows.

rows: Master of weapons; cultivator of inner Aura. Walks to the front of the line.
Aura Stars; imbue body and weapon with cultivated radiance.
Scholar of the inscribed traditions. Heart becomes a library.
Mana Circles; channel formal arcane working.
Survivalist with sharpened instincts. Power taken, not granted.
Edge Dice; the cultivated reflex of one who has lived through everything.
Cultivator of strange truths and the world's quieter currents.
Mysteries; permanent alterations of the soul that unlock the unseen.

blocks.html: See Chapter Seven for the full Ordinary class trees, including specific talents, foundation talent options, and Heroic-tier progression paths.

All characters begin with access to the Core Skill Trees — twenty general skills covering combat, academics, and practical knowledge. In addition to the starting skills from your Heritage, History, and Class, you begin with 10 skill points to purchase additional skill ranks and talents from your available trees.
Skill points may be spent on:

items: Core Skill Trees: Combat skills (Light Weapons, Heavy Weapons, Defense, etc.), Academic skills (Studies, Occult, Memory, etc.), Practical skills (Crafting, Stealth, Survival, etc.).
Your Elite Tree: The class-specific 17-talent tree granted by your chosen Ordinary class. Most starting characters spend several skill points purchasing the first few talents of their Elite Tree.
General Talents: Skill-tree-independent talents available to all characters meeting their prerequisites.

blocks.html: As a 1st-level character, you may only purchase Rank 1 skill bonuses and talents from any tree. This means you cannot fully advance a single skill tree at character creation; instead, you should build a foundation of breadth and depth that fits your character's intended path. You must spend all your skill points at 1st level; you cannot save them for later.
Each skill bonus or talent costs 1 skill point. They must be purchased in order — you cannot acquire advanced skills or talents without first purchasing their prerequisites.

Disciplines are learned abilities unlocked through skill ranks: weapon techniques (Aura Techniques and martial techniques), arcane spells (Incantare), divine miracles, Glamours (Performance enchantments), Lesser Rites, and similar discrete capabilities. Unlike Talents, Disciplines consume resources — Vigor for martial Disciplines, Mana or Grace for magical and spiritual Disciplines — to activate.

Your Heritage, History, Class starting talents, and any skill talents you purchased at Step 4 may grant access to specific Disciplines. Record these — they are your starting moves, the techniques and spells you will draw on most heavily in early adventures.
See Chapter Ten for the full Discipline catalog, including Aura Techniques, Incantare Spells, Glamours, Miracles, and the other discrete learnable abilities available at each rank.

Resources are your derived values spent to take actions or sustain hits: Health, Durability, Vigor, and Mana (or Grace). Calculate these after you have determined your final Ability Scores, Foundation Talent, Heritage bonuses, and any first-level skill purchases that modify them.

items: Durability: Stamina Score + Resolve Score.
Health: Stamina Score + 1d10.
Vigor: 4d6 + 20.
Mana: 4d6 + 20.

blocks.html: Your Heritage and Foundation Talent may add to these baselines. Many heritages grant +Durability, +Vigor, or +Mana at character creation; your chosen Foundation Talent adds substantial additional resource scaling. See Chapter Five for Resource details and the Foundation Talent options below.

Every character chooses one Foundation Talent at character creation. Foundation Talents are class-restricted — your chosen Ordinary class determines which Foundation Talents are available to you (typically two or three options per class). Each Foundation Talent provides substantial starting resources and continued scaling per level.

items: Arcane Reservoir. Greatly increases starting Mana. Gain +2d4×10 Mana at 1st level and +2d6 Mana at each level up. Available to: Mage, Spiritualist.
Steel Mantle. Greatly increases starting Durability. Gain +2d4×10 Durability at 1st level and +2d6 Durability at each level up. Available to: Armsman.
Iron Constitution. Modestly increases both Durability and Vigor. Gain +1d4×10 to each at 1st level and +1d6 to each at each level up. Available to: Armsman, Rogue.
Tyrant's Might. Modestly increases both Mana and Vigor. Gain +1d4×10 to each at 1st level and +1d6 to each at each level up. Available to: Spiritualist.
Ancient Mettle. Modestly increases both Durability and Mana. Gain +1d4×10 to each at 1st level and +1d6 to each at each level up. Available to: Mage, Spiritualist.
Endless Endurance. Greatly increases starting Vigor. Gain +2d4×10 Vigor at 1st level and +2d6 Vigor at each level up. Available to: Rogue.

blocks.html: Every character begins with a minimum amount of gear and cash on hand — important things for a young cultivator about to leave home. Your History grants a starting kit appropriate to your background (an Acolyte's robes and prayer book, an Artisan's tools and Masterwork item, a Criminal's lockpicks and concealed daggers, etc.). Your Class may grant additional starting equipment appropriate to your cultivation engine.

Beyond your History's starting kit, you begin with 50 gold pieces' worth of common gear of your choosing — clothing, basic adventuring equipment, a personal weapon or implement of modest quality, food and water for several days of travel, and similar essentials. The exact items are recorded on your character sheet; specific equipment lists are available in Chapter Nine.

If your character has acquired extraordinary abilities or items through their Heritage or History (a Half-Ogre's natural weapon, a Charlatan's signet ring, an Artisan's Masterwork tool), record these in your character's equipment section. They will certainly come in handy.

Your character is more than a set of numbers and values describing their ability to fight or cast. Beyond your scores, traits, and talents, your character has a personality and history all their own. The following exists to help you flesh out the unique persona of your character — their name, their appearance, their voice, their Temperament, and the small details that will distinguish them from every other cultivator in the world.

When your character is introduced, what kind of impression will they make? What do other adventurers remember about them after the first meeting? What do they remember about themselves when no one is watching? Take the time to develop these details. The mechanical bones of your character will serve you, but the personality and history are what make a character truly yours, and what will give you something to play during the long sessions when the dice are quiet.

Your proper noun — how you are addressed and referred to. Is it a birth name? A sect name given at apprenticeship? A title earned during formative years? A name your enemies gave you that you have decided to keep? Perhaps you do not know your birth name and only carry a clue to your origins which has become how the world addresses you. The choice is yours, but remember — make it memorable, distinctive, and appropriate to the world and story for your character. Names in Radiant Age vary by heritage and region; see Chapter Six for naming conventions.

What you look like is generally dictated by what you are. Consider how your character's Heritage features, ability scores, and trained skills might impact their appearance and physical bearing. A character with high Strength might have heavy shoulders and corded arms; a character with high Speed might be lithe and rangy with long fingers; a character with high Stamina might carry visible weight-of-experience — old scars, weathered skin, perhaps a slight stoop from years of carrying heavy loads.

Cultivated characters often show visible signs of their cultivation. A multi-Star Armsman walks differently than an untrained fighter — there is a center of gravity to their motion, a stillness when they stop moving. A multi-Circle Mage may carry a faint inner luminance visible at the throat or wrists. A multi-Mystery Spiritualist's eyes may seem to see more than is physically present. These small markers are how cultivators recognize one another in passing, and they shape how strangers respond to your character at first glance.

Your character's sex, gender identity, and orientation have no mechanical bearing on the Radiant Age system. They may, however, carry significant social and cultural weight depending on the region and culture your character inhabits.

Across the Realms of Ouroboros, attitudes toward gender vary widely. The high sects of the Northern Kingdoms maintain strict roles assigned to specific genders, with cultivated practice often segregated by tradition. The trade-cities of the central continents are more pragmatic and considerably less concerned. The wandering tribes of the steppe and the long-lived Ceith of the deep forests recognize a range of gender identities far beyond the binary, with specific cultural roles for those who walk between. The Beastrals — Wulver, Leodon, and Saurian — often have culture-specific gender expressions tied to species characteristics. The Lustrate, having no fixed biology, are considered genderless by most, though many adopt a presentation by personal preference.

Your character's gender and presentation should reflect what feels right for the person you are creating. Some campaigns will give this weight; others will treat it as flavor text. The GM and player should align on what the campaign intends to explore in this area before play begins.

Your Temperament describes the dueling nature of your personality — do you follow your head, or your heart, and to what degree? Temperament is a roleplaying tool to help you understand how your character thinks and reacts. Are you a fiery Zealot who always listens to your heart, reason be damned? Or are you contemplative and Methodical, only letting feelings intrude when it suits you?

The following are brief outlines of the ten Temperaments. None of these are rigid rules — no one is perfectly exemplary of these general characterizations. Creatures with only limited intellect may still behave in a manner that is rational and calculating, whereas a brilliant character can be completely ruled by their emotions. Use the framework as a starting point, not a cage.

Methodical. Cold, calculating, unflinching. Methodical characters actively detach from emotional impulse and try not to let emotion in at all. They are rarely impulsive, never spontaneous, and notoriously poor at expressing feelings. The disciplined sect master, the master assassin, the apex scholar who has burned away their attachments.

Analytical. Objective and considered, but with specific areas of emotional vulnerability — a temper, a hopeless romance, a sentimental attachment to specific causes. Analytical characters can show real emotion when their critical thinking permits it. The strategist who plans every detail, the brilliant detective with a soft spot.

Reasonable. Thinkers first, but adaptive when situations call for emotional response. Reasonable characters think through what matters and act on instinct when the moment requires. The veteran who has learned when to lead with the head and when with the heart.

Passive. Neither rational nor emotional takes a primary role. Passive characters are often conflicted, indecisive, and easily swayed by stronger personalities. Big decisions are especially difficult; the scale tips one way or the other only with great effort. The bystander, the witness, the one who is genuinely uncertain.

Zen. Balanced and harmonious. Zen characters look at both their emotions and reason and weigh them against contemplative observation. They act with sublime confidence — no decision is second-guessed, all actions align with both head and heart. The achieved ascetic, the master cultivator, the senior practitioner who has resolved their inner contradictions.

Passionate. Fiery and opinionated, but with enough thought to temper their actions. Passionate characters act first on feeling but justify their choices with rational thought, often expressing things in terms of morals and right action. The crusading hero, the loyal friend, the lover who is also a philosopher.

Impulsive. Act first, think second. Beliefs and instincts matter more than reason; reflection happens, if at all, after the moment has passed. Impulsive characters are hard to predict but easy to manipulate; they are emotional and passionate but can be reasoned with after the fact. The wild warrior, the heart-led believer, the romantic.

Zealous. Feel life rather than think about it. Zealous characters never really consider situations or consequences; they go with what their heart tells them and unquestioningly follow their beliefs. Mistakes are forgotten before they are reflected upon. The fanatical warrior-priest, the true believer, the consumed lover.

Instinctual. Lacking the capacity for self-reflection and abstract intelligence, an Instinctual creature is functionally incapable of making a moral or ethical choice and acts according to instinct and stimulus alone. Beast Familiars, low-intelligence creatures, and some specific magical beings fall into this category.

Mindless. Unintelligent creatures, constructs, machines, and beings without problem-solving or cognitive ability. Mindless beings simply follow the command of whatever force guides them to act. Most Constructs and many forms of undead are Mindless.

While most Radiant Age campaigns begin at Level 1, some campaigns are designed to start at higher levels — for shorter campaigns aimed at specific narrative scope, for one-shot scenarios with experienced characters, for campaigns picking up after a prior chronicle, or for adult-tier story arcs where the characters are already accomplished cultivators when the game begins. The following guidelines help GMs and players construct characters above Level 1.

A character begins with 10 skill points at Level 1. Each level above 1 grants 1 additional skill point. Therefore, a character starting at Level +9 total skill points. Skill points may be spent on any combination of skills, talents, and Elite Tree progression that respects rank prerequisites.
Examples:

items: Level 3 character: 12 total skill points.
Level 5 character: 14 total skill points.
Level 10 character: 19 total skill points.
Level 15 character: 24 total skill points.
Level 20 character: 29 total skill points.

blocks.html: Unlike a 1st-level character (who may only purchase Rank 1 talents), a character starting at higher level may purchase talents at higher ranks, but only based on their skill tree progression. This means a character starting at level 5 can only have one rank 5 talent, or two rank 4, or three rank 3, or four rank 2; provided they have purchased all the proceeding talents in that tree.

Prerequisites must still be respected — Rank 3 talents require Rank 2 talents in the same path, and so on. A character starting at Level 5 may take up to Rank 3 talents but must still purchase the requisite Rank 1 and Rank 2 talents leading to them.
Characters starting at Level 14 or higher may begin play having entered a Heroic class. To do so, the character must:

items: Meet all Heroic prerequisites (ability scores, skill ranks, and engine thresholds) for the chosen Heroic class. See Chapter Seven for specific Heroic class requirements.
Complete all 17 Ordinary class talents in their first class (or use class-agnostic alternate prerequisite paths if applicable).
Allocate skill points across both Ordinary and Heroic Elite Trees as appropriate.

blocks.html: As a general rule, characters starting at Level 14 may purchase up to 3-4 Heroic-tier talents. Level 17+ characters may purchase up to half of their Heroic class's 11 talents. Level 20+ characters may be considered fully Heroic, having completed both the Ordinary and Heroic stages of their cultivation.
Resources scale with level as follows. Apply these gains at character creation for any character starting above Level 1:

items: Durability: +6 per level (+12 for characters with Steel Mantle Foundation Talent).
Vigor: +4 per level (+8 for characters with Endless Endurance, +6 with Iron Constitution or Tyrant's Might).
Mana: +4 per level (+8 for characters with Arcane Reservoir, +6 with Ancient Mettle or Tyrant's Might).

blocks.html: Your Heritage may grant additional per-level resource scaling beyond the Foundation Talent baselines. Confirm these with your GM at character construction.
Higher-level characters have accumulated equipment and wealth through prior adventures. Use the following guidelines:

items: Levels 2–4: Standard starting gear plus 200 gold pieces and one item of Masterwork or modest enchanted quality.
Levels 5–8: Standard starting gear plus 500 gold pieces and one item of Heroic-rank inherent magic, plus 2-3 items of Masterwork quality.
Levels 9–13: Standard starting gear plus 1500 gold pieces, one item of Heroic-rank enchanted magic, plus 3-4 items of Masterwork or modest enchanted quality.
Levels 14+: Standard starting gear plus 3000+ gold pieces, multiple items of Heroic-rank enchanted magic, and one item of Epic-rank inherent magic. Discuss specifics with your GM.

blocks.html: Cultivation engines (Stars, Circles, Edge Dice, Mysteries, Vyana, Chalices) advance with Ordinary and Heroic class purchases. A character starting at higher level should have engine counts consistent with their Ordinary and Heroic talent purchases. Use the following rough guidelines:

items: Levels 1-4: 1 engine grant (R1 starting cultivation only).
Levels 5-8: 2 engine grants (R1 + R3 middle).
Levels 9-13: 3 engine grants (R1 + R3 + R5 middle, completing Ordinary engine).
Levels 14-17: 4 engine grants (R1-R5 + 1 Heroic engine grant).
Levels 18-22: 5 engine grants (Ordinary complete + 2 Heroic grants).
Levels 23+: Begin Epic class entry; engine count exceeds 5 grants.

blocks.html: These are approximate. Specific engine count depends on how the character has chosen to spend their skill points and which classes they have committed to.
Characters starting at higher levels may have accumulated Titles and Deeds (see Chapter Seven of the Promethium Core for the Titles and Deeds system). As a rough guideline:

items: Levels 5-9: 1-2 Titles, 0-1 Deeds.
Levels 10-14: 2-4 Titles, 1-2 Deeds.
Levels 15-19: 3-5 Titles, 2-3 Deeds.
Levels 20+: 4-6 Titles, 3-4 Deeds.

blocks.html: Discuss specific Titles and Deeds with your GM at character creation. These should feed naturally into the character's backstory and may influence the campaign's early scenes — a Famed Slayer of Beasts may find that other beasts hear of their reputation and behave accordingly; a Healer of the Plague may be sought out by communities suffering from new illnesses.
The GM should consider the following when running a campaign that starts above Level 1:

items: Make sure all party members start at the same level. Mixed-level parties strain the system's encounter balance.
Discuss the prior backstory of each character — what they have done, who they have antagonized, who owes them favors, and what their established reputation in the world looks like.
Adjust starting encounters and challenges to the party's cultivated power. A Level 10 party facing a Level 3 encounter is not engaged; a Level 10 party facing a Level 15 encounter is overwhelmed.

Consider whether the higher-tier characters have specific obligations or attachments (a Contracted spirit's demands, a sworn oath to a sect, a Forbidden binding that must be maintained). These should be developed into specific narrative hooks for the campaign.
Higher-tier characters cast longer shadows. NPCs respond to known cultivators differently than to unknowns. Bring this dynamic into the campaign as appropriate.

sections.title: Chapter Five – Ability Scores and Resources

blocks.html: The body lifts, the mind reaches, the soul burns. To be a hero in the Radiant Age is to know the measure of each — and to learn where your measure ends, so you may cultivate past it.

The measure of a person in the Realms of Ouroboros is taken along ten axes: nine Ability Scores that every living soul possesses, and a tenth that most, thankfully, do not. From these come your reserves of body, breath, and spirit — the Durability that buys you a moment more on the battlefield, the Vigor that fuels impossible motion, and the Mana that flows in circles through your heart or burns in stars at your core. This chapter defines those scores, shows how to generate them, and introduces the four cultivation engines — Aura Cores, Mana Circles, Edges, and Mysteries — that separate the four Ordinary Classes from the first session onward.

Abilities are ranked from 1 to 100 or more. A score of 11–15 is average for a human adult. At 15, and every 5 points thereafter, you gain +1 Heroic Bonus Die to action checks governed by that ability.
Many of your traits — Health, Durability, Vigor, Mana, Movement, Languages — are derived from your Ability Scores. Talents gained through skills and classes will build further on those foundations.

Corruption is a tenth score that begins at 0 for nearly every character born of Ouroboros. It is the residue of forbidden contact — with the hungering dark below, with bindings that should not have been sealed, with spells whose runes your soul was not meant to speak. Corruption is never rolled at creation. It is earned, in the worst sense of the word: by walking a Corruption Path, by striking bargains with Eldritch powers, by learning rites that gnaw at the speaker, or by deliberate transgression in the presence of infernal influence.

Corruption generates Bonus Dice at the same thresholds as other Ability Scores (+1 BD at 15, +1 per 5 thereafter). Certain pacted, cursed, or forbidden Disciplines draw their power from this pool. For every 5 points of Corruption you accrue, however, you develop a Malediction — a curse, a mark, a hunger, or a disfigurement that compounds as your score climbs. The full catalog of Corruption Paths and Maledictions appears in Appendix B.

Every character in a party should generate their nine Ability Scores the same way. Corruption is not generated at this stage. There are three methods: Random Rolling (preferred), Point Buy, and Ability Array.
Roll 3d8 nine times, once for each Ability Score, and place them in order. This method gives the widest range of outcomes and suits players who want the dice to suggest a concept.
After rolling, apply your Heritage's ability score modifiers.

When you roll maximum value on one or more of your Ability Score dice, roll one additional die of that type and add it on. You can only gain one Critical Ability Score die per Ability Score, no matter how many maximum values you roll.
Place the following array wherever you see fit: 22, 19, 17, 15, 15, 13, 11, 10, 10. Reliable, balanced, ideal for organized play.
All Ability Scores begin at 8. Distribute 60 points as you wish, to a maximum of 25 per score. All points must be spent before play; they cannot be banked or converted.

The most common way to increase an Ability Score is by spending skill points on talents within skill trees. Class features, Heritage abilities, legendary Deeds, and — at a cost — certain Corruption Paths may also raise scores.

Raw physical power. Add your Strength Bonus Dice to melee and unarmed damage and to feats of force. High Strength characters are imposing and confident in the press of combat; low Strength ones lean on finesse, speed, or the arcane.
Encumbrance. A Medium creature carries 10 lbs per point of Strength. Large creatures carry 20 lbs per point.
Strength Skill Checks. Applied to lifting, breaking, grappling, and overpowering.
Balance, grace, and coordination. Add your Agility Bonus Dice to attack rolls and most physical skills. Little is derived directly from Agility, but almost everyone needs it.
Agility Skill Checks. Applied to precise movement, weapon attacks, stealth, and acrobatic feats.
Physical resilience and endurance. Durability and Vigor are both seeded by Stamina. High Stamina endures; low Stamina flags.
Stamina Skill Checks. Applied to long exertion, resisting exposure, and holding ground under strain.
Reaction time and mobility. Add your Speed Bonus Dice to Defense reactions.
Movement. On foot, your base combat movement as a single action is 10 feet + 5 feet per Speed Bonus Die. You may take any number of Move actions on your turn.
Speed Skill Checks. Applied to dodging, sprinting, and reflexive catches.
Spiritual resilience, eldritch essence, intuition, destiny. Mana is derived from Soul. Aura-users and Mage-casters both rely on it for raw power.
Soul Skill Checks. Applied to spellcasting, ritual work, sensing the unseen, and contending with beings beyond.
Emotional intelligence and personal magnetism. Add your Empathy Bonus Dice to most interpersonal and performative skills.
Empathy Skill Checks. Applied to persuasion, reading faces, calming and charming, and all Performances.
Cognitive recall and capacity to learn. The number of Disciplines — techniques, spells, Performances, Formulas — you can actively maintain scales with Memory.
Languages. You are literate and fluent in your native tongue. At 1st level, you know one additional language per Memory Bonus Die.
Memory Skill Checks. Applied to recall, lore, linguistics, and learning new maneuvers.
Logic, deduction, and invention. Add your Reason Bonus Dice to Awareness, investigation, engineering, and problem-solving.
Reason Skill Checks. Applied to puzzles, forensics, diagnostics, and strategic calculation.
Strength of will and mental fortitude. Many Fortitude saves, concentration checks, and a handful of Traits derive from Resolve.
Resolve Skill Checks. Applied to intimidation, enduring terror, holding a spell through pain, and confronting knowledge that would break lesser minds.

You will add one relevant Ability Score's Bonus Dice whenever you make a skill check, depending on how the skill is used. An Athletics check to sprint takes Speed; to grapple takes Strength; to endure a forced march takes Stamina. The GM is the final arbiter of which score applies in ambiguous cases.
When you are targeted by an attack, you are entitled to a Reaction — a Defense check against physical blows, or a Fortitude check against mental and magical assaults.

Destined is a special condition that represents the swell of a character's fate — the moment their story tilts from adventure toward myth. When you are Destined, you stand on the brink of something extraordinary.
You may become Destined by:

items: Rolling a natural 20 on an Action roll.
Succeeding after taking 3 Chances.
Bursting 3 Bonus Dice on a single roll.
Performing a narratively heroic or iconic action (GM discretion).

blocks.html: You may only hold one Destined state at a time. You may spend it to:

items: Gain +3 Bonus Dice to any Action or Reaction.
Recover ¼ of your maximum Durability, Vigor, or Mana as an Action on your turn.
Take one Action out of turn, so long as it is not on another player's turn.
Activate your Ultimate on your turn (if you have the required Vigor or Mana).

blocks.html: Four reserves govern what your character can sustain, push past, and pour out: Durability, Health, Vigor, and Mana. A fifth concept — your Cultivation Engine — is not itself a reserve but the structure through which Mana is shaped into extraordinary effect.

Your starting Durability equals your Stamina + Resolve. Damage strikes Durability first; this represents minor wounds, bruises, and mounting fatigue. Each level, your Durability increases by +1d6, plus any gains from class Foundation Talents and purchased talents.
Your starting Health equals your Blood score + 1d10. Each level, it increases by +1d10. When Durability is depleted, damage begins to cut into Health. Health recovers slowly. At 0 Health, you die.

Your initial Vigor is 4d6 + 20. You spend Vigor on Techniques, Edges, and any physical feat beyond ordinary exertion. Each level, Vigor increases by +1d6; talents and Foundation Talents will stack further.

Your initial Mana is 4d6 + 20. Mana fuels Spells, Mysteries, rituals, and feats of the spirit. Each level, Mana increases by +1d6; talents, Foundation Talents, and — most importantly — your cultivated Aura Cores or Mana Circles stack on top.

Those who wield divine power possess or are granted Grace in place of mana. Grace cannot be used to cast arcane spells, but can be used for reactions and activate many mana based abilities, resulting in unique effects.
While you hold Grace you cannot recover, channel, share, or manipulate Mana. The maximum amount of Grace you can hold is equal to your maximum Mana.

Grace takes on the characteristics of its domain, if any. The Grave of a god of like is luminous and golden, the Grace of a frost deity is chilly and invokes rime, and the grace of the god of darkness is a liquid shadow that strangles the light.

Through mana it is possible to defy nature and achieve superhuman power. Warriors create Aura that can empower their body, arms, and armor. Mages turn the force outward, transforming mana into potent Magic.

To cultivate this power, one must either forge an Aura Core or engrave a Mana Circle. Each of these Cultivation engines shapes how extraordinary power is stored, channeled, expressed, or rewritten. Every step of cultivation is a Talent purchased from your class's elite skill tree. As such, Cultivation is never automatic, It is earned through
The warrior does not cast. The warrior blazes.

A Warrior's mana does not pool idle in the chest — it is forged, through long discipline, into a radiant core at the solar plexus, around which Stars ignite one by one. Each Star is a focus of cultivated force, visible to trained eyes as a mote of inner light; together they compose the warrior's Aura Core. When the Warrior unleashes a Technique empowered by aura, those Stars flare through the body, lending weight to a blow, edge to a blade, or terror to a stance.
Each Aura Star you cultivate grants:

items: +1/2 your Soul Score to Mana.
+1/2 your Stamina Score to Vigor.
+1 Bonus Die to all aura discipline effects.
Access to higher-tier Aura Techniques, gated by the total number of Stars you possess.

blocks.html: Stars are cultivated by purchasing the corresponding Talent from your Warrior tree or Heroic, Epic, and Legendary Classes that feature Aura. You may possess from 1 to 10 Stars. The First Star is the hardest — it requires a genuine awakening of the inner core — and the Tenth is the province of Sword Saints and their kin.
The mage's heart is a library in motion.

Where the Warrior ignites, the Mage inscribes. Through study, ritual, and meditation, the Mage cultivates formal Circles of Mana inside the heart — concentric rings of structured power, architectural in their discipline. Each Circle is a body of spellwork made permanent in the self, and each spell the Mage casts passes through the Circles like light through stained glass, taking on color and force from the rings it crosses.
Each Mana Circle you cultivate grants:

items: +Your soul Score to maximum Mana.
+1 Bonus Die to all Spell effects.
Access to higher-rank Spells, gated by the total number of Circles you possess.

blocks.html: Circles are cultivated by purchasing the corresponding Talent from your Mage tree (or its Heroic, Epic, and Legendary continuations). You may possess from 1 to 10 Circles. The First Circle is the Apprentice's triumph; the Tenth is the province of Archmagi and those the world names Sorcerer-Saints.

A character may develop more than one engine. A Warrior who takes a Mage Heroic class can cultivate Stars and Circles; their Mana pool gains from both, and they gain the appropriate Bonus Dice to each engine's relevant Disciplines. Combined engines are powerful but demanding — each Star or Circle is a full Talent purchase, and a divided cultivator climbs each ladder slower than a singular one. The mechanics of class transitions, shared talents, and multi-engine interplay are covered in detail in Chapter Eleven.

Vigor is the fire you feed on to push your body past its limits: to sprint through a collapsing hall, to strike twice where one blow was earned, to stand when you should be down. Mana is the deep quiet beneath thought, the current that carries spells and senses beyond the fleshly. Both are finite. Both are recoverable. Both, used well, are the difference between a survivor and a corpse.
Regardless of class or engine, every character may use the following baseline Vigor and Mana actions.
Vigor Cost: 8

When you take an Attack action or use a light weapon Technique on your turn, you may make an Off-hand Attack as part of the same action. The Off-hand Attack suffers a 2 Challenge penalty to hit and damage.
Vigor Cost: 10
For 1 minute, your Speed Score is treated as 1.5× its value for calculating movement.
Vigor Cost: 5
As a Reaction to an opponent creating an Opening, you may make a regular weapon or unarmed attack against them, so long as you are within range.
Vigor recovers quickly. At the end of each combat round, all combatants recover 1 point of Vigor per 5 points of Stamina. During a Break or Sleep, all Vigor is restored.

When your Vigor is exhausted, you become Winded. While Winded, you cannot take any Actions in combat. Outside combat, you may only move at Slow Pace and speak until you have recovered at least 5 points of Vigor.
Mana Cost: 10

As an Action, you enter a meditative trance, channeling Mana into recovery. For every uninterrupted minute of meditation, you recover 1d4 Durability. After one uninterrupted hour, you recover 1d6 Health.
Mana Cost: 15
As an Action, channel Mana to sharpen your perception. Your intuition grants one of the following:

items: Become aware of invisible or ethereal creatures within 30 feet on a successful Awareness check.
Instantly become aware of Fiends and beings from beyond within 30 feet.
Identify whether a person, object, or area is influenced by Eldritch or Mystical force, even if normally concealed.

blocks.html: Mana Cost: 10
At the start of each of your turns, you may spend 10 Mana to ignore the effects of 1 Condition until the end of your turn. You can nullify a number of Conditions equal to 1 plus your Soul Bonus Dice.

A creature who can manipulate arcane energy may willingly lend up to ½ their Mana to you. Up to 12 creatures can combine their Mana in this way. The excess may be used to restore your reserves or, briefly, supplement your maximum — held no longer than 1 minute.

Anyone able to touch arcane energy can refill their reserves from an external source of Mana. Otherwise, Mana recovers only through rest. Each night's rest restores 20 points of Mana, plus your Soul Bonus Dice in additional points.
Mana Cost: 0

By mindful stillness, you can deepen your Mana's return. For every uninterrupted minute of meditation, you recover 1 point of Mana, up to your Soul Score. Cultivation Meditation may be practiced once per day.

In the Realms of Ouroboros, Cultivation Meditation is the shared inheritance of all four paths — the Warrior's morning forms, the Mage's silent inscription, the Rogue's stillness before the strike, the Spiritualist's listening prayer. It is the first discipline every apprentice learns, and the last one any master ever fully leaves behind.

Grace is consumed invoking miracles and blessing. While you are holding Grace, you can still use Resistance Reactions and Will to Power, Deep Meditation, and Intuition by consuming Grace in place of Mana. Normally Magical and Aura abilities cannot be activated while you hold Grace.
Unless a creature is born with innate Divine Energy, Grace must be accumulated through prayer and supplication to one or more divine entities.

To gain grace, you must be at full Mana, and offer that energy up to your divine patron over 30 minutes of prayer and supplication. At the end of this time, your mana is consumed, and you gain an equal amount of grace. Once Grace is exhausted, a creature cannot recover Grace through prayer, they must wait for their mana to recover so they may offer it in ritual again.

For creatures without Divine Energy, grace does not recover with rest. However so long as they still hold at least 1 Grace, they can recover Grace through prayer. Each minute in prayer and supplication can recover 1 point of Grace, up to your soul Score. You can recoup grace up to once per day through prayer for each of your Soul Bonus dice.

You are what you were born, and you are what the world did to you. The first is written in bone and breath; the second is written in scar and story. Between them stands the person you choose to become.

In the Realms of Ouroboros, every hero begins with two answers: what they are, and how they were shaped. The first is Heritage — the shape of your body, the cast of your blood, the innate gifts and burdens of your lineage. The second is History — the life you lived before the adventure found you, the trials that broke or tempered you, and the skills you carry forward. Neither alone defines you. A Wulver raised in a merchant collegium is a scholar with a wolf's nose; a Human raised by mountain Dwergar thinks in stone and steel. In this game, Heritage and History are distinct layers, and together they produce characters far more textured than either could alone.
Any Heritage may take any History. Your Heritage is what you are; your History is who you were. What you become is a matter for the dice, your choices, and the road ahead.
Lineage, ancestry, and bloodline. To some a blessing, and to others a burden.

Heritage defines your biology: your physical shape, your innate senses, the cast of your ability scores, and the organic gifts your bloodline grants. Heritage is always innate and never cultural. A Nox Ceith raised among Humans still sees in the dark; a Dwergar fostered by elves still hears iron ring in the walls. Culture, upbringing, and learned behavior belong to History, not Heritage.

Radiant Age is built to be portable. Each heritage is presented with deliberately broad flavor — a silhouette, a biology, a temperament — so that any group can drop it into any campaign: a high-courtly fantasy of duelists and arcanists, a grim sword-and-sanity mire, a wandering pilgrimage through ruined empires, or a cultivation-sect epic of rival schools. A short "At the Table" note on each heritage offers suggestions for how it adapts to different campaign registers.

Broad-shouldered, broad-minded, and everywhere. The descendants of old migrations and long adaptation, Men in the Realms of Ouroboros are as varied as the lands they walk. From the glass-skyed capitals to the coldest peaks, they fit in because they have always fit themselves in — sometimes with grace, sometimes with force, and sometimes by being the first to arrive with a name for the place.

Humans are the great improvisers of Ouroboros — neither the strongest nor the keenest, but uncannily adaptable, quick to learn, and quicker still to rebuild a life after it has been taken from them. They live short lives by the measure of the Ceith and long lives by the measure of many Beastrals, and they have stamped their mark on every continent.

Physiology. Humans vary more in appearance than any other heritage. Heights range from barely five feet to well over six; complexions run from the palest snow-country whites through every shade of brown to the deepest sun-polished black. Hair colors, eye colors, and facial structures change by region and lineage more than by any universal rule. Musculature runs from wiry runner's-build to heavy-boned wrestler to soft round-shouldered scholar. On every individual axis Humans are unremarkable — no exceptional sense, no signature resilience — but in the aggregate, the balance of their faculties proves remarkably difficult to outwit over a lifetime. They reach adulthood in twenty years and rarely see past a hundred.

Disposition. Humans tend toward ambition and restlessness. They tell stories about themselves and then try to become the people in those stories. They keep company across lines that older peoples refuse to cross, and they carry on when older peoples would retreat. Most of the largest cities in Ouroboros are Human cities; most of the smallest and strangest cults are also Human cults.
At the Table. Default to Human when concept outpaces mechanics, or when you want a character defined almost entirely by History and class. Humans fit any campaign register without adjustment.
Type. Mortal (Hominid)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d4 to any two Ability Scores of your choice.
Human Ingenuity. Gain 1 additional Skill Point at 1st level and 1 additional Skill Point every 4 levels thereafter.
Adaptable. You may re-roll any one Ability Score check once per day, keeping the better result.

The Oni-blooded. Ogres are the largest of the Men — nine to eleven feet of charcoal-skinned, horned, tusked giant-kin whose blood runs with the memory of the old mountain Oni. To children, they are the shapes in campfire stories. To armies, they are the shapes that wore the armor in campfire stories. Most Ogres wear the weight of both with the patience of people who have been stared at since they could stand.

Physiology. Ogres stand between nine and eleven feet at adulthood, weighing twelve to fifteen hundred pounds, with slab-muscled frames, broad flat chests, and disproportionately wide hands. Their skin runs dark — most commonly deep charcoal, ashen slate, or smoked-iron grey, with some lineages showing a dusky crimson or a mottled basalt-blue. Curved horns rise from the brow, thick at the root and sweeping forward or outward — some lineages curl them ram-like, others bear two or four straight-thrusting horns arrayed along the ridge of the skull. Heavy lower tusks jut upward past the line of the lip, short and blunt in youth, growing longer with age until elders carry tusks nearly the length of a human's forearm. Their hair is coarse, most commonly white or iron-grey, worn long and often braided with brass, bone, or hammered iron. Eyes are gold, amber, copper, or a deep volcanic red. They mature slowly — full adulthood past thirty — and live two full centuries, eating three times what a human does.

Disposition. Ogres tend toward deliberate speech and slow decisions. They are patient, methodical, and difficult to provoke — and very difficult to stop once provoked. Most Ogres carry a lifelong awareness that their size and silhouette shape every room they enter before they have said a word. Many learn to speak softly to spare others the trouble; a few learn to speak loudly to save themselves the same.

At the Table. Ogres fit as northern giants in grim fantasy, as patient monastic smiths in a cultivation setting, or as exiled noble-born warriors in a high-court game. Their Oni silhouette also slots naturally into any campaign drawing on Eastern-fantasy aesthetics — sect enforcers, mountain hermits, demon-coded champions who are not, in fact, demons.
Type. Mortal (Hominid, Giantkin)
Size. Medium (counts as Large where beneficial).
Ability Score Modifiers. +2d8 Strength, +1d8 Stamina.
Oni Blood. Where it is beneficial — encumbrance, resisting knockdown, grappling — you count as a Large creature. You need not qualify as Large where it would disadvantage you.
Tusks and Horns. Your horns and tusks together count as a natural Unarmed weapon dealing 1d10 Piercing damage on a charge or headbutt, or 1d6 Bludgeoning damage in close combat.
Giant Appetite. You require twice the food and water of a human. Armor, clothing, and mounts cost half again as much due to your size.
Enduring Flesh. Gain +20 starting Durability and +10 starting Vigor.

The children of mixed Ogre and Human parentage. Half-Ogres inherit the stature and features of one parent and the quickness of the other, and the proportions vary enough from individual to individual that two siblings can look very different. They are uncommon but not rare, and typically pass for very large, oddly-featured Humans until they stand shoulder-to-shoulder with one.

Physiology. Seven to eight feet tall, weighing five to seven hundred pounds. They carry visible echoes of their Oni heritage — most have a softened charcoal, ashen, or dusky cast to the skin; many have short, curved horns that grow in at adolescence and remain visible rather than ornamental; a few have small upward-jutting tusks. Their features are broader and heavier than a human's, with thicker brows and larger hands, softened by the human admixture. Some Half-Ogres pass more easily for Ogre; some for Human; most, for neither. They live as long as a long-lived human, rarely more than a century and a half.

Disposition. Half-Ogres are accustomed to not quite fitting. They learn early to be larger than a room, and most of them either lean into it or carry a quiet, practiced restraint. Their emotional range tends to be wider than their Ogre parents and calmer than their Human ones.

At the Table. Excellent fits for any campaign needing a physically imposing hero who is still culturally a Man. Works equally well as a reluctant gentle giant, a village protector, a wandering duelist, or a legitimate heir whose bloodline gives a court pause.
Type. Mortal (Hominid)
Size. Medium (counts as Large where beneficial).
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Strength, +1d8 Stamina.
Straddling Blood. Where it is beneficial, you count as a Large creature. You need not qualify as Large where it would disadvantage you.
Small Horns. Your horns are a natural Unarmed weapon dealing 1d6 Piercing damage on a charge or headbutt.
Broad Bearing. Gain +10 starting Durability.
Two Worlds. At 1st level, gain Novice rank in one skill of your choice, representing a foot in two cultures.

The old peoples. Long-lived, long-remembering, never quite at ease with hurry. The Ceith are three living branches of an older and vanished trunk, and the difference between them is carved in bone and light.

The tallest and most slender of the Ceith, Eld Ceith are the heirs of the old high courts — bright-eyed, sharp-featured, and carrying in their blood a diffuse ancestral recall that lets them draw on the knowledge of their lineage at moments of need. They are the rarest of the three Ceith branches and the most formally organized.

Physiology. Six to seven feet tall, finely proportioned, with a slenderness that reads as fragile until one watches them move. Complexions run from moonlit white through warm ivory to soft gold, with a faint inner luminosity that becomes more pronounced under starlight. Hair is typically silver, platinum, deep blue-black, or a rare pale honey; it is fine and keeps its color through all their long years. Eyes are often luminous at the edges — a faint violet, silver, or pale-green rim to the iris. Their ears are long and elegantly tapered, swept back along the line of the skull. They live four to six centuries, growing visibly more refined with age rather than frailer.

Disposition. Eld Ceith are precise, reserved, and inclined to think in generations rather than seasons. Most find human haste exhausting; most treat Ceithlin as country cousins and Nox Ceith as unfortunate reminders of older griefs. Their formality is sometimes mistaken for coldness; it is more often an inherited sense that words, once given, become permanent in a way shorter-lived peoples do not fully understand.

At the Table. Perfect for high fantasy and courtly cultivation games. Adjust tone to your table: an Eld Ceith can be an imperious aristocrat, a melancholy exile from a vanished high kingdom, or the quiet last student of a dying school.
Type. Mortal (Ceith)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Soul, +1d6 Memory.

Ancestral Memory. Once per day, when you fail a skill check involving lore, language, or arcane knowledge, you may draw on ancestral memory to succeed instead, as if you had rolled 20 + your relevant Bonus Dice.
Long Years. You are immune to natural aging and do not grow frail with time. You still die of violence and disease, but not of years.
Luminous Eyes. You see clearly in dim light. In total darkness, you suffer no more than 1 Challenge to sight-based checks.

Smaller, quicker, and more earthborn than their Eld cousins, the Ceithlin are the wild Ceith — wood-walkers, moon-watchers, the old woodland blood. They are shorter-lived than Eld Ceith but sharper in the moment, and far more numerous across the settled lands.

Physiology. Five to five and a half feet tall, lithe and quick, with compact, efficient musculature and an easy athleticism that persists into old age. Complexions range from pale green-tinged ivory through rich golden-brown to deep bronze — some Ceithlin lineages carry faint freckling along the shoulders and bridge of the nose that darkens to a constellation-like pattern in summer. Hair is often leaf-green, copper, deep red, or chestnut, sometimes worn long and braided, sometimes cropped short for woodsmanship. Their ears are sharper and more upswept than the Eld, often flushing visibly with emotion. Eyes are typically green, amber, or pale grey, with pupils that expand widely in low light. They live two centuries.

Disposition. Ceithlin are quicker to laugh, quicker to anger, and quicker to leave than Eld Ceith. They value freedom and dislike the deep courts. Most prefer wilderness, small towns, or the outskirts of civilization to its center. The word the Eld use for Ceithlin in their old language roughly translates as "the uncounted" — a designation Ceithlin themselves often wear with pride.
At the Table. The woodland adventurer, the moonlit duelist, the scout at the edge of the map. Ceithlin fit any campaign that wants a fey-feeling hero without the formal archaism of the Eld.
Type. Mortal (Ceith)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Agility, +1d6 Speed.
Moonsense. Under moonlight or starlight, you gain +1 BD to Awareness, Stealth, and Survival checks.
Dimvision. You see clearly in dim light and suffer no more than 1 Challenge in total darkness.
Light Step. You move through natural undergrowth at full speed and leave no trail a common tracker can find.

The dusk Ceith, sometimes called the twilit or the sundered. Their line was changed in an old catastrophe, and the change is carried in skin, eye, and shadow. They are rarer than their cousins and more quietly feared — a reputation most of them carry with cultivated poise because the alternative is tiring.

Physiology. Five and a half to six and a half feet tall, slender but less reed-thin than the Eld, with complexions that run dusky, ashen, or violet-tinged — some deep slate-grey, some warm umber, some almost lavender in direct sunlight. Hair shifts from white through every cool shade to pure black, and many Nox Ceith have hair that shifts subtly in color between direct light and shadow. Their eyes are large, reflective, and colored in violet, cold silver, mirror-grey, or pure black; in low light the iris opens wide enough to swallow the pupil. Their ears are pointed and slightly swept back, often hung with small ornaments of old silver. They live three to four centuries.

Disposition. Nox Ceith are known for their poise and their unreadability, a reputation most of them cultivate because they have little choice. The old catastrophe is a cultural touchstone; most Nox Ceith carry an awareness of inherited grief that outsiders find unnerving even when it is not directed at them. Beneath the composure, many are deeply emotional — the stillness is a discipline, not an absence.

At the Table. The mysterious stranger, the assassin-diplomat, the shrine attendant who hears voices no one else does. Nox Ceith shine in gothic, occult, and spiritualist-flavored campaigns, and their aesthetic fits especially well in cultivation settings where old grief and older magic sit side by side.
Type. Mortal (Ceith)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Agility, +1d6 Soul.
Dimvision. You see clearly in dim light and suffer no more than 1 Challenge in total darkness.
Shadowsight. You can see faint traces of lingering magic and recent spiritual contact within 30 feet, as a brief afterglow visible only to you.
Stillness. You suffer no Challenge for being motionless for long periods; your breath, pulse, and body temperature can be lowered at will to evade both mundane and spiritual detection.

Broad of chest, broad of will, and very hard to move. The Dwerin peoples are the earth-kin — the ones whose blood remembers stone, whose hands remember craft, and whose feuds last longer than kingdoms. Three branches walk the world: the great-shouldered Dwergar, the light-footed Hauflin, and the Dwerlin who stand between.

Classic mountain-dwarves: stocky, bearded, stone-loving, and stubborn to a fault. Dwergar live in great holds and mountain-cities, keep long grudges and longer crafts, and are the unmatched smiths of Ouroboros — a reputation seated not in skill alone but in a bloodline sense that reads the bones of iron the way other peoples read a book.

Physiology. Four and a half to five feet tall but densely built, often weighing as much as a Medium Human despite being a full head shorter. Their bones are thick and their musculature heavy; a Dwergar grip is the stuff of proverbs. Complexions run from pale mountain-white through ruddy bronze to deep loam-brown, and most show a ruddy flush along the cheekbones that deepens with work or drink. Hair is thick, coarse, and takes braids well — beards grow generously on all genders and are a point of personal expression, with some traditions weaving them through with brass rings, small iron tokens, or polished hematite beads. Eyes are often dark, iron-grey, or the deep amber of forge-glow. They live two to three centuries and seem to grow denser rather than frailer with age.

Disposition. Dwergar are direct, pragmatic, and invested in their work to a degree outsiders sometimes mistake for fanaticism. Honor, debt, and craft are the three axes of Dwergar ethics; a Dwergar who feels no obligation to any of the three has already left their people in spirit, whatever roof they sleep under.

At the Table. Fits any campaign wanting a classic tough-as-stone character. Adjust for tone: a Dwergar can be a boisterous tavern-goer, a silent warrior-monk, a court-sworn artificer, or a grim veteran whose hands remember the forge better than they remember faces.
Type. Mortal (Dwerin)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Stamina, +1d6 Resolve.

Lodestone Sense. You possess an innate ferromagnetic sense: you perceive the presence, quantity, and rough shape of iron, steel, and other ferrous metals within 30 feet, even through stone, wood, or flesh. You can taste the purity and rhythm of a forging — Dwergar smiths describe worked iron as "singing" or "silent." Gain +1 BD to Crafting checks involving metal and to Awareness checks that could be solved by detecting weapons, armor, or metal objects.
Stoneborn. You have advantage on checks to resist poisons, toxins, and eldritch effects. Gain +15 starting Durability.
Deep Sight. You see clearly in dim light and can navigate unlit tunnels and caves with no Challenge.

The small folk. Hauflin are short, round, and disproportionately hard to kill, with a quiet talent for slipping out of places other people are still entering. They are the least fearsome of the Dwerin at first glance — and by far the hardest to corner. Something in their blood is stubborn against the rot that eats at other peoples, and it shows.

Physiology. Three to three and a half feet tall, compact and solid, weighing around eighty to a hundred pounds. Their proportions are rounder and softer than the wiry Dwerlin or the dense Dwergar — broad across the shoulders, thick through the middle, with short powerful legs and nimble fingers that move faster than their round bodies suggest. Complexions run warm pink through soft brown to deep umber; hair is thick, often curly, worn long by some and shaved short by others. Their feet are large for their bodies and often tough-soled enough to go unshod in most weather. Eyes are large and expressive, typically brown, hazel, or green. They live well past a human's span, some to 180 years, and grow visibly wrinkled and weather-worn rather than frail.

Disposition. Hauflin are sociable, practical, and deeply invested in the small business of living well: good food, good company, a warm hearth, and the firm refusal to be involved in other people's wars unless the war comes to them. Once it does, they are formidable — partly because everyone else assumes they will not be.
At the Table. The unassuming burglar, the village healer, the cheerful spy, the scout no one sees coming. Hauflin slot easily into any game that wants a hero shorter than the adversity.
Type. Mortal (Dwerin)
Size. Small
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Agility, +1d6 Empathy.
Lucky Step. Once per day, when you would fail a Defense reaction, you may instead treat the check as having rolled a natural 15. Something always seems to give you a half-step of warning.
