Small and Quick. You can move through the space of any Medium or Large creature without provoking an opening, and you gain +1 BD to Stealth checks.

Stubborn Blood. Hauflin blood resists the rot that afflicts other peoples. Your Corruption Malediction threshold is 10 points rather than the standard 5 — your first Malediction arrives only at 10 Corruption, the second at 20, and so on. You also have advantage on checks to resist poisons and intoxicating effects.

The middle branch of the Dwerin kin — lean where Dwergar are dense, taller where Hauflin are short, and quicker on their feet than either. Dwerlin are what happens when the stonebound blood of the Dwergar meets the light-footed blood of the Hauflin and the result walks a third road: a compact, agile people who read the world through stone-and-footstep rather than through song-of-iron or hearth-comfort.

Physiology. Three and a half to four feet tall, leaner and wirier than Hauflin, with long arms for their size and quick clever fingers. Their features are sharper than a Hauflin's and less heavy-browed than a Dwergar's — some have the round-cheeked look of their small-folk kin, some the long-jawed cast of their mountain kin, most have some mix. Complexions run warm umber, pale sandstone, or deep clay-red; hair is usually thick and often worn short and practical, though a few Dwerlin grow short beards in the Dwergar fashion. Eyes are dark, coppery, or pale grey. They live to around 160 years, a comfortable middle between their Dwergar and Hauflin kin.

Disposition. Dwerlin are observant, restless, and quicker to travel than either of their cousin peoples. They are the mapmakers and couriers of the Dwerin, the scouts who run long roads between holds, the cousins who left home because home was loud and came back rarely because the road was interesting. Most carry the Dwergar's patience in long work and the Hauflin's warmth in close quarters, and neither quite dominates.

At the Table. The ranger-cousin, the stone-roader, the light-footed guide. Fits any campaign that wants a small, agile Dwerin character who is neither a classic dwarf nor a halfling. Particularly good as scouts, couriers, and wayfinders in any setting where long overland travel matters.
Type. Mortal (Dwerin)
Size. Small
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Agility, +1d6 Resolve.
Quick Kin. Your base Speed is 5 feet higher than normal for your Speed score. You can move through the space of any Medium or Large creature without provoking an opening.

Stone Ear. You can sense the structure and stability of worked and unworked stone by touch. With a moment of contact, you know whether a wall, floor, or tunnel is sound, weakened, recently disturbed, or hollow behind. Gain +1 BD to Awareness checks involving stonework and underground environments.
Endurance of the Road. Gain +10 starting Vigor. You recover Vigor at 1.25× the standard rate on long marches and while traveling overland.

Older than the histories of Men, older than the first cities of the Ceith. The Beastrals are the peoples whose bodies remember the shape of great beasts and whose minds remember the old covenants between hunter and hunted.

The wolf-kin. Lean, fast, pack-minded, and proud in a way that other peoples sometimes read as aloof. Wulver are the most numerous of the Beastrals and the most widely scattered, found in the far north, in forested interiors, and in every city where a strong trade runs.

Physiology. Five and a half to six and a half feet tall, lean-muscled and long-limbed, with a full coat of short fur in greys, blacks, browns, and occasional whites, reds, or silvers. Faces are canine but expressive — muzzle-shaped with prominent canines, mobile ears that shift position with emotion, and bright amber, gold, or ice-blue eyes. Hands are fully articulated despite being furred, with short claws that the well-mannered trim and the less-mannered do not. Tails are bushy and carried high or low as an easily-read emotional tell. They mature at human speed and live a century and a quarter. Older Wulver silver at the muzzle and around the eyes long before they show any other sign of age.

Disposition. Wulver are loyal beyond reason to chosen kin — blood or sworn — and they read loyalty in others more keenly than most. Most Wulver orient themselves through their pack, which is not always their blood family. A Wulver without a pack is not lost, exactly, but is visibly looking.

At the Table. Any campaign that features travel, wilderness, or found-family bonds. Wulver fit equally well in a roaming mercenary company, a wandering monk-sect, a wolf-blooded noble house, or a scattered band of survivors drawn together by shared loss.
Type. Mortal (Beastral)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Stamina, +1d6 Agility.
Keen Senses. Gain +1 BD to Awareness checks involving scent or hearing. You can track by scent in conditions a human eye could not read.
Pack Instinct. When within 30 feet of a willing ally, you gain +1 BD to attack rolls against any enemy that ally has attacked or been attacked by in the current combat.
Runner's Frame. Your base Speed is 5 feet higher than normal for your Speed score, and you do not tire from overland running nearly as quickly as other creatures.

The lion-kin. Tall, gold-maned, regal by bearing and broad by build. Leodon are rarer than Wulver and more often found in warm lands, in old warrior lineages, and — increasingly — in the retinues of kings and sect leaders who want a visible statement of strength at their side.

Physiology. Six to seven feet tall, heavy-framed and broad through the chest and shoulders, weighing two to three hundred pounds of dense muscle. Their coat is short, smooth, and colored in gold, tawny, russet, or — in rare southern lineages — black with faint tawny undercoat. A pronounced mane grows at the shoulders and around the face in all sexes, though it presents most dramatically in some lineages and most subtly in others. Their faces are broad and leonine, with high cheekbones, flat noses, and prominent canines. Eyes are amber, copper, or gold. Hands are humanoid but bear retractable claws on each finger; tails are long and tufted at the tip. They live a century and a half and keep their physical prime late, silvering at the mane first.

Disposition. Leodon are proud without often being vain. They expect respect, give it easily when earned, and remember slights for a long time. Most Leodon carry a quiet sense of personal sovereignty that shapes how they enter rooms. Some are easy-tempered and lazy in the manner of sun-warmed cats; others are drill-disciplined and intense. Almost none are easily dismissed.
At the Table. The champion, the sworn sword, the royal guard, the noble-born cultivator. Works in any campaign that wants a physically and socially commanding figure.
Type. Mortal (Beastral)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Strength, +1d6 Empathy.
Natural Weapons. Your claws are a natural Light Weapon dealing 1d8 Slashing damage. Your bite is a natural Unarmed attack dealing 1d8 Piercing damage.
Regal Presence. Gain +1 BD to Social checks when openly asserting authority, making demands, or rallying allies.
Predator's Frame. Gain +10 starting Durability and +5 starting Vigor.

The reptile-kin. Scaled, cold-blooded, patient, and significantly tougher than they appear. Saurian are found in warm coastal regions and great riverlands, and wherever they settle, they tend to endure — quietly, deliberately, and across generations that outlast the houses built around them.

Physiology. Five and a half to six and a half feet tall, heavy-boned but not bulky, with smooth overlapping scales covering the entire body in greens, greys, bronzes, and occasional deep blues, blacks, or rusted coppers. Some lineages show faint patterning — banding, reticulation, dorsal stripes — that darkens with age. Their faces are flatter and more reptilian than the furred Beastrals, with broad jaws, prominent rows of short sharp teeth, and slit-pupiled eyes in gold, amber, green, or pale yellow. Small crests or horn-ridges may run from brow to nape in some lineages. Their tails are short, thick, and counterbalancing rather than prehensile. Hands and feet bear short claws. They mature slowly — past twenty-five — and live two centuries, shedding their outer scale layer once or twice a decade in a shedding season that is culturally significant among their kin.

Disposition. Saurian are famously unhurried. They think long, speak carefully, and act decisively when action is called for. Most Saurian find the emotional volatility of Men and Ceith tiring but not unwelcome — a kind of weather they have learned to live through. They tend to value clarity, accuracy, and the long view.

At the Table. The steady hand, the philosopher-warrior, the patient hunter, the stoic survivor. Saurian fit easily into any campaign and carry a temperamental gravity that balances lighter party members.
Type. Mortal (Beastral)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Stamina, +1d6 Strength.
Scaled Hide. Gain natural Armor 2 against piercing and slashing damage. This stacks with worn armor.
Cold Blood. You are resistant to cold and heat. In extreme weather, you suffer one fewer Challenge from temperature than other creatures would.
Patient Lungs. You can hold your breath for up to ten minutes without check, and you recover Vigor at 1.5× the standard rate when at rest.

Rarer than any of the main peoples, and stranger to the eye. These three heritages share no common trunk — they emerged from distinct old causes, and each exists in small numbers across the realms. All three bring a visible, setting-flavoring silhouette to the table.

The wood-walkers. Leimora are a people whose biology blurs between flesh and growing thing — bark-mottled skin, a slow sap through the veins, leaf-fine hair that shifts color with the seasons, and the distant memory of having once taken root somewhere. They share no lineage with the Ceith despite a surface resemblance; Leimora blood is older, stranger, and rooted in the living world itself rather than in any vanished high court.

Physiology. Six to six and a half feet tall, slender and upright, with smooth skin in deep forest-greens, soft birch-greys, pale moss-whites, or warm cedar-browns, marked here and there with darker bark-ridges, lenticular patches, or whorled grain patterns that deepen with age. A few elder Leimora develop genuine bark plating along the forearms and shoulders. Their hair is not hair in the ordinary sense — it grows as fine leaf-strands, long moss-tendrils, or delicate vine-like threads, and it shifts color with the seasons: pale green in spring, deep emerald in summer, russet and gold in autumn, bare and silver-white in deep winter. Their eyes are solid-colored without visible pupils — green, amber, honey-gold, or forest-black — and they focus by cant of the head rather than iris movement. Their blood is a thick amber sap that smells faintly of pine or cut wood. They live five centuries and do not tire from long sun.

Disposition. Leimora are slow to speak and slow to anger, but they are not placid. Most carry a deep attentiveness to the land around them and a long memory for how places used to be. They are rarely found in large cities for more than a season at a time, and those who settle in stone houses often keep a small garden or indoor tree whose roots they tend as a matter of personal wellbeing.

At the Table. The druid without the word, the wandering arboreal sage, the quiet diplomat from a distant grove. Works in high fantasy, nature-focused campaigns, and cultivation settings where contact with the land is valued. In gothic or ruined-empire settings, a Leimora's slow decay can carry real weight.
Type. Mortal (Plant)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Soul, +1d6 Stamina.
Sapwood. You do not bleed like other heritages. Natural healing restores +1 Durability per day above normal rates, and you recover Health on a long rest at 1.5× the normal rate.
Sun-Fed. A full day spent in direct sunlight with some water available sustains you in place of food for one day. You may still eat normally.
Rooted Stance. When you choose to remain still for a full round, your next Defense reaction gains +1 BD and you cannot be forcibly moved by non-magical means.
Slow Bones. Your base Movement is 5 feet lower than normal for your Speed score. You gain immunity to natural aging.

The Outer-Born. Varkai are not demons, not infernal, not fallen — they are the descendants of a stranded people, survivors of a craft or calling that crossed from somewhere beyond the sky and did not make it home. Superstitious peasants call them devil-blooded because of the silhouette; older scholars, and the Varkai themselves, know better. What the world reads as demonic is simply a biology that was never meant to meet the blood of this world.

Physiology. Six to seven feet tall, lean and long-limbed, with a proportional elegance that is not quite Ceith-like and not quite human. Skin runs in banded patterns of deep red, ashen black, rust-brown, or dark charcoal, often with paler marbling along the face, throat, and the insides of the forearms. A pair of horns rises from the brow — shape varies by lineage, from short backward-sweeping ram-curves to long forward-pointing spires to thin antler-like branches — and the horns grow measurably throughout life, tracking age the way tree-rings do. Eyes are yellow, orange, red, or a pale violet, with pronounced slit pupils that dilate vertically. Their teeth are sharp, with four distinct canines rather than two. A few lineages carry vestigial dorsal ridges or small rear-facing spurs at the elbow, remnants of a biology no longer fully expressed. They live two and a half centuries.

Disposition. Varkai are accustomed to being looked at and seldom flinch. Most learn early to carry themselves with deliberate composure, because the alternative is to be read as threatening at every turn. Beneath the poise, Varkai tend toward intense convictions and deep, patient resolve — which can cut either way. Many keep fragmentary records of their people's lost origin, passed down as oral history or cryptic family texts; some still hope, in some form, to find their way back.

At the Table. The exile with a bloodline legend, the wandering warrior from a misunderstood lineage, the self-possessed hero whose very appearance drives the plot. Fits any campaign where visible difference carries dramatic weight. Excellent for gothic, cultivation, and high-drama games — and particularly rich in any setting that wants to interrogate what superstition reads as "demonic" when the truth is stranger and sadder.
Type. Mortal (Humanoid)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Strength, +1d6 Resolve.
Horns. Your horns are a natural Light Weapon dealing 1d8 Piercing damage on a charge, or 1d6 Bludgeoning damage in close quarters.

Outer Sense. You are biologically attuned to presences and phenomena not native to this world. You automatically detect the presence of Eldritch, infernal, Outer, or otherwise extraplanar forces within 30 feet. This sense does not identify them, only their presence — but you always know when something from beyond is nearby.

Offworld Resilience. Your biology tolerates environmental extremes that this world's flesh cannot. You are resistant to fire and hard radiation; you gain fire and radiation armor rank equal to your Resolve Bonus Dice.

Seen. You cannot easily pass unnoticed in most settled regions. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks in populated areas where Varkai are known and viewed with suspicion. This is not a cultural fact about Varkai — it is a social fact about everyone else.

The gem-born. Lustrate are not flesh as other peoples are flesh — their bodies are fully crystalline, a single continuous gem-structure shaped into a humanoid silhouette, colored and clouded according to the mineral of their inclusion. They do not eat, do not sleep as mortals sleep, and do not age. They shine. They ring when struck. They can be broken, and if carefully attended to, they can be made whole again. No one knows quite where the Lustrate came from, and the Lustrate themselves offer only fragments of an answer they find more interesting than troubling.

Physiology. Five and a half to six and a half feet tall, slender and uniformly proportioned, with a body that is translucent gemstone from surface to core. Color and inclusion vary by lineage: some Lustrate are pale milky chrysoprase, some deep sapphire, some champagne-clear citrine, some watermelon-banded tourmaline, some black obsidian shot with gold, some iridescent opal whose color changes as they move. A faint inner light pulses beneath the surface — brighter when they channel power, dimmer in sleep or deep reflection. Their hair is spun crystal filament, fiber-fine, in colors ranging from pale gold to deep iris-blue to transparent glass-white. Their eyes are polished faceted jewels, often of a hue distinct from the body — a pale-amethyst Lustrate may have garnet eyes; a rose-quartz one, cold obsidian. They do not bleed, do not breathe in the biological sense, and do not feel cold or heat as other mortals do. When struck hard enough, they craze, chip, or — in the worst cases — shatter; shards can be collected and, with skilled work, reintegrated into the body, though the memory of what a shard contained may be imperfect. A Lustrate can continue functioning for a time after losing a limb or a head — long enough to be found and gathered by their kin. They do not age and are not known to die of anything but violence or willing dissolution.

Disposition. Lustrate psychology is genuinely alien. Most are quiet, precise, and attentive in ways that other mortals find beautiful at first and unsettling at length. They are slow to form attachments and extraordinarily deep once formed; they think on timescales that have nothing to do with biological life; they find the urgencies of Men and Ceith and Beastrals sometimes charming and sometimes incomprehensible. Many Lustrate describe their inner life in terms of resonance — states of being that hum, states that ring, states that sit in silence. They love beauty with an intensity that defies easy description, and they grieve in the long way that only deathless things grieve.

At the Table. The porcelain knight, the gem-bodied mage, the hero whose body is the instrument. Lustrate shine in magic-forward and cultivation-forward campaigns, in gothic settings where their fragility becomes thematic weight, and in high-weird games where their alienness is a feature. They are particularly strong as characters with a long, slow arc — because they have the years to sustain one.
Type. Arcane (Construct)
Size. Medium
Ability Score Modifiers. +1d8 Memory, +1d6 Soul.

Gem-Body. You do not eat, drink, or breathe in the ordinary sense, though you take sustenance from mineral matter and sunlight over long timescales. You do not age and are immune to poisons, diseases, and biological effects. Your body rings softly when you move; at the GM's discretion, certain stealth attempts may suffer a minor Challenge as a result. Gain +15 starting Mana.

Reformed. You possess Integrity in place of Health. When you are reduced to 0 Integrity, you do not die in the ordinary way. Your body instead enters a dormant fractured state, scattered in pieces at the location of your fall. Given sufficient time, careful attention, and the presence of most of your fragments, allies or a skilled Lustrate artisan may reassemble you over the course of 1d6 weeks. But this remaking costs you some memory, knowledge, or personality detail, you lose 1 Skill point.

Crystalline Resonance. Your body is a natural conduit for arcane energy. You gain +1 BD to any Performance that uses song or spoken power — your voice carries a faint harmonic chime that instruments of flesh cannot match.
Brittle. Your body does not absorb impact the way flesh does. When an Impact damage bonus dice bursts against you take an additional +1d6 damage for each burst bonus dice.

Your History is not just where you came from — it is how the world shaped you before you became an adventurer. It defines your early environment, the skills you honed, the trials you endured, and the scars you carry.

In the Realms of Ouroboros, the ordinary world is wide and busy, and every character walked some recognizable path through it before the adventure found them. The sixteen Histories below are written as broad archetypes — a farmer, a scribe, a sailor, an entertainer — rather than as setting-specific roles. They work in any fantasy setting without adjustment. A Wulver raised as a Farmer is a farmer with a wolf's nose. A Varkai raised as a Noble is a noble whose bloodline makes the court nervous. The archetype is the shape; your Heritage and your class fill it in.
Any Heritage may take any History. Your Heritage is what you are; your History is who you were.

Most people with happy, comfortable lives do not become adventurers. The Trial from your History is the inciting incident — the event that catalyzed your step out of the ordinary and into the exceptional. Roll on your History's Trial table or select one that suits your character. Trials are narrative tools as much as mechanical ones; they give your character a past that matters, a wound not fully healed, and a reason to be sitting at the table when the adventure begins.
It is likely you will receive the same Novice skill from both your History and your Class. In that event, you may instead select one General Talent of your choice in place of the duplicate.

You served in a temple, shrine, monastery, or other house of faith — lighting candles, keeping records, learning prayers, caring for the sick, and observing the slow rhythms of devotion. The doctrine may or may not still hold you; the habits of service almost certainly do.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your temple was destroyed — by fire, by heresy's accusation, by war. You survived because you were elsewhere, and the guilt of that elsewhere has never fully eased.
You saw or heard something during a vigil that contradicted the doctrine you were taught. Your superiors refused to acknowledge it. You left quietly.
A senior of the order did something unforgivable, and the order closed ranks. You could not.
You completed your novitiate and were sent out with a specific task. The task remains unfinished, and the world keeps changing around it.
Your order's patron — deity, spirit, saint — withdrew its voice. You are one of the ones sent to find out why.
You simply outgrew the walls. The quiet work was no longer enough, and you took your leave with blessings and without regret.

blocks.html: Novice Occult.
Novice Medicine.

Acolyte Feature. You begin with an Acolyte's Kit: simple robes (Ordinary quality), a holy symbol or sect token, a prayer book or scripture scroll, an herbal satchel, and a bedroll with 4 days of rations. You also know one Ritual Discipline of Basic rank (see Chapter Ten).

You learned a trade — smithing, carpentry, masonry, tailoring, glassblowing, jewelcraft, alchemy, scribework. The apprenticeship gave you patient hands, an eye for quality, and the habit of seeing work as something the maker's body learns long before the mind explains it.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your master died, and the shop passed to someone who did not want you.
You made a piece on commission for a patron you should not have trusted. The piece is out in the world, doing something you did not intend.
A guild rival drove your shop out of business. You walked away with your tools and your reputation intact; little else.
You were discovered to have talents beyond ordinary craft. Your master sent you out to find what you really were.
You finished your apprenticeship and earned your journeyman's piece, but the trade had grown too narrow for you. You closed the shop door behind you.
You were cheated out of your rightful standing by politics or coin. You left the guild and took the road.

blocks.html: Novice Crafting.
Novice Social.

Artisan Feature. You begin with an Artisan's Kit appropriate to your trade, work clothes, and one Masterwork-quality item of your own making (weapon, tool, or article of clothing — subject to GM approval). You are recognized by your guild's mark anywhere that guild is known.

You made your living on the trust of strangers — fortune-teller, false physician, relic-seller, confidence trickster, letter-writer for the grieving. You learned the weight of a well-placed word, the theater of a cheap mystery, and the trick of knowing when to leave town.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: One of your marks turned out to be someone dangerous. They are looking for you. They will be patient.
A con went right in the immediate sense and wrong in every other sense — the victim was innocent, or young, or kind, and the guilt has changed you.
A fellow trickster sold you out for their own skin. You escaped. They did not.
You were once very nearly caught by a clever magistrate who let you go on the condition that you leave the city and never return. You are considering whether to keep the promise.
You ran a con that accidentally revealed a real conspiracy, cult, or crime. Someone is now looking for both of you.
The con stopped working — or you stopped being able to run it. Conscience, age, or simple exhaustion. You are looking for a new road.

blocks.html: Novice Social.
Novice Legerdemain.

Charlatan Feature. You begin with a Forgery Kit (pens, seals, blank parchments, colored inks), a set of disguise clothing, and one Forged Document of your choice — a letter of recommendation, a writ of passage, a credential, a bill of lading, or similar. You also carry one False Identity complete with a name, a plausible history, and modest reputation in a single city of your choice.

Thief, smuggler, burglar, fence, enforcer, back-alley assassin, road bandit — your trade ran along the edge of the law, and sometimes crossed it. You learned rooftops before you learned streets, and you learned which watchmen could be bought before you learned which could not.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: A crew leader sold you out. You survived the betrayal and made sure, in some small way, that they did not.
A rival crew took something from you — a partner, a sibling, a friend. The grief is old now, but it travels well.
A city crackdown swept up your people. You escaped; you do not know who did not.
You helped a stranger for no reason you can properly name. The help pulled you out of the life. You have not fully gone back.
A powerful patron took an interest in you. You do not know yet whether that was lucky.
You aged out. The hungry quickness of the trade is a young person's talent. You went looking for a longer game.

blocks.html: Novice Stealth.
Novice Legerdemain.

Criminal Feature. You begin with a Thief's Kit: dark clothing, a set of lockpicks, two concealed blades, a grappling hook and 50 feet of silk cord. You also have an Underworld Contact in a city of your choice — a fence, an informant, a door-holder — who will help you once before the favor is considered repaid.

Singer, dancer, actor, poet, acrobat, juggler, player of instruments — you earned your bread by making people look at you. You know how to read a room, how to hold an audience, and how to keep the purse coming at the end of the night.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: A patron turned against your troupe for reasons petty or personal. The troupe scattered. You are still writing letters, looking.
You caught the attention of a powerful person who believed the performance was for them alone. You left by a back door.
You performed a piece — a song, a play, a recitation — that offended the wrong authority. Your name carries a warrant in at least one city.
A fellow performer stole your work, your instrument, or your place in the company. You have not decided yet whether to pursue it.
You fell ill — in your voice, your hands, your feet — in a way that ended the career you had been building. You are rebuilding.
You simply wanted to see what the world looked like from outside a stage. You packed, and you went.

blocks.html: Novice Entertain.
Novice Social.

Entertainer Feature. You begin with your primary instrument (Ordinary quality) or a Troupe Kit (costumes, makeup, props, juggling implements as appropriate to your art). You also have a Patron or Recurring Venue — someone in a city or circuit who will welcome you back for at least one performance, and feed and house you while you prepare for it.

You worked the land — grain, orchard, livestock, vineyard, fish-pond, terrace, smallholding — from before you could read, if you ever learned to read. Your body knows seasons better than calendars, and your hands know the weight of a day's honest labor.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: A raid, a fire, or a plague took your family's holding. You are what remains.
A noble, a sect, or a crown claimed your land through writ and weapons. Your family was driven off.
You were conscripted — into a war, a work-gang, a corvée — and when you returned, there was no longer a farm to return to.
A blight, a flood, or an arcane misfortune made the land stop yielding. The village scattered. You scattered with it.
You fell out with your family over something neither side will now discuss. You left before the harvest, and the harvest did not go well without you.
You simply looked up one evening from the fields and realized that if you did not leave now, you never would.

blocks.html: Novice Athletics.
Novice Beast Handling.

Farmer Feature. You begin with a Farmhand's Kit: sturdy work clothes, a heavy tool that doubles as a weapon (scythe, pitchfork, hand-axe, flail, or staff), a bedroll, and 7 days of preserved rations. You are also known in your home region — among the farming and working classes of that area, you gain +1 BD to Social checks involving honest trade, hospitality, or plain speech.

You kept order in a place that needed keeping — city watch, caravan escort, gate warden, noble's house guard, temple sentinel, gaol-keeper. You learned how to stand a long post, how to read the small signs of trouble, and how to settle a fight before it became a fight.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: You witnessed a crime committed by your own superiors and refused to look the other way. You resigned before they made you.
You failed in a duty that mattered — a prisoner escaped, a charge was killed, a gate was left unlocked. The failure follows you.
You were dismissed for a trumped-up reason by a captain who feared you. The reason is on paper; the truth is not.
You caught someone powerful doing something terrible. You either spoke, and were forced out, or did not speak, and cannot forgive yourself.
A partner you trusted was killed on your watch. You walked away because the post no longer made sense.
Your post simply ended — the city fell, the noble died, the caravan route was closed. You kept moving.

blocks.html: Novice Heavy Weapons or Novice Light Weapons.
Novice Awareness.

Guard Feature. You begin with a Guard's Kit: a suit of leather armor (Ordinary quality), one melee weapon of choice (Ordinary quality), manacles, a signal whistle, a lantern, and a bedroll. You also have a Municipal Contact in a city of your choice — a former colleague on the watch, a magistrate's clerk, or a sergeant who remembers you fondly enough to answer a door at odd hours.

You led others through places they did not know — as a scout for travelers, a tracker for hunting parties, a river-pilot, a caravan outrider, a pilgrim's guide to distant shrines. You know the shape of the land you grew up on the way other people know the shape of their own hands.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: You led a group into something — a beast, a storm, a closed pass — that should not have been there. Most of them did not come back.
You were hired by someone who did not tell you what they were truly looking for. When you found it, things went badly.
A route you had known all your life was altered — by war, by magical catastrophe, by the shifting of land itself. The map in your head no longer matches the world.
You were betrayed by a client who wanted what you had been hired to find, and wanted it without you in the count.
You saw something on a remote road that no one in town will believe, and that you have not been able to forget.
You simply went further than usual one day, and the road you found there led somewhere you never left.

blocks.html: Novice Survival.
Novice Awareness.

Guide Feature. You begin with a Traveler's Kit: weather-fit clothing, a bedroll, 7 days of trail rations, a waterskin, a map case, flint and steel, and 100 feet of cord. You also carry a set of Detailed Maps covering one wilderness region, frontier, or trade route of your choice, annotated in your own hand with notes that no other map will show.

You lived alone, or nearly alone, for a long time — in a wilderness cell, a distant shrine, a ruin, a hut on a storm-coast, a cave beneath a mountain. What you were doing there is for you to answer. You came back with knowledge the settled world does not easily produce, and with habits the settled world finds peculiar.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your solitary study was interrupted by something that came looking for you. You survived the meeting. You are still deciding what it meant.
You left the settled world because of a loss you could not bear. The solitude has healed most of the wound. Most.
You stumbled across a truth that should not be widely known. You came back out to decide who ought to know it.
A teacher you had never met left you their work upon their death, and you spent years completing it. The work is now done, and you are no longer needed where you were.
You were cast out for reasons you now suspect were unjust. You are considering whether to go back and say so.
Your solitude was broken by a vision, a summons, or a portent that sent you back into the world with a purpose not yet clearly articulated.

blocks.html: Novice Medicine.
Novice Survival.

Hermit Feature. You begin with a Hermit's Kit: plain robes, a walking staff, a prayer-beads or mala, a simple herb pouch, and a bedroll. You also possess a Secret Knowledge — a specific piece of lore, technique, location, or truth that very few others know. Work this out with the GM at character creation; it may become a plot hook, a recurring advantage, or both.

You traded for a living — caravan master, shopkeeper, factor for a great house, river-boat trader, market stall-holder, traveling peddler. You learned to count in three currencies, haggle in five languages, and smell a bad deal before the first coin changes hands.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: A deal went catastrophically wrong, and your standing with a powerful concern collapsed. You carry the family's last good name.
Bandits, privateers, or raiders hit your convoy. You survived because you were quick, or lucky, or both. The caravan master did not.
A rival undercut your entire trade through means both legal and not. You closed the shop and took the road.
You were cheated by a patron powerful enough that you could not demand redress. You have become very careful about whose coin you accept.
A border was closed, a route made dangerous, a passage sealed. Your old circuit is no longer possible.
You simply wanted to see the places your goods came from before you sold any more of them. You went.

blocks.html: Novice Social.
Novice Studies.

Merchant Feature. You begin with a Trader's Purse of 3× starting silver, a well-traveled pack (bedroll, flint and steel, waterskin, 5 days rations, rain cloak), and a Merchant's Ledger: a personal accounting book that doubles as a network of contacts, debts owed, and credit extended. You have 1 contact at a significant waystation, inn, or trading post of your choice.

You were born into a house with land, title, or name. The particulars vary — a great duchy, a minor manor, a merchant-prince's estate, a sect-princely lineage, a trade dynasty — but the lessons are the same: how to bow, how to sit a horse, how to dance at a feast, and how to read the room when your father was angry.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your house fell. Betrayal from within, from without, or simply the grinding of a long fortune at last ground through. You are the wandering remainder.
Your house still stands, but cast you out. Heresy, scandal, refusal of a marriage — one of the old reasons. You left more cleanly than most.
You refused an arranged path — a betrothal, a clerical appointment, a sect assignment. The refusal has consequences you are still navigating.
You left voluntarily. The noble life is a narrow corridor, and you wanted to see what was outside it before the door closed for good.
You were the younger child or the illegitimate child, with no lands and no prospects, and you went out to make your own name. The old house will take you back if you earn it.
You are in exile because the court you loved was taken by a faction you could not abide. You intend to go back.

blocks.html: Novice Social.
Novice Entertain.

Noble Feature. You begin with a Noble's Kit: fine clothing, a signet or house-token, 4× starting silver, and a Letter of Introduction usable at one significant court, temple, or guildhouse of your choice. Your manners and bearing grant you +1 BD to Social checks when dealing with the noble or upper-merchant classes.

You pursued knowledge for its own sake — in an academy, a library, a research-temple, a magister's household, a wandering university. You read more than you slept, absorbed more than you spoke aloud, and developed the careful, methodical habits of the working scholar.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your thesis was stolen by a senior scholar who published it as their own. Your protests fell on deaf ears.
You uncovered forbidden knowledge that changed how you see the world. Someone noticed.
Funding was revoked. Your research was deemed frivolous, heretical, or dangerous, and you were dismissed.
A colleague died under mysterious circumstances during a shared experiment. You were cleared of blame. The whispers never stopped.
You grew restless. The world described in books called louder than the quiet halls of study.
You were recruited — by a lord, a temple, a secret society, or a sect — to put your knowledge to practical use in the field.

blocks.html: Novice Studies.
Novice Occult.

Sage Feature. You begin with a Scholar's Kit: literate in two languages (your native tongue plus one other), a writing set, a leather-bound journal, and a bronze astrolabe or magnifying lens. You also carry an ongoing Research Topic — a specific subject on which you are a recognized expert. In most scholarly institutions, presenting credentials on this topic grants you access and +1 BD to Social checks with other scholars.

You worked the water — ocean ships, river-boats, coastal traders, pilgrim-vessels between island shrines. The first rhythm you learned was the sway of planks under your feet, and the first language you learned was the old mixed cant of the working sea.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: The ship you grew up on was lost with most of its hands. You were ashore, or you were lucky, or you were brave. Only one of the three is true.
A harbor you called home was taken — by blockade, by sect, by raiders, by plague. You could not return.
You ran a cargo you should not have run. The coin was good. The consequences are still following the currents.
A kraken, a leviathan, or something worse crossed your bow. You saw what was under the water. You are no longer quite at ease there.
You fell in love in a port town and stayed a season too long. When you returned to the sea, the sea did not forgive you quickly.
You simply went ashore. The horizon had new shape from the road than from the rail.

blocks.html: Novice Athletics.
Novice Survival.

Sailor Feature. You begin with a Seafarer's Kit: oilskin coat and hat, a long knife, 50 feet of tarred cord, a waterproof tinderbox, a bedroll, and 5 days of ship's rations. You have a Ship's Contact — a captain or quartermaster who will take you on as crew, or carry you as a passenger with no questions, once. Further passage costs.

You worked with words on paper — copyist, clerk, secretary, letter-writer, courthouse recorder, monastic illuminator, magistrate's scribe. You know how a document is built, how it can be altered, and how a single well-placed line can change the course of a contract, a case, or a life.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: You copied a document you should not have copied. Someone found out, and you have been moving ever since.
You discovered a forgery in the archive you kept. The forgery implicated people who did not want to be implicated.
Your scriptorium was closed by edict or sacked by rival. The work you spent years on was lost or scattered.
You wrote a petition, a pamphlet, or a letter on behalf of someone powerless, and the wrong powerful person read it.
You simply could not stand another year of other people's words. You took your pens and left.
Your published work — a treatise, a chronicle, a law-commentary — drew the attention of a patron who now expects things from you.

blocks.html: Novice Studies.
Novice Social.

Scribe Feature. You begin with a Writing Kit (quills, ink, 50 sheets of parchment, wax and seal-ring, a leather portfolio) and a bound copy of one Published Work of your own — a pamphlet, chronicle, treatise, or commentary. Among literate audiences, presenting your published work grants +1 BD to Social checks when your area of expertise is relevant.

You served in the armed companies of a noble house, a city-state, a crown, or a sect — or in the mercenary companies that sold themselves to any of the above. Whether you marched under a banner or a contract, the life of arms taught you discipline, endurance, and what a blade sounds like when it finds its mark.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: Your company was routed in an ambush. You survived; most did not. The faces of the fallen still find you at night.
You were ordered to do a thing you found reprehensible. Whether you obeyed is a question you still ask yourself.
A commanding officer betrayed your unit for coin. You escaped the trap, but the dishonor drove you from service.
The war simply ended, and no one had use for soldiers. You were mustered out with a handful of silver and nowhere to go.
You were gravely wounded and left for dead. Someone pulled you from the field. You never learned who.
You distinguished yourself in a desperate action, earning commendation and the jealousy of your peers. You left before the politics consumed you.

blocks.html: Novice Heavy Weapons or Novice Light Weapons.
Novice Athletics.

Soldier Feature. You begin with a Veteran's Kit: a suit of leather armor (Ordinary quality), one melee weapon of choice (Ordinary quality), and a soldier's pack (bedroll, mess kit, flint and steel, waterskin, 4 days rations). You also carry a Letter of Service — a document proving honorable discharge, granting you courtesy in most military holdings.

You have been walking somewhere for a long time. Pilgrim, wanderer, exile, runaway, professional traveler — you have never settled anywhere long enough for the place to fully claim you. The road is more home than any single roof.
Roll 1d6 or select a trial you faced.

rows: You left home because of something that made home impossible. You are not going back. Probably.
You are looking for a person, a place, or a thing, and you have been looking for longer than you care to admit.
You were once settled — married, housed, employed — and you walked away from it one morning without quite being able to say why.
You are a pilgrim on a long journey whose ending is still years off.
Something follows you. You have shaken it off more than once, and you are not yet sure whether you have shaken it off now.
You are just good at this. Most of the people who started alongside you have stopped. You have not.

blocks.html: Novice Survival.
Novice Stealth.

Wayfarer Feature. You begin with a Wayfarer's Kit: sturdy road clothes, a traveling cloak, a bedroll, 7 days of preserved rations, a waterskin, flint and steel, and 100 feet of light cord. You also know of a Safe-House — an inn, a shrine, a farm, a ruin, or a stranger's door — where you will be taken in once for up to a week, no questions asked. After that, you owe.
The sixteen Histories above are a starting palette, not a fence. If your concept does not fit a listed history, work with the GM to build one. A custom History should include:

items: A short concept description (50–75 words) establishing where you came from and how it shaped you.
A Trial table of 3–6 inciting events the GM can roll on, or a single chosen Trial written into your backstory.

Two Novice skill trainings matching the History, drawn from any of the nineteen core skills. One must be a Personal, Physical, Academic, or Exploration skill; the other may be a Weapon, Reaction, or Esoteric skill, or a General Talent.
A Testament of starting equipment and one small feature — a contact, a piece of reputation, a minor heirloom, a single Basic Discipline — that the History grants.

blocks.html: Custom Histories should feel roughly balanced with the printed ones: two Novice skills plus a meaningful but not game-breaking Testament. A good rule of thumb is no more than one narrative resource (contact, letter, safe-house) and one material resource (kit, weapon, silver bonus) at the outset.

Your Heritage sets the silhouette. Your History sets the scars and the skills. Your Class, selected next, sets the first stride of the road you are cultivating down. None of these three decisions alone tells you who your character is. All three, taken together, start to sketch someone the table will remember — and the remainder of the sketch is your play.

Your class is a package of skills, talents, and a unique elite tree that forms the basis of how you will advance. Classes are divided into Ordinary, Heroic, Epic, and Legendary classes. As at level 1 level Character you may only select Ordinary classes, but as you advance you will have the option to unlock powerful heroic, epic, and legendary class options.

Each Ordinary Class grants three Novice Skills, one Foundation Talent, and exclusive access to an elite skill tree that introduces your cultivation engine and shapes your early growth. Your class is where your journey begins. It is up to you whether you fully develop your class's elite tree, branch early into a Heroic specialization, or cross-train into another class's techniques through general talents and multi-classing.

Ordinary Class trees are designed to remain useful after specialization. A Warrior who becomes a Knight at level 5 still benefits from every Warrior trunk talent they have purchased; a Mage who becomes an Arcanist still casts their original spells with their original Circles. Ordinary is never obsolete — it is the foundation that every higher tier is built upon.



Class Roster

Each class suggests two Foundation Talent suited to its playstyle. However, any character may choose any Foundation Talent — the recommendations are guidance, not restriction. The six Foundation Talents are defined in full in Chapter Five; their short summaries are:

items: Arcane Reservoir. +2d4×10 Mana at 1st level, +2d6 Mana per level. Recommended for Mage and Spiritualist.
Steel Mantle. +2d4×10 Durability at 1st level, +2d6 per level. Recommended for Warriors focused on defense.
Iron Constitution. +1d4×10 Durability and +1d4×10 Vigor. Balanced defensive-martial foundation.
Tyrant's Might. +1d4×10 Mana and +1d4×10 Vigor. Balanced caster-martial foundation.
Ancient Mettle. +1d4×10 Durability and +1d4×10 Mana. Balanced defensive-caster foundation.
Endless Endurance. +2d4×10 Vigor at 1st level, +2d6 per level. Recommended for Rogues and Ascetic builds.

blocks.html: The disciplined martial. The blade and the aura. The one who walks to the front of the line because that is where the line is held.

Armsmen cultivate Aura — the expression of mana through the martial body. Through training and discipline, they awaken an Aura Core at the solar plexus, around which Stars ignite one by one. Each Star is a visible focus of inner power; together they compose the Warrior's radiance. A trained eye can count a warrior's Stars at a glance; a sect master can judge a rank from across a courtyard.

Armsman are the generalist martial class. They fight with any weapon, wear any armor, and adapt their style to the battle in front of them. Their elite tree grants broad combat flexibility — weapon access, defensive tools, aura techniques that work with any style — alongside the first three Stars of their Aura Core. An Armsman who completes this tree without specializing is still a formidable combatant, and their Three-Star Aura is enough to make them a genuine presence on the battlefield.
As a Armsman you may choose from Iron Constitution or Steel Mantle foundation talent.
Armsman gain the following Talents at 1st level:

items: Novice Heavy Weapons or Novice Light Weapons (choose one).
Novice Defense.
Aura Awakening

blocks.text: Armsman Elite Tree: The Awakened Core

blocks.html: The cultivation of Aura is a discipline of body, breath, and concentrated will. The Armsman's first awakening is the hardest; every subsequent Star comes easier, because the shape of the Core is already known.

Through directed meditation and the rigors of early martial discipline, you awaken the Aura Core at your solar plexus. The first Star ignites — a visible mote of inner light, perceptible to other Aura-users and to those with magical sight.

You gain your first Aura Star. This Star increases your maximum Mana by 1/2 your Soul Score (round down) and your Vigor by 1/2 your Stamina score. Many Aura techniques and abilities scale based on the number of Stars in your Aura Core.

You gain the Basic Aura Techniques of Aura Body and Aura Strike (see Chapter Ten: Disciplines). These Basic Technique is granted free at this talent; additional Aura Techniques may be learned through training, study, or purchase as your career develops provided you have the requisite skill ranks.

You have drilled relentlessly with a single weapon, such that it feels an extension of your own body. Choose one weapon, such as longsword, battle axe, short spear, or long bow. While using that specific weapon you gain +1 Bonus Dice to attack rolls with basic attacks.

With focused attention, you perceive the cultivation of others as clearly as the cultivators themselves. You can see another Warrior's Aura Stars, a Mage's Mana Circles, and similar cultivation markers on sight, within 60 feet. You gain +1 BD to Awareness checks regarding other combatants' approximate power level, cultivation tier, and active aura or magical states.

You have codified a personal fighting stance; a weight distribution, a breath pattern, a readiness position, that grounds you in combat. You may enter this stance as an action at the cost of 15 Vigor. Choose one stance at the time of purchase: Aggressive, Defensive, or Measured. Your Signature Stance remains active until you are knocked prone, incapacitated, restrained, disarmed, or choose to end it. You may only benefit from one stance at a time.

Aggressive Stance. Your stance drives your weight forward, turning every motion into pressure. While in this stance, when you hit with a basic melee weapon or unarmed attack, you may spend 5 Vigor to add 1 Charge to your damage bonus. If one or more of your Bonus Dice Burst on the attack roll, you may instead add this Charge without spending Vigor.

Defensive Stance. Your stance settles your center of gravity and keeps your guard ready. While in this stance, you increase your Guard by 5. If one or more of your Bonus Dice Burst on a Defense Reaction, you may immediately move an additional 5 feet.

Measured Stance. Your stance balances offense and restraint, letting you read the rhythm of combat before committing. While you maintain this stance you cannot be forced to create an opening by an opponent (however, movement and other actions of your own can still create openings). Furthermore, w you take an opening, you can move up to 5 feet as part of that reaction.
Through sustained cultivation and battle experience, a Star ignites within your Aura Core.

You gain an additional Aura Star. Increase your maximum Mana by 1/2 your Soul Score (round down) and your maximum Vigor by 1/2 your Stamina Score, round down. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of aura-empowered techniques.

While wielding your signature weapon, you may add +1 Bonus Dice to Deflect technique reactions. While Destined, if you make an attack with your signature weapon against an opponent that successfully deflects your attack with a weapon or shield, you may consume your destined state to deal double damage to the opponent’s weapon or shield.

While in your Signature Stance, each technique you chain after their first adds +1 Bonus Dice to the damage of the final technique, up to +3 Bonus dice by chaining four techniques in a row. While you are destined and maintaining this stance, after performing at least two techniques, you may release your destined state to perform an additional technique. You are still required to pay the associated Vigor cost.

When you take an attack Action on your turn with your signature weapon, you may make one additional attack with that weapon as part of the same Action. The second attack suffers no penalties. The second attack is not an action for the purposes of swift weapons or riders such as double or triple slash.

When you use an Action to move on your turn and end that movement adjacent to an opponent, you may spend 10 Vigor to throw the enemy off balance, creating an Opening. While you may not be able to exploit this opening, your allies can.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.

When you take an opening against an enemy while in your Signature Stance, you may use a single basic technique instead of a basic attack. You must still pay the techniques Vigor cost, in addition to the 5 Vigor to take the opening.

While you maintain your signature stance, each time you deal damage with techniques on your turn you shatter your enemy’s ability to defend. At the end of your turn, each technique which deals damage to a target on your turn reduces that target’s Guard by 2 and imposes 1 Challenge (up to 3 challenges) on Defense reactions until the end of your next turn or 1 round, if your turn is skipped or delayed.
Your Aura Core reaches its full Ordinary-tier expression. Through sustained cultivation and battle experience, a Star ignites within your Aura Core.

You gain an additional Aura Star. Increase your maximum Mana by 1/2 your Soul Score (round down) and your maximum Vigor by 1/2 your Stamina Score, round down. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of aura-empowered techniques. Further Stars are gated behind Heroic, Epic, and Legendary class progression.
You have come to fully realize the lethal potential of your signature weapon. When you make an attack with your signature weapon, you add +1 Bonus dice to damage.
You’re mastery of your signature weapon is unmatched. When you use hit with a technique using your signature weapon, you add +1 Bonus Dice to damage.
While in your signature stance, any time you have the opportunity to make a basic weapon attack, you may instead use a basic technique, provided you have sufficient Vigor.
From your Signature Stance you may employ techniques with lethal efficiency. While in your signature Stance the cost of martial techniques is reduced by 2 to a minimum of 1 Vigor.
The learned caster. The formed Circle. The one whose heart has become a library.

Mages cultivate Mana through formal Circles inscribed in the heart. Each Circle is a ring of structured power, architectural in its discipline and permanent once established. Where the Warrior ignites, the Mage inscribes — and the inscription is both the source of a Mage's power and the shape of their education. A Mage casting a spell does not invoke raw force; they channel mana through the Circles they have cultivated, and the spell takes on the structure of its passage.

Mages are the generalist caster. Their elite tree grants access to a broad spellbook, foundational casting tools, counterspell and resistance work, and the first three Circles of their heart. A Mage who completes their Ordinary tree without specializing is a capable spellcaster in any situation, and their Three-Circle cultivation is enough to cast reliably at potent magic by the tree's end.
As a Mage you may choose from Arcane Reservoir or Ancient Mettle foundation talents
Mages gain the following Talents at 1st level:

items: Novice Occult.
Novice Resistance.
First Circle.

blocks.text: Mage Elite Tree: The First Circle

blocks.html: The Mage's craft is the inscription of mana into formal architecture. The First Circle is the longest work of an Apprentice's life. Every Circle after it is a refinement of the work already begun.

Through sustained study, meditation, and the guided work of a mentor or text, you inscribe the First Circle of Mana within your heart. The Circle is visible to those with magical sight as a faint luminous ring at the center of the chest, brightening when you cast.
You gain your first Mana Circle. This Circle increases your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Many Spells and arcane abilities scale based on the number of Circles inscribed within your heart.

You gain access to the Incantare discipline (see Chapter Ten: Disciplines) and learn three Basic Spells of your choice. Additional Spells may be learned through training, study, or purchase as your career develops, provided you have the requisite skill ranks.

You have refined a personal method of casting — the way your hands, voice, body, or implement give shape to mana. Choose one Spellcraft method at the time of purchase. While casting using your chosen method, you gain +1 Bonus Die to your casting roll. You may only have one Spellcraft method at a time, though further talents and class progression may add additional methods.

Focus. You cast through an Arcane Arm such as a staff, wand, orb, rod, scepter, or similar focus. The focus must be held in at least one hand to gain the bonus. If disarmed of your focus, you cast at -1 BD until you recover or improvise an equivalent.

Incantation. You cast through spoken word — chants, sacred phrases, recited formulae. The bonus applies only when you can speak audibly. You cannot cast at all while silenced, and casting in stealth requires a successful Stealth check at 1 Challenge to avoid detection.

Inscribed. You cast through drawn sigils, runes, or chalk-marks. You may carry up to three pre-inscribed sigils on your person; each lets you cast a known Spell at +1 BD without further inscription, consuming the sigil. Otherwise, drawing a sigil requires one Action of inscription before casting. Pre-inscribed sigils are restored on long rest.

Channeled. You cast through a wielded weapon. The bonus applies only when you cast through the weapon's striking edge or point — at melee range, the spell originates from the weapon, not your hand. If disarmed, you cast at -1 BD until you recover or replace the weapon.

Innate. You cast through gesture and presence alone, without implement or speech. The bonus applies only when both your hands are free and your body is unrestrained. If grappled, restrained, or both hands are otherwise occupied, you cast at -1 BD.

With focused attention, you perceive the architecture of cultivation in others as clearly as the cultivators themselves. You can see another Mage's Mana Circles, a Warrior's Aura Stars, and similar cultivation markers on sight, within 60 feet. You gain +1 BD to Awareness checks regarding other combatants' approximate power level, cultivation tier, and active aura or magical states.

You have learned the foundations of extended magical working — spells cast with ceremony and patience rather than speed. When you cast a Spell as a Lesser Ritual, increase its casting time to 1 minute per Action of normal casting time, or double its normal casting time, whichever is longer. The casting roll gains +1 Bonus Die per Mana Circle you possess. Lesser Ritual casting cannot be performed in combat or under duress.

Your spells have grown harder to unmake. While you are Destined, opponents suffer 2 Challenges on Counterspell Resistance reactions against spells you cast. The Destined state remains until consumed for another effect or until it ends naturally.
Through sustained study and arcane experience, an additional Circle inscribes itself within your heart.
You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

Your study of magic has sharpened into a kind of insight that the right moment can ignite. You may consume your Destined state to attempt to learn a Spell that is being actively cast in your presence, or that is currently active on a target you can examine for at least one minute. Normal Spell-learning rules apply (Memory check, skill prerequisites, etc.) — but the Destined state opens the opportunity to attempt the learning in the moment, without study.

Your casting tempo has reached the speed of conflict. Reduce the casting time of any Spell discipline cast through Occult by 1 Action, to a minimum of 1 Action. Spells with a casting time of 1 Action are unchanged.
You no longer flinch from the press of melee. Casting Spells while adjacent to an enemy does not create an opening. Movement and other Actions still may, as normal.
You gain 1 additional Action on your turn.

Your concentration has reached an architectural quality — once a Spell is held, it stays held without continual effort. Maintaining concentration on the first concentration Spell you cast does not consume an Action on your turn. Maintaining concentration on additional concentration Spells beyond the first costs 1 Action per turn each, as normal.

Your ritual castings stabilize into lasting effects. Spells cast as a Lesser Ritual or Greater Ritual last for twice their normal duration. If the Spell is concentration-based, the effect persists for 1 minute after you stop concentrating, then ends naturally.

Your reflexive command of spellwork has reached the level of instinct. When you take an Opening against an enemy, you may cast a Basic-rank Spell with a casting time of 1 Action instead of making a basic attack. You must still pay the Spell's Mana cost in addition to the 5 Vigor cost of taking the opening.

You have studied the architecture of arcane defenses to the point that they fold under your offensive workings. Your offensive Spells deal double damage to magical Wards, barriers, and similar protective effects. This bonus applies to the Ward or barrier itself, not to creatures within or behind it.

Your Mana cultivation reaches a new level. An additional Circle inscribes itself within your heart. You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

You have learned to drive a Spell beyond its intended scope. When you cast a Spell as a Lesser Ritual, you may pay multiples of the Spell's normal Mana cost to multiply its damage or effect by the same multiple. You may multiply the Spell up to your number of Mana Circles. A 3-Circle Mage may pay 2× cost for 2× effect or 3× cost for 3× effect; a 10-Circle apex Mage may push a single Spell to 10× its normal output.

You have mastered the deepest forms of patient casting. When you cast a Spell as a Greater Ritual, you may apply one of the following ritual methods. Choose your Greater Ritual method at the time of purchase. Spells cast as a Greater Ritual may be of any rank, including Expert, regardless of normal casting requirements.

Material Components. Reagents and rare materials offset Mana cost at a rate of 1 gold of components per 1 Mana. Specific named components may be required at GM discretion for spells with strong narrative weight.

Blood Sacrifice. A willing or restrained living creature is sacrificed at the casting. The sacrifice's full Mana pool offsets your Mana cost; their Vigor or Health may substitute at half rate. Sacrificing an unwilling sapient creature accrues 2d6 Corruption.
Celestial Alignment. The casting is performed during a specific celestial event — eclipse, planetary conjunction, solstice, equinox, or the rise of a named star. Mana cost is reduced by 75%.

Place of Power. The casting is performed at a ley line, sacred site, ancient tomb, or other locus of latent magical power. Mana cost is reduced by 75%. The site itself may impose narrative consequences at GM discretion.

Soul Bond. The casting is performed in physical contact with a willing bonded ally, established through a one-time pact ritual at the time of purchase. The bonded ally and the Mage split the Mana cost equally; the ally must be conscious and willing.

Celestial Witness. A Contracted, summoned, or bound spirit, archon, or eldritch entity observes the casting and offsets the cost. Reduction by witness tier: Basic 25%, Novice 50%, Expert 75%, Master 100%.

Long Vigil. The Mage prepares for at least three days through fast, meditation, and isolation. Mana cost is reduced by 75%. The vigil cannot be interrupted by combat, conversation, or significant exertion.
The survivalist. The sharpened instinct. The one whose power was never granted — only taken, studied, and honed into habit.

Rogues do not cultivate mana architecture. They sharpen themselves. Through hard-earned discipline, predatory practice, and the drilled refinement of specific survival strategies, they acquire Edges. Where Warriors ignite their power and Mages inscribe it, Rogues simply wield themselves as the weapon they have been.

Rogues are the generalist Edge class. Their elite tree grants access to a rotating palette of three Edges, each a menu of specific abilities from which the Rogue selects. Alongside their Edges, Rogues gain survival skills, mobility tools, and the ambush-and-escape techniques that make them difficult targets and worse pursuits. A Rogue who completes their Ordinary tree carries three fully-developed Edges — a flexible, dangerous package that can be taken further into Heroic specialization or kept as a generalist's arsenal.
As a Rogue you may choose from Endless Endurance or Iron Constitution foundation talent.
Rogues gain the following Talents at 1st level:

items: Novice Stealth.
Novice Awareness.
Edge.

blocks.text: Rogue Elite Tree: The First Edge

blocks.html: The Rogue's craft is the craft of survival, taken to its logical extreme. Every habit is a tool drilled until it becomes an instinct; every Edge is a small piece of knowledge made physical.

Through hard practice, lived experience, and the drilled refinement of every survival lesson you have learned, you have honed your instincts into a usable resource. You gain a pool of 3 Edge Dice which are d6. Once per round, you may spend one or more Edge Dice on an Edge Action — adding them to a roll, fueling a maneuver, or producing one of the effects listed at later ranks. Edge Dice are recovered at the end of each long rest.
Basic Edge Actions, available to any Rogue with at least one unspent Edge Die:

items: Sharp Strike. When you hit with a basic weapon attack, spend 1 or more Edge Dice and add them as Bonus Dice to the attack roll.
Sharp Defense. When you make a Defense reaction, spend 1 or more Edge Dice and add them as Bonus Dice to the reaction.
Sharp Move. When you make a physical skill check, spend 1 or more Edge Dice and add them as Bonus Dice to the check.

blocks.html: Many Rogue talents and abilities scale based on the size and pool of your Edge Dice.
When you land a conventional weapon attack, you may choose to add one or more Edge bonus dice to the damage. This includes techniques, off and extra attacks, as well as taking an opening.

Your reactions have been drilled to a standard few combatants match. You gain +1 BD to Initiative rolls. Additionally, when you successfully Defend, during the round, and any of your bonus dice burst, you recover 1 spent Edge Die.

Your hands have become reliably useful. You gain +1 BD to Legerdemain checks to lockpicking and disarming, or adjusting clockworks. You may attempt to open and disarm locks, traps, and similar mechanisms with 1 fewer actions needed, minimum of 1.

Your dedication to the predator's path awakens an inner ember — a single Aura Star that ignites quietly at the back of your awareness, the cultivation of one who has fought so often that the body has begun to remember on its own. Choose one specialization at the time of purchase: Ranged or Swift.

You gain your first Aura Star. This Star increases your maximum Mana by ½ your Soul Score (round down) and your Vigor by ½ your Stamina Score (round down). Many Aura techniques and abilities scale based on the number of Stars in your Aura Core. Choose one of the following aura specializations.
Ranged Specialization. You gain the Aura Shot Aura technique.
Swift Specialization. You gain the Aura Strike Specialization

Your accumulated practice deepens the resource your craft is built on. Your Edge Dice pool increases to 4. Your Edge Dice rank increases to d8. Spent Edge Dice are still recovered at the end of each full rest.

Your habits of survival have produced specific small competencies — narrow but reliable advantages drawn from circumstance, practice, and the kind of knowledge that comes from doing something many times in similar conditions. Choose one skill and one circumstantial trigger at the time of purchase. While the trigger is met, you gain +1 Bonus Die to that skill check. Some examples include:

items: +1 BD to Stealth in dim light or shadow
+1 BD to Legerdemain when handling locks
+1 BD to Awareness when looking for traps
+1 BD to Survival in the wilderness you grew up in
+1 BD to Social when trading or haggling.

blocks.html: You may purchase Skill Edge multiple times, each time choosing a different skill and trigger.

You have matured using your edge on ranged weapons. When you add one or more Edge dice to an attack roll with a ranged weapon in long range or beyond, your Edge bonus dice ignore challenges due to range.
You have mastered striking with lethal precision. When you make an attack using a weapon with the slight or swift feature, you also gain Threat (3) for each Edge dice you add to your attack roll.
You gain 1 additional Action on your turn.
Your wits are sharpened by your will to survive. You may add edge dice to academic and occult skill checks, including casting checks.
You have internalized the habit of checking your surroundings for danger. You may spend edge dice on Exploration skills, such as awareness and survival.

You have mastered the range at which your Violent edge is at its most lethal. When you add Edge dice to a successful ranged weapon attack at 0 range or at extreme range, for etch edge dice you add to damage you roll 2 edge bonus dice.

The Violent edge of your blades soaks in the power for darkness. When you add edge dice to the damage of a swift or slight weapon while you are in Dim Lighting or Darkness, for each edge dice you add, your roll 2 dice.
Your Edge Dice reach their apex. Your Edge Dice pool increases to 5. Your Edge Dice rank increases to d10. Spent Edge Dice are still recovered at the end of each long rest.
You may add edge dice to Entertain skill checks. When you use a Performance, you may add edge dice to the random effects of performances you invoke, if any.

Your works of the arcane are born from a place of survival. You may add Edge dice to attack rolls with Arcane Arms, casting checks and other Occult skill checks. You may add Edge dice to the damage of spells.
The bearer of strange truths. The one who studies what the world prefers to forget. The cultivator whose path runs through the unseen, by way of communion or by way of bargain.

Spiritualists cultivate Mysteries — esoteric truths bound into the soul through revelation, initiation, or quiet earned understanding. Unlike the Warrior's Stars or the Mage's Circles, a Mystery is not a resource engine; it is a permanent alteration of what the Spiritualist is. Mysteries augment, modify, and unlock the abilities of other engines — a Mystery may strengthen a companion Warrior's aura, stabilize a Mage's spellwork, sharpen a Rogue's senses — and most of all, they give the Spiritualist access to the world's stranger regions: spirits, pacts, wards, bindings, and the quiet corners where the ordinary rules thin out.

Spiritualists are the generalist spiritual class. Their elite tree grants three Mysteries, along with foundational occult work — warding, minor summoning, basic ritual, spiritual perception. A Spiritualist who completes their Ordinary tree is a modest but capable occult practitioner, useful in any party and strange in the best sense of the word. From here, the Heroic arcane half casters open, each taking one aspect of the Spiritualist's craft to apex.
As a Spiritualist you may choose from Ancient Mettle, Arcane Reservoir, or Tyrant's Might foundation talent.
Spiritualists gain the following Talents at 1st level:

items: Novice Occult.
Novice Awareness.
Lesser Mystery.

blocks.text: Spiritualist Elite Tree: The First Mystery

blocks.html: The Spiritualist's craft is the craft of attention paid to what others have stopped seeing. Each Mystery is a single permanent truth bound into the spirit; the deeper you carry it, the further it carries you. Some Spiritualists devote their lives to a single Mystery, deepening it from minor knowing to perfect command. Others gather Mysteries the way a scholar gathers languages, taking new ones rather than refining old ones.

Through vigil, teaching, pact, or simple stubborn attention, the world's quieter currents have answered you. Choose one Mystery from those listed below. Once you make this selection it is permanent and cannot be changed. Each mystery is outlined in greater detail in the following section.
Beast Tongue. You have the ability to speak and communicate with all manner of animal and beast.
Bound Names. You have the power to see, and seal, the true names of beings.
Deadspeak. The dead hear and see you, and you see them.
Grace. You are a conduit for the divine and may cultivate Grace in place of mana.
Hexer’s Mark. You possess the Evil Eye and may curse and hex your foes.
Oracle. You possess the auger of truth and may invoke the unknownable.
Second Sight. You possess the ability to perceive the magical and arcane.
Wyrd Witness. You have the Wyrd, the precognitive echoes of possible futures.

Your attunement to the unseen has sharpened into usable perception. You see spirits, odic forms, and intangible entities within 30 feet as if they were solidly visible. You gain +1 BD to Awareness checks involving the spiritual, ethereal, or occult, and you can sense the presence of warded, consecrated, or defiled spaces within 30 feet.

Once per turn as an action, you may attempt to borrow Mana (or Grace if you possess Grace) directly from the source of your First Mystery. The amount borrowed equals 5 times your Soul Bonus Dice. The borrowed Mana is added to your current Mana pool and may be spent on any Mana-cost effect during your turn, however you may not exceed your maximum Mana with this ability. Any borrowed Mana not spent by the end of the turn returns to its source. The source remembers the loan; details vary by Mystery and may have narrative consequences at GM discretion.

There are dark and terrible forces that will gladly offer power – for a price. Once per turn as an action you may gather power from forbidden sources, in effect created fake circles which add bonus dice to the damage or effect of magical abilities. You choose how deeply you reach. The depth determines what you gain, and what you will pay.

rows: 1d8 Health and 2d10 Vigor; 1 rank of fatigue

blocks.html: This borrowed power last still the end of your turn, and then is lost.
Through formal study, you have inscribed a single Circle of Mana within your heart. Your spiritual practice now intersects with the architecture of arcane casting.

You gain a Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. You gain access to the Incantare disciplines (see Chapter Ten) and learn 2 Basic-rank Spells of your choice. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.
Your spiritual cultivation deepens. Choose one of the following:

items: Deepen First Mystery: Your first Mystery upgrades from Minor to Major. Refer to the Mystery's catalog entry for the new tier's passive and active effects.
Acquire Second Mystery: Choose a new Mystery from the catalog and gain it at Minor tier. Your First Mystery remains at Minor.

blocks.html: You have crossed a threshold of the forbidden. Through ritual contact, dark study, or simple necessity, your spirit has begun to take on the marks of forbidden traffic with what should not have been called.

You gain a Corruption score of 1d6 (rolled at the time of purchase). If you already possess a corruption score, increase your Corruption Score by 1d8 and gain one Tainted Talent of choice. If you do not possess, choose one of the following Corruption Paths: Vile Names, Pail Grace, Seven Eyes, or Devouring Flame; you gain its rank 1 talent ability.

Through fortune, initiation, or sheer spiritual gravity, you have drawn the attention of a lesser power that now lingers at your shoulder as a conduit, witness, or meddling patron. This being acts as a focus for your magical force. Choose one of the following Spirit Focuses. Once you make this selection, it is permanent and cannot be changed.

Elemental Sprite. A minor elemental intelligence has attached itself to your soul, flickering about you in idle circles of ash, frost, wind, stone, or stormlight. Choose one element: Earth, Fire, Water, Wind, Lightning, or Ice.

You may cast Arcane Inscription spells and Arcane Arms spells through your Elemental Sprite instead of through a wielded arcane armament. When you do so, the spell deals your chosen elemental damage at Ordinary rank.
The sprite must remain within 10 feet of you to serve as your focus. It is intangible, cannot be directly harmed by mundane attacks, and may be dismissed or recalled as a free action on your turn.

Spirit of the Dead. A dead thing has not fully left you. Whether ancestor, revenant, or nameless grave-shadow, it lingers near, whispering of endings and unfinished truths. Choose Umbral or Entropic. You may cast Arcane Inscription spells and Arcane Arms spells with your chosen damage type at Ordinary damage through your ghostly companion.

However, the dead do not remain quiet. You are visibly or spiritually haunted. Spirits, shades, the recently dead, and creatures sensitive to the unseen can identify you as marked. In addition, once per scene after using this Focus, the GM may impose one minor haunting manifestation: whispers, frost on nearby surfaces, involuntary speech from the dead, ghostly images, or similar signs.

Spirit of Fate. A subtle intelligence of omen, chance, and nearly missed disaster has twined itself into your casting hand. It nudges outcomes, spoils certainties, and occasionally demands to be obeyed.

When making a casting roll for a spell or Arcane Arms ability, your Bonus Dice Burst on a 9 or 10 instead of only on a 10. You may use this benefit once per turn.
