The action cannot directly harm them or those they hold dear, and the compulsion ends after one turn.

Illusion. Create a sustained sensory illusion (sight, sound, smell, or all three) within 30 feet of you, of an area up to 10 feet across. The illusion persists as long as you continue Performing (one Action per turn to maintain). Observers may make an Awareness check VS your Entertain check to see through the illusion.

Soothe. Choose one creature who hears your Performance. Until the end of their next turn, they may not take hostile action against you or those you indicate, unless they are already actively harmed by you in the moment. Multiple targets may be soothed at +5 Mana per additional target. Hostile creatures may resist with a Soul Resistance check.

Mock. Choose one creature who hears your Performance. They suffer one of the following effects until the end of their next turn: -2 Bonus Dice to a chosen roll type, the loss of one Action point on their next turn, or the inability to use a chosen technique or spell. The target makes a Soul Resistance check to negate.

blocks.html: Additional Glamour types may be learned through later talents and class progression. Many Orator abilities scale with the number of Glamours you know, the Audience you have, and your Performance skill rank.

Your Performances have matured beyond the casual recital — you have learned to recognize and cultivate the cumulative attention of those listening. Each time you cast a Glamour, you track the number of attentive listeners present (creatures who can hear, see, and understand your Performance, who are willing or compelled to listen, and who are not actively hostile to the act of Performance). This is your Audience for the Glamour.

Audience scales the Glamour's power: at Audience 5+, all listeners benefit (or suffer) the Glamour's effect simultaneously; at Audience 10+, the effect duration doubles; at Audience 50+, the effect duration triples; at Audience 100+, the effect persists until the end of your next long rest. Audience scaling does not increase Mana cost beyond the base; the scaling is pure additive power. Audience is recalculated each time you Perform — if your listeners disperse or stop paying attention, the Audience for your next Glamour drops accordingly.

Additionally, you learn 2 additional Glamour types from the catalog (your choice, including any unlocked at higher tiers). Many Orator capstones and high-tier abilities require minimum Audience thresholds to function.

Your voice has matured into a cultivated instrument capable of carrying significantly farther than ordinary speech. Your Glamours may now be cast at any audience that can perceive you, regardless of physical distance — a Glamour cast to a crowd of a thousand in an open square reaches all of them simultaneously, even those at the back. Your voice carries clearly to all observers.

Additionally, you gain +2 Bonus Dice to Entertain checks made to capture, hold, and direct an audience's attention. Once per scene, you may make a Performance that draws hostile or unwilling listeners into Audience: targets within 60 feet who would not normally attend to your Performance must make a Soul check at 1 Challenge or become Audience for your next Glamour. Critical failures on this check leave the target captivated for an extended Performance of up to 1 minute.
You learn 3 additional Glamour types of your choice, expanding your catalog substantially. Advanced Glamours include:

items: Embolden. Choose all allies who hear your Performance. Until the end of their next turn, they gain +1 Bonus Die to all rolls. Costs 20 Mana.
Discomfit. Choose all enemies who hear your Performance. Until the end of their next turn, they suffer 1 Challenge to all rolls. Costs 25 Mana.

Suggest. Choose one creature who hears your Performance and is at Audience for you. The target accepts a stated suggestion as plausible truth for the duration of the current scene. Costs 30 Mana. The target may make a Soul Resistance check at 1 Challenge to recognize the manipulation.

Beguile. Choose one creature who hears your Performance. The target's emotional state toward you shifts one step toward favorable (hostile to suspicious, suspicious to neutral, neutral to friendly, friendly to enamored) for the duration of the current scene. Costs 25 Mana, with full Soul Resistance check available.

Bind by Word. Choose one creature who hears your Performance. Until they break the binding through significant action against your stated intent, they cannot speak, write, or otherwise communicate the specific information you have just bound. Costs 30 Mana, with Soul Resistance check at 1 Challenge per round of speech-bound silence.

blocks.html: Your performative skill has reached the level where your Glamours may be invoked rapidly within a single turn. You gain 1 additional Action on your turn.

Your Glamours have reached the threshold where they may layer and resonate with each other. When you cast a Glamour that targets the same listener (or listeners) as a Glamour you cast in the previous round, the new Glamour gains +1 Charge to its damage or effect duration. Resonant Glamours may stack across multiple rounds, with each successive Glamour amplifying the cumulative effect.

Additionally, when you cast multiple Glamours in the same round (via Action Bonus or other accelerations), each Glamour after the first reduces the next Glamour's Mana cost by 5 (minimum 5 Mana total).

Your accumulated practice has refined your relationship with the Audience to the level where you may shape its character actively. When you cast a Glamour, you may now exclude specific creatures from the Audience even if they would normally qualify (allies you do not wish to affect, neutrals you do not wish to draw in, particular targets you wish to leave aside). The exclusion costs 1 Mana per excluded creature.

Additionally, your Audience scaling thresholds are halved: an Audience of 3+ triggers effects normally requiring 5+; an Audience of 5+ triggers effects normally requiring 10+; an Audience of 25+ triggers effects normally requiring 50+. The Heroic Orator with cultivated mastery can produce significant Audience-scaled effects with relatively modest crowds.
You learn 3 additional Glamour types of your choice, drawn from the expanded catalog. Advanced Glamours unlocked at this tier include:

items: Confound. Choose one creature who hears your Performance. The target suffers Confusion for 1 round per listener in your Audience (maximum 10 rounds). Costs 30 Mana. Soul Resistance check at 2 Challenges to negate.

Embellish. Reframe a recent event (within the last 24 hours) through Performance. Listeners in your Audience accept the embellished version as their memory of what happened. Costs 40 Mana. Soul Resistance check at 2 Challenges for skeptical or particularly attentive listeners.
Inspirit. Grant the entire Audience temporary Vigor recovery: each listener regains 5 Vigor at the start of their next turn, and 5 again at the start of their following turn. Costs 35 Mana.
Despirit. Drain the entire Audience: each hostile listener suffers 5 Vigor damage and the Despondent condition for 1 round. Costs 35 Mana. Soul Resistance check to negate Despondent.

blocks.html: Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

Your Performance has reached the apex of broadcasted reach. Once per long rest, you may invoke a Greater Voice: for the duration of one Performance (up to 10 minutes), your voice carries across an entire region — a city, an army, an open landscape up to 10 miles in diameter — and every creature within that region perceives your Performance as if they were directly in front of you. Glamours cast during a Greater Voice apply to the entire region's Audience, with the scaling effects of an Audience numbering in the thousands.

The Greater Voice is the working of legend — the rebellion incited from a balcony, the army halted by a single sustained note of melody, the prophet's sermon that converted a kingdom. The Orator who has mastered this capstone is recognized by their voice alone, and historical accounts of their major Performances are preserved as cultural events. Hostile authorities may, after a Greater Voice, hunt the Orator across years and continents — the reputational weight is genuine.

Your Glamours have learned to outlast their original casting. Glamours you cast may now be sustained beyond their normal duration through ritual maintenance: a 1-minute Performance per day extends a Glamour's duration by an additional day. With sustained daily Performance, a Glamour may persist indefinitely on its original targets, effectively becoming a permanent enchantment until you choose to end it or another Heroic-tier counter-Glamour or magical effect dispels it.

Additionally, when you cast a Glamour as a Lesser Ritual (taking 10 minutes with full Performance), the Glamour's effects scale as if cast under triple your current Audience. A Glamour cast as a Ritual to a single attentive listener becomes effectively a Glamour cast to three, with all corresponding scaling bonuses applying.

The cultivated Orator who maintains permanent Glamours on their network of allies, contacts, and informants becomes something genuinely formidable: a person whose web of cultivated relationships is itself an instrument of power, sustained through patient daily practice.

Your cultivated relationship with the Audience has reached the threshold where the spoken word briefly rewrites limited reality. Once per long rest, you may invoke a Reality-Shaping Voice: state a single fact within the Audience's hearing, and if your Performance succeeds (Entertain check VS the highest individual Soul Resistance in the Audience), that fact becomes briefly, partially, but observably true.

Reality-Shaping is bounded and specific. You may state: that a specific event happened or did not happen recently; that a specific person did or did not do a specific thing; that a specific object is or is not what it appears; that a specific path is or is not safe. The stated fact does not become permanently true — but for the duration of the current scene (up to 1 hour), the Audience's understanding, the local witnessing of the world, and small details in the immediate environment align with your statement. People will recall the event the way you said. The path will, briefly, be passable. The object will, briefly, demonstrate the property you ascribed to it.

Reality-Shaping Voice cannot create matter, kill creatures, or alter the past in lasting ways. It cannot override the will of beings of Legendary or higher tier, or the convictions of those who have made formal binding oaths or sworn statements directly contradicting your claim. Within its limits, it is the working of myth — the storyteller whose words make the story true, briefly, for everyone who heard. Most uses of Reality-Shaping Voice are forgotten within hours; but the consequences of the small reality-shifts often linger far longer, as the cascading effects of "what people briefly believed" reshape political, economic, and personal landscapes.
The word is the weapon. The formula is the blade. The will is the hand that wields them both.

Arcanists are the scholars of the Incantare tradition — the deepest students of the grammar of spells. Where an ordinary Mage casts from a memorized repertoire, the Arcanist has studied the structure of magic itself: the underlying syllables and runic foundations beneath the spoken words of power. This understanding lets them deconstruct and reconstitute spells on the fly — altering range, duration, damage type, and casting signature in the moment of casting. The tradition they call the Lexicon is what separates the Arcanist from every other practitioner of the Incantare.

Most Arcanists are first and foremost academics: scholars who have spent more than a decade in some library, citadel, or remote tower mastering the foundations of their craft. The path to Arcanist passes through Memory and Soul both — the mind that can hold the structure, the spirit that can rewrite it. Those who take the title and walk away from court life are considered queer and unusual by their peers; their reasons for choosing the road over the lecture hall vary, but the answer is rarely simple. To the world beyond the academies, an Arcanist is viewed with some superstition and a great deal of unease. Magic without religious or martial framing is a daunting concept for common folk, and when an Arcanist is seen in the field, most assume that something important — or something terrible — is afoot.
To enter the Arcanist Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Memory Score of 15 or higher.
Soul Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Occult.
Novice rank in Studies.
At least 2 Mana Circles from any source.

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Lexicon Initiate.

blocks.text: Arcanist Elite Tree: The Grammar of Magic

blocks.html: Most casters learn to recite. The Arcanist learns to read. Most casters cast the spell they know; the Arcanist casts the spell as it should be — rewritten in the moment to fit exactly the shape required. The Lexicon is not a school of magic. It is the discovery that there are no fixed spells, only fixed habits.

You have completed the trials of the Arcanist and earned the right to read the underlying grammar of Incantare. You may now perceive the runic structures beneath the words of power in every spell you witness being cast — schools, casting signatures, modifications, and the architectural choices the original caster made.

You gain access to a single Lexicon modification, Amend: when you cast a spell, you may spend +50% of its Mana cost to alter one property of the casting. You may double the spell's range, double its duration, or change its area from Target to Small Area (or increase its area by one step). Only one Amendment may be applied per casting unless a later talent permits otherwise.
Additionally, you learn 3 additional Basic or Novice Spells of your choice at the time of purchase, expanding your active spellbook.

Your knowledge of Incantare deepens, and a new Circle inscribes itself within your heart as the formal expression of your expanded grammar. You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

You learn 4 additional Spells (up to Novice rank) at the time of purchase, and you gain access to one additional Incantare school of your choice. Many Arcanist abilities scale with the breadth of your spellbook and the depth of your accumulated repertoire.

You also learn the Lexicon modification Annotate: when you cast a Spell, you may spend +50% Mana to add +1 Charge to the spell's damage dice, healing dice, or condition duration. Annotate may be combined with Amend, paying both surcharges — a spell so modified costs triple its base Mana, but arrives at extended range with amplified power.

Your command of Incantare has reached the threshold of true expertise. You may now cast Expert-rank Spells, and you learn 3 additional Expert-rank Spells of your choice at the time of purchase. Expert spells represent a significant escalation in power — area-denial effects, battlefield control, serious damage, and complex enchantments. The Arcanist who reaches Expert Spellcraft becomes a genuine threat to large groups of enemies and begins to shape encounters through spell selection alone.

Through scholarly discipline and ruthless economy, you have learned to cast more for less. Spells that cost 10 or fewer Mana to cast now cost 2 less (minimum 1). This reduction is cumulative with the spell's base cost and applies before any Lexicon surcharges or other modifiers.

Additionally, when you successfully Counterspell an enemy's magic, you recover Mana equal to half the countered spell's base casting cost (round down). Over the course of an extended encounter, Efficient Casting saves enormous quantities of Mana — the difference between running dry in the sixth round and still having reserves in the twelfth.

You have learned to strip components from your spells, casting them in ways that should not be possible. For +25% Mana, you may cast a Spell without verbal components (silent casting), or for +25% Mana, you may cast without somatic components (no hand gestures). For +50% Mana, you may cast without both. A Redacted spell has no visible casting signature — it simply happens. No one sees you speak the word, no one sees your hands move, and the spell arrives as though from nowhere.
Redact may be combined with Amend, Annotate, or other Lexicon modifications, paying each surcharge separately.

You can now cast Master-rank Incantare Spells — the most powerful magic available to mortal casters. You learn 2 additional Master-rank Spells of your choice at the time of purchase. Master Spells are reality-altering events: storms that reshape battlefields, enchantments that bind the will of nobles, and destructive forces that can level fortifications. An Arcanist who reaches Master Spellcraft is one of the most powerful individuals in their region, and everyone with the wit to recognize it knows it.
Through deepening study and the slow accumulation of arcane experience, another Circle inscribes itself within your heart.

You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast. The combined breadth of your repertoire and the depth of your inscribed architecture begins to make you a notably dangerous individual in any conversation about practical arcane work.

You have learned to cast the same Spell twice in a single Action by speaking the underlying formula along two parallel grammatical paths simultaneously. For 2× the normal Mana cost, you may cast a known Spell twice as part of the same casting Action. Each instance resolves separately against its target — they may be directed at the same target (for devastating double damage) or at two different targets (for battlefield control).

Duplicate may be combined with Annotate, Amend, or Redact, paying each surcharge separately. A Duplicated Annotated spell means two Charged copies of the same spell arriving simultaneously — a 6× base-cost casting that lands hard enough to end most encounters in a single Action.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

You have learned to gather every breath of your accumulated craft into a single perfect casting. Once per long rest, you may declare a Grand Incantation: cast any Spell you know at +3 Charges to its damage, healing, or effect dice, ignoring all normal limits on Charge stacking. The Spell costs no Mana; the cultivated essence of your accumulated study supplies the energy directly from the architecture of your mind.

Grand Incantation may be combined with any Lexicon modifications you know (Amend, Annotate, Redact, Duplicate), paying their surcharges from your normal Mana pool. The Spell's visual and sensory effects are dramatically amplified — a Flame Bolt becomes a pillar of fire visible for miles, a Shield of Mana becomes a dome of blazing light, and a single bolt of lightning becomes a brief thunderstorm at the casting site.

Grand Incantation is the Spell that ends encounters. One casting per day, that lands harder than anything else in the game. Witnesses describe it the way they describe a force of nature — without explanation, only acknowledgment of what they saw.

You have learned to apply the Lexicon to itself. You may apply two Lexicon modifications to a single Spell, paying both surcharges. Additionally, when you use Amend to alter a Spell's area or range, you may now also change the Spell's damage type to any type you have previously cast — turning a Fire spell into a Frost one, an Entropic blast into a Light pulse. Cosmetic changes follow the new damage type automatically.

The Arcanist who masters the Lexicon does not merely cast Spells. They rewrite them. Every Incantare in their repertoire becomes a family of Spells, endlessly adaptable, and any opponent who studied a previous casting will find the next one entirely unrecognizable. A Lexicon Master may, with sufficient preparation and Mana, cast a single Spell that combines the effects of two or three lesser Spells through layered modification — the working that other casters cannot quite explain but cannot quite forget.

Your understanding of magical theory has become so deep that you no longer need to commit Spells to fixed memory in order to cast them. You may prepare a number of Spells equal to your Memory Bonus Dice that do not count against your normal known-spells limit. These prepared Spells are held in your active grammar — accessible at any time, swappable during any Break, and effectively giving you access to your entire collected library rather than just the spells you have most recently memorized.

Additionally, once per scene, you may attempt to cast a Spell you do not currently know but have witnessed cast within the last 24 hours. Make an Occult check at 2 Challenges; on success, you cast the Spell using your normal Mana cost. The Spell is not added to your known repertoire — it is a one-time act of grammatical reconstruction performed in the moment. On a Critical, the Spell is added to your active library and may be cast again normally.
The element pledged. The Circle inscribed in flame, in frost, in light. The cultivator whose heart has become a furnace, a glacier, a storm, or all of these at once.

Elementalists are the masters of Elemental Circles — a specialized form of Mana Circle inscribed not merely in the heart but pledged to a specific element. Where a Mage's Circle is neutral architecture through which any spell may pass, an Elementalist's Circle is bound: it knows the element it serves, it conducts that element's energy with no resistance, and it functions as a permanent Heroic-rank Arcane Arm of its pledged element bound to the Elementalist's soul. An Elementalist needs no physical wand or staff — their Circle is the wand, and the wand is them.

The Elementalist's craft is older than formal Incantare. It descends from the elemental traditions that predate the Conclave, from the fire-priests and frost-binders of the early peoples, from the storm-callers who shaped weather as easily as they shaped speech. Modern Elementalists have absorbed these traditions into the architecture of inscribed Circles, but the older flavor remains: an Elementalist is recognized by their pledge. A pledged Fire Elementalist carries the warmth in their breath, the slight redness at the edges of their vision, the patience of a hearth-fire that knows it will eventually be needed. A Frost Elementalist's words come slightly slow and slightly clear; a Lightning Elementalist's hands twitch with stored potential. The pledge is visible to those who know how to look.
To enter the Elementalist Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Resolve Score of 15 or higher.
Soul Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Arcane Arms.
Novice rank in Resistance.
At least 2 Mana Circles from any source.

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Elemental Focus.

blocks.text: Elementalist Elite Tree: The Pledged Circle

blocks.html: To pledge is the first lesson of the Elementalist. The Circle inscribed in flame is not less than the Circle inscribed in plain Mana; it is more, but only of one thing. The Elementalist trades breadth for depth, and the depth is so absolute that the trade is rarely regretted.

You inscribe your first Elemental Circle — a specialized Mana Circle pledged permanently to a single element. Choose one element at the time of purchase: Fire, Frost, Lightning, Earth, Wind, or Water. The Circle is bound to this element and cannot be repledged. This Elemental Circle replaces one of your existing Mana Circles (your choice at time of purchase) or is inscribed in addition to your existing Circles, at your option.

Your Elemental Circle grants the standard Mana Circle benefits: +½ Soul Score to your maximum Mana (round down), and +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast through it. Additionally, the Elemental Circle functions as a permanent Heroic-rank Arcane Arm of its pledged element, bound to your soul. You may make Arcane Arms basic attacks and cast Arcane Arms spells through your Elemental Circle without needing a physical Arcane Armament — the Circle is the armament, and it scales as a Heroic-rank weapon for all damage, range, and crit purposes.

If you also wield a physical Arcane Armament of matching element, you gain the benefits of both — the physical weapon's specific properties stack with the Circle's Heroic-rank base for crit, range, and weapon-feat purposes. If you wield a physical Arcane Armament of a different element than your pledged Circle, you must choose which to channel through on a per-attack basis. The Elemental Circle's visible signature appears as a faint coronal hue tinging your hands when you cast or attack — the color and quality matching your pledged element.

Your Elemental Circle has matured into a stable conduit. An additional Mana Circle inscribes itself within your heart (a standard Mana Circle, not Elemental — though you may convert this to an Elemental Circle of a second pledged element if you take a later talent that grants such conversion).
You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

Additionally, when you make Arcane Arms basic attacks through your Elemental Circle, you add your full Mana Circle Bonus Dice to the damage roll. (This stacks with the standard Arcane Arms Elemental Armiger ability for those who possess it; an Elementalist who learns this Heroic talent doubles their effective Circle BD contribution to Arcane Arms basic attacks.) Many Elementalist abilities scale with the depth of your inscribed architecture and the number of Elemental Circles you possess.

You inscribe a second Elemental Circle, pledging it to a different element than your first. The Secondary Pledge may use either an existing Mana Circle (converting it to Elemental) or a future Mana Circle you have not yet inscribed (specifying the pledge at the time of inscription). You now possess two Elemental Circles of different elements, each functioning as a Heroic-rank Arcane Arm of its pledged element.

When making Arcane Arms basic attacks, you may channel through either Elemental Circle on a per-attack basis, as a Free Action. Spells with elemental damage types may be cast through whichever Circle matches their type, or through your second Circle at -1 Bonus Die to damage (the cross-element casting is less efficient than the pledged path).

Your bond with your pledged element has begun to alter your body. You gain full immunity to environmental damage of your primary pledged element (you do not take damage from being in fire if your pledge is Fire, you do not freeze in cold if your pledge is Frost, you do not take electrical damage from natural lightning if your pledge is Lightning, etc.). Hostile elemental damage of your primary type is reduced by 1d6 per Elemental Circle you possess (minimum 1).

Additionally, you gain +1 Bonus Die to Resistance checks against effects of your opposing element (Fire/Frost, Lightning/Earth, Wind/Water are the canonical opposed pairings). The opposed element does not damage you more, but you resist its conditions and lingering effects more readily than other casters.

Your understanding of elemental affinity has deepened to the point where you may shift the flow of one element into the form of another in the moment of casting. When you cast an Arcane Arms spell of your pledged element, you may spend +25% Mana to declare it Mutable: the spell's damage type and visual signature shift to a different element you have pledged (or to a non-pledged element at -1 Bonus Die to damage), while retaining all other properties of the original casting. A Mutable Flame Bolt becomes a Frost Spike with the same casting parameters; a Mutable Lightning Lance becomes a Stone Shard.

Mutable Element may be combined with Resonant Strike Chance from Arcane Arms — a Mutable spell may resonate with the new element's previous damage to the same target, multiplying its impact. The cosmetic transformation is genuine: a Frost-Mutable spell deals Frost damage and applies Frost conditions, fully replacing the original element.

You inscribe a third Elemental Circle, pledging it to a different element than your first two. You now possess three Elemental Circles of different elements, each functioning as a Heroic-rank Arcane Arm of its pledged element.

Additionally, you may now begin to explore exotic elements — pledges to forces beyond the standard six. Available exotic elements include: Light (radiant; bonus vs undead and corrupted), Shadow (umbral; bonus vs lit and holy targets), Crystal (geomantic; bonus to durability-rending), Acid (corrosive; armor reduction), Storm (hybrid lightning/wind; area effects), or other elements at GM discretion and table approval. Your Tertiary Pledge may be to any standard or exotic element you choose.

Through deepening study and elemental attunement, another Circle inscribes itself within your heart. This Circle is inscribed as a standard Mana Circle, though you may convert it to an Elemental Circle (pledging it to any element you already wield) by taking later talents that permit such conversion.
You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

Your control of multiple Elemental Circles has reached the threshold where you may cast through more than one at once. Once per round, when you cast an Arcane Arms spell, you may declare it a Convergence: the spell channels through two of your Elemental Circles simultaneously, dealing damage of both pledged elements and applying conditions appropriate to both. The Convergence costs an additional 50% Mana, and the spell's damage Bonus Dice are split equally between the two elements (round down).

A Convergence between Fire and Lightning creates Plasma effects (combined Burning and Stunned); between Frost and Wind creates Blizzard effects (combined Freeze and area-knockback); between any element and Light creates Radiant Burst effects (combined elemental + Lucent Damage to undead and corrupted). Custom Convergences are at GM discretion.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

You have mastered the art of changing the element of a casting after it has begun. Once per long rest, you may declare an Elemental Shift on any spell or Arcane Arms attack you have cast within the previous 1 minute: the spell's element changes retroactively to a different element you have pledged, with all damage and conditions reapplying as if the spell had originally been cast in the new element. Targets previously unaffected by the original element may now suffer the new element's effects; targets resistant to the original may now be vulnerable to the new.

Elemental Shift requires you to spend your Reaction in the round of the originally cast spell, or to take an Action in a subsequent round to declare the Shift. The retroactive change is genuine — the world's record of the spell is rewritten. Witnesses who saw the original casting will remember it as having been the new element, with rare exceptions for highly perceptive observers (Spirit Sight or Mana Sight may reveal the trick).

Your pledges have reached the threshold of full mastery. All Elemental Circles you possess function at +1 effective tier for all damage, range, and crit purposes — your Heroic-rank Arcane Arms now scale as Epic-rank weapons. Additionally, you may now pledge a fourth Elemental Circle, drawn from either standard or exotic elements; the fourth pledge does not require an additional Mana Circle slot, as your accumulated mastery has freed the architecture of your inscribed heart to bear it.

Furthermore, when you make Arcane Arms basic attacks through any of your Elemental Circles, you add your full Circle Bonus Dice to damage (a 5-Circle Elementalist adds +5 Bonus Dice to every basic attack). When you cast Arcane Arms spells through your pledged Circles, the spells are treated as having an additional Charge of damage scaling, free of Mana surcharge.

Your body has begun to take on the properties of your primary pledged element. The transformation is irreversible at the point of taking this capstone — flesh becomes infused with elemental essence, blood runs with the element's signature current, and the visible markers of your pledge are now constant rather than activation-bound.

You gain Heroic Resistance to your primary pledged element's damage type (only Epic or better damage of that type harms you normally; lesser damage is ignored entirely). You become immune to all environmental hazards related to your primary element (a Fire Elementalist walks through inferno; a Frost Elementalist breathes in vacuum-cold; a Lightning Elementalist conducts electrical surge harmlessly). You no longer need to breathe, eat, or sleep in conditions that align with your element (a Fire Elementalist needs only warmth to sustain themselves; a Water Elementalist needs only immersion).

Once per long rest, you may briefly become your element entirely — for 1 minute, your body dissipates into the pledged form (flame, frost, lightning, etc.), during which you may move freely through any space the element could naturally occupy, cannot be physically damaged, and may pass through cracks, vents, and small openings. You may not make weapon attacks or cast Spells while in elemental form, but you may cast Arcane Arms basic attacks through your Elemental Circle without restriction. At the end of the duration, you return to physical form at any point within the elemental form's reach.

The Elemental Body is the apex of the pledge. The Elementalist who walks this path is no longer entirely mortal in the conventional sense — they have become a partial elemental themselves, and most temples and academies will regard them with the careful courtesy reserved for forces of nature.
The hand that inscribes. The will that binds magic to matter. The cultivator whose grimoire is the world.

Artificers are the masters of enchantment — the inscribed magic that binds runic formulae to physical objects, permanent and portable. Where Mages cast their spells fresh from memory, the Artificer crafts: laying down Force, Concept, and Structure Circles into a sword, a ring, a doorway, a tool. The result is an item that carries its own magic, usable by anyone who knows how to invoke it, persistent through the ages, and often outliving its creator by centuries.

The Artificer's craft is the deep work of the magical traditions. It is slower than spellcasting, requires more components, demands the kind of patience that most casters never develop. In exchange, it produces results that no spell can match: a sword that holds a Flame Burst, a ring that grants Frost Ward, a circle drawn in chalk that compels honesty within its bounds for a day and a night. Artificers are recognized by what they carry — most wear or wield at least one major work of their own making, and seasoned Artificers are often laden with subtle enchantments visible only to those with magical sight. Their tower workshops, when they have them, are notorious: full of stacked sigils, half-inscribed tablets, and the kind of crystalline residue that only forms when serious working has been done in the same room over many years.
To enter the Artificer Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Reason Score of 15 or higher.
Memory Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Occult.
Novice rank in Crafting.
At least 2 Mana Circles from any source.

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Engram Mastery.

blocks.text: Artificer Elite Tree: The Inscribed World

blocks.html: To inscribe is to commit. The spoken word fades; the written word remains. The Artificer chooses the harder path because the harder path is the one that lasts. A spell is a candle; an enchantment is a hearth. Both give light. Only one warms the house for generations.

You have completed your apprenticeship in the inscribed tradition. You may now craft enchantments according to the formal Circle Structure of Force × Concept × Structure (see Chapter Six: Enchantments). At the time of purchase, you learn 3 Force Circles, 4 Concept Circles, and 4 Structure Circles of your choice (from Novice tier; one may be from Expert tier in any category if you have Expert rank in the appropriate Crafting skill).
Additionally, you gain the following Artificer benefits to all enchanting attempts:

items: Crafting time for any enchantment is reduced by 25% (round down).
Crafting Mana cost is reduced by 1 per Mana Circle you possess (minimum 50% of base).
You may make multiple enchanting attempts on the same item before the components are spent; only on the third failed attempt are the components consumed.

blocks.html: Many Artificer abilities scale with the breadth of Circles you know and the items you have inscribed.

Your work has begun to require deeper reserves. An additional Mana Circle inscribes itself within your heart as the formal expression of your inscriptive practice. You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast.

Additionally, you may now imbue Charge slots into your inscribed items. When you craft an enchantment with the Charge Structure or Activation Structure, you may pre-load the item with a number of Charges equal to your Mana Circles + your Memory Bonus Dice (instead of the standard Charge count). These pre-loaded Charges replenish on long rest if the enchantment normally recharges; they replenish on ritual maintenance if the enchantment is single-use. Items inscribed by you carry your signature pattern — a small visible glyph that other Artificers will recognize.
You may also now learn 2 additional Circles of any tier and category, expanding your inscriptive vocabulary.

Your understanding of how enchantments activate has reached the threshold of expertise. You learn all Novice-rank Structure Circles you do not already know (free), and gain access to Expert-rank Structure Circles. Learn 2 Expert-rank Structure Circles at the time of purchase. Expert Structures include On Guard, Aura, Charge, and Cast Aid — significantly more powerful and versatile than Novice Structures.

Additionally, when you craft an enchantment with an Expert Structure, the VS Increase for the structure is reduced by 5 (from +10 to +5). This makes Expert structural work meaningfully cheaper for you than for ordinary practitioners, and lets you stack more complex effects without overwhelming your Crafting checks.

Your understanding of the thematic energies that power enchantments has reached the threshold of expertise. You learn all Novice-rank Concept Circles you do not already know (free), and gain access to Expert-rank Concept Circles. Learn 3 Expert-rank Concept Circles at the time of purchase. Expert Concepts include Sound, Lightning, Motion, Sun, Darkness, Decay, Change, Enlightenment, and Mana — concepts that significantly expand the range of effects you can inscribe.

Additionally, when you craft an enchantment with an Expert Concept, the Mana surcharge is reduced by 10 (from +50 to +40), and the Concept Power is increased by 1 die / 5 base value above standard. Your inscribed works carry the additional clarity and force of a master practitioner.

Your enchanting work has reached the durability of master craft. Inscriptions you create resist normal wear, dispelling, and counter-enchantment. Your enchanted items cannot be unmade or stripped of their inscriptions by anything less than a Heroic-tier Counterspell, a deliberate Sundering by an equivalent Artificer, or destruction of the item itself. Even then, attempts to strip an Artificer-inscribed item suffer 2 Challenges due to the resilience of your work.

Additionally, you may now make crafting attempts on items of Quality up to one rank higher than your normal limit (Heroic Artificer may inscribe Epic-quality items; Master Artificer may inscribe Legendary). The work takes proportionally longer, but the resulting items hold more enchantments and bear higher-tier Force Circles than would otherwise be available.

Your mastery of the foundational power that drives all enchantments has reached the threshold of true expertise. You gain access to Master-rank Force Circles (Mythic-tier). Learn the Mythic Force Circle at the time of purchase, alongside any Expert Force Circles you do not already know.

Additionally, when you craft an enchantment using a Greater (Heroic-tier) or Mythic (Epic-tier) Force Circle, the base VS is reduced by 10 due to your accumulated mastery. Mythic enchantments produce Epic-tier effects — flames that burn through magical barriers, frosts that freeze the souls of the warded, lightning that strikes through walls. Few practitioners ever earn the right to inscribe at this tier; you have.
Through deepening study and accumulated inscriptive practice, another Circle inscribes itself within your heart.

You gain an additional Mana Circle. Increase your maximum Mana by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effects of Spells you cast. Your Circle count now begins to support enchanting work at scales that ordinary practitioners cannot match — single inscriptions costing 300+ Mana, multi-Charge items, and the rare Forbidden inscription that requires specific rare components and specific ritual conditions.

Your craft has reached the threshold where you may inscribe more than one item at a time. When you perform a crafting ritual, you may inscribe up to a number of items equal to your Mana Circles simultaneously, provided the items share the same enchantment specification (same Force, Concept, and Structure Circles). The Mana cost is paid once per item; the Crafting check is rolled once for the entire batch, with the result applying to all items in the batch. Failed inscriptions consume the components of all items in the batch.

Additionally, items inscribed in a Mass Inscription session bear an additional small property: they are recognized by you as your own from any distance, and you can perceive their general location and any active state at will (within the same plane of existence). This is the working of arming an entire retinue — the Artificer who has equipped their party with a matched set of enchanted gear, or the warlord who has supplied their army with a thousand identical inscribed blades.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

Your craft has reached the apex of standard inscription. You may now inscribe multiple enchantments on a single item beyond its normal Quality limit — a Heroic item that would normally hold 2 enchantments may now hold 3; an Epic item may hold 4; a Legendary item may hold 5. The additional inscriptions require proportionally more Mana and crafting time, and the item must be of at least Heroic quality to support multi-stacking.

Additionally, you may now craft Permanent Enchantments — inscriptions that cannot be dispelled by any means short of destroying the item itself. Permanent Enchantments require a Master-rank Force Circle and triple the normal Mana cost, but the resulting enchantments are functionally indestructible. The work of a Master Inscription is the kind of relic that founds dynasties, anchors temples, and outlives the civilization that produced it.

Your craft has reached the threshold of the forbidden. You may now access Forbidden Force Circles (Legendary-tier enchantments). Forbidden inscriptions are the working of legend — weapons that decide wars, rings that grant their wearer mortal-godhood, items that should not exist but do because someone, once, took the trouble to make them.

Forbidden inscriptions require: (a) the Mana cost of 600+ from a single casting session, (b) rare components specified individually by the GM at the time of inscription, (c) a Place of Power or Long Vigil setting (see Mage Greater Ritual options), and (d) a successful Crafting check at base VS 85+ which is rolled with full Challenges from the inscription's complexity.

Forbidden inscriptions may also generate Corruption in their creators. The Artificer who has crafted multiple Forbidden items is marked — visibly to magical sight, and reputationally to anyone who knows the trade. A Forbidden inscription is, in some respects, the Artificer's testament: it is the work that will outlast them, and that other practitioners will study for centuries.

Your craft has reached the threshold where you may inscribe a self-acting Engram — a construct, automaton, homunculus, or animated tool with limited autonomous behavior. The Living Construct is your apex creation: an item that does not merely hold magic but uses it on your behalf.

At the time of purchase, you design and craft your Living Construct. The Construct may be a Small to Medium-sized automaton (clockwork servant, golem, animated armor, etc.), a familiar-sized creature (homunculus, mechanical bird, articulated cat), or an animated tool/weapon (an enchanted sword that fights on its own, a tower shield that interposes itself before strikes). The Construct functions as a Heroic-tier ally that follows your direction. It has its own Action economy: it acts on its own initiative immediately after your turn in combat, performing one Action per round as directed by you or by its preset behavioral inscriptions.

The Construct has Durability and Health appropriate to a Heroic-tier creature, Resistance to most physical and magical damage, and one signature attack of its own design (inscribed by you using your accumulated Concept Circles — typically dealing 3d8 + Concept BD damage of a chosen type). The Construct does not require food, sleep, or rest, but it does require monthly maintenance (1 day of focused crafting, no Mana cost) to remain operational.

If destroyed, the Construct may be rebuilt with sufficient time (1 week) and components (Mana cost equal to original creation + 25%). The Living Construct is the apex of the Artificer's craft and the working that all serious practitioners eventually attempt. The construct that walks beside its master is the Artificer's signature, their argument with the world, and their genuine companion.

sections.title: Chapter Seven – Classes, Part Two: Heroic Classes — The Divine Apex

blocks.html: The Priest stands alone among the Heroic classes. Where the others cultivate Aura, Mana, Edge, or the bound relationships of the spirit, the Priest cultivates a connection to a divine patron — and from that connection draws Grace, the radiant resource that replaces Mana entirely for those who walk the consecrated path. The Priest is the divine apex of the Heroic tier, the cultivator whose power is given by hand from above rather than built within.

This single class concludes the Heroic class roster. Thirteen Heroic classes in total — four Aura, three Mana, two Edge, three Spirit, and the Priest standing distinct. The Priest is unique in that they may not hold Mana at all while walking their path; they have made the trade for Grace, and the trade is complete and singular.

The cultivator of consecrated grace. The bearer of the Chalice of inner devotion. The one whose patron is not within them or beside them but above them, and whose hands have learned to hold light not their own.

Priests are the masters of Grace — the divine resource that replaces Mana for those who have given themselves to the service of a divine patron. Where Mages cultivate inscribed Circles within the heart and Warriors awaken Stars in the body, Priests cultivate Chalices: sacred receptacles for divine essence, deepened through the consecrated work of devotion and the patient inner practice of holding what does not belong to oneself. A Chalice is the architectural counterpart to a Mana Circle — but where the Circle inscribes mortal will into the heart's geometry, the Chalice opens a space within the consecrated soul to receive what flows from above.

The Priest's craft is the oldest of the divine traditions. It descends from the temple-keepers of the early peoples, from the sworn priestesses of the great sects, from the wandering ascetics whose poverty was their wealth and whose words carried the weight of formal sanctity. Modern Priests are recognized by their visible Grace: golden, frost-white, shadowed, flame-bright, or some other characteristic light that flickers around their hands during prayer and intensifies during invocation. A Priest's Chalice — the visible inner working — is often manifest as a small luminous form at the center of their chest, sometimes shaped like the iconography of their patron. The Priest who walks among the people is a recognizable figure: their patron is, in some respect, walking with them.
To enter the Priest Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Soul Score of 15 or higher.
Empathy Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Occult.
Novice rank in Medicine.
Either the Grace Mystery at Minor tier or higher, OR a formally established devotional relationship with a divine patron (GM determination).

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: First Chalice.

blocks.text: Priest Elite Tree: The Consecrated Vessel

blocks.html: The Priest's first lesson is that the Chalice must be empty before it can hold anything. The work of devotion is the work of becoming the kind of vessel into which Grace will flow. The Priest who fills themselves with their own ambition has no room for what the divine offers; the Priest who has emptied themselves is the one through whom Grace comes. This is the trade. This is the path.

Through formal initiation, sustained devotion, and the consecrated work of opening yourself to a divine patron, you inscribe the First Chalice within your soul — a sacred receptacle for Grace. The Chalice is visible to those with divine or magical sight as a luminous vessel-form at the center of your chest, glowing with the characteristic color and quality of your patron's Grace (golden for sun-aligned patrons, silver for moon, frost-white for winter, shadowed for night, flame-bright for fire and forge, or other appropriate manifestations).

Your Mana is now replaced with Grace. You may no longer hold Mana while you walk the Priest's path; the conversion is complete and singular. Your maximum Grace equals what your maximum Mana would have been, augmented by the Chalice's structural contribution: increase your maximum Grace by your Soul Score. Grace may be used to fuel Blessings and Miracles, as well as the standard cross-compatible Mana abilities (Resistance Reactions, Will to Power, Deep Meditation, Intuition). Grace cannot be used to cast arcane spells or activate Aura abilities.

Grace is recovered through prayer rather than rest. Once per day, you may pray for 30 minutes to convert your full Mana reserve to Grace (your Mana must be at full to perform this conversion). When Grace is exhausted, you must wait for your underlying Mana pool to recover before you may pray again. Innately divine creatures (born or initiated into direct divine lineage) may recover Grace through pure prayer — 1 point per minute of focused devotion, up to your Soul Score per session, with as many sessions per day as your Soul Bonus Dice.
Many Priest abilities scale with the number of Chalices you possess. The First Chalice grants +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effect of Blessings and Miracles you invoke.

Your devotional craft has matured into a regular practice that strengthens both your reservoir and your relationship with your patron. Once per scene, you may spend an Action in focused prayer to recover 1d6 Grace + your Soul Bonus Dice. This recovery does not require your Mana pool to be full — the prayer draws directly from your patron's reserve through the bond you have cultivated. Devotional Practice represents the active relationship of a working Priest; the discipline is the practice, and the practice is the discipline.

Additionally, you learn the foundational Blessings and Miracles that form the bedrock of the Priest's craft. At time of purchase, you learn 2 Basic Blessings and 2 Basic Miracles of your choice from the catalogs detailed in this section. Further Blessings and Miracles may be learned through later talents and study.
Many Priest abilities scale with the breadth of Blessings and Miracles you have learned and the depth of your relationship with your divine patron.

Your devotion has matured to the point where you may bestow more substantial blessings on those who would benefit. Learn 3 Novice-rank Blessings of your choice from the following catalog (or from any Blessings the GM has approved for your patron):

items: Blessing of Healing. Touch a willing creature. Restore 2d8 + your Soul Bonus Dice in Durability. Costs 15 Grace.
Blessing of Protection. Bestow on an ally; they gain +1 BD to Defense reactions and +1 BD to Resistance checks for the duration of the scene. Costs 15 Grace.
Blessing of Strength. Bestow on an ally; they gain +1 BD to attack rolls and physical skill checks for the duration of the scene. Costs 15 Grace.

Blessing of Sanctuary. Bless a 30-foot radius for 1 hour; allies within gain +1 BD to Resistance against fear, despair, and similar mental effects; corrupted creatures within suffer 1 Challenge to their actions. Costs 20 Grace.

Blessing of Guidance. Bestow on an ally; once before the end of the scene, when they face a significant decision, the GM provides a small divine insight or warning. The blessed creature may act on or ignore the guidance. Costs 15 Grace.

Blessing of Resolve. Bestow on an ally; once during the scene, when the blessed creature would be reduced to 0 Health, they instead remain at 1 Health, and the next attack against them suffers 1 Challenge. Costs 25 Grace.

blocks.html: Through sustained devotion and the deepening of your relationship with your patron, a second Chalice forms within your soul.

You gain an additional Chalice. Increase your maximum Grace by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effect of Blessings and Miracles you invoke. Your divine signature visibly intensifies — others who can perceive Grace see your Chalice work in deeper coloration and greater clarity, and other Priests of your tradition will recognize you on sight as a peer rather than a junior.

Your craft has matured to the point where you may invoke significant miracles in the field. Learn 3 Novice-rank Miracles of your choice from the following catalog (or from any Miracles the GM has approved for your patron):

items: Banish. Target a hostile spirit, eldritch creature, or undead within 60 feet. They must make a Soul Resistance check at 1 Challenge per Chalice you possess; on failure, they are forced to flee for 1 minute, taking no hostile action against you or your allies. Costs 25 Grace.

Smite the Corrupted. Strike a target with a holy blow. Deal 2d10 + Chalice BD Lucent Damage to a target within 30 feet, with the damage doubled if the target has any active Corruption Score. Costs 20 Grace.
Cleanse. Remove one curse, hex, condition, or magical effect of Heroic-rank or lower from a willing creature you touch. Costs 20 Grace; doubled cost for Heroic-tier effects.

Sanctify Ground. Bless a 30-foot radius for 1 minute. Within the radius, allies gain Fast Healing 2; hostile undead and corrupted creatures take 1d6 Lucent Damage at the start of their turns. Costs 30 Grace.

Mend Severance. Restore a recently severed connection — a sundered cultivation engine, a broken Contract, a destroyed Chain, a wounded oath. Restoration requires 1 minute of focused prayer and 30 Grace per tier of the severance (Basic 10 Grace, Novice 20, Expert 30, etc.).

Holy Fire. Manifest a column of consecrated flame at a point within 60 feet. The column is 10 feet in radius and 30 feet high, persisting for 1 round. Hostile creatures within take 3d6 Lucent Damage at the start of their turns; allies within are healed for 1d6 Vigor. Costs 30 Grace.

blocks.html: Your devotional discipline has reached the threshold where your patron's favor moves at the speed of need rather than the speed of ritual. When you would invoke a Blessing or Miracle, you may declare it a Faithful Service: the Blessing or Miracle is invoked at half normal Grace cost (round down, minimum 5 Grace), but you must vocalize a brief prayer or invocation appropriate to your patron. Faithful Service may be invoked once per scene.
Additionally, while in combat, you may invoke any Basic Blessing or Miracle as a Bonus Action rather than an Action. The invocation must still be paid for in Grace as normal.

Your relationship with your patron has matured to the threshold where you may invoke advanced Blessings and Miracles. Learn 3 Expert-rank Blessings or Miracles of your choice (mixed in any combination) from the catalogs. Expert-rank options include:

items: Blessing of Healing (Expert). Mass Heal — touch one ally and a chain extends to up to 5 additional allies within 30 feet, each restoring 2d8 + Soul BD Durability. Costs 30 Grace.

Blessing of Light. Bestow on an ally; they emit a radiant aura that grants them +1 BD to all rolls, illuminates 30 feet around them, and reveals invisible or concealed hostile creatures within range. Lasts for the scene. Costs 25 Grace.
Greater Smite. Strike with a more substantial holy blow. Deal 4d10 + Chalice BD Lucent Damage; on critical hit, the target is also Stunned until the end of their next turn. Costs 30 Grace.

Great Sanctuary. Bless a 60-foot radius for 4 hours. Inside the sanctuary, allies recover Grace, Mana, Vigor, and Durability at twice normal rate during any rest, and hostile creatures cannot enter the radius without making a Soul Resistance check at 2 Challenges. Costs 50 Grace.

Divine Vision. Receive a brief vision from your patron about a specific person, place, event, or object. The GM provides substantive information about the subject, drawing on the divine's perspective. Costs 30 Grace.

blocks.html: Your devotional discipline has reached the threshold of true coordinated action. You gain 1 additional Action on your turn. Additionally, when you use this additional Action to invoke a Blessing or Miracle, the Grace cost is reduced by 5 (minimum 5).
Choose from the following three transformative capstones. The third (Third Chalice) is the engine grant; the other two are mode-specific transformations.

Your devotional craft has reached the apex of bestowal. Once per long rest, you may bestow a Permanent Blessing on an ally — a sustained working that lasts indefinitely, until you specifically dismiss it or until the blessed creature commits an act so contrary to your patron's nature that the blessing is sundered.

A Permanent Blessing is a substantially more powerful version of any Novice or Expert Blessing you know. The blessed creature is permanently marked with your patron's favor — visible to those with divine sight, recognized by other Priests of allied or rival traditions, and granted the full effects of the blessing at all times. A creature with Permanent Blessing of Healing gains Fast Healing 3 (Durability) at all times. A creature with Permanent Blessing of Protection has +1 BD to all Defense and Resistance reactions, always. A creature with Permanent Blessing of Strength has +1 BD to all attack rolls and physical skill checks, always. Permanent Blessings cost 100 Grace to bestow, and only one Permanent Blessing may be active at a time per Priest.

The Priest who has bestowed a Permanent Blessing has formally extended their patron's favor to another mortal — a significant act in the divine hierarchy. The blessed creature may be expected to honor the patron's nature in their conduct; serious violations may sunder the blessing and may incur the patron's displeasure with both the blessed and the Priest who bestowed.

Your devotional cultivation reaches its full Heroic-tier expression. A third Chalice forms within your soul, completing the triad that other Priests will recognize as the mark of a senior practitioner.

You gain an additional Chalice. Increase your maximum Grace by your Soul Score. Add +1 Bonus Die to the damage or effect of Blessings and Miracles you invoke. Further Chalices are gated behind Epic and Legendary class progression. With three Chalices, your Grace reservoir is now substantial — you can sustain extended workings, multi-blessing scenes, and significant healing operations without depleting your reserves.

Additionally, you learn one additional Expert-rank Blessing or Miracle of your choice from the catalog at the time of purchase. Your devotional craft is now visible to passing Priests of your tradition as a senior practitioner; in temple-cities, you are recognized on sight and may expect appropriate courtesy from the lower clergy.

Your devotional craft has reached the apex of intercession. Once per long rest, you may invoke a Great Miracle — a working that would normally be impossible to achieve through ordinary divine practice. Great Miracles are major divine interventions performed at the limit of what your patron permits, and each invocation requires the patron's tacit assent (which is rarely automatic and may incur narrative obligations).
Great Miracles available to a Heroic-tier Priest include the following:

items: True Resurrection. Bring back a creature who has been dead for up to 1 day. The resurrection requires the deceased's body (or, at the Priest's choice, sufficient remains and a clear name), 1 hour of focused ritual prayer, and the consumption of all your current Grace. The resurrected creature returns at 1 Health, fully restored to physical wholeness, with all memories intact.

Mass Cleanse. Within a 100-foot radius, cleanse all creatures and the environment itself of all Heroic-or-lower curses, hostile enchantments, lingering corruptions, and magical taints. Requires 30 minutes of ritual prayer and the consumption of all your current Grace.

Divine Warding. Bless a region of up to 1 mile in radius (a town, a fortress, a stretch of countryside) for 1 year. Within the warded region, your patron's favor manifests in small visible signs: improved harvests, reduced illness, the unusual frequency of small graces. Hostile divine beings and corrupted entities cannot easily operate within the warded region (suffer 2 Challenges to all actions within). Requires 1 hour of ritual prayer, the consumption of all your current Grace, and the formal participation of at least one local community in the warding ceremony.

Mass Healing of Plague. Cure a single named disease in all creatures within a chosen city or region. Requires 1 hour of ritual prayer, the consumption of all your current Grace, and the formal cooperation of one or more local communities. The disease is permanently removed from the affected region; creatures who survive but had been suffering are restored to full health.

blocks.html: Great Miracles are recognized by witnesses as genuine divine interventions. The Priest who has performed one is, in some respect, more than mortal in the eyes of their community — and the GM may treat the Priest's reputation, social standing, and patron-relationship accordingly. Each Great Miracle leaves a lasting mark in the world; communities remember them for generations, and the Priest's name often becomes attached to specific blessings, healings, or miracles in oral tradition.

The cultivator of partnership. The bearer of the names that other practitioners only invoke. The one whose Contracted spirits answer not because they were called, but because they have agreed to be called.

Summoners are the masters of Contracts — durable pacts with named spirits whose terms are negotiated, sustained, and renewed over the long arc of a Summoner's career. Where Spiritualists encounter spirits through Spirit Focus, Mystery, and ritual, the Summoner has chosen to make the encounter into a relationship. A Contract is not a summoning spell. It is an ongoing pact — the spirit grants the Summoner specific powers and presence in exchange for stated obligations, and both sides understand that the agreement will outlast any single battle or scene.

The Summoner's craft requires patience, social skill, and the cultivated ability to negotiate with beings whose reasoning is fundamentally different from mortal thought. Some Summoners specialize in a single tradition (elemental Contracts, ancestor-spirit Contracts, fae Contracts, archon Contracts); others maintain diverse benches. Veteran Summoners are recognized by their bound retinue — the half-seen presences that travel with them, the names they speak under their breath in difficult moments, the quiet assurance that comes from knowing one is rarely truly alone. A Summoner's wedding day, naming ceremony, and major business deals typically include their Contracted spirits as witnesses or co-signatories.
To enter the Summoner Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Soul Score of 15 or higher.
Memory Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Occult.
Novice rank in Awareness.
At least one Spirit Focus (from Spiritualist Rank 4 or equivalent) OR at least 2 active Mysteries.

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Major Contract.

blocks.text: Summoner Elite Tree: The Bound Retinue

blocks.html: The Summoner's first lesson is that the unseen world is full of beings who want things, and who will exchange parts of themselves for what they want. The work is the work of careful proposal, careful listening, and careful keeping of agreements. A Summoner who breaks their Contracts is no longer a Summoner; they are something else, and not something pleasant.

You have negotiated and sealed your first Major Contract — a durable pact with a specific named spirit of significant power. The Contract is the work of careful ritual preparation, formal proposal, and the willing assent of the bound spirit; the specific terms are determined collaboratively with the GM at character entry. Common Major Contract types include: a named elemental of intermediate tier, a specific ancestor-spirit with battlefield prowess, a fey lord of a particular grove or city, a divine archon of intermediate rank, or a peer-spirit drawn from any pantheon or tradition appropriate to your character.
Each Major Contract grants you:

items: A passive benefit. An always-on capability granted by the spirit's nature — typically a +1 BD bonus to a specific skill or category of action (a Fire Elemental Contract might grant +1 BD to Fire-elemental damage; a Wisdom Archon Contract might grant +1 BD to Studies and Occult).
An active ability. A specific signature power, usable once per scene, that draws directly on the spirit's nature. Costs 15 Mana per use.

A summoning option. Call the spirit forth as a manifest combat ally for up to one scene, paying 25 Mana to manifest. The spirit acts as a Novice-tier ally with its own Action economy, taking one Action per round directed by you or by mutually-agreed behavioral parameters.

Obligations. Ongoing requirements set by the spirit — periodic tribute, occasional service, narrative ties to be honored. Specifics negotiated with the GM at the time of Contracting. Failure to honor obligations may result in the Contract weakening, suspension, or — in severe cases — termination by the spirit.

blocks.html: You learn the protocols of Contract maintenance, the formal language of pact-craft, and the basic etiquette of the unseen world's social architecture. Many Summoner abilities scale with the number and tier of your active Contracts.

Your craft has matured to the point where you can maintain a second Major Contract simultaneously. You may negotiate and seal a Second Major Contract using the same framework as your first. The second Contract grants the same array of benefits (passive, active, summoning, obligations) and counts toward your active Contract roster.

Additionally, you have refined the technique of multiple-spirit coordination. While you have two or more Contracts active, you gain +1 Bonus Die to all Occult checks involving any of your Contracted spirits, and your Contracted spirits may communicate with each other freely and with you regardless of intervening distance (within the same plane of existence).

Many Heroic Summoner abilities scale with the count of your Major Contracts. By Heroic apex, the Summoner who has invested fully in this engine has 3-4 Major Contracts and 1 Greater Contract, forming a genuine retinue of bound spirits.

Your accumulated practice has refined your ability to commune with your Contracted spirits at will, even when they are not summoned to physical presence. As a Free Action on your turn, you may briefly commune with any active Contracted spirit — asking a question, receiving counsel, or requesting that the spirit perform a brief intelligence-gathering task at a place where the spirit has presence.

Spirits may answer truthfully, partially, or evasively depending on their nature and on your standing with them. Once per scene, you may invoke a Deep Communion: a 1-minute focused session in which a Contracted spirit shares their full perspective on a chosen topic, providing information that ordinary social or investigative work could not produce. Deep Communions count as service performed by the spirit and may shift your Obligation balance with that spirit accordingly.

Your summoning technique has reached the threshold of true speed. The Mana cost of summoning a Contracted spirit is reduced by 10 (minimum 10 Mana), and summoning may now be performed as a Bonus Action instead of an Action. Additionally, once per scene when you summon a Contracted spirit, you may declare the summoning Resonant: the summoned spirit acts immediately on your initiative (rather than waiting for their own turn), and gains +1 Charge to their first action's damage or effect.

Through deliberate cultivation, you may strengthen one of your existing Major Contracts to greater capability. Choose one Major Contract you possess. That Contract's active ability now scales to twice per scene, its summoning duration extends to two scenes, and its passive bonus increases from +1 BD to +2 BD. The Strengthened Contract's obligations correspondingly deepen — the spirit demands greater tribute, more frequent service, or more committed alignment with their interests.
You may purchase Pact Strengthening multiple times at higher tiers, each time choosing a different Major Contract or further strengthening an already-Strengthened one.

Your relationship with your Contracted spirits has matured to the threshold of true partnership. When a Contracted spirit is summoned to your aid, you may, as a Free Action on your turn, share your own Mana or Vigor with the spirit — replenishing its reserves at a 1:1 rate. Additionally, the summoned spirit may now act as a true ally rather than a directed servitor: it makes its own tactical decisions in alignment with your stated objectives, takes appropriate reactions, and may operate independently within the scope of your shared interests.

Furthermore, your Contracted spirits may now perceive their fellow Contracted spirits as kin. They will not act against each other absent specific instruction from you, and they may coordinate during combat — sharing observations, synchronizing attacks, and supporting each other's actions as naturally as a longstanding band of allies would.

You have negotiated and sealed your first Greater Contract — a pact with a Heroic-tier spirit of considerable power and corresponding demands. Greater Contracts are with beings of substantial significance: named elementals of Heroic rank, regional ancestor-spirits with established cults, archfey of recognized courts, divine archons of senior rank, or major-tier representatives of any pantheon's middle hierarchy.

Greater Contracts grant the same benefit array as Major Contracts but at significantly greater scale: passive bonuses of +2 BD, active abilities usable twice per scene at 20 Mana each, summoning at 35 Mana producing a Heroic-tier ally with full Action economy and signature abilities of Heroic rank. Obligations are correspondingly substantial — Greater Contracts often involve formal alignments, ongoing tribute, or named long-term services that the Summoner must arrange across many sessions.

You may purchase a second instance of Pact Strengthening, strengthening either a different Major Contract or further deepening one already Strengthened. A doubly-Strengthened Contract's active ability scales to thrice per scene, summoning duration extends to three scenes, and the passive bonus rises to +3 BD. The Obligation deepens correspondingly.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

Your accumulated relationships have reached the threshold where you may convene all your Contracted spirits simultaneously. Once per long rest, you may invoke a Spirit Council: every Contracted spirit you possess manifests at your location for the duration of one scene, regardless of intervening distance or planar separation. The Council operates as a single cohesive presence — your gathered spirits may collectively answer questions (with truth aggregated from all their perspectives), perform combined actions (with all spirits acting in coordinated harmony), or vote on a course of action you propose, with the result becoming the Council's recommendation.

During a Spirit Council, all summoned Contracted spirits remain in full action capacity, providing you with a retinue of multiple Heroic-tier allies for the scene. The visual signature is unmistakable — half a dozen named presences gathered around the Summoner, each speaking with their own voice, each acting in their own characteristic manner. Witnesses who have not previously seen the Summoner in this state often find the experience deeply unsettling; the Summoner clearly has friends, and the friends are not entirely of this world.

After a Spirit Council, the Obligation owed to your collected spirits increases significantly — they have all shown up for you, and they will all expect their share of the gratitude. Plan accordingly.

Your bond with your Contracted spirits has reached the threshold of partial merger. Once per long rest, you may invoke Spirit Form: choose one Contracted spirit, and for the duration of one scene, you become partially that being. Your form takes on visible elements of the spirit's nature (a Fire Elemental's burning skin and flame-eyes; a Dragon-spirit's scaled hands and slit-pupil sight; an Archon's golden aura and faintly heard celestial music). Mechanically, you gain the spirit's signature damage type as your primary, the spirit's tier-appropriate Resistance to that type, and the ability to use the spirit's active ability without paying its normal Mana cost (the spirit is, briefly, you).

Additionally, while in Spirit Form, your Mana and Vigor pools are augmented by 50% (a 40-Mana Summoner becomes effectively 60-Mana for the duration), and your speech is briefly partly the spirit's — you may speak in their voice, with their authority, to beings who recognize that authority. Most witnesses recognize the Spirit Form as a significant event; the Summoner who walks into a court in Spirit Form is making a statement that will be remembered.

When Spirit Form ends, you suffer 1 rank of fatigue, and the spirit briefly departs your presence (cannot be summoned for 24 hours). The brief identity-blur leaves a small but real residual: each use of Spirit Form makes you slightly more like the spirit you partly were. Frequent users of this capstone develop visible long-term traces — hair color shifts, ambient temperature changes around them, faint accent or speech patterns drawn from the spirit's nature.

You have undertaken the long ritual work required to negotiate a Legendary-tier Contract — a pact with one of the great named entities of the unseen world. Greater Pacts are with beings of mythic significance: gods, archfey of the great courts, primal elementals, named devil-lords of the infernal hierarchy, ancestor-saints of recognized lineage, or the rare unique-named beings whose history fills the books of long-dead scholars.

A Greater Pact requires substantial in-fiction effort to negotiate — multiple sessions of preparation, alignment with the target being's recognized priorities, ritual components and locations appropriate to their nature, and the formal assent of the entity in question (which is rarely automatic and often requires meaningful service or proof before negotiations begin). The specifics are worked out with the GM at the time of capstone purchase and may take significant downtime to complete.

A Greater Pact grants benefits proportional to the Legendary tier of the bound being: passive bonuses of +3 BD, active abilities usable thrice per scene with reduced Mana costs, summoning that produces a Legendary-tier ally for two scenes, and ongoing relational benefits that may shape entire campaigns. Obligations are correspondingly substantial: the Pact-holder's life and the bound entity's interests are now woven together. Breaking a Greater Pact is functionally impossible without catastrophic consequence. The Summoner who has achieved this capstone has chosen a side of cosmic significance, and the world will eventually notice.

The cultivator of the seal. The bearer of the locked vessel. The one whose Corruption is not a wound but a working — accumulated power held under tight rein, distributed through Chains that take what was forced and turn it into the cultivator's instrument.

Binders are the masters of Chains — bound entities held in vessels by ritual seal, by force of will, and by the cultivator's accumulated Corruption used as a fuel rather than a stain. Where Summoners negotiate Contracts with willing spirits, Binders capture, restrain, and exploit hostile or unwilling beings. The Chain is the working: a sealed vessel (talisman, weapon, body-part, ritual object) that holds the bound entity in submission, and a Binder who has done their work properly can wield the bound entity's powers as if they were their own.

The Binder's craft is morally precarious. Most Binders have already paid the price of their craft — through Spiritualist Forbidden Lore at the lower path, through ritual contact with dark powers, through the slow accumulation of Maledictions and Corruption that marks a long career in eldritch work. The Binder's class accepts that price and turns it into an instrument. Where the Spiritualist's Forbidden Lore *generates* Corruption to gain access to dark paths, the Binder's Chains *consume* and *scale on* existing Corruption — using it as a usable resource rather than allowing it to weigh on the soul as passive accumulation. A Binder with 15 Corruption is not more cursed than a Binder with 5; they are more powerful, having more accumulated current to draw on. The Binder who walks this path has decided that their darkness will work for them, not against them.
To enter the Binder Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Soul Score of 15 or higher.
Resolve Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Occult.
Novice rank in Resistance.
At least 5 Corruption Score, with at least one Corruption Track or Tainted Talent already active.
