blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Major Chain.

blocks.text: Binder Elite Tree: The Locked Vessels

blocks.html: The Binder's first lesson is that what was once a price can become a payment. The Corruption that the Spiritualist accrued through dark study is not a curse for the Binder; it is the cultivated current that powers the Chains they have built. The Binder is the cultivator who decided that they would not be ruined by what they had taken. They would instead use it.

You have completed the ritual work required to seal your first Major Chain — a binding of a hostile or unwilling entity into a vessel held by your accumulated craft. The specific entity is determined collaboratively with the GM at character entry. Common Major Chain types include: a captured demon of intermediate rank, a bound shade or wraith with battlefield capacity, a captured fey-spite (a wronged fey whose grudge is now your weapon), a chained eldritch fragment (a small piece of something larger held in a sealed container), or the bound soul of a vanquished named enemy.
Each Major Chain grants you the following, with scaling based on your current Corruption Score:

items: A passive benefit. An always-on capability drawn from the bound entity's nature. The benefit's strength scales with your Corruption Score: +1 BD to a specific skill or action per 5 Corruption (a 10-Corruption Binder has a +2 BD bonus from a single Chain; a 20-Corruption Binder has +4 BD).

An active ability. A specific signature power drawing on the bound entity's nature, usable once per scene at base cost of 15 Mana. Each active ability has a baseline effect; the effect amplifies by +1 Bonus Die per 5 Corruption Score you possess.

A release option. Briefly unleash the bound entity for greater effect at higher risk. As an Action, you may unseal the Chain for one round, gaining triple the active ability's normal effect and the bound entity's signature damage type and properties at greater scale. The release is contested; at the end of the round, make a Resistance check (Soul) at 1 Challenge per 10 Corruption Score; on failure, the entity briefly escapes, dealing damage to you equal to 1d8 per 5 Corruption Score and requiring 1 minute of focused work to re-seal.

blocks.html: Critically: Chains do not generate Corruption. The Binder's accumulated Corruption is the resource that powers the Chains, not a side effect of using them. A Binder may complete their entire Heroic tree without gaining a single additional point of Corruption, using only the accumulated price already paid through other paths.

Your craft has matured to the point where you can maintain multiple Major Chains simultaneously. You may negotiate, seal, and maintain a Second Major Chain using the same framework as your first. The second Chain grants the same array of benefits and counts toward your active Chain roster.

Additionally, while you have two or more Chains active, you gain +1 Bonus Die to all Occult checks involving binding, sealing, or compulsion of entities. Bound entities within your Chains may not communicate or coordinate with each other (they are sealed in separate vessels); but they all collectively recognize you as their binder, and their resentment of you may, over time, become a unified front.

Many Heroic Binder abilities scale with the count of your Major Chains and with your accumulated Corruption Score. By Heroic apex, the Binder who has invested fully in this engine has 3-4 Major Chains and 1 Greater Chain, forming a genuine arsenal of bound powers.

Your accumulated practice has refined your ability to compel bound entities to specific service. Once per scene, you may compel a bound entity in one of your Chains to perform a specific task you have designed: scout a location, deliver a message, attack a specific named target, or hold a specific position. The compulsion lasts until the task is completed or until the end of the scene, whichever comes first. The entity may resist with a contested Soul check at 1 Challenge per 10 Corruption Score (your accumulated Corruption is the leverage that compels them).

Additionally, you may now "loan" the active ability of any of your Chains to a willing creature within 30 feet for the duration of one scene. The recipient gains use of the loaned ability at its current scaled effect. Loaning a Chain temporarily reduces your own access to that ability (you cannot also use the loaned ability while it is in use by another).

Your craft has reached the threshold where you may inscribe a Chain's binding into a physical object — distributing the bound entity's power across multiple vessels. Choose one of your Major Chains; the bound entity may now be partially expressed through a number of inscribed objects equal to your Corruption Score divided by 5 (round down, minimum 1).

Each inscribed object holds a small expression of the bound entity's power: passive benefits while held, single-use activations of the Chain's active ability, or specific signature properties. Inscribed Chains may be gifted, sold, or hidden — the bound entity's power becoming portable and distributable. Loaning a Chain through an inscribed object is functionally identical to direct loaning (one ability use at a time), but the inscribed object is permanent and may be reclaimed or destroyed at the Binder's discretion.

Your accumulated discipline has reached the threshold where the bound entities can no longer easily threaten your control. You gain +2 Bonus Dice to all Resistance checks involving the bound entities in your Chains, and the Resistance threshold for entity escape on Release attempts is reduced (failures now occur on 2 Challenges per 10 Corruption rather than 1). Additionally, while you have at least one active Chain, you gain +1 Bonus Die to all Corruption-related Resistance checks — your accumulated darkness serves you, even when it resists you.

Your craft has matured to the point where your Chains may resonate together — each strengthening the others through their proximity in your accumulated practice. When you activate the active ability of one of your Chains, you may, as a Free Action immediately afterward, also activate the active ability of a different Chain. The second activation costs no Mana and produces its full effect, but the activation consumes the once-per-scene cap of both Chains simultaneously.

Additionally, while you have three or more active Chains, your accumulated Corruption Score effectively functions at +5 for the purposes of Chain scaling (a Binder with actual Corruption 12 functions as if they had 17 Corruption when activating their Chains). This is a genuine amplification — your Chains have begun to feed each other.

You have completed the long ritual work required to bind a Heroic-tier entity into a Greater Chain. Greater Chains hold beings of substantial significance: named demons of regional reputation, captured wraiths of recognized lineage, eldritch fragments of considerable size, archfey-spite born of named slights, or the soul of a Heroic-tier vanquished enemy who has earned the right to be remembered.

Greater Chains grant benefits proportional to their Heroic-tier nature: passive bonuses scaling at +2 BD per 5 Corruption Score, active abilities usable twice per scene at 20 Mana with effects amplifying at +1 BD per 5 Corruption Score, release options producing genuinely terrifying combat effects (the bound being's full Heroic-tier presence manifests briefly during release), and obligations of significantly increased complexity — the bound entity is more dangerous, and your hold on them more contested.

Your control over your Chains has reached the threshold of true mastery. You may now Release any of your Chains as a Bonus Action rather than an Action, and the contested Resistance check at the end of the Release round is rolled with 2 Challenges (favoring your control over the entity's escape). Additionally, when you Release a Chain, you may, as a Free Action immediately afterward, choose to maintain the Release into a second round by paying an additional 10 Mana — the bound entity's expression continues uninterrupted, with the second Resistance check rolled at the end of the extended duration.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

Your craft has reached the apex of inscribed binding. You may now inscribe Major and Greater Chains into permanent vessels that may be wielded by others. The inscribed Chain functions as a single-use or multi-use item depending on its construction, with the bound entity's power accessible to the bearer (often a specific ally, occasionally a wider distribution for a Binder building organizational power).

Inscribed Chains of this tier are recognized as genuine magical items of Heroic to Epic tier, depending on the Chain's underlying entity. They may be passed down across generations, sold for substantial value, or used to equip an entire conspiracy. The Binder who has achieved Distributed Power may be the wealthiest single practitioner in their region — their workshop produces items that other Binders cannot match, and the obligations they have accumulated form a network that often functions as a small organization.

Most importantly: inscribed Chains continue to draw on your Corruption Score for scaling, even when wielded by others. The Binder is the cultivated source; the wielders are channels. This is the working of legend — the Heroic Binder whose name is whispered in markets that sell items they never directly handle.

Your accumulated practice has matured to the threshold where you may briefly manifest the bound entities of all your Chains simultaneously. Once per long rest, you may invoke a Bound Legion: every Chain you possess Releases its bound entity for the duration of one scene, manifesting them as your commanded retinue. The legion fights at your direction, with each bound entity expressing its full released form for the scene's duration.

The Bound Legion is the working of myth — the Binder whose dark menagerie fights as one, the cultivator who has decided that the contained will, for one scene, be uncontained. The visual signature is overwhelming: half a dozen or more hostile beings, simultaneously released and yet acting in alignment with the Binder's intent, present a force few opponents can match.

At the end of the scene, every bound entity that was Released attempts to escape simultaneously. Make a Resistance check at 1 Challenge per 5 Corruption Score (your accumulated darkness is the only thing holding them); on failure, one or more Chains may break, with the bound entities escaping into the world in their full released forms. The Binder who has used this capstone unwisely may find themselves the target of half a dozen newly-free hostile entities, all of whom remember being held.

Your binding has reached a depth from which neither you nor the bound entity can separate. Once per long rest, you may invoke an Apocryphal Pact: choose one of your Chains, and for the duration of one scene, you merge with the bound entity — taking on its form, its abilities, its damage type, and its essential nature in addition to your own. The pact is irreversible in the moment; you cannot release the merger early.

During an Apocryphal Pact, you gain the bound entity's full Heroic-tier (or Greater-tier) Resistance to its primary damage type, its signature active ability at no Mana cost and unlimited uses, its base statistics added to your own where applicable (the higher of yours and the entity's), and the entity's appearance and presence overlaid on your form. Witnesses recognize the merger as a genuinely uncanny event; the Binder is, briefly, partly something else.

When the Apocryphal Pact ends, the bound entity does not fully separate. A permanent piece of the entity remains in you — a subtle but real change to your form, your speech, your reflexes, or your dreams. The Binder who repeatedly invokes Apocryphal Pacts becomes a different being over time, accumulating layers of bound essence as their identity slowly blurs with their Chains. This is the apex risk and the apex working of the path: the Binder who has, at the end, become partly the things they once locked away.

Apocryphal Pact does not break the Chain; the bound entity remains bound. But the Binder is no longer entirely the Binder they once were, and at sufficient accumulation of Pacts the line between bound and binder may blur to the point of full inversion. This is the path that leads to the Archfiend Legendary class — the Binder who has bound so deeply that they themselves are now what was once held.

The cultivator of the long partnership. The bearer of the bonded creature whose growth runs parallel to the cultivator's own. The one whose family includes someone who is not, strictly speaking, a person.

Tamers are the masters of the Bonded Beast — a physical creature raised, partnered, and grown alongside the Tamer through long shared life. Where Summoners pact with spirits and Binders seal hostile entities, Tamers raise creatures: hatchlings hand-fed in their early years, wild beasts won over through patience and care, exotic mounts inherited through family tradition, sometimes the wounded survivors of greater predators who chose the Tamer as the one who would keep them alive. The bond is consensual, gradual, and deeply personal. A Bonded Beast is not summoned, captured, or borrowed — it is family.

The Tamer's craft is the slowest of the Spirit-cluster paths to mature, and the most narratively rewarding. The Bonded Beast advances tier alongside the Tamer's Heroic progression, growing from a small ally at character entry to a Heroic-tier party member by apex. The relationship deepens through shared adventures, shared meals, shared dangers, and the small daily work of feeding and care that the Tamer must keep up if the bond is to remain. Veteran Tamers are recognized by their Beast — the dragon-rider, the wyvern-knight, the manticore's keeper, the woman who travels with the great wolf. The Beast is, at the table, a co-protagonist.
To enter the Tamer Heroic class, you must meet all of the following:

items: Empathy Score of 15 or higher.
Resolve Score of 15 or higher.
Novice rank in Beast Handling.
Novice rank in Awareness.
Either Beast Tongue Mystery at Minor tier or higher, OR a successful narrative-driven encounter with the Beast that becomes the Bonded creature (GM determination).

blocks.html: When you enter this class you gain the following Talent:

items: Bonded Beast.

blocks.text: Tamer Elite Tree: The Long Bond

blocks.html: The Tamer's first lesson is that the bond is built. There is no shortcut to a Bonded Beast — only the long work of presence, the daily feeding, the shared danger, the small moments of trust that build into something that no other cultivation can replicate. The Bonded Beast that fights beside the Heroic Tamer is the work of years, and the Tamer wears that work as both armor and identity.

You have completed the long process of raising, partnering with, or otherwise bonding to a single creature that will travel with you, fight with you, and grow alongside you. At the time of class entry, you and the GM design your Bonded Beast collaboratively: species, size, signature features, personality, and history are determined in the moment of class commitment.

Your Bonded Beast begins as a Novice-tier creature appropriate to its species. Common Bonded Beast types include: a young dragon (Small-sized; will grow), a wyvern (Medium-sized), a large wolf or lion (Medium-sized), an exotic mount such as a kirin or manticore (Large-sized), a great bird (Eagle, Roc-fledgling, or similar), or other creatures appropriate to the campaign world and the Tamer's background.
Your Bonded Beast has:

items: Its own action economy. It takes one Action and one Bonus Action per round on its own initiative (typically immediately after your turn, though it may be delayed by tactical considerations). The Beast may take Reactions normally.

Its own statistics. Health, Durability, Vigor, Defense, and signature attacks appropriate to a Novice-tier creature of its species. A Bonded Wolf has a bite attack (1d10 damage), a Bonded Hatchling Dragon has a small flame-breath, etc. The GM provides statistics; the Tamer may guide species choice.

A bond connection to you. While within 100 feet, you may communicate with the Beast freely, share simple emotional states, and direct its general behavior. The Beast retains personality and may occasionally diverge from your direction (the GM has discretion here; the Beast is a co-protagonist, not a puppet).
A growth trajectory. The Beast tier advances with your Tamer Heroic class purchases. Talents at later tiers explicitly increase the Beast's stat tier, abilities, and combat presence.

blocks.html: The Beast requires care to remain bonded: appropriate food, daily attention, shelter from environmental hazards beyond its tier. Failure to maintain the bond may result in the Beast becoming distant, hungry, sick, or — in severe neglect — leaving the Tamer's company. Tamers who do not feed their Beasts find that the Beasts do not, eventually, fight for them.

Your Bonded Beast has matured through shared adventures and your sustained care. The Beast advances from Novice-tier to Expert-tier, gaining significant boosts to its statistics: Health and Durability increase by 50%, Defense improves by 1, the Beast's signature attack damage scales up one rank, and the Beast gains a second signature ability appropriate to its species and tier (a Bonded Dragon at Expert might gain limited flight or a stronger breath weapon; a Bonded Wolf might gain pack tactics or a howl that boosts allies).

Additionally, the bond strengthens. You may now communicate with the Beast at any distance (within the same plane), share complex emotional states bidirectionally, and direct the Beast's actions through specific tactical commands rather than general guidance. The Beast may now also operate independently for extended periods — scouting, gathering information, or performing tasks at the Tamer's request — and reliably return when the task is complete.
Many Tamer abilities scale with your Bonded Beast's tier and the depth of the cultivated bond. By Heroic apex, the Beast will have grown to genuine Heroic-tier party-member status.

Your accumulated bond has reached the threshold where you and your Bonded Beast may share perceptions. Once per scene, as a Free Action, you may briefly perceive through your Beast's senses for up to 1 minute — seeing what it sees, hearing what it hears, sensing what it senses. This sharing is two-way; the Beast may equally perceive through you (with the Beast's natural curiosity sometimes leading to it requesting these sessions on its own initiative).

Additionally, you and the Beast may now coordinate combat actions: as a Free Action on your turn, you may direct the Beast to perform a specific combat action immediately rather than waiting for its turn. The directed action is performed at +1 Bonus Die to its execution. This may be used once per round.

Your Bonded Beast has learned the rhythm of fighting alongside you. When you and your Beast both target the same enemy in the same round, the second of your attacks (whether yours or the Beast's) gains +2 Bonus Dice to its damage. Additionally, when either of you is targeted by an attack while adjacent to the other, the targeted creature gains +1 Bonus Die to its Defense reaction (the other's presence is a tactical asset for both).

Once per round, when your Beast is targeted by an attack within your reach, you may use your Reaction to interpose, taking the attack yourself; conversely, when you are targeted by an attack within the Beast's reach, the Beast may use its Reaction to interpose. The bond is mutual protection.

Your accumulated practice with your Bonded Beast has enabled you to teach it new tricks. Choose one new signature ability or technique for your Beast at the time of purchase. Examples include: a defensive technique (the Beast guards you with reflexive defense), a movement technique (the Beast carries you at full speed without tiring), a stealth technique (the Beast may approach silently and surprise enemies), a healing-touch technique (the Beast's bond restores 1d6 Vigor to you per turn while in contact), or other techniques appropriate to the Beast's species and your collaborative work.

You may purchase Beast Training multiple times across the Tamer tree, each time choosing a different ability or technique to instill in the Beast. By Heroic apex, the Beast may have 3-4 trained abilities beyond its species's natural arsenal.

Your Bonded Beast has reached the threshold of true Heroic-tier capability. The Beast advances from Expert-tier to Heroic-tier, with substantial improvements: Health and Durability double, Defense improves by 2, signature attacks scale to Heroic damage ranks, and the Beast gains one Heroic-tier signature ability appropriate to its species and to your cultivated partnership (an Adult Bonded Dragon at Heroic might gain a full breath weapon and limited flight; a Bonded Lion-king at Heroic might gain a roar that imposes Frightened on enemies and significant combat presence).

Additionally, the Beast may now operate with full autonomy as a true Heroic-tier ally. It takes two Actions and one Bonus Action per round on its own initiative, may use any signature ability without Mana cost (the Beast's reserves are its own), and may make tactical decisions in alignment with your stated objectives even without specific direction. The Beast is, by this tier, a full party member.

Your bond with the Beast has reached the depth where your resources flow naturally between you. You may, as a Free Action on your turn, share Vigor, Mana, or Durability with your Bonded Beast at a 1:1 rate — replenishing its reserves from yours, or vice versa. The sharing is limited only by the closer creature's maximum capacity and the available shared resources.

Additionally, while your Bonded Beast is conscious and within 100 feet, you both gain +1 Bonus Die to Resistance checks against fear, despair, and mental-control effects. The presence of the other is a stabilizing force; you each remember who you are while the other is near.

Your coordination with your Bonded Beast has reached the threshold of true synchrony. You gain 1 additional Action on your turn. Additionally, when you use this additional Action to coordinate directly with your Beast (a combined attack, a shared maneuver, or a coordinated defensive position), the action gains +1 Bonus Die to its primary roll.
Choose from the following three transformative capstones.

Your Bonded Beast has matured beyond Heroic capacity into the threshold of Mythic existence. The Beast advances from Heroic-tier to Mythic-tier — a being of genuine legendary significance, capable of operating as a co-equal hero rather than a partner-companion. A Mythic Bonded Dragon is now an Adult Dragon of substantial size and breath weapon, capable of carrying riders, contesting other legendary creatures, and shaping battlefield events through its presence alone. A Mythic Bonded Lion is a great king of beasts, recognized by other apex predators, whose roar can rout small armies. Similar scaling applies to other species.

The Mythic Beast has its own action economy comparable to a Mythic-tier creature (typically 3 Actions and 2 Bonus Actions per round), signature abilities of genuine Mythic significance, and presence at the level of a major NPC. Witnesses recognize the Beast as a Mythic being on sight, and the Tamer's reputation expands accordingly — the world now knows them as the rider, the keeper, or the partner of a being whose name may be remembered for generations.

Your accumulated practice has matured to the threshold where you may form a second Bonded Beast partnership. The second Beast begins at Novice-tier, raised through similar long work, and over time may be advanced through additional class purchases at Epic and Legendary tiers (or through narrative campaign work). The two Beasts may be of the same or different species, with your collaborative bond extending to both.

The two Beasts may coordinate naturally with each other, sharing the same emotional bond that connects them to you, communicating freely, and operating as a small pack. In combat, the two Beasts often fight as flanking partners — one drawing attacks while the other strikes, or both attacking the same target in coordinated sequence. The Pack Bond is the Tamer who has decided that one partnership was not enough — that the family will be larger.

Your bond with your Bonded Beast has reached the threshold of partial merger. Once per long rest, you may invoke Beast Form: for the duration of one scene, you assume the form of your Bonded Beast — fighting with the Beast's abilities, statistics, and damage type, while retaining your own cognition and will. Your physical body briefly transforms into a mirror of the Beast's form (with personalized markers — your features still recognizable, your color or coat showing your individual identity).

While in Beast Form, you have the Beast's full Heroic-tier (or Mythic-tier, if you have taken Mythic Bond) statistics, abilities, and combat profile. You also retain your own talents, spell access, and Edge or Mystery resources at their normal capacity. Your actual Bonded Beast is, during this time, also active and may fight beside you in their normal form — meaning two of your Beast-form-and-original-Beast may be on the battlefield simultaneously.

When Beast Form ends, you return to your normal form (or your most recent normal form) and suffer 1 rank of fatigue from the transformation. Each use of Beast Form leaves a small but real residual on you — minor changes to your hair, your eyes, your bearing — that accumulate over many uses. The Tamer who frequently invokes Beast Form becomes, over time, visibly part of their Beast's species: the long-time Bonded Dragon's Tamer eventually has scales at the edges of their hands; the long-time Bonded Wolf's Tamer's eyes have begun to reflect light at night. This is the apex of the Tamer's path: not merely partnered with the Beast, but slowly becoming part of the same kin.

Skills are applied knowledge; when you roll to use a weapon, understand a language, or remember a formula, this is a skill roll. When you gain bonuses to a specialized skill, such as a bonus to attacking with swords, or a bonus while performing biological research, this is a Skill bonus. When you apply a unique ability or gain a special passive trait, this is generally a talent.

Having a skill tree does not provide you with any of the skill bonuses or talents in that tree. These must be purchased with skill points. Purchasing skill bonuses or talents will provide you bonus dice with some skill checks, ability bonuses, or other feats related to that skill tree. For example, the Sprinting talent under Athletics will allow you to run faster. The Throwing skill bonus under Aim provides a bonus dice to attacks made with thrown weapons.
Each skill bonus or skill talent costs 1 skill point. They must be purchased in order; you cannot purchase advanced skills and talents until you have purchased the prerequisites.

As a 1st-level character, you may only purchase Rank 1 skill bonuses and talents from any skill tree. This means you cannot spend all your skill points to fully advance a single skill tree as a beginning character. You must spend all your skill points at 1st level; you cannot hold on to your initial skill points past level 1 to spend them all on advanced skills.
Each time you gain a level of experience, you will gain 1 skill point. Furthermore, you may gain additional skill points as boons from some extraordinary Deeds you accomplish in play.

A skill is an action you can take. All skill trees begin with a skill check you can make using one of your Ability Scores. All characters can attempt an action defined by the core skill trees. You don't need to learn any of the skill specialties or talents first. You can always use Aim to throw a stone, Defense to dodge, Studies to research a legend, and Athletics to climb a wall.

When you make a skill check, the GM will determine the relevant Ability Score, be it Strength, Memory, or even Soul. Which Ability Score depends entirely on the situation. You might make an Awareness (Empathy) check to notice someone lying to you or an Awareness (Reason) check to note an overlooked detail in an investigation. Most attack skill checks use Agility, but you might make a combat skill check under a different Ability Score to identify a technique, appraise an opponent, or learn a combat technique. When you make a Defense reaction, you usually use Speed. In this way, any action can be described as a combination of a Core Skill, and an Ability Score. You can also take Chances on skill checks, increasing the difficulty but offering additional benefits.

When you make an action roll, you can also take up to three (3) Chances, increasing the difficulty but offering additional benefits. Each Chance you take increases the Victory Sum of your action by +10 but adds a Charge to the outcome of the action roll. Each core skill includes the types of chances you can take, and the benefits they provide.
You can only take chances on action rolls, not reactions, even if that skill could normally take a chance, such as an attack roll while taking an opening.
You can evolve a skill by spending skill points on the skill tree, gaining Skill bonuses to specializations of the skill, and talents that give you passive and active bonuses and abilities.

The core combat, academic, and practical skills are known to all characters. At 1st level, you may put skill points into any of these skill trees. These are general areas of knowledge and ability available to all characters.

Talents refer to abilities purchased with skill points. This can include skill talents that give you bonus dice to skill checks such as Novice Light Weapons, or Novice Crafting. You always add these bonus dice when making the appropriate skill check, regardless of the ability score. For example, if you have the Expert Ranged Weapons, you add 2 bonus dice when you make a Ranged Weapon attack roll, regardless of what ability score you also may add. Similarly, if you are called upon to remember some trivia or historical knowledge, this may be a Studies check, but you would also get to add the bonus dice from the Expert Studies talent, if you have it.

Talents grant special abilities, such as a new action, or increase your traits or ability scores. The more prerequisites a talent has, the more powerful that talent is. For Rank 5, talents often grant high bonuses or powerful abilities. The same is true of general talents that can only be purchased with high ability scores or after having accomplished a special Deed.
Every character has nineteen Core Skills that cover the basic actions available to all characters, from combat to comradery. All characters can attempt these actions, though not all are very skilled.
Athletics: Running, climbing, swimming, lifting, leaping, sailing, rowing, and boating.
Arcane Arms: Attacking with arcane weapons and casting elemental magic.
Awareness: Spotting danger, noticing details, or conducting investigations.
Beast Handling: Taming, training, steering wagons or riding mounts.
Crafting: Forging, tailoring, woodworking, gemcutting, or construction.
Defense: Dodge, parry, or block Reactions against weapon-like attacks.
Entertain: Music, dance, acting, or performance for inspiration, distraction, or influence.
Heavy Weapons: Attacks with two-handed weapons (greatswords, axes, hammers, polearms).
Legerdemain: Lockpicking, sleight of hand, theft, or trickery.
Light Weapons: Attacks with one-handed weapons (swords, knives, rapiers).
Medicine: Treating injuries, surgery, diagnostics, or pharmacology.
Occult: Casting spells, performing rituals, reading arcane symbols, or crafting potions.
Ranged Weapons: Attacks with bows, thrown weapons, or crossbows.
Resistance: Overcoming physical, mental, or supernatural assaults and conditions.
Natural Philosophy: Astronomy, mathematics, geometry, natural science, and the rational study of the observable world.
Social: Charm, persuasion, deception, intimidation, or reading people.
Stealth: Hiding, moving silently, or avoiding detection.
Studies: Academic lore, law, history, languages, and cryptography.
Survival: Tracking, hunting, foraging, simple navigation, or surviving in the wilderness.
Unarmed: Strikes with martial arts, brawling, or natural weapons.
Strength: Acts of physical power or might.
Agility: Acts of balance, grace, and hand-eye coordination.
Stamina: Acts of physical endurance, fortitude or resilience.
Speed: Acts of physical reflexes, reaction time, and movement.
Memory: Acts or memorization, recall, and learning.
Empathy: Acts of social grace, understanding others, intuition.
Reason: Acts of problem solving, deduction, and logic.
Resolve: Acts of bravery, willpower, and conviction.
Soul: Acts of spiritual power, faith, magical awareness, and meditation.

You are called upon to make an Arcane Arms check when attempting to strike a target with an elemental blast, magical projectile, or weapon-like spell released through an Arcane Armament such as a wand, rod, scepter, or staff. You may add your Soul Bonus Dice to this roll, if any. that channel the user’s ambient Mana into a coherent elemental attack. Each Arcane Arm has an elemental descriptor, such as Fire, Lightning, or Ice. Unless a spell or weapon states otherwise, the elemental descriptor determines the damage type of the Arcane Arm’s basic attacks and spells. Exotic elemental arms exist, but are rare.

Special: Arcane Arm Damage. You do not automatically add Bonus Dice to damage from Arcane Arms. When you attack with an Arcane Arm’s basic elemental blast, those with sufficient training may add Circle bonus dice, if you have any, to damage. When you cast an Arcane Arms spell, the spell determines whether you add any Bonus Dice, or another effect to damage.

Special: Mana Requirement. All Arcane Arms require a minimum Mana reserve to activate. Meeting this requirement allows the Arcane Arm to function, but basic attacks do not normally consume Mana. Arcane Arms spells consume Mana normally.

Special: Arcane Arms Spells. Characters with Arcane Arms Novice may use Basic Arcane Arms spells and learn Novice Arcane Arms spells. More advanced Arcane Arms spells require higher ranks in this skill tree.
You can take the following Chances with Arcane Arms action rolls.
Damage. Add 1 Charge to your Damage Bonus Dice.
Elemental Pierce. Thread the spell through a weakness in armor, aura, or resistance. Ignore armor or elemental protection of a lower quality than your Arcane Arm or spell effect.
Elemental Curve. Bend the magical projectile around a shield, obstruction, or defensive angle, adding 1 Challenge to the opponent’s Defense reaction, if taken.

Overchannel. Increase the spell’s elemental pressure. If the attack deals damage and causes a condition, the target suffers 1 Challenge on the first Resistance check made to end or avoid that condition.

Resonant Strike. Strike a target already damaged by the same element this round. Add 1 Charge to the damage Bonus Dice, but only if the target has taken damage of the same elemental type since the beginning of your last turn.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to Arcane Arms attacks and action rolls. You may use Basic Arcane Arms techniques and learn Novice Arcane Arms spells.

You have learned to stabilize the basic elemental discharge of an Arcane Arm through your Mana Circles. When attacking with an Arcane Arm’s basic elemental blast, you may add your Mana Circle Bonus Dice to damage. For example, if you had 3 Circles, you would add +3 Bonus Dice to damage.
You have learned to aim magical force without wasting motion or Mana. Increase your Soul Score by 1d6.
With training you have learned to employ arcane arms as part of your practiced casting. You may cast spells and incantations that require your hands to be free while holding an arcane arm.

By overcharging your basic elemental blasts you can release devastating bolts of energy. As an action on your turn you may charge up your arcane arm. When you finally release the elemental blast, you have charged up, each action you spent charging your arcane arm increases the damage of your elemental attack by the weapon damage, up to +3x your base weapon damage. You cannot hold elemental charges across rounds, and each elemental charge you add to the attack inflicts 1 Challenge to your attack roll.
When you make attacks and action rolls with Arcane Arms, you add +1 Bonus Dice, for a total of +2. You may learn Expert Arcane Arms techniques and Expert Arcane Arms spells.

While armed with a rod or staff, you may hold an elemental blast in the head of your arcane arm and deal a physical blow. Make a Light (rod) or Heavy (staff) weapon attack with agility with 1 Challenge. On a hit, you may add 2 dice of ordinary impact damage to your elemental blast damage.

When you land a hit with an Arcane arm against a target, and one or more of your bonus dice to damage burst, you inflict a condition matching your arm’s element on the target. This condition lasts for 1 turn for each of your soul bonus dice, or until Resisted.
Fire. The target suffers burning (2).
Lighting. The target is stunned.
Ice. The target suffers freeze.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.
Once per turn, when you make 2 consecutive Arcane Arms basic attack actions on your turn with the same focus, you may make an additional basic Arcane Arms attack with that focus.

You have mastered the finer control of Arcane Arms. You may compress and arcane arm’s elemental force into a long-range lance. Using Arcane Lance allows you to attack in an additional extreme range category equal to 2x the Arcane Arm’s long range.
Arcane Lance carries a 2 Challenge penalty to the attack roll. However, the attack adds +2 weapon dice of damage.

As an action on your turn you may use your Arcane Arm as a focus to gather environmental mana. Roll yrou weapon damage dice – you collect this much Mana. You must spend this Mana before the end of your turn, and you cannot use this energy to replenish your own internal reserves or charge items designed to hold mana.
When you make attacks and action rolls with Arcane Arms, you add +1 Bonus Dice, for a total of +3. You are skilled enough to learn Master Arcane Arms techniques and Master Arcane Arms spells.

When you hit a creature with an Arcane Arms attack or spell, your next Arcane Arms attack of the same element against that same creature before the end of your turn gains Threat 4. This is cumulative for each successful Arcane Arms hit you make against that target during the same turn. If the target is vulnerable to the Arcane Arm’s element, increase this to Threat 6.

Once per turn, when you make an Arcane Arms attack against a target and hit, you may force each target adjacent to your first target to make a Defense reaction or take weapon damage (but not Bonus Dice) as well.
You are called upon to make Ranged Weapons check when attempting to hit a target with a thrown or projectile weapon. You may add your Agility bonus dice, if any, to this roll.

Special: Range Damage. You do not automatically add your Strength Bonus dice to damage from ranged weapons. You require a talent to add this bonus damage. Firearms are unique in that you will add your skill bonus to damage if you are proficient, as you do not add any ability scores bonus dice to firearm damage.
You can take the following chances with Ranged Weapons.
Damage. Add 1 Charge to your Damage Bonus Dice.
Piercing Shot. Aim for the gaps in armor, ignoring armor with a lower quality than your weapon.
Ricochet Shot. Bounce the Projectile off a surface to hit, adding one Challenge to the opponent’s Defense reaction, if taken.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to Range Weapon attacks and action rolls. You may use Basic Ranged Weapon techniques and learn Novice Ranged Weapon Techniques.
When attacking with a pull bow or similar weapon, you may add your Strength Bonus Dice to damage.
You have increased balance and accuracy. Increase your Agility Score by 1d6.
When attacking with a firearm, crossbow, or similar mechanical ranged weapon you add your skill bonus dice to damage. You may take advantage of mechanical weapons and firearm features, such as Burst.
When you make an attack with a thrown weapon, you may add your Strength Bonus Dice to your damage.

While using a bow or similar pulled weapon you may take a Chance to fire two of the same arrows at a single target within normal range. If you hit, you deal double weapon damage and expend 2 rounds of ammunition.
When you make attacks and Actions rolls with ranged weapons you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +2). You may learn Expert Ranged Weapon techniques.

Armed with a firearm, crossbow, or similar mechanical weapon, you may dedicate your entire turn to aiming precisely. On your next turn, your first attack action with the rifle receives +2 bonus dice to the attack roll.

When you throw a weapon with the Slight feature you may take a Chance to hurl two projectiles at a single target within normal range. If you hit, you deal double weapon damage and expend 2 rounds of ammunition.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.
When you make 2 consecutive attack actions or ranged weapon techniques with a bow or similar drawn weapon, you can make an additional free basic ranged attack action with your weapon.

While using a bow or similar pulled weapon you may take a Chance to fire three of the same arrows at a single target within normal range. If you hit, you deal three time weapon damage and expend 3 rounds of ammunition.

Armed with a firearm, crossbow, or similar mechanical weapon, you can load or fire 2 rounds in quick succession, counting as one attack roll. If you hit, you deal double weapon damage and expend 2 rounds of ammunition. By combining Double Tap with a Burst weapon you can unleash a spray of bullets.
Armed with a firearm, crossbow, or similar mechanical weapon, you reduce the action cost to reload your weapon by 1 action, to a minimum of 0 actions.
You can hurl weapons with your off-hand. You may make an off-hand Throw attack on your turn.

While using a bow or similar pulled weapon you may fire your weapon up at a parabolic arc, allowing you to fire projectiles much further. Using an Arc Shot you can fire in an additional extreme range category that is equal to 2x your weapon’s long range. Arc Shots carry a hefty 2 Challenge penalty to your attack roll, however, your weapon’s damage Bonus Dice receive 1 Charge. Ordinary quality arrows and bolts used in an arc shot are destroyed.

While using a bow or similar pulled weapon you may place your shots directly through each other. Each attack with your bow that hits and deals damage on your turn grants your next arrow a threat of 4, cumulatively. This bonus ends at the end of your turn.
When you make attacks and Actions rolls with ranged weapons you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +3). You are skilled enough to learn Master Ranged Weapon Techniques.

When you throw a weapon with the Slight feature you may take a Chance to hurl two arrows at a single target within normal range. If you hit, you deal three-times weapon damage and expend 3 rounds of ammunition.
When using a 1-handed firearm or similar mechanical ranged weapon you may add your skill bonus dice to your initiative.

When you use Snipe, you can target enemies in a third extreme range category that is equal to 2x your weapon’s long range. This attack carries a hefty 2 Challenge penalty to your attack roll, however, your enemy cannot use Defense Reactions against the attack.

When attacking with large weapons that rely on power and mass, such as two-handed axes and hammers, you will make a Heavy Weapons check. Heavy Weapons skills include Crushing Weapons, Great Blades, and Pole Arms.
You can take the following Chances with Heavy Weapons action rolls:
Devastating Strike. Add 1 Charge to your Damage Bonus Dice.
Armor Break. Ignore armor of a lower quality than your weapon.
Sweeping Attack. Hit an additional target adjacent to the primary target, who gains 1 Charge to defense.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Heavy Weapon attack and action rolls. You may use any basic techniques with Heavy Weapons, and you may learn Novice heavy-weapon techniques.
Your sturdy stance lets you swing without end. Increase your Strength Score by 1d6.
When attacking with a two-handed axe (or any cleaving blade) that has the Impact or Sweep feature, you may take advantage of those weapon features.
When attacking with a two-handed mace, club, or hammer that has the Impact or Crushing feature, you may take advantage of those weapon features.
When attacking with a spear, glaive, halberd, or similar polearm that has the Reach feature, you may take advantage of its features.
When wielding any fashion of two-handed sword or similar weapon you may take advantage of its features such as Sweep or Deft.
While wielding a two-handed sword or similar blade, you may make attack actions while wielding the blade one-handed
When you wield a heavy axe or other cleaving blade with the sweep feature you may take a chance when you attack to strike a second adjacent enemy with your sweep.

When you hit with a two-handed blunt weapon, you may take a Chance to knock the target Back 10 feet; if the weapon has push you knock them back an additional 10 feet. If they collide with terrain or another creature, they fall prone.
When you make Heavy Weapon attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +2).
You can take a Chance while attacking with a polearm or staff to add an additional 5-foot Reach to your attack to attack at distance.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.
You can cleave through multiple enemies. You can make an additional Sweep attack with a great sword on your turn.

When you make an attack action or weapon technique with a greatsword or similar weapon with the Sweep or Impact feature, you may choose to for-go that feature to grant your sword Threat (6) for the attack.
Crush and rend all those before you. The impact damage from your two-handed axe weapons ignores Armor Rank.
When you use Crushing Swing, you may take a second Chance to slam an additional adjacent enemy, dealing weapon damage and knocking them back 5 feet.

When you make an off-hand attack with the reverse weapon of a double weapon such as a staff or dual-bladed spear, the cost is only 5 Vigor, and you only suffer 1 Challenge to your attack and damage rolls.

When you strike with a pole-arm in the reach range you can add additional force to your blow thanks to the leverage. Regardless of the other features of the weapon the attack gains an additional Threat (6).
Wade through enemies like the crashing tide. When employing a two-handed sword with the sweep feature, there is no limit to the number of uses of that feature per turn.

Cut through all opposition. Armed with a two-handed sword or similar weapon, you may forgo all other weapon feature effects to deal damage equal to twice your Strength Bonus Dice as damage directly to Health or Integrity.

Shatter the earth whith every swing. When you miss on a weapon attack with a blunt two-handed weapon and miss or are defended against, you deal the number of your Strength Bonus Dice damage to all enemies adjacent to your target.
When you use Crushing Swing, you may spend two Chances to deal triple weapon damage instead of double.
When you make Heavy Weapon attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +3).
You have mastered the lethal grace of double weapons. When you make an off-hand attack with a double weapons reverse side you suffer no Challenges..
When you make an attack with a reach weapon and your bonus dice to damage burst, you can instead deal damage from the burst dice directly to Health or Integrity.

You make a Light Weapons check when you attack with a one-handed or hand-and-a-half weapon. Light Weapons add Strength Bonus Dice to damage. To take advantage of Weapon Features, you mush be skilled in a weapon’s use.
You can take the following Chances with Light Weapons action rolls:
Precision Strike. Add 1 Charge to your Damage Bonus Dice.
Armor Piercer. Ignore armor of a lower quality than your weapon.
Quick Draw. You unsheathe and strike with your weapon in one fluid motion.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Light Weapon attack and action rolls. You May use any basic techniques with Light weapons, and you may learn Novice light weapon techniques.
Your stance and footwork are impeccable. Increase your Agility Score by 1d6.
When attacking with a one-handed blade or swordlike slashing weapon with the Wieldy, Swift, or Deft Feature, you may take advantage of those weapon features.
When attacking with a knife or similar finesse weapon with the Threat, Swift, or Deft Feature, you may take advantage of those weapon features.
When attacking with a one-handed axe, mace, club, or hammer you may take advantage of Features such as Impact, Deft, and Wieldy.
While wielding a weapon in your off-hand with the Slight or Defensive Feature you may take advantage of that feature.
When you make an attack or use a combat technique with a sword, as part of the attack action your can reposition 5ft towards or adjacent to your target.
When you successfully make a knife attack or combat technique in melee, your next knife attack on that turn gains Threat (3). You may use this ability once per turn.
When you hit with a striking weapon, you may take a Chance to instead deal double weapon damage.
When you make Light Weapon attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +2) and you may learn expert light weapon techniques.
You have practiced balanced combat. When you make an Off-hand attack with a light weapon in your off-hand, you only suffer 1 Challenge to your Attack and Damage Bonus Dice.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.
Immediately after a successful basic attack with a sword or similar weapon you may make a follow up slash. On a hit the attack only deals weapon damage (no bonus dice).
Your Strikes flow with perfect precision. When you take a Chance to increase your damage with a sword strike, your attack takes on the Impact feature.

Following a successful attack action with a knife, you can use an action and drop an opponent to the ground, knocking them prone unless they can succeed a Resistance (Strength) VS your Agility Score. You must have an action to use this maneuver.
Obliterate your foes without mercy. When you hit with a striking weapon and deal damage, the opponent is Dazed for 1 turn.
When wielding a weapon with the Slight feature in your off-hand, the Vigor cost to make an Off-hand attack with this weapon is reduced by 3.
When you attempt a Defense reaction with your off-hand weapon or your off-hand shield, you reduce the Vigor cost by 1 to a minimum of 1.
Your blade dances with deadly precision. After any Double Slash, you make make an immediate follow-up slash on the same target. On a hit the attack only deals weapon damage (no bonus dice).

Your strikes cut through all defenses. When you make a sword attack or combat technique against a target and deal damage, each burst bonus dice to damage rolled, if any, is dealt directly to Health or Integrity. For example, if you have a bonus dice burst, and the burst bonus dice (the second dice you roll after a bonus dice rolls the maximum number) rolls a 4, you will deal 4 damage directly to Health.
When you make a knife attack against a melee target you may take two chances to strike at the opponent’s vitals, dealing damage directly to Health or Integrity.
When you successfully hit with a striking weapon attack or combat technique that has Threat or Impact, you instead deal that damage directly to Health or Integrity.
When you make Light Weapon attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +3) and you may learn master light weapon techniques.

When you take an opening with your main hand weapon, if you are wielding a weapon with the Swift feature in your off-hand, you may consume 8 Vigor to and use that Swift weapon to make a follow up attack with your off-hand weapon, essentially taking the opening with both weapons.
When you make an Off-hand attack with a light weapon that does not have the Wieldy feature in your off-hand, you do not suffer any Challenges to your attack and damage Bonus Dice.
Punching, kicking, and brawling, you will make an Hand-to-Hand check to attack with fists, feet, claws, and teeth. Hand-to-Hand skills include Hand-to-Hand and some martial arts abilities.
You can take the following Chances with Hand-to-Hand action rolls:
Crushing Blow. Add 1 Charge to your Damage Bonus Dice.
Stunning Strike. Rather that deal heath damage to a wounded opponent, you knock them unconscious.
Subtle Strike. You do not telegraph your strike, and attempts to defend against it are made with 1 Challenge.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Hand-to-Hand attack and action rolls. You may use any basic techniques with Hand-to-Hand strikes and learn Novice Hand-to-Hand techniques.
Rigorous training and hardened your body and expanded your endurance. Increase your Stamina Score by 1d6.
While wearing knuckle weapons or fists, you may take advantage of features such as slight or swift.
While fighting unarmed, off-hands attacks made with your hands and feet only suffer 1 Challenge to hit and damage.
Your attacks carry overwhelming power. Your Hand-to-Hand attacks take on the Impact characteristic.
You strike with fluid furry and grace. While unarmed, the Vigor cost to make Hand-to-Hand Off-Hand attacks is reduced by 3.
When you make Hand-to-Hand attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +2). You may learn Expert Hand-to-Hand techniques.
You gain 1 additional action on your turn.

Your strikes flow like a deadly rhythm. When you make a Hand-to-Hand attack on a target that is wearing no or light armor, when you take a chance for damage, your double your base unarmed attack weapon damage.

Flow between Hand-to-Hand attacks with ease. While fighting completely unarmed, off-hand attacks made with your bare hands or feet are made with no Challenge penalties to Hand-to-Hand attack or damage bonus dice.

Your unarmed attacks are exceptionally forceful and able to transmit force deep into your target, breaking bricks and boards with ease. Your Hand-to-Hand attacks gain the Crushing feature, and will deal double damage to inanimate objects and armor.
When you Block an attack with fist weapons such as knuckles or gauntlets you add your weapon damage to the damage absorption.
When you make Hand-to-Hand attacks and action rolls, you add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +3). You are skilled enough to learn Master Hand-to-Hand Techniques.
Your fists and feet take on the Swift weapon feature. On your turn, if you take 2 consecutive Hand-to-Hand attacks or technique Actions, you may make an additional Hand-to-Hand attack Action.
When you deal damage with a Hand-to-Hand attack, you can sacrifice your bonus dice to damage to deal 3 points of damage directly to health or integrity for each bonus dice sacrificed.
Your unarmed attacks are extremely lethal. Increase your base Hand-to-Hand damage to Ordinary (1d8) rather than Poor (1d6)

Strike with unyielding force. When you make a Hand-to-Hand attack or technique with a hand-to-hand weapon and deal damage, you push the target 5 feet away from you if they are Medium or smaller. Larger targets are immune but take Double weapon damage.

When you attempt to evade an attack, you will be called upon to make a Defense check. Unlike most skills, because you use these skills as reactions, they usually consume Vigor. Normally, you add your Speed bonus dice to reactions using Defense.

Special: Defense Reactions. You can take a defense reaction when you are subject to a weapon or weapon-like ability attack you are aware of in hopes of evading that attack. There are several Defense Techniques such as Dodge, Deflect, and Block. Basic Defense reactions consume 5 Vigor, where as advanced defense techniques consume more. You cannot use Defense to avoid an attack you are not aware of.
You cannot take Chances on reactions.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Defense Reactions, used to evade attacks. You may use any basic Defense techniques such as Deflect and Block.

While wearing medium or heavy armor, the Burden penalty to Vigor when taking Defense reactions is reduced by 3 points. This allows for more efficient defense techniques such as Dodges, Deflects, and Blocks without exhausting your stamina reserve.
When you are using a shield or while wielding any weapon, you increase your Guard by +5.
When unarmed, you increase your Guard by +5.
You’re an expert of defense. Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Defense Reactions (for a total of +2).
When you defend with a shield or weapon as a Deflect Reaction, you may add your equipment bonus to your Defense roll, if any.

When you use a Block Reaction, you may reduce incoming damage by an additional amount equal to 3 time the number of your Defense Bonus Dice. For example if you had +2 Bonus Dice to defense from Expert Defense, you would mitigate an additional 6 points of damage when you block.
When you Defend against a melee attack, you may immediately make a free Off-hand unarmed or weapon strike at 2 Challenge against that attacker.
While wielding a shield you gain partial cover against ranged attacks.
While wearing medium or heavy armor, your Armor Rating increases by +3 for all incoming attacks.
When you would take fall or explosion damage, you may use a Defense Reaction. On success, you halve the damage.
After you succeed on any Dodge Reaction, you may move up to 10 feet as part of that reaction.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Defense Reactions (for a total of +3).
When you use a Shield to defend against an attack you are immune to the effects of Impact or other damage that would otherwise bypass your shield.

You are well practiced in moving in armor. You do not suffer a Challenge on Athletic checks while wearing medium and heavy armor. Your armor’s quality rank counts as one rank higher when calculating Armor Rating and damage immunities.
When you, you become immune to forced movement (pushes, pulls, knocks) until the start of your next turn.
When you fail on a Defense Reaction, you are immune to being knocked prone or restrained by that attack.

Overcome and endure with Resistance, your shield against physical, mental, and spiritual assaults. When you are subjected to a mental, magical, or physical assault that cannot be evades, you may use a Reaction to make a resistance check to minimize or overcome the attack entirely.

Special: Resistance Reactions. You may make a Resistance reaction whenever you are subject to an assault from a non-weapons effect unless stated otherwise. There are several Resistance Techniques such as Endure, Overcome, and Counter Spell. Attempting to Resist consumes 5 Mana.
You cannot take Chances on reactions. In the case you may use Resistance as an action you can take the following chances:
Immunity. You cannot be affected by the same condition you over came for 1 turn.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Resistance reactions made to overcome or evade assaults. You may use any basic Resistance techniques such as Endure and Overcome.

While wearing medium or heavy armor, the Conduction penalty (the interference effect that increases Mana cost or prevents spellcasting while armored) is reduced by 3 points when making Resistance reactions or casting spells.
Your mind steels itself against intrusion. Increase your Resolve Score by +4.
Your body endures toxins and pain. Increase your Stamina Score by +4.
When you make any Resistance Roll, add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +2).
Even when you fail a mental Resistance Check you are not completely overcome, and the duration of mental assaults is reduced by half.

The more you are burdened the more you are resolved to push through. While under the effects of Burning, Freeze, Bleeding, Dazed, Dazzled, Poisoned, Stunned, Silenced, or Confused, your Ward is increased by +10.

When you fail a Resistance check against poison, disease, or environmental damage, reduce incoming damage by an additional amount equal to 3 time the number of your Resistance Bonus Dice. For example if you had +2 Bonus Dice to Resistance from Expert Resistance, you would mitigate up to 6 points of damage.
When the situation becomes dire, you have the will to survive against all odds. While you are Wounded, you increase your Ward by +10.

You have the fortitude to resist even death. When you are reduced to 0 health, you may make a resistance reaction VS 45. On a success, you remain conscious with 1 Health. You may use this ability once, and recover use after a full rest.
Your psyche is a citadel. After successfully resisting a mental assault, you gain temporary immunity to Dazed, Dazzled, and Enthralled until your next turn.

Your flesh hardens under duress. After successfully resisting a physical assault, you gain temporary Armor Rank equal to twice your Resistance Bonus Dice against the next physical attack before your next turn.
When you make any Resistance Roll, add +1 Bonus Dice (for a total of +3). You may learn Master Resistance techniques.
Your focus and mind have become an unassailable bastion. You are immune to any mental assault if the Resistance VS is less than your Resolve Score.

While you are afflicted with an ongoing poison or toxin, as an action you may attempt to make a Resistance (physical) check with three Chances. On a success, you consume 10 Mana and metabolize the poison, ending the effect. Furthermore, you recover 1d8 Health and 2D6 Durability.

Understanding the natural world — its geometry, its mechanics, its celestial movements, and its hidden structures — calls for a Natural Philosophy check. This encompasses the pre-scientific disciplines of mathematics, astronomy, engineering, metallurgical theory, siege craft, and the empirical investigation of how the world works beneath the surface of superstition and tradition. Where the Occult studies magic and the Studies skill covers history and law, Natural Philosophy is the domain of the person who asks why — why the stars move in their courses, why certain alloys hold an edge, why a bridge stands or falls, why the mana flows stronger at certain times of year.

Natural Philosophy is the academic tradition of the Iron Citadel's secular wing, the mathematical legacy of the Vaelenic scholars, and the engineering genius of a dozen cultures that have built ships, forged steel, and charted the heavens without ever calling it "science." In Aerd, there is no distinction between natural philosophy and the investigation of magical phenomena — the mana flow is as much a subject of empirical study as the tides, and the scholar who maps Dragon Wells uses the same mathematical tools as the one who maps coastlines.
You can take the following Chances with Natural Philosophy action rolls:
Thorough Restoration. Increase the quality of durability repair dice by 1 Rank.
Refined Design. When repairing or restoring an item, you may improve it by 1 quality rank.
Efficient Method. Decrease the time required for engineering, construction, or analytical tasks by 20%.

Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Natural Philosophy action rolls — mathematics, astronomical observation, engineering assessments, structural analysis, and empirical investigation. You may use any scholarly formulae and learn Novice Formulae.

The Novice Philosopher has completed a formal or informal education in the fundamentals: geometry, arithmetic, basic astronomy, and the principles of material construction. They can calculate load-bearing limits, chart a course by the stars, identify the composition of an alloy by its color and grain, and recognize the signs of structural failure before the roof comes down. In a world where most people rely on intuition and tradition, the Novice Philosopher relies on measurement, observation, and the stubborn conviction that the world operates according to rules that can be understood.

You document every observation, measurement, and anomaly with scholarly rigor. Once you have attempted a Natural Philosophy action in a given scenario, you gain +1 BD on any subsequent related Natural Philosophy checks within the same scene, environment, or subject (at GM discretion). Your notes are precise, your diagrams are accurate, and each failed attempt teaches you something that makes the next attempt more likely to succeed.

Additionally, when performing a Natural Philosophy action with ample time (such as a full day or during Downtime), you may take 1 Chance without increasing the VS, representing a deliberate, methodical application of prior findings and iterative refinement. The philosopher does not rush. The philosopher measures twice, considers thrice, and cuts once.

Your understanding of how things are made gives you the ability to put them back together. When you make a Natural Philosophy check to repair or maintain equipment — armour, weapons, vehicles, or structures — you restore twice the Durability. You understand not merely what broke, but why it broke, and your repairs address the underlying weakness rather than merely patching the surface.

This applies to any physical object whose construction you can examine and understand: a cracked sword, a shattered wheel, a collapsed section of wall. You are not a smith or a carpenter — you are the person who tells the smith and the carpenter what went wrong and how to fix it properly.

You see new uses in old materials. Where others see wreckage, you see components. So long as you have access to parts — broken equipment, ruins, salvaged debris — you can make use of them to affect repairs or construction. You can substitute salvaged materials for proper components with only 1 rank reduction in quality. You can substitute proper components for salvaged materials of the same quality. You can disassemble complex constructions (siege equipment, runic mechanisms, collapsed structures) into 1d4 usable components and 1 core material of the same quality as the original.

The Salvager does not need a perfectly stocked workshop. They need a ruin, a pile of debris, and twenty minutes of careful examination. The world is full of things that were built by people who are now dead, and those things still have value if you know how to take them apart.

You maintain a compact collection of instruments and reference materials — an astrolabe, a set of callipers, a plumb line, reference tables for celestial movements, and whatever else you have accumulated through years of fieldwork. You know how to improvise a full analytical workspace from sparse equipment, allowing you to conduct advanced investigation and experimentation outside of a proper scriptorium or workshop.

While using your Field Study equipment, you gain +1 BD on Natural Philosophy checks made to investigate materials, analyses of structures, survey terrain, or calculate astronomical positions. The Field Study is the portable version of the Iron Citadel's great library — smaller, cruder, but functional in the places where scholars are most needed and proper facilities are furthest away.
Your expertise deepens. Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Natural Philosophy rolls (for a total of +2). You may learn Expert Formulae.

The Expert Philosopher has moved beyond the fundamentals into genuine mastery. They can calculate orbital mechanics, design load-bearing structures, analyze the mana-flow properties of geological formations, and identify materials with a glance and a scratch test. Their reputation opens doors — Jarls consult them on fortification design, Magisters defer to them on matters of mathematical theory, and the Iron Citadel considers them a peer.

You carry a carefully assembled collection of specialized tools: precision callipers, a surveyor's chain, astronomical charts, a magnifying lens of ground crystal, a set of standardized weights, and reference materials specific to your area of expertise. When you use your Scholar's Instruments for a Natural Philosophy check involving construction, repair, analysis, or measurement, you gain +1 BD.

The instruments represent years of careful acquisition — each tool selected for a specific purpose, maintained with the obsessive care of a craftsman, and irreplaceable outside of a major city. Losing your instruments is not merely an inconvenience. It is a professional catastrophe.

You have developed a mind for the deep structures of the world — the patterns beneath the patterns, the mathematics that governs not just numbers but the movement of stars, the flow of rivers, and the resonance of mana through stone. Your thinking has become so precise and so accustomed to complexity that you process information faster and retain it more completely than anyone without your training. Increase both your Reason and Memory Ability Scores by 1d6.
You specialize in one of the great disciplines of natural philosophy and have the knowledge and skill to turn theoretical understanding into practical results. Choose one of the following fields:

Metallurgy and Material Theory. You understand the deep properties of metals, alloys, composites, and exotic materials at a level that transcends simple smithing. Compounds, alloys, and treated materials you prepare or oversee are always +1 rank higher quality. You can identify any metal, alloy, or special material (including Orichalcum, Moonsilver, Dragon Stone, and Adamantine) by sight and touch, and you know the precise conditions required to work each one.

Mechanics and Siege Craft. You understand force, leverage, trajectory, and structural stress with mathematical precision. When solving engineering or force-related problems — calculating the trajectory of a siege weapon, designing a bridge, assessing whether a wall can be breached — you receive 1 free Charge on your Natural Philosophy check, provided you have time to run the numbers. Your siege weapons, fortifications, and mechanical constructions are built to specification, not to guess.

Anatomy and Physiology. Your understanding of living systems — the structures of bone and muscle, the flow of blood, the mechanics of injury and healing — bridges the gap between Natural Philosophy and Medicine. You receive 1 free Charge on Medicine checks, so long as you have proper instruments or reference materials. You understand why a treatment works, not merely that it does.

Alchemical Theory. You have studied the philosophical foundations of alchemy — not merely the recipes, but the principles of transmutation, distillation, and purification that underpin the art. Alchemical preparations you create or oversee are +1 rank higher quality. You can identify unknown substances, predict the results of novel combinations, and detect adulterated or poisoned materials with a Natural Philosophy check.

In addition, whenever you succeed on a Natural Philosophy check involving your chosen field, you may produce a Treatise — a written summary of your findings and methods. This Treatise can be shared with others or referenced later to grant +1 BD on a single related action roll. A Treatise represents the philosopher's greatest contribution: knowledge that persists beyond the moment and serves anyone with the wit to read it.

You are a master of celestial mathematics — the computation of orbital mechanics, the prediction of eclipses, the mapping of mana flow through ley lines, and the calculation of auspicious or inauspicious alignments. Charts, calculations, star maps, and almanacs you produce are +1 rank higher quality. When performing a Natural Philosophy check involving celestial observation, mana-flow calculation, Dragon Well analysis, or temporal prediction, you receive 1 free Charge, provided you have access to your instruments and reference tables.

In addition, you may produce an Almanac Entry after succeeding on a relevant Natural Philosophy check. An Almanac Entry is a reusable reference — a calculated prediction, a mapped alignment, a charted mana current — that grants +1 BD on a related task to you or an ally. Only one Almanac Entry may be referenced per action. The stars do not change their courses for the convenience of mortals, but the philosopher who has charted those courses can tell you exactly when the mana will be strongest, when the tides will turn, and when the shadow will fall.

You specialise in the design, analysis, and construction of physical structures — buildings, bridges, fortifications, siege works, and ships. Structures you design are 1 rank higher quality by default. Structures, vehicles, and fortifications you repair regain double the Durability. You can identify structural weaknesses in any construction with a Natural Philosophy check VS 30, and you can estimate the force required to breach or collapse a structure with mathematical precision.

The Architectural Theorist is the person who designs the castle that withstands the siege, the bridge that carries the army, and the ship that survives the storm. They are also the person who looks at the enemy's castle and tells you exactly where to hit it.

You specialize in the physical properties of mana — its flow through materials, its interaction with Dithicite, its behavior in Dragon Wells, and the engineering principles that govern enchantment infrastructure. Mana-conductive materials you prepare are 1 rank higher quality by default. You may reroll any 1s you roll on Bonus Dice when repairing, analyzing, or interfacing with mana-conductive materials, enchanted mechanisms, or runic infrastructure.

The Mana Engineer bridges the gap between the Occult and the empirical. Where the Magus understands mana through intuition and the Wyrd through tradition, the Mana Engineer understands it through measurement, experiment, and the conviction that even magic obeys rules — you just have to figure out what they are.
Your mind is a wellspring of understanding. Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Natural Philosophy rolls (for a total of +3). You may learn Master Formulae.

The Master Philosopher is among the most learned individuals on the continent — a polymath whose understanding of the natural world, from celestial mechanics to metallurgical chemistry to mana-flow dynamics, places them in the company of legends. The Iron Citadel calls them Magister Philosophae. The Woed call them wise. The Valini call them dangerous, because a person who understands how the world works is a person who can change it.

You are a master of abstract systems — the mathematical structures that underlie everything from celestial mechanics to mana-flow to the architecture of enchantment. Your understanding operates at a level of abstraction that most scholars cannot reach, and many cannot even follow.

Up to once per full Rest, you may instantly resolve an analytical or engineering task (bypassing the need for a check) if the VS would be equal to your Reason Score or lower and you have access to your instruments and reference materials. The answer simply arrives — not through magic, but through a depth of understanding so thorough that the solution is obvious to you even when it would take a lesser mind hours of calculation.

You may maintain 2 active Almanac Entries at once. Both may be referenced in the same action if relevant, granting a combined +2 BD. The Grand Theorist does not merely chart the stars. They understand the mathematics that make the stars move, and that understanding gives them a predictive power that borders on prescience.

While attempting to create, analyze, or design with a Natural Philosophy check while using your Field Study, you can take 2 Chances to trigger a Eureka — a moment of sudden, crystalline insight that bypasses a normally insurmountable limitation. An unstable alloy can be stabilized. A structure that should not be possible can be built. A material that resists all conventional analysis yields its secrets to a flash of lateral thinking. The GM determines what specific limitation is bypassed, but a Eureka represents the philosopher's rarest and most valuable gift: the ability to see a solution that no one else can see because no one else was looking at the problem from the right angle.

You push materials and constructions past their designed tolerances, using raw knowledge and calculated recklessness. It takes at least 2d6 minutes of work and a Natural Philosophy check VS 40, and requires you to consume one core component. For the next 2 minutes, your modifications effectively increase the construction's quality by 1 rank — increasing speed, load capacity, damage output, range, or other factors depending on the object. A siege weapon fires farther. A ship sails faster. A weapon strikes harder. After the effect ends, the object takes 5d8 Durability damage from the strain. The philosopher knows exactly how far they can push. They also know exactly what will break.

You can deconstruct any physical, mechanical, or mana-conductive system you can examine. With 1d6 minutes of study and a Natural Philosophy check VS 40, you may spend 1 salvaged component to bypass a mechanism's intended function and issue a new command — open a sealed door, disable a runic trap, jam a siege engine, redirect a mana conduit, or collapse an enchantment's structural foundation. You may also trigger catastrophic failure in complex systems, causing a destructive cascade equal to the system's power source or structural capacity (GM discretion).

The difference between the Unraveller and the vandal is precision. The vandal breaks things. The Unraveller understands exactly how they work, and then breaks the one thing that makes everything else stop working. It is the most elegant form of destruction, and it is the philosopher's final argument: I understand what you built. And now I am going to take it apart.

Understanding systems, working with, designing, and repairing complex machines and computers calls for Science & Technology check. This encompasses general technical knowledge and repair, as well as sophisticated science and engineering. Orbal engineering falls under this skill.
You can take the following Chances with Science & Technology action rolls:
Durable Repair. Increase the quality of durability repair dice by 1 Rank.
Innovative Technology. Repairing a damaged piece of technology you can improve it by 1 quality rank.
Time Efficiency. Decrease the time required for engineering tasks by 20%.
Add +1 Bonus Dice to all Science & Technology action rolls (machines, experiments, or systems work). You may use any technical blueprints and learn Novice Blueprints.

You document every experiment, observation, and anomaly with scientific rigor. Once you've attempted a Science & Technology action in a given scenario, you gain +1 Bonus Die on any subsequent related Science & Technology checks within the same scene, environment, or subject (at GM discretion).

Additionally, when performing a Science & Technology action with ample time (such as a full day or during Downtime), you may take 1 Chance without increasing the VS, representing a deliberate application of research and prior findings.
Your hands know how to put things back together. When you make a Technology and Science check to repair or maintain your equipment, you can restore twice the Durability to damaged armor or vehicles.

You see new uses in old parts. So long as you have access to parts, you can make use of them to affect repairs or construction.
