You no longer dream of your own life. Every sleep is a negotiation chamber — quiet rooms, long tables, faces you do not quite recognize asking questions you do not quite remember answering. You wake unrested unless you sacrifice 1 Vigor and 1 Mana before lying down as informal tribute. Without the tribute, you suffer 1 Challenge on the first roll of the day.

The dead remember. They remember badly, often, and partially, and what they remember is rarely flattering — but they remember, and a Cold-Hand who knows how to listen can build a life out of what they say. The Path of Pale Grace is the path of the recently dead, the cooling bone, the funerary tradition turned sour. It is not the glory of liches or the conquest of necromancer-kings; it is grimmer, smaller, more domestic. You learn how to keep the things that should have stopped. You learn how to ask the dead questions, and how to make them work when they would rather lie still. The price is that you grow, slowly and unmistakably, to resemble what you work with.
Cold-Hand Initiate

You are no longer affected by the natural pathogens of corpses. You can handle the dead — newly fallen, long buried, anything between — without contamination, without nausea, without the involuntary reverence that grips most mortals in the presence of human remains. Your hands run cooler than they should. People notice when they shake them.

By placing your bare hands on a corpse and concentrating for one full minute, you can ascertain the cause of death, the approximate time of death, and one significant detail about the dead — a name once called, a face last seen, a wound that was not the killing blow. You sense the recently dead within 60 feet as a faint, directional cold.

You may animate one Skeletal Servant — a small, mute, lightly armored construct of bone — by spending 30 minutes and 20 Mana over an intact corpse or assembled skeleton. The servant is Small, has 30 Durability, Armor Rank 2, deals 1d8 damage on its attacks, and persists for 1 day per Soul BD. You may have one such servant at a time.
Gain an initial Corruption Score of 2d6.
Grave-Sense

You can see the resonances the dead leave behind. As a free action, with 5 Mana, your vision sharpens for one minute to perceive the residues of death within 100 feet — bloodstains old and washed away, the cooling tracks of the recently dying, the faint silhouettes of where bodies once lay. You gain +2 BD to Awareness checks involving any matter touching death, burial, murder, or grave-disturbance. The dead within range will sometimes turn their faces, where you cannot quite see them, to look at you. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Bind the Bones

You may now animate a Greater Skeletal Servant — Medium, armored, capable of crude speech if you bind it from a recently dead intelligent corpse. The ritual takes 1 hour and 40 Mana, plus a suitable skeleton. The servant has 80 Durability, Armor Rank 3, attacks twice per round at 2d8 + Strength BD damage, and persists for 1 week. You may maintain a number of Greater Skeletal Servants equal to your Corruption Bonus Dice. Lesser Skeletal Servants do not count against this limit. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Necrotic Touch

Your bare hand has become a small weapon. As an Unarmed attack, on a hit, you deal 1d8 + Corruption BD Entropic damage and the target loses 1 point of Vigor in addition to ordinary damage. Undead, plant matter, and unliving constructs are unaffected. If you strike an undead creature with this attack instead of an enemy, the undead recovers Durability equal to the damage that would have been dealt. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Marshall the Dead

The corpse is a resource. You have learned to use it efficiently. You may now control a number of mindless undead equal to four times your Corruption Bonus Dice (instead of the prior single greater servant + corruption-BD limit). You may issue compound orders — "guard the gate, attack any creature without my mark, do not pursue beyond the bridge" — without occupying your Actions. Your skeletal servants gain +1 BD to attack rolls so long as they act within 60 feet of you. A Cold-Hand with this talent and an old battlefield is a logistical problem. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Death's Whisper

You can ask questions of the recently dead and they will answer, however poorly. With 10 minutes and 20 Mana spent over a corpse dead no more than 1 week (or 1 day for those who died violently), you may ask the dead up to three questions. Responses are short, often confused, sometimes deceptive — the dead are not always wiser than they were in life — but they cannot lie about facts they directly perceived. The corpse twitches as it answers. Witnesses generally do not return. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Embalmer's Stillness

You have learned the long, slow trick of partial death. As an Action and 15 Mana, you enter a state of preservation: your heart slows nearly to stopping, your skin cools, your wounds close at half-rate but do not bleed. While in Embalmer's Stillness, you do not need to breathe or eat, you are immune to poison and disease, and undead and other corpse-feeders treat you as one of their own and ignore you. You take 1 Challenge on all Social and Empathy checks while in this state because you are, frankly, unsettling. Maintained at 5 Mana per minute. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Corpse Wreath

As an Action and 30 Mana, every corpse, skeleton, or partial set of remains within 30 feet that is not already animated rises to defend you. These hasty risen are mindless and weak (1d4 damage, 10 Durability, single Action), but there may be many of them, and they last for the duration of the encounter or until destroyed. The talent is most effective in graveyards, battlefields, charnel houses, and the aftermath of plague. A Cold-Hand walking through a recent disaster is a fortified position waiting to happen. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Lichlike Vigil

Your body has slowed. You age perhaps a third as fast as you should. You no longer require sleep — you require vigil, four hours of motionless awareness in which you are functionally inert, and from which you arise fully restored. You are immune to natural diseases and the fatigue of sleeplessness. You may now activate Embalmer's Stillness as a free action. You no longer warm with exertion, blush with embarrassment, or sweat. The mirror, when you look in it, increasingly is not flattering. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Knell-Voice

You can pronounce the death of those already nearly gone. As an Action and 40 Mana, target a creature you can see within 30 feet that is currently at less than half its maximum Health. The target must Resist (Stamina) VS your Corruption Score; on a failure, the creature's Health is immediately reduced to 0 and they enter Shock as if mortally wounded. The Knell-Voice does not bypass Heroic, Epic, or higher Durability — it is a finishing tool, not a battle-opener. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Reaping Hand

You have learned to strike with death's authority. Your Necrotic Touch damage increases to 3d8 + Corruption BD. On a critical hit, the target also suffers the Decaying condition for 1d4 rounds, losing 1d6 Durability at the start of each turn as their flesh breaks down in real time. Healing magic targeting a Decaying creature is halved. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Pale Sovereign

You are the lord and authority of the local dead. Your undead servants gain +2 BD to attack and damage rolls. Mindless undead within 100 feet that you did not raise treat you as their commander on a failed Resistance (Corruption) check VS your Corruption Score, and will not attack you unless directly opposed by a stronger controller. You may now raise a number of Greater Skeletal Servants equal to four times your Corruption Bonus Dice, and they need not be skeletal — once per day, with 1 hour of preparation and 80 Mana, you may raise a Corpse Champion (a Servant with stats appropriate to its life, retaining its martial techniques but not its memory or loyalty). Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
The Long Stillness

You become functionally undead while remaining alive. Your creature type changes to Eldritch (Undying). You no longer age. You are immune to natural diseases, poisons, and the effects of suffocation, starvation, or thirst. You suffer no damage from environmental cold. You still recover Health and Durability as a living creature, but Healing magic functions at half effect — your body is no longer entirely interested in being repaired. You may not be detected as undead by spells or talents that find the dead, because you are not dead. You are merely slow. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Soul Tether

You may bind a single creature's soul into a vessel of your making — a reliquary, a finger-bone, a sealed urn — at the moment of its death. With 1 hour of preparation, 100 Mana, and the dying creature within reach, the soul is bound. While the vessel remains intact, the soul cannot pass on, cannot be raised normally, and cannot be reborn. You may consult the bound soul as per Death's Whisper without time limit, and you may temporarily release it into a prepared body (your own undead, a fresh corpse, briefly your own flesh) to interrogate or use its skills. The vessel can hold one soul at a time. Destroying the vessel releases the soul, which may not respond gratefully. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Funereal Ascension

This is the threshold. You shed the last of your warmth and become what you have been building toward — not a lich, not a ghoul, but something the village priests have no word for. Your skin is the color of paper. Your eyes do not need to blink. You no longer require any sustenance of any kind. Your Health maximum is reduced by half, but you gain Permanent Durability equal to twice your former Health maximum, and your Durability now regenerates at 1d10 per round during the Recovery phase of combat. You are immune to critical hits. You can never be returned to ordinary mortal life by any means short of divine intervention.
When you Funereally Ascend, choose: do you tell the people who knew you? Most do not. It is easier, after. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d20.
Cold to the Touch

Your skin is several degrees cooler than it should be. People flinch when they shake your hand. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social checks involving physical contact (greetings, courtship, comforting). Each subsequent time you develop this malediction, the cold deepens — at the third level, you visibly fog the air with your exhalation even in summer, and the Challenge applies to all Social checks.
The Long Quiet

You speak less. You lose the habit of small talk, then conversation, then sometimes language itself for hours at a time. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social (Persuasion) and Entertain checks. The thoughts are still there. They simply no longer ask to be said.
Carrion Mark

Crows, ravens, vultures, rats, and other carrion creatures recognize you and follow at a respectful distance. They are not hostile, but they are present, and they are obvious. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks in any outdoor environment where such creatures can reach you.
The Reanimator's Burden

Once you raise the dead, you remember them. The faces of every corpse you have animated accumulate at the edge of your perception, watching, waiting, sometimes commenting. The presence is not hostile but it is dense. You suffer 1 Challenge on Resistance (Resolve) checks against fear, awe, and overwhelming sensory input.
Pale Bloom

Plants wither in your prolonged presence. After 3 days in any single location, all unprotected vegetation within 20 feet begins to die — flowers first, then grass, then the smaller plants. The effect reverses if you leave for a week, but the village memory of your visit does not.
Funereal Scent

You smell faintly of the embalmer's table — preservative, lavender, old wax, deeper notes nobody can identify. Dogs do not approach you. Horses shy. Babies cry in your arms. You suffer 1 Challenge on Beast Handling and on any Social check involving an environment of intimate trust.
The Stilled Heart

Your heart beats half as often as it should. Healers can detect this. Combat surgeons cannot save you with ordinary techniques because they cannot find a pulse to stabilize. Magical healing remains effective, but anyone attempting natural medicine on you suffers 2 Challenges.
Sleeping Among the Dead

You sleep best in graveyards, ossuaries, and similar places. Outside of such environments, you suffer 1 Challenge on the first roll of every day. The longer you go without resting near the dead, the worse the penalty — at one week away, the Challenge increases to 2, and so on.
The Embalmer's Eye

You cannot help seeing the inevitable death in any living thing you look at — the failing organ, the future wound, the way they will eventually fall. You suffer 1 Challenge on Empathy checks involving healthy living people because your detachment is visible. You gain +1 BD on Medicine checks involving prognosis or autopsy.
Mute the Pall

Sounds near you become slightly muffled, as though heard from inside a coffin. Allies within 10 feet of you suffer 1 Challenge on Awareness (Hearing) checks. The effect is constant and cannot be suppressed.

Some things you should not know about other people. There is a reason the gods designed the soul as a sealed vessel — a reason that the door between minds is heavy, and the lock is good, and the key is meant to be lost. The Path of Seven Eyes is the path of those who picked the lock. The Whispered-Voice begins with a small insight, a glimpse of what someone else is feeling, a sense of what they are about to say. They proceed, by easy steps, to inserting small convictions into other minds — nothing big at first, nothing the target would notice. By the end, the Whispered-Voice is no longer entirely sure which of their own thoughts are theirs, and which they planted in themselves a long time ago, and which were planted in them by someone walking the same path who had the courtesy not to mention it.
Whispered-Voice Initiate

You have opened the small door, and what you saw on the other side did not seem dangerous at the time. As a free action and 4 Mana, you can read the surface thoughts of one creature within 30 feet that you can see. You perceive their immediate emotional state, the gist of what they are about to say, and any single fact currently occupying their conscious mind. The target gets a Resistance (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score to refuse the read; on a success they are aware of the attempt, on a failure they are not.
You see through ordinary illusions with mild concentration — magical illusions still require Resistance, but mundane disguises, makeup, sleight-of-hand, and theatrical effects no longer fool you.

You may plant one small conviction in a willing or unaware target within 10 feet, once per day. The conviction must be plausible to the target and gentle in scope — "my purse feels lighter than it should," "I have had this thought before," "this person seems familiar." The target accepts it as their own. They will not remember it was placed.
Gain an initial Corruption Score of 2d6.
The Listening Eye

Your perception of others sharpens to an unsettling degree. You gain +2 BD on Awareness (Insight) checks and on Social checks against creatures you have observed for at least one minute. As a Reaction and 5 Mana, when an opponent within 30 feet declares an action, you may briefly look into their intent before resolution — you learn what they are attempting to accomplish (not the mechanical effect, but the goal), and may apply 1 Challenge to one roll involved in that action. Each use against the same target in a single encounter increases the Mana cost by 5. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Plant the Seed

The convictions you place can now germinate. As an Action and 15 Mana, plant a single specific belief in a target within 60 feet. The target receives a Resistance (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score; on a failure, the belief lodges and grows over the next 24 hours. The target will begin to find evidence for the belief, recall details supporting it, and eventually defend it as their own. The belief must be possible — "that merchant short-changed me," "I have always disliked the captain," "my brother knows more than he is saying" — but it need not be true. Magical detection of mind-altering effects can identify the seed only within the first hour of planting. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Borrowed Truth

You can briefly speak truths that you do not yourself know. As an Action and 10 Mana, while in conversation with a target who possesses information relevant to the matter at hand, you may make a confident assertion about something you have no rational way to know. The target must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score, or else react to your assertion as though it were a credible report from a trusted source. You can use this to draw out admissions, force lies into the open, and convince targets that you already know what you are asking them to confirm. The assertion you speak need not actually be true; what matters is the target's reaction to it. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
The Eye Behind the Eye

With 1 minute of concentration and 30 Mana, you may perceive through the senses of a target you have previously read with Whispered-Voice Initiate, regardless of distance, so long as they are on the same plane. You see what they see and hear what they hear for up to 1 hour. The target does not perceive your observation, but particularly perceptive or paranoid individuals may sense a faint and indescribable wrongness in the back of their mind. You cannot affect the target's actions through this connection; you can only watch and listen. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Drown the Voice

You can selectively remove information from a target's mind. As an Action and 25 Mana, choose one specific piece of memory the target possesses — a face, a fact, a single recent event, a name. The target may Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score; on a failure, the memory is gone, replaced by a soft blank that the target will not notice unless directly prompted. Drowned memories can be restored by powerful counter-magic or by a slow process of natural reconstruction taking weeks. You can drown the same memory in multiple targets, useful when a witness is also a friend. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Convince the Reasonable

The most elegant form of domination is the kind the target willingly walks into. As an Action and 30 Mana, with at least 1 minute of prior conversation with a target, you may make a single reasonable-seeming argument carried by the full weight of your insight into them. The target must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score with 2 Challenges. On a failure, they are convinced — not enthralled, not dominated, but genuinely convinced — that the argument is true and that the conclusion is their own. The conviction lasts until directly contradicted by overwhelming evidence. They will defend it as their own freely held position. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Webwork

You may maintain a network of subtle, persistent influences across multiple targets. You can hold a number of active Planted Seeds and Convinced Reasonables equal to twice your Corruption Bonus Dice. Once per scene, as a free action and 5 Mana, you may refresh the influences in this network within 100 miles — strengthening the convictions, deepening the planted beliefs, reminding the targets of their own conclusions. A Whispered-Voice with a developed Webwork is functionally untraceable; the conspiracy that defends them is composed entirely of people who do not know they are part of one. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Crown of Eyes

You can read a crowd. As an Action and 40 Mana, gain the surface thoughts, emotional state, and immediate intentions of every creature within 50 feet of you for 1 minute. You sense lies, recognize coordinated action by groups in your perception range, and identify which individuals in the crowd are armed, frightened, or hiding something specific. The effect cannot be hidden from creatures with mental sensitivity — corrupted creatures, spiritualists, and certain heroic classes will sense a faint humming pressure as you read them. Most people will not notice. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
The Convert

You can change someone's mind about something they would never have changed it about. With one hour of conversation, 50 Mana, and a Corruption check VS the target's Resolve Score + 20, you reshape one of the target's deeply held convictions. A devoted Templar can be made to question their faith. A loyal soldier can be made to despise their commander. A loving parent can be made to harbor doubt about their child. The change is genuine — the target's personality reorganizes around the new conviction, and they will defend the new position with the same earnestness they held the old. The Convert cannot be reused on the same target within a year. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Lash of Reason

You can weaponize insight as direct psychic damage. As an Action and 20 Mana, target a creature within 30 feet that you have previously read; the target must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score or take 4d10 + Corruption BD Umbral damage as you turn their own thoughts against them. On a failed Resistance, the target also suffers 1 Challenge to all rolls for 1d4 rounds as they grapple with the residue of the attack. A creature reduced to 0 Health by Lash of Reason does not bleed out — they stop, simply, in the manner of those who have been told something they cannot endure. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Seven-Eyed Sovereign

You are the master and maker of small influences. The maximum number of Planted Seeds and Convinced Reasonables in your Webwork doubles. Your Crown of Eyes range increases to 200 feet and lasts 10 minutes. You can now maintain three simultaneous Eye Behind the Eye observations. Once per session, you may orchestrate your network — declare that, over the course of the next week, your influenced contacts will independently arrive at a single specified outcome (a person ruined, a piece of information disseminated, a faction soured against another). The GM determines the precise narrative consequences, but the outcome happens. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
The Whisper That Walks

Your influence persists even when you do not. When you die or otherwise depart the world, your Webwork does not collapse. Your planted convictions and convinced reasonables remain active, refreshing themselves on the residue of your authority, gradually decaying over a span of years rather than the days other Whispered-Voices manage. Furthermore, while you live, you may transfer your active connections to an apprentice through a 1-hour ritual costing 100 Mana. Most Whispered-Voices do not do this. The ones who do never quite know what happens to the apprentices afterward. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Truth Beyond the Veil

You can see the actual structure of fate as it pertains to people. Once per day, as 30 minutes of concentration and 80 Mana, you may consider a person you have read with any Seven Eyes ability and learn one of the following: the most likely cause of their death, the secret they would most prefer not be revealed, the person they would betray any cause to protect, or the choice they will face within the next year that will define the rest of their life. The information is true at the moment you receive it. Truth Beyond the Veil reveals what is hidden, but it does not tell you what to do with the information, and the Whispered-Voice who learns too many such truths often discovers that wisdom did not follow. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
The Seventh Eye Opens

This is the threshold. The last barrier between your mind and the larger pattern dissolves. You can sense the thoughts of any creature you have ever directly perceived, anywhere on the same plane, with one round of concentration. Once per day, you may experience the full conscious life of a single chosen target for 1 round of real time, perceiving years or decades of their interior life in compressed form. The cost is steep: every time you use this final ability, you must succeed a Resistance (Resolve) VS 40 + 5 per prior use or permanently lose 1 point of Memory or Reasoning to the influx of borrowed perspective. You are no longer sure which of your memories are yours. You are no longer sure that the distinction means very much. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d20.
Borrowed Mannerism

You have begun to adopt the small gestures of those you have read. A turn of phrase here, a habit of touching the temple there. Anyone who knows multiple people you have observed deeply will notice the borrowings if they pay attention. You suffer 1 Challenge on Legerdemain (Disguise) checks because you are too many people at once.
The Constant Noise

Your perception of others' surface thoughts has begun to operate involuntarily. You hear the murmur of nearby minds at low intensity at all times, particularly in crowds. You suffer 1 Challenge on Studies and Awareness checks made in populated areas. Solitary environments are a relief, which others increasingly find unsettling.
Mirror-Doubt

You no longer trust your own convictions. When you express a strongly held belief, you cannot entirely rule out that you placed it in yourself, or that someone else placed it in you, or that it was once true and is no longer. You suffer 1 Challenge on the first Social (Persuasion) check of any given conversation.
The Telling Pupil

Your eyes have changed. The pupils are slightly larger than they should be, and they do not contract properly in bright light. You suffer 1 Challenge in extremely bright conditions and gain 1 Bonus Die on Awareness (Sight) checks in dim conditions. Onlookers tend to remember your gaze.
Sympathy's Cost

When you read a creature's thoughts, you involuntarily share their immediate physical sensations for the duration of the read. Their hunger, their pain, the cold of their feet. If they are in significant distress at the time, you must succeed a Resistance (Resolve) VS the strength of the sensation or be Dazed for 1 round.
The Echoes Return

The seeds you have planted and the convictions you have placed occasionally surface in your own mind, recombined. You will sometimes catch yourself believing or remembering things that were originally meant for others. The GM may, at moments of stress, present you with a memory or belief that is not actually yours and require you to determine if it is — you have no reliable way to tell.
Quiet Company

Insects and small unobserved creatures react to you oddly. Spiders pause in their webs when you pass. Mice in walls stop scratching. The effect is faint but consistent. Sensitive observers find it deeply unsettling. You suffer 1 Challenge on Beast Handling and on Social (First Impressions) checks in any environment with significant ambient fauna.
The Unforgotten Face

Anyone whose mind you have ever directly read is, on some level, aware of you for the rest of their life. They will not necessarily remember why they distrust the smell of your perfume or why a particular cadence of speech makes them anxious. But they will know you when they see you again, even if they cannot say how. Long-term targets become slowly impossible to deceive a second time.
Plurality of Voice

When you speak at length, your voice begins to drift in cadence and accent — sometimes settling, briefly, into the rhythms of people you have read. The drift is subtle but noticeable to careful listeners. You suffer 1 Challenge on Entertain and on Social checks in any setting where verbal authenticity matters (court, religious service, formal testimony).
The Final Insight

Once per session, at the GM's discretion, you may involuntarily perceive a brief and devastatingly accurate truth about someone in your immediate company — the secret they are most ashamed of, the betrayal they are planning, the lie they have just told. You may not act on the information in the moment without giving away that you know. The information cannot be unlearned.

Flame is the wrong word and the right one. The Devouring-Flame does not burn — not literally, not usually — but the hunger inside them works the way fire works. It consumes. It converts. It leaves a residue that is not what went in. Where the Cold-Hand binds the dead and the Bargained-Word negotiates with the named, the Devouring-Flame simply eats. They eat the residues of spells that have just been cast. They eat the warmth of the recently dying. They eat memories, names, the lingering essence in the corner of a room where someone screamed. By the end of the path they are not eating to live. They are living to eat. The path produces some of the most useful corrupt allies the dark arts can supply, and some of the most dangerous; the difference between the two is rarely visible in advance.
Devouring-Flame Initiate

You have learned to recognize what is left behind. The faint residue of a recently completed spell, the warmth of a freshly dead body, the lingering charge of an aborted ritual — these are no longer wasted. With an Action spent over a residue source no older than 1 hour, you may consume the residue and gain one of the following, chosen at the time of consumption: 1d6 Mana, 1d6 Vigor, +1 BD on your next single roll, or one fragmentary detail (a sensation, an image, a single word) about whatever generated the residue.

You can sense exploitable residue within 60 feet — fresh corpses, recently active magic, traces of intense emotion. The sense manifests as a faint warmth on the skin in the direction of the strongest source.

You are immune to ordinary hunger and thirst so long as you have consumed at least one source of residue in the past 24 hours. If you do not, you become Hungry, suffering 1 Challenge on all rolls until you feed.
Gain an initial Corruption Score of 2d6.
Consume the Spell

As a Reaction when any creature finishes casting a spell or ritual within 30 feet of you, you may consume the residual emanations. You gain Mana equal to half the spell's base Mana cost (rounded up, minimum 5). If the spell was cast hostilely at you and you succeeded your Resistance, you instead gain Mana equal to the spell's full base cost. You may use this Reaction once per round. Witnesses describe the experience as watching the air around you briefly thin, as though some quality of the room had been pulled into your skin. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Glean the Lifelight

When a living creature within 30 feet drops to 0 Health or dies in your presence, you may gain Vigor and Health equal to half the deceased creature's Stamina Score (rounded down) as you draw in the warmth of their departing animation. This effect is automatic and does not consume an Action. You may benefit from Glean the Lifelight a number of times per encounter equal to your Corruption Bonus Dice. Allies who notice the pattern may have words for you afterward. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Hungry Sigil

You can mark a target as your prey. As an Action and 8 Mana, scribe an invisible Hungry Sigil on a creature you can touch or that you have struck with a successful attack within the past minute. The sigil persists for 1 hour. While it persists, any time the marked creature takes damage from any source, you gain 1d4 Vigor or Mana (your choice). If the marked creature dies, the sigil is consumed and you instead gain a flat boon: +5 to your maximum Vigor or Mana for the next 24 hours. You may have one Hungry Sigil active at a time. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Eat the Memory

You can consume the memories of the recently dead and gain fragments of their knowledge. With 10 minutes spent over a corpse no more than 24 hours old (or 1 hour for those who died violently), the corpse begins to thin — its features blur, its weight diminishes — as you draw out a single specific memory. You choose the topic; the GM provides what the dead person knew about it. The corpse is afterward unsuitable for any other necromantic use; the residue is gone. You retain the eaten memory perfectly for 24 hours, after which it begins to fade unless you commit it to writing. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Swallow the Flame

You can consume incoming elemental energy. As a Reaction when an elemental effect (a fire spell, a lightning strike, an alchemical explosion, an arctic blast) would deal damage to you, you may attempt to swallow it instead. Make a Resistance (Stamina) VS the casting roll or attack roll. On a success, you take no damage from the effect and instead gain Mana equal to half the damage you would have taken. On the same turn or the following turn, as part of any attack you make, you may release the swallowed energy: the attack deals an additional dice of damage of the swallowed type per 10 Mana absorbed. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Crucible Body

Your body has adapted to constant consumption. You no longer suffer the Hungry penalty from going 24 hours without residue (though you do at 72 hours, escalating). Your Durability now regenerates at +1 per round during the Recovery phase for each source of residue you consumed in the current encounter, to a maximum of +5. You gain +1 BD on Resistance checks against poison and disease — your interior is too hostile a furnace for ordinary contaminants to survive. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Long Hunger

The window of time during which you can consume residue extends. Residues you could previously exploit only within 1 hour now persist for 6 hours. Residues you could exploit within 24 hours now persist for 1 week. A Devouring-Flame with Long Hunger walking through a week-old battlefield is not merely well-fed; they are a phenomenon. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Pyre Sense

Your awareness of death and residue expands dramatically. You sense every death, every completed spell of significant power, and every concentration of strong emotion within 5 miles of your current position, gaining their general direction and intensity. You can pinpoint individual sources within 500 feet with a moment's concentration. The sense is constant and cannot be suppressed; the world is a quietly burning landscape of opportunities, and you are always slightly oriented toward the brightest of them. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Burn the Name

You can consume the identity of a target whose corpse you possess. With 1 hour of preparation, 50 Mana, and the corpse of a named individual (with a clear social identity, not merely a generic body), you destroy their name and reputation as a coherent thing. The dead person, once burned, cannot be raised, contacted, or remembered with any spell or ability that requires their identity. References to them in living memory begin to drift and decay; their portraits become slightly off, their letters' authorship debated, their family histories confused on the matter. You gain a permanent +1 to your Corruption Score for each Name Burned this way. The world becomes faintly thinner each time. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Resonant Furnace

You can store essence rather than consume it immediately. You gain a Furnace pool with a maximum capacity equal to 10 × your Corruption Bonus Dice. When you consume residue from any source, you may direct any or all of the gain into the Furnace instead of using it immediately. The Furnace pool persists indefinitely. As a free action, you may release any amount of stored Furnace as the appropriate resource (Mana, Vigor, or a temporary +1 BD applied to a single roll). The Furnace cannot be used to exceed your normal maximum resources for more than one round. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
The Pyre That Walks

You are now a constant, low-level engine of consumption. Any creature that dies within 30 feet of you provides you with a Glean the Lifelight benefit automatically and without limit. Any spell cast within 30 feet of you (including by your allies) generates passive residue equal to 1 Mana per Mana of the spell's base cost, which falls into your Resonant Furnace if you have one. The effect is subtle but unmistakable to those sensitive to it; mages will refuse to cast near you if they have a choice; healers will report that wounds heal slightly worse in your presence; and battlefields you walk in your own time feel, after, as though something has been taken from them that the dead did not consent to give. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
All Things Burn Eventually

Once per encounter, as an Action and 80 Mana, you may declare a Consumption — a 30-foot radius centered on yourself in which every source of residue within range is immediately exhausted into you. You gain Mana, Vigor, and Health equal to your Corruption Bonus Dice × 5, plus 1d10 per body, ongoing magical effect, or significant emotional residue within the area. Active spells in the area are dispelled. Ongoing magical durations end. Recently dead bodies are rendered inert and unsuitable for any further necromantic exploitation. The ground in the area is left subtly wrong for weeks afterward — colors muted, sounds slightly absorbed. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Ash-Crowned

You may now consume residue from things that have not yet finished happening. Once per day, with 10 minutes of concentration and 100 Mana, you may declare a coming death — naming a target you have direct perception of who is currently engaged in any activity that may cause their demise (combat, illness, journey through dangerous terrain). For the next hour, if the target dies, you receive the full residue benefit at the moment of their death, regardless of distance. If the target does not die in the hour, the talent ends without effect and you cannot use it again on that target for one week. Other Devouring-Flames will recognize the technique and consider its use against named individuals an act of consequential interest. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
The Last Furnace

This is the threshold. You become, in a measurable sense, the residue you have spent your life consuming. Your creature type changes to Eldritch. Your maximum Resonant Furnace capacity doubles. Once per day, when you would be reduced to 0 Health, you may release the contents of your Furnace as a wave of consumption: you regenerate Health equal to the Mana so released, and every creature within 30 feet must Resist (Stamina) VS your Corruption Score or take damage equal to half the Mana released as Entropic damage. After triggering The Last Furnace, you cannot consume residue of any kind for 24 hours; you have, briefly, exceeded the rate at which the world produces what you eat, and the world resents being asked. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d20.
Always Just A Little Hungry

You are never satisfied. The state you achieved at Initiate — immune to ordinary hunger after a single residue meal — has reversed itself into a constant low-grade craving. You suffer 1 Challenge on Resistance (Resolve) checks against any temptation to feed inappropriately (consuming an ally's spell residue, marking a friendly target with a Hungry Sigil, exploiting an inconvenient death). The Challenge increases by 1 each time this malediction develops.
Smoke in the Lungs

You smell faintly of ash and burned silk. The scent is sweet but not pleasant. People notice. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social (First Impressions) checks and on Beast Handling. Fires in your presence burn slightly hotter than they should, which is sometimes useful and sometimes not.
The Empty Look

Your eyes have begun to do something subtly unnerving — they do not focus quite where they should, lingering instead on the air around the person you are speaking to. The effect is constant. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social (Persuasion) checks in formal or intimate contexts. Crowds in informal settings (markets, taverns) do not notice.
Spell-Hunger

In the presence of active magic, your concentration becomes difficult to maintain on anything else. You suffer 1 Challenge on Studies, Memory, and Reasoning-based checks made within 30 feet of any active spell effect or recently completed casting. The penalty applies to your own spellcasting as well.
Cold Hearth

Your prolonged presence in a location subtly drains it of warmth — not literal temperature, but the comfort and familiarity of a place. After a week of sleeping in any single building, residents will begin to find the building less welcoming, sleep less well, prefer to spend their time elsewhere. The effect is gradual but irreversible without ritual cleansing.
Borrowed Gestures

When you have consumed memories from a corpse using Eat the Memory, you sometimes catch yourself using the dead person's mannerisms, phrases, or facial expressions in casual conversation. Friends of the deceased may notice. You suffer 1 Challenge on Legerdemain (Disguise) checks for 1 month after each Eat the Memory use.
The Pyre's Light

You give off a faint visible warmth in low-light conditions — not a glow exactly, more a sense that the air immediately around you is being slightly heated. Observers in dim conditions will see your outline blur or shimmer subtly. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks in dim or dark conditions.
Dependency

The longer you go without consuming residue of significant quality, the worse you function. Beyond 72 hours, you become Hungry. Beyond 1 week, you suffer 2 Challenges to all rolls until fed. Beyond 2 weeks, you take 1d6 Health damage per day that cannot be magically healed until you feed. The malediction makes long isolations functionally lethal.
The Smoldering Wake

Plants, food, and certain refined materials in your immediate vicinity (within 5 feet) age subtly faster than they should. Fresh bread goes stale by morning. Cut flowers droop within hours. Wine sours in the bottle. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social checks in any context involving food, hospitality, or the offering of gifts.
The Unrest of the Eaten

Those whose Names you have Burned, whose Memories you have Eaten, or whose deaths you have Ash-Crowned occasionally surface in your dreams — not vengefully, but as residue. You suffer 1 Challenge on Resistance (Resolve) checks against sleep-affecting magic, and you remember them, increasingly, with a clarity that exceeds your own life's memories.

Not every dragon left through the gate at Athma Rahal. The records of the Khaasta speak, in the most carefully guarded chambers, of the ones who went down instead of out — the apostates, the Devourers, the ones who followed An Uk Ar into the underworld and were not seen again. Their bones are not Dragon Stone. Their bones do not lie in the southern barrows or the northern glaciers. Their bones lie below the world, and what leaks up from them is not the elemental fury of the Wyrms but something hungrier, colder, more lordly. The Path of the Dark Dragon is the corruption that takes hold when a mortal has touched the residue of an apostate dragon, whether by knowing consumption, accidental exposure to a deep ruin, or the unlucky inheritance of a bloodline that drank from one of those buried springs. The bearer of this corruption is sometimes called a Wyrmtouched. The dragons do not call them anything. The dragons are not aware that the small inheritance is happening. The dragons are asleep, and the small inheritance is one of the things they dream.
Wyrmtouched

Something deep and old has woken at the back of your mind, and it has begun to look out through your eyes. You speak fragments of Ur-Druka — the language of dragons — when stressed, frightened, or aroused, although you do not understand most of what you say. You gain +1 BD on Resistance checks against fear, charm, and intimidation; the thing inside you does not care to be commanded.

Your eyes catch the light strangely in dim conditions, glowing faintly with a color of your choice: gold, bronze, oxblood, ash, or jet. Your skin runs slightly warmer or cooler than ordinary mortal flesh (your choice; the choice corresponds, vaguely, to the temperament of the buried dragon you have inherited).

You gain a natural Armor Rank of 1 (stacking with worn armor) as your skin develops faint subdermal nodules that no ordinary inspection will identify. You possess a presence: creatures of Instinctual temperament become uneasy in your presence, and most domesticated animals will not willingly approach you.
Gain an initial Corruption Score of 2d6.
Hoarder's Eye

You see worth where others see clutter. With a moment's concentration on an object, you instinctively know its rough monetary value, its provenance if any, and any magical or significant properties it possesses, at no Mana cost. You gain +2 BD on Awareness (Investigation) and Studies (Appraisal) checks. You are increasingly disinclined to part with anything valuable that comes into your possession, and the GM may, at moments of stress, require a Resistance (Resolve) VS 20 to surrender, sell, or part with a particularly choice item. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Devourer's Maw

Your teeth lengthen slightly. Your jaw articulates wider than it should. Your bite, on a successful Unarmed attack roll, deals 1d8 + Strength BD Piercing damage. If you devour at least one pound of fresh meat (including, where applicable, from a fallen enemy) during a short rest, you recover an additional 1d10 Durability and 1d6 Vigor beyond ordinary recovery. The taste, you discover, is not unpleasant; it is also not, particularly, what you would have called food a year ago. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Lordly Voice

You have learned, without meaning to, the cadence in which Ur-Druka commands. As an Action and 10 Mana, speak a single sentence in Ur-Druka (which most listeners will not consciously understand). All creatures within 30 feet that can hear you must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score or be Compelled to obey one specific simple instruction lasting 1 round — "halt," "kneel," "drop your weapon," "answer." Creatures with their own Corruption Score may add their Corruption BD to their Resistance. Creatures of dragon ancestry are immune. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d6.
Scaled Hide

The faint subdermal nodules of Wyrmtouched have visibly emerged. Your skin shows scales along your forearms, your neck, the line of your jaw — not enough to disguise that you were once human, but enough to ensure that those who know you when fully clothed will not entirely recognize you stripped. Your natural Armor Rank increases to 3 (stacking with worn armor). Choose one damage type: Fire, Cold, Lightning, Entropic, or Umbral. You are now Resistant (half damage) to this type. The choice reflects the temperament of the buried dragon whose residue you have inherited; it is not entirely yours. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Hunger Within

You feed on the magic of those you harm. Each time you deal damage to a creature with a Mana pool, you absorb Mana equal to half the Bonus Dice of the damage rolled (rounded down, minimum 1). Mana absorbed this way fills your own pool, and may briefly exceed your maximum by up to 10 points for 1 round before dissipating. The talent does not function against creatures without Mana, and creatures sensitive to magic will sometimes physically feel the draw. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Wings of the Apostate

At Action and 20 Mana, you unfurl wings — not feathered, not membranous in the manner of bats or true dragons, but composed of a kind of dark fibrous matter that does not match anything in the natural world. The wings persist for 10 minutes per Corruption Bonus Die or until you choose to dismiss them. While wings are out, you may fly at your normal Speed in any direction, and you may glide at half Speed indefinitely without expending Mana. The wings are unmistakable to anyone who sees them; you cannot use this ability secretly in any inhabited area without consequence. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Crown of Judgment

You radiate an aura of ancient authority. Creatures within 20 feet of you of lower level (or Heroic Tier) than your own must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score on the round they first see you in a hostile encounter, or suffer the Anxiety condition for the duration of the encounter. The aura is passive and automatic. Friendly creatures and allies are unaffected. The aura does not stack with itself if multiple Wyrmtouched are present, which has historically been a contentious matter. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Echo of the Devourer

You have inherited a fragment of the breath. As an Action and 30 Mana, you exhale a 15-foot cone of dark energy that reflects your chosen Resistant element (Fire, Cold, Lightning, Entropic, or Umbral). The cone deals 3d10 + Corruption BD damage of that type; targets may Resist (Speed) for half. You may use the Echo once per encounter, plus once additional per Soul Bonus Die. The first time witnesses see you breathe, they must Resist (Resolve) VS 30 or suffer Anxiety. The breath is colder, hungrier, more chthonic than the elemental fury of the Khumeric Vessel; observers familiar with both will note the difference, and the implications. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d8.
Wyrmscale

Your transformation has progressed beyond the point where you can plausibly pass for human under casual inspection. Scales now cover most of your visible skin. Your hands have elongated; the fingers are tipped with hard, dark nails that function as natural weapons (1d10 + Strength BD Slashing). Your natural Armor Rank increases to 5 (stacking with worn armor). Your maximum Durability increases by 30. You become fully immune to your chosen element from Scaled Hide. People do not extend you, increasingly, the courtesies they extend to other people. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Devour the Slain

When you kill a creature with your Bite or Claws and consume at least a portion of the body afterward (a ritual that takes 10 minutes and is, on every level, deeply unsettling for any witness), you may roll a permanent benefit on the table below, once per session maximum. If you have already rolled the result, you may reroll.

1: +1 permanent Durability. 2: +1 permanent Vigor. 3: +1 permanent Mana. 4: +1 permanent Health. 5: +1 BD on attack rolls with your natural weapons for 1 week. 6: One memory or piece of knowledge from the slain (as Eat the Memory, but the knowledge is permanent). Each use of Devour the Slain on a sapient creature also increases your Corruption Score by an additional 1, beyond the cost of the talent itself. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Tyrant's Voice

Your Lordly Voice has matured. You may now Compel up to a number of targets equal to your Corruption Bonus Dice with a single use, and the instruction may be a complete sentence rather than a single command. The duration is now 1 minute. Each target receives its Resistance independently. The Mana cost is 30. A Wyrmtouched at this rank in a crowded room speaks with the weight of something that ought to be obeyed; even those who succeed their Resistance often find themselves doing what was asked, briefly, before remembering that they were free. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
Drake-Form

You may, as an Action and 40 Vigor, undergo a partial transformation into a hulking draconic shape for 1 minute. Your Size increases by one step. Your Strength and Stamina each increase by +6 for the duration. Your natural attacks (Bite and Claws) gain Threat (2), ignoring 2 points of Armor Rank per strike. You may breathe an Echo of the Devourer (if you have the talent) without expending its encounter use, once during the transformation. After the form ends, you suffer 1 rank of Fatigue. The form is unmistakable: you are a small drake wearing the remnants of your clothing, and there is no concealing what you have become. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d10.
The Apostate's Awakening

Once per day, as a full-turn Action and 100 Vigor, you may achieve full transformation. For 1 minute, you take the shape of a true drake: Large size, four limbs and wings, full scaled hide. Your natural Armor Rank increases to 7. Your Strength, Stamina, and Speed each increase by +10 for the duration. You may fly at 60 feet per round. Your Bite and Claws deal 2d12 + Strength BD damage. Your Echo of the Devourer's cone expands to 30 feet, deals 6d10 + Corruption BD damage, and may be used twice during the transformation. After the form ends, you suffer 2 ranks of Fatigue and your Corruption Score increases by 1d6. You have, for one minute, been what you are slowly becoming; the body remembers, and the slow becoming accelerates. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Dread Sovereign

Your Crown of Judgment becomes terrible. Once per scene, as an Action and 50 Mana, you may extend your aura to a 60-foot radius and intensify its effect. All creatures in the area below your level or of lower Heroic Tier must Resist (Resolve) VS your Corruption Score with 1 Challenge or suffer the Terror condition for 1 minute, fleeing or cowering as appropriate. Creatures who succeed the Resistance suffer Anxiety as ordinary Crown of Judgment. The Sovereign's use is a battlefield-clearing ability and the GM should treat narrative consequences accordingly — what you did to those men will be remembered. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
Devourer's Breath

Once per day, unleash a breath worthy of the buried dragons. As a full-turn Action and 80 Mana, exhale a 60-foot line of your chosen element 10 feet wide. The breath deals 10d10 + Corruption BD damage of the appropriate type. Resistance (Speed) halves. Structures in the line take double damage. Ground in the line is permanently altered: glassed, ash-blasted, frozen solid, or steeped in a faint chthonic residue that lingers for years. The first time witnesses see you Devourer-Breathe, all who Resist (Resolve) VS 40 or worse suffer Trauma; trained Templars will identify the technique on sight. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d12.
An Uk Ar's Echo

This is the threshold. The buried thing whose residue you inherited has, after years of slow infiltration, decided to wake — at least a little, at least as much as it can in your small mortal vessel. Your creature type changes to Eldritch (Dragon). You no longer age. You gain +20 permanent Mana and +20 permanent Vigor. Your maximum Health increases by 2d10. Your Devourer's Breath may now be used twice per day. Your Apostate's Awakening duration extends to 5 minutes, and the Fatigue cost afterward is reduced to 1 rank.

You no longer dream in any human sense. Your sleep consists of brief, vivid, distantly recollected hunts and council meetings and arguments about the carving of mountain ranges that happened long before any human war. You are reasonably certain that the buried dragon does not yet know you exist. You hope to keep it that way as long as possible. Increase your Corruption Score by 1d20.
Hoard-Madness

You cannot part with anything you have come to consider yours. Wealth, weapons, certain books, certain people. You suffer 1 Challenge on Resistance (Resolve) checks against surrendering, selling, or giving away possessions you have held for more than one month. Each subsequent time you develop this malediction, the scope tightens — at the third level, you may not abandon a position you have called home for more than a week without significant struggle.
Sun-Sick

Direct sunlight no longer agrees with you. You suffer 1 Challenge on all rolls in direct sunlight, and you cannot regenerate Health under a clear sky. The malediction worsens with each development; at the third level, sunlight deals 1d4 Entropic damage per hour of exposure.
The Cold Heart

You no longer feel warmth — neither comfort, nor affection in the way you once did, nor the simple pleasure of fire on a winter night. The pleasures of intimacy, music, and good food register as distant facts rather than experiences. You suffer 1 Challenge on Empathy checks and on Social checks where genuine warmth is required.
Wyrm-Hunger

You crave raw flesh. If you go more than 24 hours without consuming raw meat (the rarer the better), you become Hungry and suffer 1 Challenge on all rolls until you feed. Cooked food no longer satisfies the need. The longer the malediction persists, the larger and the more recent the meat must be.
Smoke and Mirror

Your reflection is wrong, very slightly. The wings, the teeth, the cast of the jaw — none of these match what others see in your face. Only you (and other Dark Dragon-corrupted) can see your reflection accurately; ordinary mirrors show you a faintly idealized human face, which is sometimes worse than the truth.
The Hoard's Light

You sleep best on top of something valuable. Without a hoard underneath you — coin, weapons, books of significance, art objects worth a small fortune — you suffer 1 Challenge on the first roll of every day after a restless night. The amount required scales with your Corruption Score.
Voice of Ur-Druka

Under stress, you involuntarily speak Ur-Druka rather than the common tongue, sometimes mid-sentence. The shift is incomprehensible to most listeners and unmistakable to anyone who has any inkling of what Ur-Druka is. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social checks made under combat or high-emotion conditions.
Tribute Demanded

Creatures of Instinctual temperament — especially predators — will sometimes approach you and present themselves. A wolf laying a fresh kill at your feet. A raven dropping a coin on your shoulder. The offerings are unsolicited, persistent, and unsettling to witnesses. You suffer 1 Challenge on Reputation and Social checks in any small community that has observed this behavior.
Sleepless Vigil

You no longer sleep deeply. Your eyes remain partly open. You may be roused fully alert in an instant, but you do not recover from fatigue or Vigor through ordinary sleep alone; you require additionally either meditation or the consumption of fresh meat. You suffer 1 Challenge on the first Resistance check against fatigue-based effects each day.
The Buried Voice

The thing inside you whose residue you inherited occasionally speaks to you in dreams, in moments of solitude, in the silence between heartbeats. The voice is not malicious. It is interested. It asks questions about your friends, your plans, the places you have been. You may answer or not; the GM may, once per session, present you with a moment where the voice asks something it should not know, and the answer you give matters.

These general talents are available to any creature with a Corruption Score of at least 5. They have no specific Path prerequisite, though most require you to have committed yourself to some Path — they are bargains made against your existing taint, not from a clean place. Each adds to your Corruption Score, and each deepens your reliance on the dark power you have already accepted. They cost one skill point apiece in the ordinary way and may be purchased only once unless otherwise specified.
Whisper of the Other

You can sense the corruption of others. You detect creatures with a Corruption Score of 5 or higher within 100 feet, perceiving their direction and rough intensity (much less, less, comparable, more, much more than yours). At 30 feet or closer, you may identify which Path they walk if it is one you know. The talent is passive and automatic. It also means corrupted creatures of sufficient awareness sense you sensing them.
Tier: Rank 2 (no Path prerequisite). Corruption Gain: 1d6.
Tainted Vessel

You have learned to convert your own life force into magical energy through the channels the corruption has opened in you. As a free action, you may sacrifice Vigor or Health to gain Mana at favorable ratios: 2 Vigor : 1 Mana, or 1 Health : 3 Mana. Mana so generated must be used within 1 round or it is lost — the wound is paid, the power is granted, the ledger closes. Health sacrificed cannot be recovered by ordinary rest for 24 hours.
Tier: Rank 3. Corruption Gain: 1d8.
Mark of the Pact

Once per session, you may call on your dark connection for a moment of intervention. As a Reaction to any failed roll, mark yourself: reroll the failed roll with +1 BD, but suffer 1d6 Health damage and accrue 1 additional point of Corruption beyond the normal cost of this talent. The intervention is not free. The dark power notes the request, and notes the increasing frequency with which you make it.
Tier: Rank 2. Corruption Gain: 1d6.
Eldritch Eyes

Your eyes change. You gain Darkvision to 60 feet, seeing in low and no light as clearly as you would in daylight. You may also see through ordinary illusions and detect the presence of eldritch and corrupted beings within 30 feet, even if they are not visually obvious. The change is visible to others — your eyes now possess a faint constant glow of a color matching your Path. You suffer 1 Challenge on Legerdemain (Disguise) checks unless your eyes are covered.
Tier: Rank 2. Corruption Gain: 1d6.
Voice of Dread

Your voice has begun to carry the resonance of what dwells in you. You gain +2 BD on Social (Intimidation) checks. Creatures attempting to mentally influence you must add their Corruption BD (or 0 if uncorrupted) to your effective Resistance — the corruption in your mind resents being commanded by other corruptions. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social (Persuasion) checks where sincerity or warmth is required.
Tier: Rank 2. Corruption Gain: 1d8.
Suppress Corruption

You can briefly conceal what you are. With 10 minutes of meditation and ritual, expending half your Mana and half your Vigor, your Corruption Score is reduced functionally to 0 for the next 8 hours. You may not activate any Corruption-based talents, abilities, or maledictions during this period. However, you are undetectable as corrupted by any means short of direct revelation — you may pass examinations, blessings, and Templar scrutiny without consequence. The effect cannot be cancelled early or extended; after 8 hours it expires, and the corruption resumes exactly where it stopped.
Tier: Rank 4. Corruption Gain: —.
Curse-Mantle

You have learned to weaponize the maledictions inflicted on you. Once per encounter as a Reaction, you may invoke one of your active maledictions against an attacker within 10 feet that has just damaged you. The malediction's aesthetic — the cold of Pale Grace, the smoke of Devouring Flame, the inscribed sigils of Vile Names — momentarily projects onto the attacker, dealing 3d8 + Corruption BD damage of an appropriate type. The damage type and any rider effect is at GM discretion based on the malediction invoked. The talent can be purchased multiple times, granting an additional use per encounter each time.
Tier: Rank 3. Corruption Gain: 1d8. Repeatable.
Carrion Mind

You may briefly read the final thoughts of a corpse no more than 1 hour dead by placing your hand on its head and concentrating for one round. You receive a single sensory impression of the corpse's death — what they saw last, what they heard, what they felt — but not necessarily who killed them or why. Multiple uses on the same corpse return progressively muddled impressions. The talent does not increase your Corruption itself, but each use accrues 1 point of incidental Corruption to a maximum of 5 per long rest.
Tier: Rank 2. Corruption Gain: 1d6 (initial purchase only).
Black Vigor

You may convert Corruption itself, briefly, into stamina. As a free action, you may pay 1 point of temporary Corruption (a temporary increase that lasts 24 hours, then dissipates) to gain 2d10 Vigor immediately. The talent may be used a number of times per day equal to your Corruption Bonus Dice. The temporary Corruption counts toward the next Malediction threshold while it persists; a Tainted creature who uses Black Vigor heavily may temporarily develop additional Maledictions that fade with the loaned Corruption.
Tier: Rank 3. Corruption Gain: 1d8.
The Familiar Wound

You have made friends with what hurts you. Once per long rest, when you would suffer damage from a source associated with your Corruption Path (Entropic damage for most paths, Umbral or Fire for some, silver for Lycan, sunlight for Vampires, etc.), you may instead absorb the damage and convert it: half the damage becomes Mana, half becomes Vigor (round down each, minimum 1 each). The damage is not dealt. The wound that would have weakened you has, instead, fed you.
Tier: Rank 4. Corruption Gain: 1d10.
Tongue of the Damned

You can speak with other corrupted creatures across language barriers, in a tongue that no scholar has cataloged. When conversing with a creature with a Corruption Score of 5 or higher, you and they understand each other regardless of native languages. The shared communication is imprecise — it carries meaning and intent more than vocabulary — but it suffices for negotiation, threat, and the sort of conversation corrupted creatures most often have with each other.
Tier: Rank 2. Corruption Gain: 1d6.
Echo of the Awakened

You have begun, faintly, to manifest the signature of your Path even when not actively using its abilities. Once per session, you may invoke a single Rank 1 or Rank 2 ability of your Path at no resource cost — the corruption is, briefly, voluntarily helpful. The GM may rule that the effect is slightly muted (half duration, reduced damage by one die) at their discretion. The use is conspicuous: anyone observing you will notice that something just happened, even if they cannot articulate what.
Tier: Rank 3. Corruption Gain: 1d8.

Beyond the curated lists of each Corruption Path, all corrupted creatures may eventually develop the generic maledictions below. These are open maledictions: any GM may select one for any character who has crossed a Corruption threshold (every 5 points) and lacks a fitting Path-specific malediction available, and any player whose Corruption has grown beyond their Path's curated list may, in consultation with the GM, select from this set. Each Common Malediction can be developed up to three times unless otherwise noted, with escalating consequences described in the entry.
Mark of the Damned

A visible brand has appeared somewhere on your body — a sigil, a pattern of discoloration, a scar that was not there yesterday. The mark is recognizable as supernatural in origin to anyone familiar with the occult, and to Templars and clergy on sight. The first time you develop this malediction, the mark is small and easily covered. The second time, it migrates to a difficult-to-conceal location (palm, throat, brow). The third time, it cannot be hidden by any ordinary means, and you suffer 1 Challenge on Legerdemain (Disguise) checks in any environment where supernatural identification is a concern.
Hollow Eyes

Your eyes change. The whites take on a tint — yellow, pink, grey, depending on your Path. Your pupils dilate or contract irregularly. People who knew you before will mention it. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social checks where direct eye contact is part of the encounter. Each subsequent development of this malediction worsens the change: the second time, your eyes no longer track quite normally; the third time, your eyes glow faintly in darkness and may visibly betray strong emotion.
Cold Breath

Your exhalation fogs the air even in mild weather. The effect is subtle in winter, conspicuous in summer, and impossible to entirely conceal. Sensitive observers and trained investigators will identify it as a marker of corruption. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks made under conditions where breath can be observed (calm air, soft light, close confinement).
Hollow Voice

Your voice carries a faint unnatural resonance — a depth or an echo or a doubled cadence — that is not present in any ordinary human throat. Most listeners cannot identify what is wrong, but they know that something is. You suffer 1 Challenge on Entertain checks involving singing and on Social (First Impressions) checks. Trained occultists will recognize the marker on hearing you speak.
Shadow Cast Wrong

Your shadow no longer matches you. The proportions are slightly off — too long, or jointed wrongly, or moving with a fractional delay. The discrepancy is visible to anyone who looks for it but easy to overlook in passing. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks in any environment with strong directional light and shadows (lit corridors, sunny streets, lamp-lit halls).
Sleepless

Your sleep has become broken and shallow. You wake too easily, dream too vividly, and recover from fatigue more slowly than you should. You require an additional hour of rest each night to gain the benefits of full sleep; if you do not get it, you suffer 1 Challenge on the first roll of the day. The malediction worsens with each development: at the second level you require two additional hours; at the third, you no longer truly sleep but enter a kind of waking torpor, and must succeed a Resistance VS 25 each long rest to recover Vigor.
Mirror-Shy

Reflective surfaces no longer accurately represent you. The first development is subtle — your reflection lags by a fraction of a second, or your features appear slightly different in still water than they do in polished glass. The second development is more pronounced: your reflection sometimes shows you doing things you are not doing, or wearing clothes you are not wearing. The third development means reflective surfaces show something else entirely — your true Path-given visage, an aspect of your patron, or simply something that should not be looking out at you. You may not bear to look at mirrors for long.
Iron-Sickness

Pure iron causes you mild physical discomfort — a low-grade nausea, a slight tremor, a difficulty thinking clearly while it is on your person. You suffer 1 Challenge on any action taken while wearing or wielding more than 1 pound of pure (90%+) iron. The malediction worsens with development: at the second level, you may not safely wield iron weapons or wear iron armor at all without taking 1d4 Health damage per round; at the third level, even brief contact with iron deals 1d6 Entropic damage and prevents you from regenerating any resource for the round.
Crow's Companion

Corvids — crows, ravens, magpies, jackdaws — recognize you and treat you with respectful interest. They follow your movements at a distance, gather in numbers above places you sleep, and call out in patterns that other birds find disturbing. The effect is constant and impossible to suppress. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks in any environment with significant corvid population, and 1 Challenge on Social checks in rural communities that read carrion-birds as omens of ill.
Smell of the Pyre

You smell faintly of ash, sour blood, decay, or sulfur — a scent appropriate to your Path. The smell is constant, undetectable to you, and impossible to mask except briefly with strong perfume. Dogs notice. Children sometimes notice. Sensitive people are made uneasy without being able to say why. You suffer 1 Challenge on Beast Handling and on Social (First Impressions) checks in any setting involving close physical proximity to others.
Aurorae

Moments of strong emotion or high exertion trigger a faint visible luminescence on your skin — a brief shimmer along the lines of your veins, a glow at the back of your eyes, a soft phosphorescence on your fingertips. The effect lasts only seconds but cannot be controlled. You suffer 1 Challenge on Stealth checks during combat or other periods of high stress.
Drawing the Eye

Other corrupted creatures are passively aware of your presence within 1 mile, even if they cannot pinpoint your location. They may dream of you. They may feel uneasy in cities where you are present. Powerful corrupted beings (named demons, ancient vampires, Wyrm-touched sovereigns) may seek you out for reasons of their own — alliance, predation, recruitment, or curiosity. The GM should treat this as a setting fact: corruption tends to find corruption, and yours is now on a list somewhere.
The Familiar Voice

Something speaks to you in the back of your mind. Not constantly — not enough to drown out your own thoughts — but present, occasionally interjecting, commenting, advising. The voice is recognizably linked to your Path (the named patron of the Vile Names, the buried dragon of the Dark Dragon, the hungry residue of the Devouring Flame, etc.). It does not control you. It only contributes. You may, at the GM's discretion, gain useful information through the voice once per session — and you may, at the GM's discretion, be subtly influenced by it in ways you cannot easily identify.
Sigil-Echo

Holy sites and consecrated grounds become subtly uncomfortable. You suffer 1 Challenge on all rolls while standing on ground that has been ritually consecrated to a power opposed to your corruption. Each subsequent development of this malediction adds another Challenge and extends the range of the effect: at the second level, you suffer the Challenge within 60 feet of consecrated ground; at the third, you cannot regenerate any resource while consecrated ground is within line of sight.
The Slow Withering

Your body bears small marks of decay that do not heal properly: a callus that has become permanent and slightly discolored, a scar that has gradually darkened, an old wound that aches in cold weather and never quite settles. You suffer 1 Challenge on Resistance (Stamina) checks against ongoing damage (poison, disease, environmental hardship). The malediction is cumulative in a small but real way: every time it develops, you appear a year or two older than you did the previous time, and the marks become slightly less easy to dismiss as ordinary aging.
The Quiet Beneath

When you are still — truly still, for more than a moment — small unsettling things happen near you. A pin drops a beat too quietly. The flame of a candle leans away. Your reflection in a still pool stops moving entirely. The phenomena are subtle and inconsistent but constant. Sensitive observers find them deeply disturbing. You suffer 1 Challenge on Social checks made in quiet, intimate settings.

A blade does not save a hero. The hand on its grip does. The shield does not turn the spear; the soldier’s nerve and footing do. But neither hand nor nerve nor footing will carry you through a winter pass, across a salt-flat, or down into a barrow without the right gear at your back. Equipment is the long, quiet argument between what the world will do to you and what you have chosen to bring along. The arms in your scabbard, the armor across your chest, the rope coiled on your belt — these are not the measure of who you are. They are the measure of what you intend. The richer adventurer is rarely the better one. But the prepared adventurer comes home, and the prepared adventurer is usually prepared because someone else taught them, the hard way, what they wished they had been carrying.

You receive an initial package of starting equipment from your class. This includes all the basic tools necessary for you to venture off into the world with some amount of preparation assumed, along with how much money you start with, if any.
Make no mistake, you won’t start out very wealthy or with the best gear. After all, why set out to make your fortune if you already have a fortune?

A Medium-sized creature with Ordinary Strength can lift and carry 10 lbs. per point of Strength. Large creatures can carry 20 lbs. per point of Strength. You can dead lift and hold up to twice your lift value. You can lift this amount for 1 round at the cost of 10 Vigor. Each round, you consume 10 Vigor, if you have it, to hold the weight up.

You can carry a weapon in each hand, or one two-handed weapon. Dropping and drawing another weapon takes one action, though dropping an item as part of an attack or move action is possible; drawing a weapon is not, and requires 1 action unless a special ability allows otherwise.

Likewise you can only wear one suit of protective gear at a time, unless stated specifically otherwise. Shields occupy one hand, and provide no benefit while worn on the back unless stated otherwise. Donning and Doffing armor takes considerable time, usually one to three minutes.
Finally, most characters can wear and attune up to 3 Relics. These magical items take minutes to attune so they cannot be swapped in combat, but they can be removed with 1 action.
Quality refers to the materials and craftsmanship of an item, which impacts its cost as well as its performance. High quality weapons deal more damage, and superior armor offers greater protection.

Ordinary By Default. Unless explicitly stated otherwise, the quality of all weapons and armor listed is Ordinary. It is assumed these are the type of readily available, mass-produced items that you can find across shops and markets.
However, not all items are created equal. Between craftsmanship, materials, and wear, you may find that the equipment you have is better or worse than you expected.

This quality is reflected in the cost of an item. The listed cost for an item is for a version at the quality listed, so a poor staff costs the listed price, but a feeble staff might cost half that — or nothing at all, as it’s basically just a branch at that point.

All characters begin with a basic minimum of equipment. You start with one set of traveling clothes, sturdy footwear suited to your homeland, a cloak or jacket appropriate to the climate, and one personal memento — a keepsake of little market value but priceless to you. To this your class adds its own starting kit of arms, armor, and tools. Beyond that, every character begins with 150 gold pieces and is expected to spend the bulk of it before they set out. You need not spend it all, but the road has very little patience for an empty scabbard.

Protective equipment, such as suits of armor and shields, is pivotal for any adventurer. Wearing armor will protect you from harm by absorbing an amount of damage from each attack. Normally, armor can only protect you from physical damage — Slashing, Impact, Piercing, and Fire damage. Energy damage, like cold and lightning, usually bypasses armor. Armor also has Durability, but armor only takes damage when it is targeted or part of another effect designed to damage it.

Armor Rank. Armor Rank is the maximum amount of damage your armor can absorb from a hit. When you are hit with an attack, you subtract up to this amount of damage from the attack. Anything above and beyond this amount is damage you sustain to your Durability.

Durability. Like everything, your armor and shields have Durability. Unless specifically targeted, armor does not take damage. When an armor or shield’s Durability is depleted, it is destroyed and no longer provides any protection.

Repairing Armor. With an appropriate Crafts or Engineering check VS 25, you can repair 3d6 Durability to ordinary quality armor. Higher quality armor adds +5 per quality rank to the repair VS; lower quality armor is easier to repair, lowering the VS by 5. Repairing armor takes 2 hours.
Features. Armor and shields can have special characteristics, such as immunity or improved Armor Rank bonus against some attacks.

Quality. Finally, armor and shields all have a quality rank, which affects the armor’s other traits. The material and craftsmanship can have a huge impact on the armor’s functionality, use, and Durability. All armor is assumed to be ordinary rank unless otherwise stated.
