Shields are a special class of armor. If you have a shield in hand when you are attacked, you may use a reaction to Deflect with your shield to intercept the attack. The advantage is you add bonus dice based on the shield you are using. When you successfully deflect an attack with a shield, your shield takes full damage from the attack.
Small Shields. Small shields, such as bucklers and lantern shields, provide +1 equipment Bonus Dice to Defense checks using the Deflect technique.
Medium Shields. Common round shields, heater shields, or kite shields provide +2 equipment Bonus Dice to Defense checks using the Deflect technique.
Large Shields. Huge tower shields and framing shields provide +3 equipment Bonus Dice to Defense checks using the Deflect technique.

When a suit of armor is created with inferior material, craftsmanship, or both, it suffers significant penalties to its primary characteristics: Armor Rank and Durability. Likewise, a masterful piece of legendary armor is going to be far and away superior to other suits of lesser make. Simply put, low quality armor will break much faster and offer less protection than armor of quality.
Ordinary Armors. By default, all armors listed are Ordinary. These are generally available armor and mass-produced products.

Armor of a superior quality becomes immune to damage from weapons and attacks of lower quality. Beginning at Heroic quality, armor becomes immune to weapons that deal Feeble damage. This does not mean you become immune to damage, but simply that the amount of damage absorbed by the Armor Rank is ignored against inferior weapons.
Armor can have several special features that modify its core characteristics. Some common features include:
Ballistic. This armor is specifically designed to repel piercing attacks. Double Armor Rank against Piercing attacks.
Bulky. This armor imposes 1 Challenge on Stealth and Athletics checks.

Burden. Heavier armor carries with it added burden, increasing the cost of Defense, Sprinting, and other Vigor uses by this amount. For example, Burden (6) increases the Vigor cost of actions by 6 Vigor.
Channel. Heavy armor also limits your ability to channel mana, this is the additional mana cost on actions. For example, Channel (8) increases the mana cost of abilities by 8.
Concealed. An Awareness (notice) check of 20 is needed to recognize this as armor.
Flack. Double Armor Rank against explosive attacks.
Grounded. This armor protects against Lightning damage normally.
Hardened. This armor is nearly impossible to slash or tear. Double Armor Rank against Slashing attacks.
Harnessed. This armor has an additional Mod slot, for 3 total slots.
Insulated. This armor protects against Cold damage normally.
Padded. This armor is made to absorb impacts and blunt force. Double Armor Rank against Impact attacks.
Thermal. This armor has double the Armor Rank against Fire attacks.

A suit of armor can support a number of mods, including attachments, inserts, and talismans. Each mod takes up 1 or more mod slots on your armor. The number of mod slots depends on the armor, with light generally supporting 1, medium 2, and heavy supporting 3 mods. Most mods can be applied by someone with Crafts or Technology skill bonuses.
Street Leathers. Worn under cloaks or coats, these stitched hides offer modest defense without alerting guards or rivals. Favored by streetwise rogues and black-market couriers alike.
Emberweave Shirt. Fashioned from fire-treated fibers, this shirt resists heat and looks unassuming. Useful for surviving both open flames and the suspicions of inquisitive eyes.
Shade Cloak. A layered black-grey cloak insulated against the cold. Its mundane look hides its purpose: protecting spies and scouts from both cold and unwanted attention.
Windwalker Robes. Woven for wanderers of the high cliffs, these robes reduce chill and shock. Their lightness conceals hidden enchantments favored by sky-path monks.
Huntsman Vest. Rugged and practical, this padded vest dulls blunt trauma. Used by beast trackers and caravan guards where brawls and falls are more common than blades.
Duelist’s Guard. Tailored for agility and showmanship, this armored shirt hides well under fine clothes. Reinforced to turn blades in close quarters or crowded ballrooms.
Ranger Tunic. Light armor with harness rigging for utility packs and pouches. Ideal for scouts operating far from supply lines, or eluding pursuit in hostile wilds.
Combat Vest. This reinforced vest includes ballistic mesh lining, offering solid defense in firefights. Favored by urban enforcers and frontline operatives expecting shrapnel and sabotage.
Shadow Garb. Armor tailored for infiltration, this muted garb masks movement and muffles sound. A favorite of assassins, insurgents, and agents without an escape route.
Flak Jacket. Heavy padding and shrapnel-resistant plating form this military staple. Excellent against explosives or concussive traps. Frequently worn by demolitionists and field engineers.
Scout Armor. A hardened light suit that offers protection without visibility. Worn by advanced reconnaissance units trained to strike first and vanish before counterattack.
Chain Shirt. A shirt of interlocked metal rings worn under outer garments. Resilient and flexible, it guards against slashes without impeding movement or drawing undue attention.
Brigandine. Steel plates sewn into heavy fabric or leather, this vest dampens forceful blows and absorbs strikes. Popular among mercenaries with spellcasting on the side.
Scale Mail. Layered metal plates form a second skin over leather padding. Noisy, but formidable, especially against wild beasts and reckless bandits lacking precision strikes.
Half-Plate. Combining mobility and protection, this armor uses solid plating over vital points. It weighs heavily on the body and the soul, especially for channelers.
Tactical Armor. Modern armor tailored for modern warfare. Engineered for bullets, bombs, and battlefields, it balances defense, fire resistance, and arcane channeling in equal measure.
Guardian Cuirass. Heavy and imposing, this suit protects against both elemental and physical threats. Reserved for elite vanguards and guardians of high sanctums and fortress walls.
Stormcoat Armor. Designed for storm riders and lightning-born. This medium-weight coat is lined with grounding weave and fire-treated fabric, perfect for elemental battlefields.
Vanguard Harness. A harness of reinforced plates and mod slots. Offers reliable defense for martial leaders and shock-troopers who stride into danger and drag others out.
Scaled Mantle. Metallic scales sewn into a weatherproof cloak. Worn by alchemists and explorers for protection against volatile chemicals and sudden, explosive misfortune.
Plate Mail. An enduring classic: full body metal armor that shrugs off most strikes. Unforgiving in weight, but a walking fortress for the determined warrior.
Ironbound Plate. Blackened steel infused with cold-forged spells. Insulated against the elements and reinforced for relentless clashes. Built for winter sieges and ash-choked strongholds.
Battleframe Cuirass. A power-assisted breastplate worn by elite shock units. Reinforced to absorb force and distribute impacts while allowing modular upgrades for frontline adaptability.
Infernal Plate. Forged in volcanic forges, this armor shrugs off fire and fury. Rumored to burn hotter when blood is spilled. Worn by the damned and devout.
Fortress Harness. Massive modular armor used by siege-breakers and royal guard. Encases the body in alloyed plates capable of withstanding cannon fire and divine wrath.
Juggernaut Shell. Forged for brutal frontline charges. This armor is nearly impenetrable, but punishingly heavy. Only the strongest can wield its weight without breaking beneath it.
Aegis Frame. The pinnacle of defensive engineering. This powered shell absorbs spellcraft and shrapnel alike. A relic of lost empires, feared even when dormant.

Battles are often won or lost by how well you can adapt to the threats around you. Armor Mods provide a way to customize your armor, enhancing its functionality and tailoring it to your playstyle. Armor Mods are broken down into attachments that are external add-ons, inserts that are added internally to the armor, and talismans that are visible charms and occult trappings. Each mod takes a number of slots, and armor is limited to the number of slots by its type and features.
Physical add-ons stitched, strapped, or bolted onto your armor. These mods emphasize utility and battlefield adaptation.

rows: When an enemy grapples you, they take 1d6 Impact damage.
Add +1 Bonus Die on Athletics (Hauling, Carrying). Increases carried weight by +1/4.
Reduces Reload time by 1 action (min Reload 1).
Grants Concealed feature; Awareness VS 25 to recognize as armor.
Increase Armor’s Durability by +1/4. Looks extra cool.
Attach retractable grappling tool. Gain +1 Bonus Die on Athletics checks to climb or scale surfaces.
Double lift value, reduce Burden by 3, add Channel (4).
Adds +1 Bonus Die to Stealth checks in one environment (woodlands, mountains, arctic, desert, or night).
Adds or doubles the Hardened feature (A.R. x4 vs Slashing).

blocks.html: These mods are armor cores reinforced with alloys, plating, or experimental energy sinks. They push durability and protection beyond normal limits.

rows: Adds or doubles Ballistic feature (A.R. x4 vs Piercing).
Adds or doubles Padded feature (A.R. x4 vs Impact).
Increases Armor Rank by +4, but armor gains Burden (2).
When taking damage from Magic, regenerate 1d8 Mana.
Adds or doubles Insulated feature (A.R. x4 vs Cold).
Adds or doubles Thermal feature (A.R. x4 vs Fire).
Adds or doubles the Grounded feature (A.R. x4 vs Lightning).

blocks.html: Mystic wards, spirit-bound seals, and charms woven into armor are called Talismans. They protect against intangible threats and channel esoteric energy.

rows: Gain +1 Bonus Die to Resistance checks vs Possession, Charm, or Fear.
As an action sacrifice 5 Health to add +3 to Strength Score for 1 turn.
+1 Bonus Die to Resistance checks against Seal and Magic Seal effects.
Stores 18 Mana per Soul Bonus Die; discharged for 1 Occult action.
When reduced to 0 Health, your armor absorbs 100% magical damage for the round.

blocks.html: Adventuring is dangerous; hostile creatures, bloodthirsty mercenaries, and incredible threats await the bold. Weapons are an essential part of surviving as an adventurer. Weapons generally deal damage on a successful attack but may have additional traits as well.
Damage. A weapon’s damage is the number of dice you roll, based on the weapon’s quality. By default, weapons are Ordinary and deal d8 damage.
Ranged weapons deal a number of dice of damage set by your ammunition type. For example, most standard ammunition deals d6 damage, where many ranged weapons deal 2 or 3 dice.
When using a melee weapon, you add your Strength Bonus Dice to damage. If you are sufficiently skilled in using a ranged weapon or magical projectiles, you add Skill Bonus Dice, if any, to damage.

Damage Type. Most weapons deal Slashing, Piercing, or Impact damage. Special weapons may deal Fire, Lightning, or Cold damage instead. Any weapon that lists “Elemental” as the damage type deals the same damage type as your Elemental Affinity.

Range. Ranged weapons have two range categories: normal and long. Attacking in normal range is no different than making a melee attack and is resolved the same way. When you make an attack against a target at long range, you suffer 1 Challenge on that attack roll.
Features. These are special weapon traits that skilled users may exploit, such as piercing armor or making additional attacks.
Characteristics. Fundamental properties of the weapon such as its size, ammunition count, or how it is held.
Quality. Finally, weapons can have a quality rank, which affects the weapon’s other characteristics. All weapons are assumed to be Ordinary rank unless otherwise stated.

Slots. This is the number of available weapon mods that can be fitted to the weapon. Weapon mod slots are listed in order of Grip, Head or Internal, and Charm. For example, a weapon with 1/0/1 mod slots can be fitted with 1 grip, no head or internal mods, and 1 Charm.

A bronze sword is no less lethal than a steel sword, but the steel sword will hold up under greater stress and can potentially deal much greater killing power, represented as different damage dice. Unless stated otherwise, all weapons listed are Ordinary quality, and deal a number of d8 as their basic weapon damage.
Weapons that deal superior damage will ignore inferior armor. Beginning at Heroic, weapons will ignore the Armor Rank of armors with a quality three or more steps lower than the weapon’s quality.

Form follows function, and as such some weapons have characteristics that are inherent to that weapon’s form and construction. Characteristics can be advantages or difficulties, or simply neutral. Unlike Features, characteristics are always present, and you do not need any special skill to take advantage, or suffer, from their effect.

Ammo. This weapon houses ammunition. The number in parentheses is the amount of ammunition that can be fired before you need to reload or recharge the weapon. For example, a weapon with Ammo (1) must be reloaded after each attack. Ammunition defines the dice type of damage, whereas the ranged weapon sets the number of dice.
Clumsy. This weapon is unwieldy, increasing the cost of Defense reactions and Combat techniques by the amount listed in parentheses. For example, Clumsy (3) increases the Vigor cost by 3.
Hands Free. This weapon is controlled via mental command. You do not need to hold or touch it.
Loud. This weapon cannot be used to make consecutive sneak attacks and automatically ends stealth when used.
Non-Lethal. This weapon cannot deal damage to Health unless you take a Chance explicitly to deal lethal damage.
Reach. This weapon has a long physical form, allowing it to extend its lethal reach to 10 feet (2 hexes). Other melee weapons have a reach of 5 feet (1 hex).

Refined. The form of this weapon leaves little room for improvement. Regardless of the quality, a Refined weapon has 1 fewer modification slot in the head, and significantly higher base cost — about double similar weapons.
Reload. This weapon must be reloaded when its capacity is exhausted. Reloading takes a number of actions listed. For example, Reload (2) takes 2 actions on your turn to reload.

Two-Handed (2H). This weapon requires two hands to be effectively used at its normal size. Exclusive 2-Handed (X2H) weapons require two hands to employ regardless of size, such as bows and other large braced implements. When attacking with a 2H weapon using 1 hand, you suffer 2 Challenges to your Attack and Damage Bonus Dice.

A weapon’s advantages and special traits are described by its features. Features, unlike characteristics, require training to use, usually in the form of a Rank 2 Weapon Skill talent. Without training, you cannot make use of a weapon’s features, even if you are otherwise skilled or use techniques.

Crush. This weapon is designed to break through material. On a hit, this weapon deals 1 additional Weapon Dice of damage against Constructs and Shields. For example, a great hammer that deals 4d8 damage on a hit would deal 5d8 against a construct.
Defensive. This weapon is designed to be used defensively, and adds +1 Bonus Die to Defense reactions made with the Deflect technique.
Deft. This weapon is designed to defeat opponents’ attacks with ease, reducing the Vigor cost of Defense reaction techniques such as Deflect or Block by 2, to a minimum of 1.
Dual Ended. This weapon has two lethal heads. On your turn you may choose which end to attack with, as well as make an Off-Hand attack with the other end.

Explosive. This weapon deals the amount of secondary damage to all creatures and objects in the area that is listed. For example, Explosive (3d8, 15ft) will deal 3d8 damage to everything within 15 feet of the target. On a miss, this weapon still deals damage to everything in the area, potentially including the original target.
Freezing. On a hit, this weapon causes the target to gain the Freeze condition for 1 round.
Ignite. On a hit, this weapon causes the target to gain the Burning condition for 1 round.

Impact. If your weapon attack hits a target but is otherwise defended against with a defense reaction roll, you can still deal damage to the target equal to the number of Strength Bonus Dice you have. For example, if you have a Strength of 25 (+3 bonus dice), you would deal 3 damage to the target.
Shove. This weapon’s mass will knock targets back. On a hit with this weapon that deals damage, you push the target back up to 5 feet straight away from yourself, assuming it is Large or smaller.
Slight. These weapons are exceptionally light and well balanced. When used to make an Off-Hand attack with this weapon, you only suffer 1 Challenge to Attack and Damage Bonus Dice.

Sweep. When you make a weapon attack against a target and hit, you may force another target adjacent to your first target to make a defense reaction or take weapon damage (but not Bonus Dice) as well. You may use this feature only once per turn.
Swift. On your turn, if you make two consecutive attack actions or technique actions with this weapon, you may make a third basic attack action using this weapon.

Tangle. This weapon is designed to grip and tangle an opponent. If you hit a creature with this weapon, you can force the creature to make a Resistance (Agility) check VS your Strength Score. On a failed Resistance check, the creature is knocked down.

Threat. This weapon has armor-piercing qualities and ignores an amount of Armor Rank on each attack equal to the number in parentheses. For example, a Threat (3) weapon would ignore 3 points of an opponent’s Armor Rank.
Thrown. This weapon can be used in melee or thrown, with a range that is listed in parentheses. For example, Thrown (15ft) gives a throwing range of 15 feet.

Wieldy. This weapon is well balanced and has an extended grip allowing it to be used with 1 or 2 hands. In its two-handed configuration it deals an additional +1 die of weapon damage. For example, a 2d8 axe will deal 3d8 damage when used in two hands.
Dagger. Knife Weapon. A simple short blade favored for its concealability and speed. Easily thrown, it is the adventurer’s standby tool in combat and utility.
Parry Dagger. Knife Weapon. This light off-hand weapon includes prongs (like a sai) or a basket hilt (such as a main gauche) to be used in defense.
Stiletto. Knife Weapon. A narrow piercing blade, excellent at exploiting weaknesses in armor. Its speed and precision make it dangerous against unarmored or lightly armored opponents.
Arming Sword. Sword Weapon. A knight’s standard sidearm. Balanced for thrusts and slashes, the arming sword is reliable and adaptable in both formation and personal combat.
Longsword. Sword Weapon. A versatile one-handed or hand-and-a-half sword designed for reach and precision. Excellent for dueling, its balance makes it popular among adventurers.
Broadsword. Sword Weapon. A heavy single-handed sword with a wide blade designed for cutting and durability. It sacrifices finesse for greater staying power in battle.
Estoc. Sword Weapon. A narrow thrusting blade optimized to pierce armor. Its stiff design lacks cutting edge but excels in breaking through plated defenses.
Rapier. Sword Weapon. A slender thrusting sword, prized by duelists. Designed for speed and precision, the rapier is devastating in skilled hands.
Scimitar. Sword Weapon. A curved, single-edged blade optimized for slashing strikes. Light and fast, often associated with cavalry and lightly armored warriors.
Katana. Sword Weapon. A refined, curved blade of exceptional sharpness. Its balance and cutting power make it a weapon of honor as well as war.
Hand Axe. Axe Weapon. A versatile tool and weapon, the hand axe is light enough to throw, chop, or fight with in brutal close quarters.
Battle Axe. Axe Weapon. A heavier axe designed for combat. Its head delivers devastating force, though it lacks the precision of lighter weapons.
Mace. Blunt Weapon. A metal-headed club designed to crush bone and armor. Brutal and straightforward, often favored by clerics and guards.
Tonfa. Club Weapon. A paired short baton with a side grip. Used defensively or offensively, it allows fast strikes and redirection of force.
Maul. Blunt Weapon. A heavy striking hammer, optimized to break armor and bone. Requires strength but delivers immense concussive force.
Pick. Piercing Weapon. A war tool with a pointed head designed to puncture armor. Slow but lethal against defenses.
Flail. Blunt Weapon. A spiked or blunted head on a chain, the flail delivers unpredictable, crushing blows and is hard to counter.
Whip. Exotic Weapon. A flexible lash of leather or chain used for control rather than raw damage. Effective at keeping foes at bay and disarming.
Chain Whip. Exotic Weapon. A reinforced whip with metal links or weighted ends. Its unpredictable strikes can tangle weapons or inflict dangerous slashing wounds.
Kama. Exotic Weapon. A short sickle adapted from farming tools. Excellent for quick slashing strikes and can be thrown short distances for surprise attacks.
Kusarigama. Exotic Weapon. A chain-and-sickle weapon requiring great skill. The sickle slashes while the chain entangles, allowing both control and damage at range.
Greatsword. Sword Weapon. A massive two-handed blade delivering overwhelming cleaving power. Requires strength and reach, but cuts through multiple foes with wide, sweeping blows.
Great Scimitar. Sword Weapon. A curved great blade emphasizing speed and slashing arcs. Its agility makes it lighter than other greatswords but still demands two-handed use.
Zweihander. Sword Weapon. A colossal Germanic blade designed to break pike formations. Its length grants control of the battlefield, though it is slow and heavy.
Claymore. Sword Weapon. A Scottish two-handed sword with a broad blade and crossguard. Balanced between thrusts and wide sweeps, it remains versatile for frontline fighters.
Nodachi. Sword Weapon. A long, refined Japanese greatblade. Designed for battlefield slashing power, its honed edge delivers devastating strikes at the cost of speed.
Great Axe. Axe Weapon. A heavy two-handed axe, brutal in power but unwieldy. Its wide, crushing swings make short work of armor and shields.
Heavy Mace. Blunt Weapon. A large spiked or flanged mace, forged to break armor and bones with sheer impact. Unsubtle but highly effective.
Warhammer. Blunt Weapon. A long-handled hammer designed for armored warfare. Its crushing head and back spike deal concussive trauma or puncture plate.
War Pick. Piercing Weapon. A heavy two-handed pick used against plate armor. While clumsy, its piercing tip devastates fortifications and armor.
Morning Star. Blunt/Piercing Weapon. A long-handled spiked club, heavier cousin of the mace. Delivers brutal, armor-penetrating strikes with lethal force.
Quarter Staff. Pole Weapon. A simple hardwood staff, weighted for striking. Light, versatile, and deft, often favored by monks or travelers.
Pole-Hammer. Pole Weapon. A polearm with a hammer head. Its length allows wide sweeps and crushing impacts, controlling the field against cavalry and infantry.
Short Spear. Pole Weapon. A lighter spear usable with one or two hands. Quick thrusts and throws make it an adaptable battlefield weapon.
Spear. Pole Weapon. The standard battlefield weapon, combining reach with strong thrusts and sweeping arcs. Effective against cavalry and formations.
Pike. Pole Weapon. A long spear designed for formation fighting. Its extreme reach controls space but makes it unwieldy in close quarters.
Trident. Pole Weapon. A three-pronged spear used in gladiatorial and hunting combat. Its points trap weapons and deliver precise thrusts.
Javelin. Throwing Spear. A light spear for ranged combat. Balanced for throws, though serviceable in melee. Favored by skirmishers and hunters.
Guisarme. Polearm Weapon. A polearm with a blade and hook. Used to sweep cavalry and tangle foes, controlling the battlefield through reach.
Scythe. Polearm Weapon. A farming tool repurposed into a weapon of grim reaping. Its curved blade excels at sweeping motions but is slow and awkward to wield.
3-Section Staff. Exotic Weapon. Three wooden or metal rods linked by chains. Flexible and versatile, it can strike at varied angles or extend to longer reach.
Meteor Hammer. Exotic Weapon. Twin weighted balls attached by a long chain, spun with deadly force. Hard to master but devastating in momentum and impact.
Tetsubo. Blunt Weapon. A massive iron-studded club from the east, wielded by oni and giants in myth. Overwhelmingly heavy, it crushes both armor and courage.
Shortbow. Bow Weapon. A light, simple bow designed for skirmishing. Quick to draw and fire, though limited in power and range.
Recurve Bow. Bow Weapon. A bow with tips that curve away from the archer, granting greater power without added size. Reliable and favored by hunters.
Longbow. Bow Weapon. A tall, powerful bow requiring significant strength. Excels at range and penetration, though unwieldy in close combat.
Compound Bow. Bow Weapon. A modern refinement using pulleys and cams. Allows high draw power with stability. Expensive but efficient.
Hand Crossbow. Crossbow Weapon. A small, concealable ranged weapon. Quick and light but lacks damage. Useful for rogues and close-range surprises.
Light Crossbow. Crossbow Weapon. A versatile and accessible ranged weapon. Easy to use with decent power, though slower to reload than bows.
Arbalist. Crossbow Weapon. A heavy siege crossbow. Devastating at long range but slow to reload and cumbersome to carry.
Sling Bow. Exotic Weapon. A hybrid of sling and bow, launching bolts or stones with amplified force. Difficult to master but surprisingly deadly.
Chakram. Exotic Weapon. A razor-edged throwing ring. Capable of sweeping arcs of slashing damage. Balanced for return catches with training.
Boomerang. Exotic Weapon. A curved throwing weapon that arcs back when missed. More distracting and disabling than lethal, but versatile in skilled hands.

Arcane Arms straddle the boundary between weapons and relics. Forged of conductive woods, crystal cores, and reagent-treated metals, these implements channel ambient mana into coherent blasts of elemental force at modest range — and, for those trained in the discipline, they serve as casting foci for the Arcane Arms spell list (see Chapter Ten — Disciplines). The basic attack of an Arcane Arm consumes no mana; spells cast through it do. Each Arcane Arm has an elemental affinity that determines both its damage type and the spells it can cast through.

To employ an Arcane Arm at all, the wielder must possess a minimum current Mana pool — the implement will not draw from a reservoir that is too shallow to sustain its resonance. Attack rolls with Arcane Arms use the Occult (Arcane Arms) skill in place of an ordinary weapon skill, and add Soul Bonus Dice to damage in addition to any Skill Bonus Dice.

Arcane Wand. A 14-inch length of magically refined material set with a small focal crystal. Light, easily concealed, and modest in output, the wand is the entry-level Arcane Arm of choice for hedge mages, scholars, and traveling apprentices. The wand has no meaningful melee profile — it is a focus, not a weapon. Mana minimum: 20.

Arcane Scepter. A short, ornate rod of office, often crowned with a worked finial of gilt, gem, or bone. One-handed and noticeably heavier than the wand, the scepter strikes a balance between focus and ceremony — it is the implement of magisters, court-mages, and ordained casters who must look the part as well as play it. May be used as a melee weapon, dealing 1d6 Impact damage on a successful Unarmed or improvised-weapon attack. While the scepter is openly carried, the wielder gains +1 BD to Social checks involving authority, command, or formal rank. Mana minimum: 30.

Arcane Rod. A two- to three-foot rod, often capped with an ornamented head or set with an inset crystal. The rod balances power against demand: it strikes harder than a wand and accepts most Arcane Arms spells, but requires a deeper reservoir to function. One-handed. May be used as a melee weapon, dealing 1d8 Impact damage on a successful attack. Mana minimum: 40.

Arcane Staff. A five- to six-foot staff bearing a complex focal head — frequently a crystal cluster, a runic cage, or a bound spirit-token. Heavy, two-handed, and demanding, the staff produces the highest damage and most efficient spellcasting of the three. May be used as a melee weapon, dealing 2d6 Impact damage on a successful attack. Mana minimum: 60.

Where the wand, scepter, rod, and staff descend from a single shared tradition of focal craft, the exotic Arcane Arms emerged sideways — from priesthoods, secret libraries, bound-spirit traditions, and the workshops of caster-craftsmen unwilling to accept the limits of the standard form. Each requires specialized training to use, in the form of a Rank 2 Occult (Arcane Arms) talent specific to the exotic.

Grimoire. A bound tome the size of a thick ledger, its cover plated in worked metal and its pages stitched with conductive thread. Two-handed. The Grimoire serves both as a focus and as a magazine of prepared workings. The wielder may inscribe up to four Arcane Arms spells onto its pages during a long rest (1 hour and 10 Mana per spell inscribed). Spells cast through the Grimoire from inscribed pages cost their normal Mana. As an Action, the wielder may instead tear an inscribed page free; the loose page functions as a one-use scroll usable by any creature with the Occult skill, casts the spell at the Grimoire-wielder’s casting level when triggered, and crumbles to ash on use. The Grimoire’s basic melee strike is 1d8 Impact (a hard, undignified bookbash). Mana minimum: 40.

Orbital Focus. A floating crystal sphere roughly the size of a clenched fist, bonded to its wielder by a brief ritual. The orb hovers within arm’s reach of the caster’s free hand and follows their movement automatically. Hands-Free — both of the wielder’s hands remain available for weapons, shields, or a second focus. Basic attack deals 1d6 Elemental damage at the standard Arcane Arm range. If knocked more than 5 feet from the wielder, or successfully Dispelled, the orb dissipates; re-summoning it requires 1 Action and 5 Mana, provided the bonded crystal is still intact. Mana minimum: 15.

Censer of Resonance. A bronze incense vessel suspended from a long chain, its interior packed with smoldering reagent-coals. Reach. Wieldy. Basic attack deals 2d8 Fire damage on a successful strike — the chain swings the censer like a flail, and the live coals strike with both impact and heat. As an Action the wielder may instead hold the censer aloft and swing it gently overhead; while doing so, the censer exudes a 10-foot aura that grants the wielder and allies within it +1 BD to Resistance checks against Fear, charm, and other mental effects until the start of the wielder’s next turn. The Censer cannot be used to attack on the same turn it is held aloft. Mana minimum: 30.
Caestus. Unarmed Weapon. Leather wraps reinforced with metal studs. Light, flexible, and excellent for fast flurries of strikes.
Knuckles. Unarmed Weapon. A set of brass or iron knuckle dusters. Simple but brutal, adding bone-crushing force to every punch.
Gauntlet. Unarmed Weapon. Heavy armored gloves designed for striking. Sacrifices speed for resilience and improved defense against blades.
Katar. Exotic Unarmed Weapon. A South Asian punch dagger worn on the forearm. Devastating in close combat, piercing armor with straight-line stabbing strikes.
Iron Rings. Exotic Unarmed Weapon. Weighted metal bands worn around the wrists or knuckles. Add crushing force to punches and enhance grappling techniques.
Tekko-Kagi. Exotic Unarmed Weapon. Iron claw bracers designed for climbing or disarming. Their hooked blades can entangle weapons or rake across unprotected flesh.
Bola. Thrown Consumable. Weighted cords designed to entangle legs or arms. Excellent for disabling foes without lethal force.
Flash Grenade. Thrown Consumable. A blinding burst of light and sound. Disorients enemies in close quarters, opening opportunities for attack or retreat.
Smoke Bomb. Thrown Consumable. A simple alchemical device producing thick smoke. Creates cover, blocks line of sight, and aids in stealthy escapes.
Frag Grenade. Thrown Consumable. A fragmentation explosive. Deadly against groups, especially unarmored targets caught in its shrapnel storm.
Plasma Charge. Thrown Consumable. A high-energy device unleashing destructive plasma. Burns through armor and structures with raw elemental power.
Elemental Detonator. Thrown Consumable. A specialized orb charged with elemental forces. Explodes with magical precision, inflicting Fire, Cold, or Lightning damage.
Adhesive Mine. Planted Consumable. A sticky explosive that clings to surfaces. Perfect for ambushes, chokepoints, or sabotage tactics.
Standard Bodkin. Arrow Ammunition. Slim iron-tipped shaft designed for penetration. Favored by archers for balance of cost and effectiveness against most armors.
Broadhead. Arrow Ammunition. Wide-bladed hunting arrow. Creates massive wounds but loses penetration power. Common among hunters and ambushers relying on devastating first strikes.
Needle Point. Arrow Ammunition. Hardened steel tip for piercing gaps in armor. High precision design grants Threat (3), making even plated foes vulnerable to skilled shots.
Explosive. Arrow Ammunition. Alchemically-packed arrowhead detonates on impact, dealing 2d6 explosive damage in a 10ft radius. Inaccurate, but devastating against clustered or entrenched enemies.
Blunt Head. Arrow Ammunition. Rounded wooden or metal tip designed for stunning, not killing. Delivers non-lethal concussive force, ideal for capturing foes alive.
Fire Arrows. Arrow Ammunition. Oil-soaked shafts wrapped in pitch ignite upon launch, setting targets ablaze. Inflicts the Burning condition and terror in enemies as much as wounds.
Silver. Arrow Ammunition. Inlaid with purified and hardened silver to deal extra damage against vulnerable creatures.

rows: Blinds enemies in a 15 ft. radius for 1d4 rounds.
Creates a 20 ft. cloud of smoke for 2d4 rounds, granting concealment.
Deals Piercing damage in a 10 ft. radius. Flack: +2d8 vs. unarmored targets.
Deals higher energy damage in a 10 ft. area.

blocks.html: Weapons can be customized and upgraded with Weapon Mods. Weapon mods improve or expand the weapon’s capabilities or features. Each weapon has up to three mod slots: a Grip, a Head or Internal, and a Charm. The slots a weapon has indicate the number of weapon mods it can support.
A character with Crafts or Technology skill bonuses can attach and modify weapon mods with a crafting check during downtime.
These mods alter how a weapon is held and controlled, improving precision, speed, or stability. Grips emphasize balance, draw speed, and defensive handling over raw power.

rows: +1 BD to normal attacks vs Blooded opponents
Reduces one Challenge from Called Shots
Weapon gains +1 BD to attacks vs Beasts or Natural creatures
Weapon gains +1 BD to attacks vs Eldritch creatures

blocks.html: Head Mods replace or enhance a weapon’s striking surface. Head mods reshape damage profiles, add armor-piercing edges, or enable versatile attack types.

rows: Causes Bleeding (1) on a Burst Damage BD
Causes Poison (1) on a Burst Damage BD
Inlays of pure silver to damage those weak to the material

blocks.html: These mods are hidden mechanisms or enhancements fitted inside weapons. These are limited to crossbows and Arcane Arms. Internal mods boost ammo capacity, reduce reload time, and unlock experimental power surges.

rows: (Arcane Arms only) Reduces Mana minimum to use by 10
(Arcane Arms only) Spells cast through this weapon cost -2 Mana (min 1)

blocks.html: Mystical inscriptions and spirit-bindings woven into weapons. Charms grant elemental power, protective wards, or occult blessings, blending craftsmanship with magic to transform weapons into conduits.

rows: When you Block magical damage, reduce by Soul BD
On successful Deflect, deal 1d8 damage back
On damaging a Wounded foe, regain 1d6 Mana
Weapon gains Tangle vs summoned/spiritual foes

blocks.html: A life of adventure will require more than weapons and armor. You will need light, protection from the elements, and probably some tools to get things done. Below is an outline of common gear you might make use of on your adventures.
Blood Tonic. A crimson serum thick with vitae extracts. Restores 2d6 Durability to equipment or armor. Used after scuffles or close brushes with ruin.
Wyrmroot Tonic. A syrupy, bitter liquid that restores 1d6 Mana. Favored by novice casters to re-anchor their spells after a miscast.
Blackroot Shot. Restores 1d6 Durability and grants immunity to Fear for one scene. Strong aftertaste; popular among low-born knights.
Mechanist’s Balm. A foamy vial applied to armor. Restores 2d6 Durability over one minute. Ineffective under combat stress but excellent for rapid recovery.
Voidleaf Tonic. Restores 1d6 Mana and suppresses Fatigue effects for 10 minutes. Multiple uses within an hour cause nausea (Sickened, 1 minute).
Vital Surge Potion. Restores 3d6 Durability and 1d6 Vigor. Feels like being struck by lightning — revives movement but leaves tingling limbs.
Gravedust Mixture. Restores 2d6 Durability and ends one Bleeding or Poison effect. Reeks of wet ash and cemetery lichen.
Ether Bloom Potion. Restores 2d6 Mana and 1 Burnout. Glistens with pale energy. Frequent use dulls the senses (Sickened for 1 round after third use in a day).
Phantom Draught. Restores 1d6 Mana and grants +1 Bonus Die to your next action. Heightens perception with eerie clarity.
Armorgrit Suspension. Restores 3d6 Durability instantly. Must be poured directly on armor. The liquid hardens into crystalline scales before vanishing.
Bloodwake Elixir. Restores 4d6 Durability and 2d6 Vigor. Surges through the body like boiling blood. Second dose in a scene causes fatigue (Sickened 2 rounds).
Sanctified Elixir. Restores 2d6 Durability, 1 Mana, and ends 1 Curse or Hex. Only effective if crafted under ritual purity. False versions induce seizures.
Ironbrew Elixir. Restores 4d6 Durability and removes 1 Trauma. Thick, metallic flavor; common among veteran siege engineers.
Lucid Elixir. Restores 2d6 Mana and removes 1 Madness or Delirium. Causes sharp pain in the temples. Two uses in a day result in disorientation.
Forgeheart Elixir. Restores 5d6 Durability and grants Resistance to Slashing and Piercing for 10 minutes. Extremely rare. Using twice in a scene causes nausea (Sickened, 1d4 rounds).

Alchemy Kit. Every alchemist’s portable workshop. Six glass vials, a mortar and pestle, alembic, cucurbit, retort, and a basic alchemy guide. Without an alchemy kit, or a laboratory, you cannot craft anything of one rank higher quality using a talent earned in the Alchemy skill tree.
Cold Weather Kit. Everything you need to survive the cold. Cold weather clothes, an insulated tent, waterproof matches, and a kettle for melting snow.

Craftsman Kit. A basic craftsman’s kit contains a ball-pein hammer, a metal file, multi-tool, tongs, wrench, wood saw, sandpaper of multiple grit, cloth needles, leather needles, multiple pouches, chisels, and polish. Without this kit you cannot craft anything of one rank higher quality using a Rank 2 talent in the Crafts skill tree.

Electronics Kit. A set of screwdrivers, tweezers, standard fuses, switches, relays, solder, solder gun, protective eyewear, a grounding strap, rubber gloves, multimeter, and common replacement batteries.

Laboratory Kit. A portable lab containing six vials, three graduated cylinders, a Bunsen burner with fuel, and an assortment of reagents. Without a Laboratory kit or a laboratory, you take 2 Challenges when performing a laboratory check.
Larceny Kit. Commonly known as thieves’ tools. A set of lockpicks, a bolt cutter, a set of metal files, a crowbar, a grappling hook with climbing rope, and a climbing harness.
Maintenance Kit, Weapons. Cleaning cloths, oil, three differently sized whetstones, fifty fletchings, a hammer, an awl, connector rods, wedges, and a repair and maintenance guide.
Mechanics Kit. A 250-piece case with ratchets, sockets, wrenches, pliers, screws, bolts, and various common replacement parts.
Medicine Kit, Alchemy. Healing tinctures, medicinal herbs, and pain-relieving leaves; aids the body’s natural healing processes.
Medicine Kit, First Aid. Gauze, bandages, stitching supplies, antivenom for common threats, all wrapped in a waterproof metallic box.
Metalworking Kit. Portable crucible, bellows, hammers, tongs, metal file, steel hammering block, and leather strips. Required for one-rank crafting bonuses with Crafts Rank 2 talents.

Stone Cutting Kit. A pick shovel, rock pick hammer, steel chisels, gloves, pouches, jeweler’s loupe, and a gemology handbook. Used to gather and identify materials; gems cut without a full lapidary set are produced at poor quality.
Survival Kit. A tent, bedroll, sleeping bag, dried rations that last a week, flint and steel, a compass, two lengths of 20ft rope, and a large canteen, all packed in a canvas hiking pack.
Tailoring Kit. Ten spools of thread, a dozen needles, thimble, pins, fabric scissors, thread scissors, needle threader, seam ripper, measuring tape, spare buttons, and spare zippers.

Woodworking Kit. Cut-resistant gloves, whittling knife, detail knife, hook knife, leather sharpening strap, mallet, six chisels, metal file, wood saw, nails, screws, screwdriver, hatchet, felling wedges, and various grit sandpaper. Required for one-rank crafting bonuses with Crafts Rank 2 talents.
Lute. A pear-shaped stringed instrument with a rounded soundboard and fretted neck. Typically four to six strings, it suits ballads, accompaniment, and intimate performances.

Lyre. Compact open-frame stringed instrument plucked with fingers or plectrum; commonly accompanies recitations, rituals, and camp songs, offering clear harmonic support for storytellers and singers alike.
Fiddle. A bowed string instrument with four strings and a small hollow body; versatile for dances, folk tunes, and fast reels; prized for expressive, nasal timbre.
Flute. A slender woodwind tube, played by blowing across an embouchure hole; produces clear, piercing melody lines; portable and favored for signaling, lullabies, and pastoral tunes.
Brass Horn. A long brass horn producing loud, resonant blasts; used for battlefield signals, hunting calls, and ceremonial proclamation. Its tones carry over great distances and inspire troops.
Hand Drum. A shallow frame drum struck by hand or mallet; provides rhythmic backbone for marches, dances, and rituals. Tunable heads produce varied timbres and resonant pulses.
Guitar. A flat-bodied stringed instrument with six strings and a fretted neck, suited for chords, rhythm, and solo performance across taverns, courts, campfires, and dances.

Arcane Lyre / Arcane Harp. Stringed instruments whose frames have been carved from resonant wood and inlaid with focusing runework. When played by a character with the appropriate Performance discipline, the instrument couples directly to ambient mana, allowing Performances that ordinarily require ten minutes to manifest in seconds.
Spirit Chimes. A frame of suspended bronze and crystal chimes, each tuned to a specific spirit’s resonance. Used by spiritualists in invocation, divination, and contract negotiation.
Flare, Fusee. Sometimes mistaken for dynamite by children, these flares provide light for an hour, and can be safely held for 40 minutes providing a mobile source of light.
Flare, Glow. Two chemicals that glow when mixed; cracked and shaken, this plastic stick provides light for 12 hours.
Flashlight. Produces light via electricity flowing through a filament. While in use, the flashlight turns darkness into dim lighting, and dim lighting into well lit.

Flood Light, Portable. Powered by large battery packs or directly from a power source, allowing operation in the darkest conditions. While in use, the flood light turns darkness into well lit; looking at them without eye protection produces bright light.
Lantern, Oil. A hooded oil lantern that provides gentle light. While in use, the lantern turns darkness into dim lighting, and dim lighting into well lit.
Torch. Constructed by putting anything flammable on a stick. Can be made in the field as long as you have something flammable or can soak something with an accelerant.
Harness, Weapon. Available in two size variants, these leather back-mounted or side-mounted harnesses double as weapon scabbard and harness for combination weapons.
Wilderness Guide. Not just for alchemy. While using this guide, you can more easily identify edible, harmful, and helpful plants in the region.
Sleeping Bag. Used while sleeping in cold temperatures, this bag lets you treat Chilly temperatures as Temperate; in conjunction with a cold-weather tent you can treat Frigid as Temperate.
Tent, Cold Weather. Used while sleeping in cold temperatures and high altitudes, this tent lets you treat Chilly temperatures as Temperate and protects you from the elements.
Tattoo, Decorative. Worn as a personal memento, art, status, or culture, these tattoos are performed by injecting ink underneath the skin to create designs.

Tattoo, Runic. A live runic inscription tattooed into the skin using mana-bonded ink. Functions as a single-use Charm equivalent, expended when triggered. The application requires Occult (Sigilcraft) and a willing recipient; the tattoo is permanent until discharged.
Dosimeter, Personal. A patch made from material that changes color when introduced to ionizing radiation. When the color changes black, you’ve been exposed to the maximum safe amount.
Dosimeter, Alerting. A dosimeter that sets off an audible alarm when you’re approaching the safe radiation limit. Can be made to vibrate for the hearing impaired.
Radiation Sensor. Made using gases sensitive to ionizing radiation, this tool shows the level of radiation in a given area.
Generator, Portable. Durable; can burn nearly any highly combustible fuel source to create electricity. Fits in the bed of a truck or trailer.

Goggles, Infrared. Picks up heat signatures and allows sight in dim light or complete darkness. Since these detect body heat, remain vigilant for things hiding in a high-temperature environment or creatures who have no pulse.
Goggles, Night Vision. Amplifies ambient light, letting you see in dim-light environments.
Rope, Twine. Natural fibers; weakest rope, best used in crafts; weight limit of 25 lbs.
Rope, Natural Fiber. Three-strand rope; holds tents, hammocks, and children; weight limit of 100 lbs.
Rope, Parachute Cord. Multiple synthetic strands in a tubular sheath; lightweight; weight limit of 550 lbs.
Rope, Climbing. Thousands of synthetic fibers; weight limit of 2,000 lbs.
Sand, Bag. A large bag, filled with sand.
Shelter, Portable. Generally mounted on or towed behind a vehicle, sleeps 4–6 people.
Shovel, Survivor. A true multitool: teeth on the side of the shovel to saw, hammer and pick at the end of the handle.

Across the world, hidden in deep ruins and high crags, lie treasures of a stranger kind than gold — materials born of magical pressure, ancient fire, or impossible chemistry. These are the materials with which legendary craftsmanship is built.
Some metals, woods, and substances bear properties no mundane forging can replicate. Crafted into arms and armor, they elevate quality and grant supernatural benefits — at a price most can never pay.
Heroic Quality Material

A radiant crimson-orange metal found in small and impure quantities in deep tombs and the ruins of the ancients; pure orichalcum is rumored to exist only in mythic quantities. Also known as sunstone, scarlet steel, or giant’s bronze. Forging requires magical flames of Heroic or better quality and a hammer of Orichalcum or Adamantine.

Orichalcum Weapons. Melee weapons and arrows crafted from alloyed and forged orichalcum possess a faint orange glow. Regardless of the weapon’s actual quality, it is treated as two ranks higher quality for the purpose of bypassing armor (up to Legendary). If the weapon’s actual quality is raised so high that this bonus would make it Mythic or beyond, the weapon or arrow gains Threat (20).

Orichalcum Armor. Metal armors alloyed with orichalcum take on adamantine hardness. Such armor has twice the Durability of similar armor. The armor’s quality rank for the purpose of determining damage immunity is considered two ranks higher (up to Mythic).
Cost. +500 gold to the base item cost, before quality multipliers.
Ordinary Quality Material

A delicate silvery ore found exclusively in moonfall craters. With physical properties much like fine bronze, Moonsilver is no harder than iron, but it imprints magic with extraordinary ease. Rarely found in quantities larger than a few ounces.
Moonsilver Arcane Arms & Runic Items. Items crafted from Moonsilver have half the standard activation cost, and half the permanent Mana needed to affix a runic enchantment to them.
Moonsilver Weapons & Armor. Items crafted predominantly of Moonsilver gain +1 Bonus Die to Occult checks made by the wielder while holding or wearing the item.
Cost. +120 gold to the base item cost per ounce of Moonsilver required (most items require 1–4 ounces).
Legendary Quality Material

A lustrous golden-silver metal that naturally emits a faint glow, found only in the catacombs of forgotten empires and the deepest senatorial tombs. Also called Imperial Gold. Aurium is impervious to mortal craft — only natural, unshaped fragments, magical implements, and arcane or eldritch creatures can touch and work the metal.

Aurium Weapons. A blade of pure Aurium passes harmlessly through ordinary mortal flesh and armor, but strikes true against arcane and eldritch beings, dealing full damage that ignores all Armor Rank against creatures of those types.

Aurium Armor. A shield or set of armor crafted from Aurium will deflect any spell that targets the wearer; the spell is automatically nullified, but the armor suffers Durability damage equal to the spell’s Mana cost.
Cost. +3,000 gold to the base item cost, before quality multipliers.
Epic Quality Material

An incredibly hard, glassy metal of unknown origin, found only in dragon barrows and the deepest mines. Under the effects of a strong Mana field, the otherwise invulnerable stones become malleable and shapeable. Once forging is complete, a simple affixed rune renders the finished metal indestructible. Chunks cannot be mixed or combined — the ore fragment must possess greater mass than the finished object.
Adamantine Weapons & Armor. Items crafted of Adamantine are +2 quality ranks (minimum Ordinary, maximum Mythic). Once enchanted-sealed, the item is functionally indestructible by mundane means.
Cost. +2,500 gold per pound of Adamantine, before quality multipliers.
Ordinary Quality Material

Harvested from arcane trees of the deep wilds, Eldwood is a medium-weight hardwood of unparalleled quality. The deep mahogany lumber will never rot or decay and is harder and tougher than ordinary oak. Inherently magical.
Eldwood Items. Wooden items of Eldwood (staves, bows, shafts, hafts, shields) are automatically +1 quality rank and are immune to acid, rot, and corrosive damage.
Cost. +60 gold per pound of Eldwood, before quality multipliers.
Heroic Quality Material

Leather made from the hides of arcane beasts — chimeric predators, dire wolves, and other monstrous fauna. Goods crafted from this superb leather are markedly tougher than ordinary leather, and the material accepts enchantment far more readily than its mundane counterparts.

Fellbeast Leather Items. Leather armor, leather grips, scabbards, and similar items crafted from Fellbeast Leather are +1 quality rank. Enchantments affixed to Fellbeast Leather items have their permanent Mana cost reduced by 1 (minimum 1).
Cost. +80 gold per square yard of Fellbeast Leather, before quality multipliers.

The following components are used in crafting weapons, armor, accessories, relics, and artifacts. They often modify the item’s quality, imbue special effects, or alter crafting rules. All components fall into standardized types, broken down by type and then by quality.

Scavenged from machines, discarded tools, or salvaged equipment, these parts serve as the baseline for most crafting or repair tasks. At low quality, they might consist of rusted wires or broken plates. Higher quality ranks represent precision-grade systems and relic-level mechanisms that improve equipment stability and capability.

Every functional device needs a heart — a power coupler, a logic drive, a focal matrix. These are the defining components of any arcane invention or mechanical construct. Low-quality cores may short out under pressure, while legendary ones can support complex enchantments or bind elemental forces.

Trophies, tissues, and residues harvested from slain beasts and arcane creatures. These materials can be used to enhance physical resilience, magical properties, or create specialized items like toxin-coated blades or aura-infused armor. The rarer the creature, the greater the power latent in its remains.

Adventurers frequently rely on professionals to handle tasks beyond their expertise. Whether it’s commissioning blacksmiths to upgrade weapons, obtaining permits for restricted areas, or hiring specialists, services and hired help play a vital role in any journey. From bureaucratic paperwork to specialized training, many professionals across the realms offer assistance, if you can afford their fees.

Below is a Services Table detailing common costs for hiring skilled labor, acquiring essential documentation, and maintaining your equipment. Prices may vary based on region, urgency, or the expertise of the professional.

rows: Authorization for trade, hunting, or restricted activities in major cities.
Provides the knowledge needed to construct or modify an item.
Training in alchemical and arcane chemistry techniques.
Intensive training in combat or movement techniques.
Standard medical treatment, covering minor injuries and illnesses.
Surgery, serious wounds, or long-term treatment requiring expert care.
Official travel documentation, allowing passage between national borders.
Repairs for common weapons, armor, and tools.
Specialized repair for Arcane Arms, enchanted items, and runic gear.
Craftsmen, researchers, or specialists available for short-term hire.
Standard airship travel within a single country.
Economy rail ticket for regional travel.
High-class rail accommodations, often including private cabins and meals.

blocks.html: Whether you need a blacksmith, engineer, pilot, or mercenary, skilled professionals are available for hire — for a price. Rates may increase based on their expertise or the danger involved. If your Reputation is high enough, some skilled laborers even offer exclusive services, such as crafting unique weapons, modifying gear, or training you in rare techniques.

Not all services cost the same everywhere. Major cities offer better access to skilled labor, while remote locations charge premium rates due to scarcity. Military factions, private companies, and underground groups may also offer services at negotiable but sometimes risky prices. Planning ahead and securing the right services before setting off on an expedition can save time, money, and lives.

A loyal steed, a cunning ferret, a brave falcon. It is not uncommon for characters to train, raise, or receive truehearted beasts to serve and protect them. But companions and mounts are still animals, and they require a firm hand to keep them calm in the heat of action or a kind heart to tend to their injuries and needs.
Mounts and Companions are player minions as outlined in Appendix A and have minimal characteristics to describe them.
Cost. How much a young, trained specimen would cost to purchase from a reputable trainer or breeder with ordinary quality training.
Upkeep. The cost to regularly feed and care for the animal each month in food and other necessities.
Handling VS. When in a stressful situation (such as combat) this is the base VS needed to direct and command the animal with a Beast Handling check, be it riding, hunting, or attacking.

Cat. Domestic cats are common companions across the settled lands. They are often tied to folklore and seen as lucky omens — many academies, libraries, and adventuring guilds tolerate or actively encourage feline residents.

Corvid (Crow/Raven). Ravens and crows are seen in many cultures as omens or spirit messengers. Some priesthoods recognize corvids in their regional iconography, and they are occasionally trained as hunters, scouts, or messengers.

Dog, Herd. Herding dogs remain essential in agricultural regions and across the open ranges. Their ability to respond to whistles and guide livestock makes them invaluable to ranchers, shepherds, and nomadic peoples.
Dog, Hunting. Trained to flush game and track prey. Noble houses across many kingdoms maintain kennels, both for sport and as a mark of prestige.

Dog, War. Military units, particularly in border garrisons, sometimes employ war hounds trained to ignore the clamor of battle. These animals are conditioned to restrain suspects, run down fugitives, and disrupt cavalry charges.
Donkey. A dependable pack animal in countryside, mountain, and desert regions alike, the donkey thrives where larger animals would falter. Prized for endurance over long, rough terrain.
Eagle. The martial tradition of training giant eagles thrives in certain highland and mountain clans. They are symbols of honor and freedom, and their keen sight is unmatched for hunting or scouting.
Falcon. Falconry remains a status symbol among the nobility of many lands. A trained falcon is a mark of aristocratic wealth and is still featured in some noble heraldry.

Horse, Riding. Common throughout the settled lands, riding horses are bred for stamina and steady temperament. They remain widely used despite the slow encroachment of mechanized and arcane transport.

Horse, Warhorse. A legacy of imperial military tradition, warhorses are bred and trained to remain calm in the chaos of battle. The armored cavalry of older kingdoms would not have functioned without these mounts. They still receive honor in parades and ceremonial guard.
Mule. A hardy hybrid, mules are preferred for trade caravans and mountainous routes — inexpensive, sturdy, and capable in terrain where rails and airships cannot reach.

Beyond Skills and Talents lie Disciplines. Disciplines are codified bodies of knowledge that can be studied, practiced, and mastered by any character with the skill, time and will to learn. Whether it’s a sword technique, an arcane spell, a musical performance, or a complex alchemical formula, each discipline represents a capability gained through deliberate effort. Unlike Talents purchased with skill points, you can pursue disciplines at any time. Learning a discipline may require specific prerequisites, resources, research, or training, and often involves meeting a set Victory Sum to unlock its use. With enough dedication, any discipline can become part of your arsenal.

Characters gain access to various disciplines by learning one or more Novice, origins, or special talents. Once you have access to disciplines you can learn them at any time through research, study, or training. Some schools require special talents or deeds to unlock as well.

You can attempt to learn any number of disciplines you have the appropriate talents for, regardless of rank, and you may learn them at any time. Learning a discipline requires 10 or more hours to be spent with a master over no less than one week. During this time, you learn and practice the skill, and at the end make a Memory skill check under your relevant skill, such as Light Weapons, Entertain, Occult, etc. against a VS set by the discipline’s rank.

While it is possible to learn disciplines from personal research and from schools you have not been trained in, it is more difficult. More information on training can be found under “Using Downtime” in Chapter 2. Some disciplines, such as secret techniques or master abilities may require considerably more time or effort to learn and master.
Like many abilities in the Promethium System, Disciplines are categorized by rank to reflect their complexity, potency, and the depth of understanding required to master them.

Basic. Basic Disciplines are accessible to any character with relevant training. These represent foundational knowledge, be it simple spells, elementary formulas, or rudimentary techniques. Normally you automatically learn basic disciplines with your skills, otherwise the VS to learn a Basic Discipline is 20.

Novice. Novice Disciplines require some formal training. You must have at least a Novice skill or general talent in the related field (e.g., Light Weapons, Occult, Entertain, Technology). The VS to learn an Advanced Discipline is 30.

Expert. Expert Disciplines demand considerable specialization and practice. To learn one, you must have at least an Expert from the relevant skill. These are complex, powerful, and often situational. The VS to learn an Expert Discipline is 40.

Master. Master Disciplines represent the pinnacle of specialized knowledge: devastating techniques, miraculous enchantments, or transformative formulas. You must have a Master talent in the associated skill to attempt learning one. The VS to learn a Master Discipline is 50.
Martial techniques embody the essence of sophisticated combat skills and maneuvers. These techniques, accessible through your heraldry allow you to engage in battles with flair skill.

Technique schools are linked to specific weapon skills, offering a diverse range of combat styles and strategies. Utilizing these techniques consumes Vigor, reflecting the physical and mental exertion of combat. In combat, the choice of technique and its timing are strategic decisions, adding depth to combat encounters.

Techniques are actions or reactions which employ special martial skill. Techniques may attack, defend, change your stance, or change your position. When you use a technique, it will consume some amount of your Vigor. If you have insufficient Vigor to pay the cost of an ability, you are unable to use that technique.
All techniques require you to use an action; however, some may be used defensively as reactions or offensively when you take an opening.

The range of a technique is dependent on the weapon or school, unless otherwise noted. For example, sword techniques are generally all melee attacks, bow techniques are ranged, etc. The notable exception is techniques that change your stance or position, in which case they affect you or nearby comrades.
Many techniques result in lingering effects on the target. Such techniques will have their duration listed in their description.
You can attempt to learn any number of techniques from martial schools you have access to. While it is possible to learn techniques from other schools, it is exceedingly difficult.
Most techniques require 1 or more actions on your turn and consume a set amount of Vigor. You cannot use a technique while you are winded.

Techniques that change your position, or your opponent’s position, change your stance. Stances are not the exclusive territory of any one class. Any character with sufficient skill in a weapon may learn a stance taught by its schools. A stance is a deliberate combat posture — a weight distribution, a breath pattern, a readiness — entered at the cost of Vigor and maintained until ended by the wielder, broken by circumstance, or replaced by another stance.

With only a few exceptions, such as the armsman’s signature stance, only one stance may be active at a time. Entering a new stance ends the previous one. Stances are typically broken if the wielder is knocked prone, restrained, disarmed of the relevant weapon, or rendered unable to act. Each stance lists its exact ending conditions in its description.

Stances integrate with the Signature Stance system. A character who has chosen a Signature Stance (Aggressive, Defensive, or Measured) may treat any school stance that shares its temperament — Hard stances as Aggressive, Soft stances as Defensive, Swift stances as Measured — as their Signature Stance for the purposes of class features and stance-dependent abilities. This means a Knight in a Hard Light Weapons stance is also in their Aggressive Signature Stance, and gains its kickers in addition to the school stance's own effects.

Most techniques in this catalog work whether or not the wielder is in a particular stance. Where a stance amplifies a technique, the amplification is listed at the end of the technique entry in italics.

Because techniques usually take only 1 Action, it is possible in many cases to chain techniques, one after another, provided you have sufficient Actions and Vigor. Technique chains can devastate an unprepared opponent. Furthermore, some techniques will provide added effects when they are used at the beginning or end of a chain. For example, a rush proceeding with a power attack will deal additional damage.
These are fundamental defensive reactions that anyone with Novice Defense may make use of.
Vigor: 8
You evade an incoming attack by repositioning. On success, the attack misses, and you move 5 feet in any direction.
Vigor: 8

You use your weapon or shield to deflect an attack. Roll Defense, and add your equipment bonus (e.g., shield, parrying weapon) to the check. On success, the attack is nullified; on a tie, damage is reduced by half.
Vigor: 5

You take the hit head-on, bracing against it. Roll Defense with +1 Bonus Die, on success, reduce the incoming damage by 1d8, regardless of weapon traits. On failure, you still reduce incoming damage by your Armor Rank.
These are the basic reactions that anyone with the Novice Resistance talent can employ.
Mana: 8

You resist physical non-weapon assaults (grapples, stuns, knockdowns, poison gas) through shear brawn. Even on a failure the you reduce its duration of the condition by 1 turn, or impose 1 Challenge on the attacker to maintain the assault.
Mana: 8

You steel your mind against fear, charms, illusions, or psychic intrusion. On a success you not only nullify the assault, you become immune to Assaults that call for Memory, Empathy, Reason, or Resolve until the end of your next turn.
Mana: Special

As a reaction to an opponent in sight casting a spell or ritual with a discernible casting window that targets you, you may attempt a Counter Spell Resistance Reaction. Unlike normal resistance reaction before you roll you must declare your Mana spent. The caster resolves their casting roll (Occult) as normal. You then compare your Mana spent, if it is at least 1/4 or more of the casting cost, you may roll your Counterspell Resistance, and if your beat the casting roll the spell is completely nullified. If you spend is at least 2x the casting cost, the magic rebounds onto the caster on a success.
These fundamental combat maneuvers are available to any character with the Novice Light Weapons, Heavy Weapons, or Hand-to-Hand talents.
Action Cost. Unless stated otherwise, a technique take 1 action on your turn.
Vigor: 6

You launch a brutal all-out attack on an opponent. By Consuming 6 Vigor, you can add +1 bonus dice to the damage of your attack. If you chain a rush followed by a Power Attack, you add an additional +1 bonus dice of damage.
Vigor: 3+

A Feint is a tactical bluff, you give all appearance of one kind of attack while positioning for another. When you use this maneuver, you may inflict 1 to 3 challenges on your opponent’s defense reaction roll against the attack, each challenge you inflict consumes 3 Vigor. If the target chooses not to defend, the feint becomes increasingly lethal. For each challenge you chose to inflict toward defense you add 1 weapon dice of damage to your attack against a target that does not defend.
Vigor: 6

The Grapple technique is a setup and does no damage, however, it changes the stance of both you and target. Make an attack roll with your weapon or unarmed skill. On a hit, you have grappled your opponent. Following a successful grapple, you may make one off-hand or secondary weapon attack that cannot be dodged or use a follow-up technique. Grapple is required to perform any kind of hold, casting throw, or pin. After the first turn, you may choose to maintain the grapple for 2 Vigor. No roll is required unless the target attempts to break the hold with a Resistance (Strength) check VS your Strength Score.
Vigor: 5

Unarmed versus Armed: While both hands are empty, you may attempt to take an enemy’s weapon with a melee attack. You make your Hand-to-Hand attack roll normally. If the attack fails or the target successfully defends, you create an opening for that opponent, and the target may make an attack against you. Otherwise, you disarm them and now have their main-hand weapon.

Armed versus Armed: When two armed opponents face off, the Disarm functions similarly: you make your attack roll, and the target defends. On a success, your opponent’s weapon is knocked from their hand, flying 5 feet (1 hex) in a random direction. However, the defender is not entitled to take an opening in the case that you fail to disarm them.
Vigor: 6

Usually performed with a kick or a weapon such as a staff or spear, but any weapon can trip. With a Trip technique you strike, sweep, or tangle the opponent’s legs to remove their support, causing them to fall. The advantage of a trip maneuver over a tackle is that the attacker maintains distance and balance. Trip is performed like a regular attack: if successful, the target is knocked down. Targets may attempt to defend themselves normally.
Vigor: 9

You blitz a target with a full sprint, moving at least half your total Speed, either slamming into the target or using another technique as you hit them. If you only spend 1 action to rush, you bodyslam the target, dealing unarmed damage and knocking them down. If you have the actions, you can spend 1 action as a follow up to your rush to attack with a weapon or use another technique. If you hit with your follow-up, the target is knocked down. If you Rush an enemy and follow up with a power attack, you deal +1 bonus dice of damage to your power attack.
These basic ranged techniques are available to anyone with a +1 rank in an Aim skill. These focus not only on aiming range weapons but the realities of fighting in mixed combat situations.
Vigor: 9

You strike an adjacent enemy with your ranged weapon and then shoot them. This attack requires a melee attack roll using your ranged weapon skill and Agility. If your target makes a successful Deflect of the melee attack (but not other defense reactions), the shot is wasted. Otherwise, on a hit you deal 1d6 (or bayonet) damage from the melee strike, and unless they deflect the target takes normal ranged weapon damage, and receive 1 charge to your weapon damage Bonus Dice.
If you follow a Rush with a Body Shot you may add your Strength Bonus Dice to the strike damage.
Vigor: 15

With this technique you make a ranged attack in melee while using your ranged weapon, and immediately disengaging. Your attack receives 1 challenge to hit. However, at the end of this action you have backed away 10 ft. At this point you could flee and you will not create an opening.
Vigor: 15

This melee shot is used to trip then finish an enemy. You make a swift feint, then trip your opponent with your weapon. This is a Ranged Weapon (Strength) Check to hit. Unless the opponent defends, creatures of your size or smaller are knocked down, and become prone and you immediately fire a prepared shot to which you receive 1 charge to your weapon damage Bonus Dice. If you miss the initial trip, the technique is wasted.
Vigor: 10

This is practically a trick shot, as you fire your weapon to cause the projectile’s trajectory to curve laterally. This allows you “fire around corners” ignoring the protection of targets with Partial Cover, so long as the target is within long range.

The following basic martial arts techniques are available to anyone with the Novice Hand-to-Hand talent. These techniques use weight, leverage, and spin to add power to unarmed attacks, such as punches, elbow strikes, kicks, knee strikes, and throws.
Vigor: 3

A fast hard punch with your leading hand (the hand closest to the opponent). While it does no more damage than an ordinary unarmed strike (not counting modifiers), the key to a jab is that it does not telegraph, give away the attack, making it harder to defend against. Opponents attempting to defend against your Invisible Jab do so with 1 challenge.
Vigor: 6

Usually reserved for close quarters, this strike is an effective way to deliver force into an opponent, as well as through protective covering. A Shattering Palm blow ignores Armor Rank against creatures, and deals double damage to objects and constructs.
Vigor: 6

The Launch Kick has two uses: as an attack to off-balance or repel a standing opponent, or as a devastating blow against stationary targets and objects. The launch kick is performed by raising the knee to the chest and then launching the foot out, sole first, into the center of an opponent. Against standing opponents, the kick does 1 point of damage for each of your Strength bonus dice on a hit, and a target that is your size or smaller is knocked down and pushed back 5 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Strength Bonus Dice. Against stationery or restrained targets, such as an opponent tied to a tree or a locked door, you deal full unarmed damage +1 bonus dice of damage.
Vigor: 5

Much like the launch kick, the attack is performed by raising the knee to the chest and launching the foot out. However, the power comes from the speed and legs in the Snap Kick, not the hips and weight. Further, the snap kick usually uses only the ball of the foot to make contact. A snap kick does not add strength bonus dice to damage, but it is fast and recovers quickly, allowing you to make two kicks for 1 Action.
Vigor: 9

Named so because in this maneuver the martial artist makes a full rotation of the body, drawing the leg up and spinning the whole body on the ball of the planted foot. The spin ends with the shin used as the contact point for the attack. In a Heel Kick, the maneuver is performed by rotating in the opposite direction, making contact with the heel. Slow and easy to dodge, the attack is nonetheless devastating, dealing +2 bonus dice damage. Defenders receive a Charge on their defense rolls against a roundhouse kick.
Vigor: 6

This maneuver begins exactly like a side kick, however, rather than launch the foot directly at the opponent, you kick to the side and then whip the foot back, heel first, into the defender. This attack is difficult to defend against, resulting in a 1 Challenge penalty to the target’s Defense roll.
Vigor: 5

Often considered the throw of choice for grappling martial arts, this difficult throw involves using an incoming opponent’s inertia and force to throw them over the shoulder. Unlike most techniques, you may use the Shoulder Throw only as a reaction; the character attempting this throw must roll Defense (block) check against an incoming melee attack with a 1 Challenge penalty. If successful, the attack is blocked, and the opponent is lifted off the ground, over your shoulder, and tossed to the ground. The attacker takes a flat 1d10 impact damage from the fall as well as being rendered prone.
Vigor: 6

This throw involves using the manipulation of the target’s center of gravity to spin and toss them to the ground. Usually this involves lowering one’s own center and using the hips for leverage or as a fulcrum. On a hit, you throw the target to the ground for a flat 1d10 impact damage, and they are rendered prone.
Vigor: 7

This technique uses pure strength to lift and hurl a grappled opponent. To perform this technique, you must grapple the target, and they must be within your maximum lift value. You have two options at this point:
Head throw: You toss the target 5 feet away. The target takes 2d10 impact damage (no bonus dice, +1d10 per size above medium) and is prone.

Targeted throw: You use the target as a weapon and throw them at another target, requiring a normal ranged weapon attack roll. As long as the target of the thrown victim is the same size or smaller, the target and thrown victim take 1d10 damage, and both are rendered prone. If the target of the thrown victim is larger, the thrown creature takes 1d10 damage and is rendered prone, while the target is unaffected. The range of the thrown victim is 5 feet, plus 5 feet for each of your Strength Bonus Dice.
Generic Martial Schools
Hard, Soft, and Swift — Light Weapons and Heavy Weapons

Every weapon skill in Radiant Age can be practiced through one of three universal forms: Hard, Soft, and Swift. These are not the names of any particular tradition. They are three answers to the same question every fighter must answer: what happens when two armed combatants meet, and how do you end the meeting in your favor?

Hard forms accept the trade and end it with more weight on their side of the scale. Soft forms refuse the trade and convert the opponent's commitment into the opening. Swift forms outrun the trade entirely, striking and withdrawing before the opponent has finished their first commitment.

Each school presented here is generic by design. The mechanics are fixed; the names and flavor are not. A Hard Light Weapons school in one campaign may be called the Iron Way, the Tyrant's Form, the Mountain's Path, or any name a setting demands. A Swift Heavy Weapons school may be the Whirling Dance, the Storm Walk, or the Way of the Tempest. GMs and players should rename freely. The chassis remains.

The school of force met with force. The practitioner does not flinch from contact. They commit fully to every blow, knowing that the heavier strike ends the trade. The weapon is held forward. The weight is on the front foot. The intent is to break the opponent or break what the opponent carries — armor, weapon, balance, will. Every technique in this school assumes the wielder will be hit at some point, and answers that hit with a heavier one of their own.

Heavy Stance — Vigor 6 to enter. You settle your weight into the strike. While in this stance, you gain +1 Bonus Die to weapon damage rolls, but you may move no more than 5 feet on your turn and suffer -1 Bonus Die on Defense reactions. Ends when you move more than 5 feet on a turn, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Aggressive Stance for class features that key off it.

Pressing Stance — Vigor 10 to enter. You drive forward, refusing your opponent room. While in this stance, when you hit with any Light Weapons attack the target cannot willingly move more than 5 feet on their next turn unless they spend 5 Vigor. On any successful damage roll where your Bonus Dice burst, the target also loses 5 Guard until the end of your next turn. Ends when you fail to make an attack for two consecutive turns, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Aggressive Stance.

Final Stance — Vigor 15 to enter. You set your feet for the last exchange. This stance may only be entered while you are Wounded.
