Severing Wind — Vigor 25. Choose any straight line up to your full Speed in length. Move along this line, making one basic ranged attack against every enemy within range as you pass each. Each attack gains Threat 4. Each target defends separately. The movement does not create Openings. In Measured Stance, gain +1 Bonus Die to damage on each attack.

Final Volley — Vigor 20. Move up to twice your full Speed, then make a single basic ranged attack with +3 Bonus Dice to damage; on a hit, the damage Bonus Dice automatically burst. If this attack reduces the target to 0 Health, your turn does not end — you may take an additional movement of up to your full Speed and one further Action. In Measured Stance, the additional movement after a kill becomes twice your Speed.

Hawk's Hunt — Move and Shoot → Eagle Eye Shot → Slip Shot performed in sequence within a single round, each technique following at least 5 feet of movement. On completion, gain 5 Vigor and your next basic ranged attack before the end of your next turn gains +1 Bonus Die to damage and Threat 2.

Three Arrows — Three basic ranged attacks against the same target in a single turn, by any combination of attacks, techniques, or held shots. If all three hit, the third gains +2 Bonus Dice to damage and Threat 4, applied retroactively.

The school of the conditioned body. The Hard Unarmed practitioner has spent years making their bones harder than the bones of others, their knuckles into hammers, their shins into iron, their grip into a vice. They do not flinch from contact. They invite contact. Every blow they take is one their body is prepared for; every blow they give is meant to break what it touches. Where the Soft school yields and the Swift school evades, the Hard school stands and exchanges, and intends to come out the survivor by simple superiority of structure.

Iron Body Stance — Vigor 6 to enter. You hold yourself low and braced, your weight committed. While in this stance, you gain +1 Bonus Die to Unarmed weapon damage rolls and +3 Guard, but you suffer -1 Bonus Die on Defense reactions and may move no more than 5 feet per turn. Ends when you move more than 5 feet on a turn, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Aggressive Stance for class features.

Anvil Stance — Vigor 10 to enter. You plant your feet and refuse to be moved. While in this stance, you cannot be Pushed, Pulled, or Knocked Prone except by a successful contested check with three or more Challenges. You gain +2 Bonus Dice to Unarmed damage and suffer -1 Bonus Die on attack rolls (your strikes are slower but heavier). Ends when you take any willing movement, are restrained, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Aggressive Stance.

Iron Mountain Stance — Vigor 15 to enter. Your body becomes the unyielding form of long discipline. This stance may only be entered while you are Wounded. While in this stance, you gain +2 Bonus Dice to Unarmed damage, recover 1d4 Durability or Health at the start of each of your turns, and ignore the first source of damage of each type you take each round. Ends when you are no longer Wounded, are knocked unconscious, or choose to end it. Considered the apex stance of the Hard Unarmed school. Qualifies as Aggressive Stance.

Iron Fist — Vigor 2. A fully committed strike with a hardened fist. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. In Aggressive Stance, if any damage Bonus Die bursts on the hit, the target loses 5 Guard until the end of your next turn.

Pressing Step — Vigor 2. You step into your opponent's space as you strike. Make a basic Unarmed attack; on a hit, the target cannot willingly move more than 5 feet on their next turn unless they spend 5 Vigor. In Aggressive Stance, increase the restriction to 10 feet.

Bone-Jarring Punch — Vigor 3. A heavy strike to the head, jaw, or temple meant to rattle. Make a basic Unarmed attack; on a hit, the target suffers 1 Challenge on their next basic attack roll. In Aggressive Stance, the Challenge also applies to their first Defense reaction before the end of their next turn.

Conditioned Strike — Vigor 3. Your conditioned body lets you strike through armor that would turn a weaker hand. Make a basic Unarmed attack ignoring armor of one Quality Rank lower than your Unarmed strike damage rank. In Aggressive Stance, ignore armor of equal Quality Rank as well.

Anvil Punch — Vigor 6. The signature blow of the school — a strike delivered with the full weight of the body behind it. Make an Unarmed attack with +2 Bonus Dice to damage. If the target is Wounded, add 1 Charge to those Bonus Dice. In Aggressive Stance, Large-size and smaller targets are knocked prone on a hit.

Joint Smasher — Vigor 7. A blow aimed specifically at a joint — knee, elbow, shoulder. Make an Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. On a hit dealing Health damage, reduce one of the target's physical Ability Scores (target's choice) by 1 until they receive medical treatment. In Aggressive Stance, you choose which Ability Score is reduced.

Battering Combination — Vigor 8. Two heavy strikes in succession — a punch followed by an elbow, or fist followed by knee. Make two basic Unarmed attacks against a single target. The second gains +1 Bonus Die to damage if the first hit. In Aggressive Stance, if the first attack hit, the target cannot use Defense reactions against the second.

Crushing Throw — Vigor 5. A throw that ends with the target on the ground at your feet. Make an Unarmed attack with -1 Bonus Die to damage. On a hit, the target is knocked prone and creates an Opening that allies may exploit. If you are in a Grapple with the target, this technique automatically succeeds without an attack roll, and you may choose to end the Grapple or maintain it. In Aggressive Stance, also deal an additional 1d6 damage as the target hits the ground.

Sundering Hand — Vigor 7. A strike aimed at the object the enemy holds rather than the enemy. Make an Unarmed attack. If you hit and any damage Bonus Die bursts, choose one of the target's held items and apply your Unarmed damage directly to it, ignoring its Armor Rank. In Aggressive Stance, the chosen item also drops 1 Quality Rank until repaired.

Iron Grip — Vigor 6. You hold the opponent immovably. When you successfully Grapple a target, you may immediately apply 1d6 Unarmed weapon damage at the start of each of your turns while the Grapple holds. You suffer -1 Bonus Die on attack rolls against creatures other than the grappled target while maintaining this hold. In Aggressive Stance, the damage increases to 2d6 per turn.

Bonebreaker Strike — Vigor 12. A strike intended to maim. Make an Unarmed attack with +3 Bonus Dice to damage. If the attack deals Health damage, the target suffers a Broken Bone: lose 1d4 from one physical Ability Score (target's choice) until they receive surgical or magical healing. In Aggressive Stance, you choose which Ability Score is reduced.

Splitting Palms — Vigor 10. Two simultaneous strikes against adjacent enemies. Make a single Unarmed attack that strikes your primary target and one adjacent target. Both defend separately and take full Unarmed weapon damage on a hit. In Aggressive Stance, on a hit, the primary target is also knocked prone.

Pulping Fist — Vigor 14. A blow timed to land precisely between the target's breaths. Make an Unarmed attack with +2 Bonus Dice to damage; on a hit, these dice automatically burst. The target is Stunned until the end of their next turn. In Aggressive Stance, the target is Stunned until the end of your next turn instead.

Heart-Striker Palm — Vigor 12. An open-palm strike to the chest that disrupts internal rhythm. Make an Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. If the attack deals Health damage, the target suffers Bleeding (internal — 1d6 per round) until they receive medical attention. In Aggressive Stance, the Bleeding damage Bonus Die bursts on 9 or 10.

Stand and Crush — Vigor 10. A planted stance executed mid-combat. Until the end of your next turn, you cannot be Pushed, Pulled, or Knocked Prone, and you gain +2 Bonus Dice to Unarmed damage. You cannot move further than 5 feet during this time. Requires Aggressive Stance to use. While maintained, this technique's effects stack with any active Hard Unarmed stance.

Shieldbreaker Hand — Vigor 12. A strike aimed at the defender's defense. Make an Unarmed attack targeting an opponent's shield. The attack ignores the shield's cover bonus and applies your Unarmed damage directly to the shield's Durability, ignoring its Armor Rank. In Aggressive Stance, if the shield breaks, the wielder is also knocked prone.

World-Breaking Fist — Vigor 22. The strike that the Hard Unarmed school exists to deliver. Make an Unarmed attack with +4 Bonus Dice to damage; on a hit, all damage Bonus Dice automatically burst. The target is knocked back 10 feet and falls prone. In Aggressive Stance, the attack gains Threat equal to the number of distinct Hard Unarmed techniques you have used since the start of the encounter, to a maximum of Threat 6.

Tyrant's Embrace — Vigor 25. A spinning blow with the full reach of the body. Make a single Unarmed attack that strikes every enemy within your reach (typically 5 feet, more with reach techniques). Each defends separately. On each hit, apply Unarmed weapon damage with +2 Bonus Dice and knock the target prone. In Aggressive Stance, the attack cannot be answered with Defense reactions.

Skull-Crushing Doom — Vigor 20. The killing strike, delivered as if there will be no second blow. Make an Unarmed attack with +3 Bonus Dice to damage. If the damage dealt exceeds the target's remaining Durability, double the overflow damage applied to Health. In Aggressive Stance, the doubled overflow applies even when Durability is not exhausted.

Killing Hand — Vigor 25. The technique the Hard Unarmed school is feared for in any setting. Make an Unarmed attack against a target at 0 Durability or below half Health. On a hit, the target must succeed a Resistance (Stamina) check VS your attack roll or be killed instantly. In Aggressive Stance, if the target is below 25% Health, no Resistance check is allowed.

The Three Strikes — Iron Fist → Anvil Punch → any Expert or Master Hard Unarmed technique against the same target within one round. If all three techniques hit, the final technique's damage is doubled when the target is prone, restrained, Stunned, or grappled at the moment the strike lands.

The Crushing Press — Pressing Step → Joint Smasher → Bonebreaker Strike performed in any order against the same target within one round. On completion (all three succeed), the target suffers 1 Challenge on all attack rolls and Defense reactions until the end of their next turn.

The school of yielding control. The Soft Unarmed practitioner does not meet force with force. They meet force with absence. The opponent's attack becomes the opening for the opponent's fall. The strike that would have broken a Hard practitioner's guard finds nothing to hold, and the attacker's own momentum carries them to the ground. Throws, joint locks, redirections, the patient grip that becomes the inescapable hold. The Soft school does not need to strike to win, and often does not need to strike at all. The opponent commits, and the opponent is taken to the floor.

Yielding Stance — Vigor 6 to enter. You hold yourself loose and ready to move with any attack. While in this stance, you gain +1 Bonus Die to Defense reactions made against melee attacks, and after any successful Defense reaction, your next basic Unarmed attack before the end of your next turn gains +1 Bonus Die to damage. Ends when you make an Unarmed attack action while not following a successful Defense, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Defensive Stance for class features.

Mirror Stance — Vigor 10 to enter. You move only when your opponent moves. While in this stance, when an adjacent enemy makes a melee attack that misses you, you may immediately make a basic Unarmed attack against them with no Vigor cost; the attack gains +1 Bonus Die to damage. You may benefit from Mirror Stance no more than once per turn. Ends when you take a non-reaction attack action, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Defensive Stance.

Master Aikidoka Stance — Vigor 15 to enter. You appear to act last, deliberately. While in this stance, you may delay your Initiative by up to 10 in any combat round, choosing to act at any point during the round after that delay. All melee attacks against you made by creatures who acted before you in the round suffer 1 Challenge. When you do act, your first attack or reaction this round gains +2 Bonus Dice to damage. Ends at the end of the round; must be re-entered each round at the listed Vigor cost. Considered the apex stance of the Soft Unarmed school. Qualifies as Defensive Stance.

Wrist Grab — Vigor 2. A simple catch and hold of the opponent's weapon hand. Make an Agility check VS the target's Strength or Agility Score. On success, the target cannot make weapon attacks with that hand until they spend an Action to break free, succeeding a Strength check VS your skill roll. In Defensive Stance, the target's break-free check is at 1 Challenge.

Catch and Toss — Vigor 3. Reaction. When you successfully Defend against a melee attack from an adjacent enemy, you may immediately attempt to throw them. They make Resistance (Strength) VS your Defense roll or are moved 5 feet in a direction of your choice. If thrown into terrain, they fall prone. In Defensive Stance, the Resistance is at 1 Challenge.

Yielding Step — Vigor 2. Reaction. When targeted by a melee attack from an adjacent enemy, move 5 feet in any direction without creating an Opening. If you move out of the attacker's reach, the attack misses automatically. If you remain in reach, the attack proceeds normally. In Defensive Stance, gain +1 Bonus Die on the Defense reaction if you choose to defend after the step.

Counter-Punch — Vigor 3. Reaction. When an adjacent enemy makes a melee attack against you and misses, you may immediately make a basic Unarmed attack against them with no damage bonus. In Defensive Stance, the Counter-Punch gains +1 Bonus Die to damage.

Hip Throw — Vigor 6. A major throw using the hip as fulcrum. Make an Unarmed attack with -1 Bonus Die to damage. On a hit, the target is moved 5 feet and falls prone. If you are in a Grapple with the target, this technique automatically succeeds and you may break the Grapple as part of the throw. In Defensive Stance, deal an additional 1d6 damage as the target hits the ground.

Counter-Time Hand — Vigor 7. Reaction. When an enemy declares a Technique against you that requires multiple Actions to complete, you may interrupt it by making a Grapple attempt against them. If the Grapple succeeds, the enemy's Technique fails but the Vigor is still spent. The Grapple is established as normal. In Defensive Stance, if the Technique was Expert or Master rank, gain Threat 2 on the Grapple attempt.

Drawing the Storm — Vigor 5. Stance change. Until the end of your next turn, enemies who attack you in melee suffer 1 Challenge on their first attack roll against you. When such an attack misses, you recover 2 Vigor. In Defensive Stance, missed attacks also create an Opening you may exploit.

Riposte Hand — Vigor 7. Reaction. After successfully Defending against an adjacent enemy's melee attack, make a basic Unarmed attack against them with +1 Bonus Die to damage. In Defensive Stance, the Riposte gains an additional +1 Bonus Die to damage.

Defense Flow Hand — Vigor 6. You set yourself to defend repeatedly. Until the start of your next turn, you may use Defense reactions against up to two different attacks without paying additional Vigor for the second reaction. In Defensive Stance, the second Defense reaction also gains +1 Bonus Die.

Joint Lock — Vigor 8. A controlling hold that disables an enemy's limb. Make an Agility check VS the target's Strength Score (with 1 Challenge if you are not already in a Grapple with them). On success, you place the target in a Joint Lock: they cannot use the affected limb (and any item held in it) and suffer 1 Challenge on all physical actions until they spend an Action to break free, succeeding a Strength check VS your original skill roll. In Defensive Stance, the target's break-free check is at 1 Challenge.

Perfect Throw — Vigor 10. A throw that ends with the target pinned. Make an Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. On a hit, the target is moved 5 feet, falls prone, and is automatically established in a Grapple with you. Released or escaping the Grapple requires a successful Strength check VS your original attack roll. In Defensive Stance, also deal an additional 1d6 damage as the target lands.

Iron Counter — Vigor 12. Reaction. After a successful Defense reaction against an adjacent enemy's melee attack, make a basic Unarmed attack against them with +2 Bonus Dice to damage and Threat 2. In Defensive Stance, the Threat increases to 4.

Time-Stopping Touch — Vigor 14. Reaction. When an enemy declares a Technique against you, force them to make a Resolve check VS your Defense roll to complete it. On failure, the Technique fails but the Vigor is still spent. This may be used against any Technique that targets you specifically. In Defensive Stance, the Resolve check is at 1 Challenge.

Mirror's Hand — Vigor 12. Reaction. The next melee attack made against you within the next round is deflected onto an adjacent enemy of your choice. The original attacker rolls their attack against your Defense; on success, the damage is applied to the redirected target instead. No deflection is possible if no adjacent target exists. In Defensive Stance, you may also choose to deflect to any enemy within 10 feet rather than only adjacent.

Cutting Hand Counter — Vigor 11. Reaction. After a successful Defense reaction, make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. If the attack deals Health damage, the target suffers Bleeding (1d6 per round, from a struck pressure point or internal damage) until they receive medical attention. In Defensive Stance, the Bleeding damage Bonus Die bursts on 9 or 10.

Master's Sense — Vigor 10. Stance change. Choose one opponent within 30 feet you have seen attack in this combat. Until the end of your next turn, you gain +2 Bonus Dice on Defense reactions against that opponent and +1 Bonus Die on Unarmed damage rolls against them. You may only have one Master's Sense active at a time. In Defensive Stance, the duration extends to the end of the combat or until you choose to end it.

Untouchable Form — Vigor 22. Until the end of your next turn, all melee attacks against you automatically miss. You may not make attack actions during this time, though you may make Defense reactions for allies within your reach and use techniques that do not require an attack. In Defensive Stance, you may also make one basic Unarmed attack at the end of the duration as a free action with +1 Bonus Die to damage.

Storm's Eye Hand — Vigor 20. Reaction. When targeted by a melee attack you can see, you may consume your Destined state to redirect the full intended damage of that attack back to the attacker. The attacker takes the damage they would have dealt to you, with all its Bonus Dice and effects. You take no damage from the attack. In Defensive Stance, the redirected damage gains +2 Bonus Dice.

Mirror's Master — Vigor 25. Until the end of your next turn, whenever an enemy makes a melee attack against you, they roll the attack twice and take the worse result. Any attack that misses you deals Unarmed weapon damage (yours, no Bonus Dice) to the attacker as the failed strike turns on them. In Defensive Stance, missed attackers also drop 5 Guard until the end of their next turn.

Final Yield — Vigor 20. Reaction. When targeted by any attack — melee or ranged, weapon or technique — you may redirect it to any other valid target within the original attacker's reach. The attacker rolls again against the new target, using all original modifiers, against the new target's Defense. The original attack against you is treated as never made. In Defensive Stance, you may also choose to be the new target yourself, gaining +3 Bonus Dice on the resulting Defense if you do.

Wave Pattern Hand — Wrist Grab → Joint Lock → Hip Throw performed in sequence against the same target within one round. On completion (the Wrist Grab holds, the Joint Lock succeeds, and the Hip Throw hits), the Hip Throw deals +3 Bonus Dice to damage and the target also gains 1 rank of Fatigue from the prolonged hold.

Mirror's Cycle Hand — Three reactions performed in a single round against attacks from adjacent enemies. On completion, gain one Bonus Action that may be used during your next turn to take any Action requiring a single Action.

The school of evasion and barrage. The Swift Unarmed practitioner is rarely standing where the enemy expected. They flow around the strike, deliver three light blows in the space of one heavy one, and are six feet away before the opponent has finished their motion. Their weapons are hand, foot, knee, elbow — all of them in motion at once. They strike from the flank. They strike from below. They strike from above. They retreat. They circle. They strike again. Where the Hard school stands and the Soft school yields, the Swift school is never in the place the strike was aimed at.

Light Step Stance — Vigor 6 to enter. You hold yourself ready to move at all times, weight on the balls of the feet. While in this stance, you gain +1 Bonus Die to Defense reactions and may move 5 feet without spending an Action once per turn; this movement does not create Openings even if you are within enemy reach. Ends when you fail to use the free movement for two consecutive turns, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Measured Stance for class features.

Hawk Step Stance — Vigor 10 to enter. You move and strike in the same motion. While in this stance, your basic Unarmed attacks gain Threat 2 against any target within your reach. If you moved 10 feet or more on your turn before the attack, increase to Threat 4. Ends when you fail to move at all on two consecutive turns, are knocked prone, or choose to end it. This stance qualifies as Measured Stance.

Dance of Hands Stance — Vigor 15 to enter. Hand and foot are continually in motion. While in this stance, you may make up to three basic Unarmed attacks per turn against any targets within your reach as a single Action. Each attack costs 5 Vigor and suffers -1 Bonus Die to damage. You may move 5 feet between each attack without creating Openings. Ends after one round; must be re-entered each round at the listed Vigor cost. Considered the apex stance of the Swift Unarmed school. Qualifies as Measured Stance.

Quick Strike Hand — Vigor 2. A fast strike with no commitment. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to the attack roll (not damage). You receive 1 Challenge on Defense reactions until the end of your next turn. In Measured Stance, the Challenge is removed.

Slip and Strike — Vigor 3. You step inside the opponent's reach as you strike. Move up to 5 feet in any direction as part of this action, then make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. The movement does not create Openings. In Measured Stance, you may move 10 feet instead.

Skirmisher's Hand — Vigor 2. Strike and immediately reposition. Make a basic Unarmed attack. Immediately after the attack resolves, move up to 5 feet in any direction without creating an Opening, even if you are within enemy reach. In Measured Stance, move up to 10 feet instead.

Twin Hand — Vigor 3. Two simultaneous strikes — fist and elbow, or fist and knee. Make a basic Unarmed attack with your primary hand or limb, then a free additional basic Unarmed attack at -1 Bonus Die to damage. The second attack does not consume an Action. In Measured Stance, the second attack suffers no damage penalty.

Eagle's Punch — Vigor 5. A swift but precise strike followed by an exit. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to the attack roll. Immediately after the attack resolves, move up to 10 feet without creating an Opening. In Measured Stance, gain +1 Bonus Die to damage as well.

Flicker Strikes — Vigor 6. A rapid combination — fist, fist, elbow. Make two basic Unarmed attacks against the same target with -1 Bonus Die to damage on each. If both hit, the target suffers Bleeding (1d4 per round) until they receive medical attention. In Measured Stance, the second attack does not suffer the damage penalty.

Lunge and Strike — Vigor 7. Extend your reach by 5 feet for a single strike. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to the attack roll at extended reach. After the attack resolves (hit or miss), return to your starting square as a free action. No Openings are created by this movement. In Measured Stance, extend reach by 10 feet instead.

Whirling Hand — Vigor 8. Strike, move, strike again. Make a basic Unarmed attack against one target. Move up to 5 feet. If you end your movement adjacent to another enemy, you may make a second basic Unarmed attack against that target at -1 Bonus Die to damage. In Measured Stance, the second attack suffers no damage penalty.

Tempo Break Hand — Vigor 5. Reaction. When an adjacent enemy hits you with a melee attack, you may immediately move 5 feet without creating an Opening, and create an Opening on the attacker that your allies may exploit until the end of your next turn. In Measured Stance, move up to 10 feet instead.

Phantom Step Hand — Vigor 6. Move up to your full Speed in feet without creating Openings, even when moving through enemy reach. Until the end of your next turn, you gain +1 Bonus Die to Defense reactions. In Measured Stance, the Defense bonus extends to +2 Bonus Dice.

Killing Tempo Hands — Vigor 12. Three strikes against the same target with escalating force. Make three basic Unarmed attacks against the same target at -2 Bonus Dice to damage on each. The first hit deals normal damage with the penalty; the second adds 1 Charge to the damage roll; the third adds 2 Charges. Each attack resolves independently against the target's Defense. In Measured Stance, remove the damage penalty entirely.

Edged Dance Strikes — Vigor 10. Move through the enemies, striking each. Move up to 30 feet in a path of your choosing. Make one basic Unarmed attack with -1 Bonus Die to damage against up to three targets you pass within your reach. Each defends separately. In Measured Stance, remove the damage penalty and gain Threat 2 on each attack.

Storm of Hands — Vigor 14. Hand and foot flicker constantly. Make four basic Unarmed attacks split between primary and secondary limbs (any combination of fist, knee, elbow, kick). Each attack suffers -2 Bonus Dice to damage and is resolved independently against any targets within your reach. In Measured Stance, the damage penalty is reduced to -1 Bonus Die.

Cutting Wind Hands — Vigor 11. Stance change. Until the end of your next turn, your basic Unarmed attacks gain Threat 4 and you may move 5 feet without creating an Opening with each attack you make. Requires Measured Stance to use. The duration extends to the end of the round when used in Measured Stance.

Vanishing Strike Hand — Vigor 12. Strike and disappear. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to damage. Immediately after the attack resolves, make a Stealth check VS the highest Awareness of any enemy that can see you. On success, you are Hidden until your next attack action or until an enemy passes a contested Awareness check to spot you. In Measured Stance, gain +1 Bonus Die on the Stealth check.

Tempo Lock Strike — Vigor 10. A strike that disrupts the target's rhythm. Make a basic Unarmed attack with +1 Bonus Die to attack roll. On a successful hit dealing damage, the target loses one Action on their next turn; their entire action economy is disrupted by the precision of your strike. In Measured Stance, on a hit where any damage Bonus Die bursts, the target also loses their reaction for one round.

Hundred Hands — Vigor 22. A flurry of strikes so rapid the eye loses count. Make as many basic Unarmed attacks as your Speed Bonus Dice count against any targets within your reach during your movement. You may move up to your full Speed as part of this action. Each attack suffers -3 Bonus Dice to damage. Whenever any damage Bonus Die bursts during this technique, recover 5 Vigor. In Measured Stance, the damage penalty is reduced to -2 Bonus Dice and the Vigor recovery increases to 8 Vigor per burst.

Untouchable Step Hand — Vigor 20. Until the end of your next turn, you are immune to Openings and to opportunity attacks. Your basic Unarmed attacks during this duration gain +1 Bonus Die to attack roll and you may move 10 feet without spending an Action once per turn. In Measured Stance, the duration extends to the end of the round.

Severing Wind Hand — Vigor 25. Choose any straight line up to your full Speed in length. Move along this line, making one basic Unarmed attack against every enemy you pass within your reach. Each attack gains Threat 4. Each target defends separately. The movement does not create Openings. In Measured Stance, gain +1 Bonus Die to damage on each attack.

Final Strike Hand — Vigor 20. Move up to twice your full Speed, then make one perfect Unarmed attack with +3 Bonus Dice to damage; on a hit, the damage Bonus Dice automatically burst. If this attack reduces the target to 0 Health, your turn does not end — you may take an additional movement of up to your full Speed and one further Action. In Measured Stance, the additional movement after a kill becomes twice your Speed.

Hawk's Descent Hand — Eagle's Punch → Lunge and Strike → Phantom Step Hand performed in any order within a single round. On completion, gain 5 Vigor and you may make one additional basic Unarmed attack as a free action with +1 Bonus Die to damage.

Three Strikes Hand — Three basic Unarmed attacks against the same target in a single turn, by any combination of attacks, techniques, or secondary-limb strikes. If all three hit, the third gains +2 Bonus Dice to damage and Threat 4, applied retroactively.
Aura Techniques
The Riders, Stances, and Manifestations of Aura

Aura Techniques are the martial skills of those who have cultivated an Aura Core, and the ability to imbue their attacks with Mana. They are not, by and large, a system of standalone attacks. They are riders: workings that attach to other martial actions — basic attacks, weapon-skill techniques, defense reactions, movement — and transform them through the application of cultivated Aura.

This is the fundamental difference between Aura and the elemental magic of Arcane Arms, or the spoken magic of Incantare. A Mage casts a spell that does a thing; a Mage with a wand throws fire. An Aura-cultivator does not throw aura — they pour aura into the strike they were going to make anyway, and the strike that lands is no longer ordinary. Aura Strike does not exist as a punch. Aura Strike exists as the punch you were going to throw, made into something that cuts through stone.

Aura Techniques cost Mana, not Vigor. This is the second fundamental distinction. The Aura-cultivator's body is the storage, the channel, and the focus; the Mana drawn from their reserve fuels the working. A character with no Mana cannot use Aura Techniques, regardless of their Aura Star count or martial skill. A character with deep Mana and high Aura cultivation can sustain a battle's worth of Aura Techniques and still have reserves left.

Most Aura Techniques add the character's Aura Star Bonus Dice to a roll — damage, attack, defense, an opposed check. The Aura Star is the scaling currency of the cultivator: every Star gained translates directly into the working effect of every Aura Technique they know. A character with three Aura Stars casts the same technique as a character with seven, but the seven-Star version is qualitatively different. Scaling notes appear in italics at the end of each technique's entry.

Most Aura Techniques are riders. A rider activates when its trigger condition is met — typically when the character makes a specific kind of attack, takes a specific kind of action, or uses a martial technique from another discipline. The rider modifies the underlying action; both occur simultaneously, with the rider's effects layered onto the base action's. Riders never replace the underlying action: an Aura Strike happens with the punch, not instead of it.

A small number of Aura Techniques are stances: persistent workings that modify the cultivator's body or surroundings for as long as they are maintained. Stances have entry costs and ongoing effects, end on specific conditions, and can be replaced by other stances. Only one Aura stance may be active at a time. A small number are standalone actions: workings cast directly without a rider trigger, typically for utility, sensing, or pure projection of Aura into the world.

The foundation. Three of these — Aura Body, Aura Strike, Aura Shot — are taught to every cultivator the day their first Aura Star opens. The rest fill out the working vocabulary of the cultivator's first years: extension of reach, sensing of presence, the small movement that carries them six feet farther than any non-cultivator could go in the same step. Few of these techniques will end a fight on their own. Almost all of them will shape a fight that the cultivator is already winning.
Aura Body

blocks.text: Mana: 4 Trigger: Stance: 1 Action to enter Range: Self Duration: Concentration up to 10 minutes

blocks.html: You sheathe your body in a thin layer of cultivated Aura — invisible to most observers, perceptible to other Aura-cultivators as a faint shimmer at the edges of your form. While the stance holds, you add your Aura Star Bonus Dice to your Guard score, gain +1 Bonus Die on Resistance (Stamina) checks, and reduce all incoming physical damage by 1 per Aura Star.

Aura Body is the first stance any cultivator learns and the last one they let lapse. A cultivator with Aura Body active walks into a fight that would maim a non-cultivator and walks out with bruises. The stance does not block magical attacks meaningfully — it is forged for the meeting of body and weapon. Against a sword, an arrow, a fist, the Aura sheathe holds. Against a fireball, the cultivator burns like anyone else.
Aura Star Scaling. The damage reduction scales directly with Aura Stars (1 reduction per Star). At Aura Star 4+, the stance also blocks the first point of damage from each elemental source per round.
Aura Strike

blocks.text: Mana: 4 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack or martial technique attack Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: You pour cultivated Aura into the strike as it lands. Add your Aura Star Bonus Dice to the damage roll of the triggering attack. The added damage is Aura damage — a damage type that bypasses mundane armor reduction (though Armor Rank still applies normally) and treats most Magical and Elemental Resistances as half-effective.

Aura Strike is the cultivator's primary expression of cultivated power in combat. Every Aura cultivator who ever swings a blade or throws a punch in earnest learns this rider first, and most of them use it on every meaningful strike. The technique is cheap, fast, and reliable — 4 Mana per swing, no casting time, no telegraphed buildup. The blow that arrived is the blow that was already coming, but the blow that landed is heavier.
Aura Star Scaling. The base scaling is automatic — each Aura Star adds another die to the damage roll. At Aura Star 5+, the Aura damage also gains Threat 2.
Aura Shot

blocks.text: Mana: 4 Trigger: Rider on basic ranged attack or ranged technique attack Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: You channel cultivated Aura into a ranged attack as it leaves your hand or your weapon. Add your Aura Star Bonus Dice to the damage roll of the triggering attack. The added damage is Aura damage and treats armor and resistance as Aura Strike does.

Aura Shot is the ranged counterpart to Aura Strike, and works on any ranged attack: bow, crossbow, thrown weapon, sling, even an unarmed projectile thrown at speed. The Aura that wraps the arrow is not visible to the target before impact; the strike arrives with the speed of the underlying weapon and the weight of the cultivator's cultivated power.
Aura Star Scaling. The base scaling is automatic — each Aura Star adds another die to the damage roll. At Aura Star 5+, the Aura damage also gains Threat 2.
Aura Edge

blocks.text: Mana: 3 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack with bladed or pointed weapon Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: Aura forms at the edge of your weapon, extending its reach. The triggering attack's effective reach is increased by 5 feet for this single strike. The extended portion is a translucent shimmer of cultivated Aura that mimics the weapon's shape — a longsword becomes a longsword with a phantom blade five feet beyond its tip, a spear projects another length forward, a dagger reaches into the space a sword would.

Aura Edge is the rider that lets a swordsman strike a polearm wielder before the polearm wielder strikes them. It is one of the first riders any cultivator with a bladed weapon learns; the strategic value of an extra five feet of reach in a duel is significant enough that many traditions teach it before Aura Strike.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the reach extension per 2 Aura Stars (Star 2: +10 feet; Star 4: +15 feet; Star 6: +20 feet).
Aura Sense

blocks.text: Mana: 5 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (30-foot radius) Duration: 1 minute

blocks.html: You extend your awareness through cultivated Aura. For the duration, you perceive the precise location and intensity of every Aura-bearing source within 30 feet of you — every cultivator, every enchanted weapon, every magical creature, every living being with magical aptitude. You sense their direction, distance, and rough magnitude (a Master cultivator burns bright; a sleeping kitten flickers).

Aura Sense is the cultivator's first detection working. It does not see through walls — it senses Aura through them. A hidden cultivator behind a stone door is not hidden from Aura Sense; only a creature with no Aura signature whatsoever (most non-cultivator humanoids, most ordinary beasts) can move undetected through a sensing cultivator's awareness. The working is the reason that two cultivators rarely surprise each other; the moment they enter each other's range, both know exactly where the other stands.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 30 feet to the radius per Aura Star. At Aura Star 4+, the working also penetrates magical concealment and invisibility within range.
Aura Step

blocks.text: Mana: 3 Trigger: 1 Action (does not consume Movement) Range: Self (10 feet) Duration: Instant

blocks.html: You push against the air with cultivated Aura and translate yourself up to 10 feet in any direction. The movement does not create Openings, does not require a path along the ground (you may step laterally through narrow gaps, briefly skim over water, or cross a small pit), and does not provoke any reactions tied to movement. You land in a stable stance and may immediately attack, defend, or take another Action.

Aura Step is not flight. The cultivator's feet leave the ground for the duration of the step and land at the destination; there is no sustained air-walking. But the step covers ground impossible for an uncultivated body: a ten-foot leap from a standstill, a horizontal traverse over a chasm, a sudden shift sideways during a swordfight that leaves the opponent's cut passing through empty air.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the step distance per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the step does not require a path of any kind — pass through ordinary cover during the step.
Aura Sheathe

blocks.text: Mana: 3 Trigger: Rider on Defense reaction Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: You channel cultivated Aura into your Defense reaction. Add your Aura Star Bonus Dice to the Defense roll of the triggering reaction. If you successfully defend, the attacker also takes 1 point of Aura damage per Aura Star from the brief discharge as their attack meets your sheathe.

Aura Sheathe is the rider that lets a swordsman parry a blow that should have killed them. The added Bonus Dice make the Defense more likely to succeed; the discharge punishment makes successful Defenses costly for the attacker. A cultivator who has Aura Sheathe prepared can afford to let an enemy attack them — every successful parry hurts the attacker, every missed attack costs them nothing on the cultivator's part.
Aura Star Scaling. The Bonus Die addition is automatic per Aura Star. At Aura Star 4+, the discharge damage on successful Defense increases to 2 per Aura Star.
Aura Pressure

blocks.text: Mana: 4 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (15-foot radius) Duration: 1 minute Resistance: Resolve

blocks.html: You release a wave of dense Aura — not as attack, but as presence. The very space around you grows heavy with cultivated will. Hostile creatures of significantly lower cultivation than you who can perceive you within 15 feet must succeed Resistance (Resolve) VS your Aura skill roll or be Shaken for the duration (1 Challenge on attack rolls, Defense reactions, and Resistance checks while they remain within range).

Aura Pressure is the working that ends bandit ambushes before they begin. A high-cultivation Knight walks into a room of common cutpurses and the cutpurses' will simply gives way — they step backward, they lower their weapons, they decide they have somewhere else to be. The working does not affect creatures of equal or greater cultivation than the cultivator, nor does it affect creatures incapable of recognizing what they are seeing (most beasts, most undead, most truly fearless opponents).
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the radius per Aura Star. At Aura Star 4+, the Resistance is at 1 Challenge for affected creatures.

Ten workings. At this rank, cultivated Aura begins to leave the cultivator's body. Aura Slash sends a wave of compressed cutting force across thirty feet of empty air. Aura Cleave splits one strike into two. Aura Wings carry the cultivator briefly through the sky. The moment a watching crowd realizes the cultivator is not bound by the rules that bind ordinary bodies — that moment is the moment of the Novice tier. Most Aura Techniques in this tier are still riders, but many of them produce effects no rider in the Basic tier can match.
Aura Slash

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack with bladed or pointed weapon Range: Medium Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You channel the cut into a wave of compressed Aura that travels across the battlefield. The triggering attack instead targets a creature within Medium range; the wave streaks in a straight line from you to the target, dealing the attack's weapon damage plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage. Any creature in the line between you and the target must succeed Resistance (Speed) VS your attack roll or take half damage (no Bonus Dice).

Aura Slash is the working that makes a swordsman a ranged threat. The cut leaves the blade as a horizontal arc of pale energy and crosses the open ground at speed; the target sees the blade swing and the arc arrives a fraction of a second after. The technique requires a bladed or pointed weapon (a blunt weapon does not produce the same coherent wave). At Novice rank, the Slash deals respectable damage. At Master rank, the Slash separates whole formations.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 10 feet to the maximum range per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the Slash continues past the primary target, striking up to one additional target in line beyond at full damage.
Aura Cleave

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack Range: Self (extends reach by 5 feet for the cleave) Duration: Instant

blocks.html: You extend the strike into a sweeping arc of cultivated Aura. The triggering attack hits its primary target normally; a second strike of the same kind, at half damage Bonus Dice (round down), automatically hits one additional enemy adjacent to the primary target within your reach. The cleave does not require an additional attack roll — if the primary attack hit, the cleave hit.

Aura Cleave is the spell that turns a swordsman into a formation-breaker. A single cut becomes two adjacent strikes; a single charge through a line of soldiers becomes three falling bodies instead of one. The technique works with any melee weapon, but is particularly effective with broad-cutting weapons (sabers, broadswords, battle axes) whose physical motion already approximates the working's geometry.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 1 additional adjacent target struck per 2 Aura Stars (Star 2: 3 targets total; Star 4: 4 targets; Star 6: 5 targets).
Aura Burst

blocks.text: Mana: 10 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (15-foot radius) Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You release a concentrated burst of cultivated Aura in all directions. All other creatures within 15 feet must succeed Resistance (Speed) VS your Aura skill roll or take damage equal to 2d8 plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage, and be pushed 10 feet directly away from you. Targets who succeed take half damage and are pushed half the distance.

Aura Burst is the working the cultivator uses when they are surrounded. The pulse does not discriminate between friend and foe; allies in the radius are affected normally. The cultivator at the center of an Aura Burst becomes briefly the center of a vacated zone — every creature around them either backed away or was thrown back. A swordsman who has been bound by three opponents simultaneously becomes a swordsman with thirty feet of fighting room.
Aura Star Scaling. Add +1d8 Aura damage and 5 feet to the push distance per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the radius extends to 20 feet.
Aura Heart

blocks.text: Mana: 10 Trigger: Stance: 2 Actions to enter Range: Self Duration: 1 hour or until ended

blocks.html: You enter a sustained state of cultivated readiness. Your Aura runs through your body in a slow steady current, ready to surge into any working. While the stance holds, all Aura Technique Mana costs are reduced by 1 (minimum 1), you gain +1 Bonus Die to Initiative checks, and your basic melee and ranged attacks gain a small amount of Aura damage equal to your Aura Star count (without any Mana spent).

Aura Heart is the stance the cultivator walks the world in. It is not aggressive — the small passive Aura damage is incidental, the Mana reduction is modest. But it is the stance that means the cultivator is always ready. A Mage casting Mage Armor before the fight begins is a Mage who anticipated trouble. A cultivator in Aura Heart is a cultivator who never stopped anticipating trouble.
Aura Star Scaling. Add +1 to the passive Aura damage per Aura Star above the first. At Aura Star 5+, also gain +1 Bonus Die on Resistance (Stamina) checks.
Aura Compression

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Trigger: 1 Action (held until next attack) Range: Self Duration: Until next attack or end of turn

blocks.html: You compress your gathered Aura into a single coiled pulse, holding it in reserve. Your next basic melee or ranged attack made before the end of your turn deals double Aura Bonus Dice damage (the dice roll twice, not the result doubled). After the attack resolves or your turn ends without using the pulse, Aura Compression dissipates without effect.

Aura Compression is the working that lets a cultivator deliver one heavy blow when one blow is what the situation requires. The reduced flexibility — you must follow up with an attack the same turn — is the working's tradeoff: it is not for a cultivator who needs to remain reactive. It is for the cultivator who has decided the next attack will land hard, and is willing to invest in making sure it does.
Aura Star Scaling. Add an additional double of the Aura Bonus Dice per 2 Aura Stars (so at Aura Star 4, the dice roll three times).
Aura Shield

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Trigger: Reaction (triggered by attack against ally within 10 feet) Range: Close Duration: Instant

blocks.html: Reaction. When an ally within 10 feet of you is targeted by an attack, you project a sheet of compressed Aura between attacker and ally. The ally gains +2 Bonus Dice on the Defense reaction to that attack, plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice. If they successfully Defend, the shield discharges into the attacker: 1d8 Aura damage per Aura Star.

Aura Shield is the bodyguard's working. A high-cultivation Knight walking next to a vulnerable companion can interpose Aura between their companion and any attack within ten feet — the shield is invisible until it deflects, and deflects with prejudice. The technique does not protect the bodyguard themselves; for that, the cultivator uses Aura Body. Aura Shield is for the people around them.
Aura Star Scaling. Add +1 Bonus Die to the Defense reaction and +1d8 discharge damage per Aura Star.
Aura Wings

blocks.text: Mana: 10 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self Duration: 1 minute

blocks.html: You manifest wings of luminous Aura along your back — visible, distinctive, often the cultivator's signature. The wings grant a Fly Speed equal to your normal walking Speed for the duration. You may hover, dive, climb, and maneuver freely. The wings persist even when you are not actively flying; they fold against your back when you walk, unfurl when you take to the air.

Aura Wings are not subtle. The cultivator with them deployed is the cultivator everyone in the engagement is looking at. Many cultivators avoid the working in delicate situations for this reason. Others — particularly Knights and Striders — wear their Aura Wings as a deliberate statement of identity. The wings each cultivator manifests are unique to them, and a cultivator's Wings become part of how they are recognized.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 1 minute to the duration per Aura Star. At Aura Star 4+, the Fly Speed equals 1.5x your walking Speed. At Aura Star 6+, double your walking Speed.
Aura Wave

blocks.text: Mana: 9 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (30-foot cone) Duration: Instant Resistance: Strength

blocks.html: You sweep your weapon (or your open hand, for unarmed cultivators) in an arc, releasing a wave of cultivated Aura in a 30-foot cone before you. All creatures in the cone must succeed Resistance (Strength) VS your Aura skill roll or be pushed 10 feet directly away from you, and any creature pushed into terrain or another creature also falls prone. The wave does no damage by default.

Aura Wave is a battlefield-clearing working. It is not lethal, but it ends engagements: enemies pushed back, formations broken, the cultivator suddenly at the center of breathing room. The technique pairs well with movement riders (Aura Step, Aura Wings) — a cultivator who has just pushed a line of enemies backward and stepped forward into the cleared space has fundamentally changed the geometry of the fight.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the cone length and 5 feet to the push distance per Aura Star.
Aura Resonance

blocks.text: Mana: 7 Trigger: Rider on technique chain (any martial school) Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: When you perform a Named Chain from any martial school, you may channel Aura through the sequence as a single coherent flow. Each technique in the chain gains your Aura Star Bonus Dice as additional damage, and the chain's listed payoff effect (the bonus that triggers on successful completion) is improved as if you had completed it perfectly — even if some component techniques failed, the chain's payoff still triggers as long as at least one technique in the sequence hit.

Aura Resonance is the rider that turns a martial artist's technique-chain into a cultivator's chained working. The Three-Hit Doctrine, the Wave Pattern, the Storm's Path — all become more reliable, all hit harder, all pay off even when partial. A cultivator who has trained both a martial school and Aura Resonance can lean into the chain structure of their school as a primary expression of cultivated power.

Aura Star Scaling. Add +1 Bonus Die per Aura Star to each technique in the chain. At Aura Star 5+, the chain's payoff effect activates even if no technique in the chain hit (as long as the chain itself was performed).
Aura Veil

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self Duration: Concentration up to 10 minutes

blocks.html: You wrap yourself in a layer of cultivated Aura that suppresses your own Aura signature and physical visibility. Aura Sense and similar detection workings cannot detect you within their range. Your visual silhouette becomes blurred and indistinct — observers must succeed an Awareness check VS your Aura skill roll to see you clearly enough to target you with precision (failed checks impose 2 Challenges on attacks against you).

Aura Veil is the working that lets a high-cultivation warrior walk into places where high-cultivation warriors are not welcome. It is not invisibility — the cultivator can still be seen. It is concealment from the kind of attention that other cultivators bring to a room. The technique is favored by Striders, Ascetics, and any Knight whose work occasionally requires moving through a hostile court without being recognized.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 10 minutes to the maximum duration per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the Awareness check to see you clearly is at 1 Challenge.

Seven workings. At Expert rank, cultivated Aura becomes a presence on the battlefield in its own right. The cultivator does not merely strike harder — they shape the space around themselves into a zone where their will is the dominant force. Aura Domain compresses ten feet of ground into ground that obeys the cultivator. Aura Severance simply ignores armor as a concept. Heaven's Edge sends a single strike across two hundred feet. These are the techniques that distinguish the formal cultivator-class from any common warrior with a sword.
Aura Domain

blocks.text: Mana: 18 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (20-foot radius) Duration: Concentration up to 1 minute Resistance: Resolve (initial)

blocks.html: You declare a Domain — a 20-foot radius around you in which your Aura is the dominant force. Hostile creatures who begin their turn in the Domain must succeed Resistance (Resolve) VS your Aura skill roll or suffer 1 Challenge on all rolls (attacks, Defenses, Resistances, ability checks) while within the area. Creatures who succeed treat the Domain as Difficult Terrain but suffer no other penalty. You and creatures you designate at casting are unaffected.

An Aura Domain is the working a Knight enters a battlefield in. It is not subtle; the air within the Domain visibly thickens, and the cultivator at its center radiates the sense of being the most important person in the immediate space. Other cultivators of equal or greater rank can resist the Domain by entering one of their own; the two Domains overlap and exclude each other in the overlapping region, creating a brief zone of contested ground at the boundary where the working flickers and fails for both casters.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the radius per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the Challenge to affected creatures increases to 2.
Aura Storm

blocks.text: Mana: 20 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack Range: Self (10-foot radius around target) Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You channel your strike into a storm of cultivated Aura around the impact point. The triggering attack hits its primary target normally. Then, every other creature within 10 feet of the target must succeed Resistance (Speed) VS your Aura skill roll or take the attack's weapon damage plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage. The primary target also takes this additional damage automatically (no Resistance).

Aura Storm is the cultivator's answer to a tight formation. The single strike that should kill one soldier instead radiates outward, the cultivator's blade flickering in fractal directions for a fraction of a second. Targets near the primary strike see the impact and feel the secondary cuts simultaneously — and a moment later, the storm subsides and the cultivator's blade is back in its original position, as if nothing had happened.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 5 feet to the radius and +1 Bonus Die to the secondary damage per Aura Star.
Aura Severance

blocks.text: Mana: 18 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack or martial technique attack Range: Self Duration: Instant

blocks.html: The triggering attack ignores all armor entirely — Armor Rank treated as zero, all damage reduction from armor and shields suppressed. The attack also ignores cover other than full obstruction (the strike threads around partial cover). Magical defenses (Aegis spells, force shields, magical barriers) take half damage from the attack and continue protecting their bearer normally.

Aura Severance is the working that defeats armor as a category. A blade with Aura Severance applied passes through plate as if it were silk, through a shield as if it were paper, around the corner of a stone column as if the column were not there. The technique works on any kind of attack — sword, arrow, fist, technique — and is the rider every cultivator carries as their answer to armored opponents.

Aura Star Scaling. The cost reduces by 1 per Aura Star (minimum 10 Mana). At Aura Star 5+, the attack also ignores magical defenses normally — Aegis spells and similar workings provide no protection against the strike.
Aura Beast

blocks.text: Mana: 22 Trigger: 2 Actions Range: Close Duration: 1 minute Resistance: None

blocks.html: You manifest a creature of pure cultivated Aura at a point within Close range. The Beast takes a form that reflects your inner self — for some cultivators a wolf, for others a great cat, a serpent, a bird of prey, a wholly fictional thing. Each cultivator's Beast is unique to them. The Beast has Health equal to 30 plus 10 per Aura Star, Speed 40, Armor Rank 12, and makes a basic attack as a free action on your Initiative with attack roll equal to your Aura skill roll and damage equal to 2d10 plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice in Aura damage.

The Beast obeys your spoken commands and acts as a separate combatant within Close range of you. If the Beast moves further than Close range, it dissipates. If reduced to 0 Health, it dissipates. At the end of the duration, it dissipates of its own accord. The working is taxing to maintain — while the Beast is active, your own basic melee attacks lose 1 Bonus Die to damage, as part of your cultivated Aura is invested in the manifestation.
Aura Star Scaling. Add +10 Health, +1 Armor Rank, and +1 Bonus Die to the Beast's damage per Aura Star. At Aura Star 6+, two Beasts may be manifested simultaneously (each at 22 Mana).
Aura Eyes

blocks.text: Mana: 15 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (60-foot radius) Duration: 10 minutes

blocks.html: You open your perception to the flow of Aura through the world. For the duration, you see every Aura signature within 60 feet through any non-magical barrier (stone walls, wooden doors, terrain, foliage). You also see the Aura-currents of cultivated workings as they pass through space — incoming spells appear as bright filaments tracking their flight path, Aura Techniques visible as auras around their casters, magical traps and wards luminous in their active layouts.

Aura Eyes is the working that ends ambushes. A cultivator with Aura Eyes active cannot be surprised by another cultivator, cannot be approached unseen by a Mage of any active rank, and cannot be lured into a magical trap without seeing its outline first. The working is one of the most-used Expert techniques in the catalog precisely because of how much it negates.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 30 feet to the radius per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the working also sees through magical concealment, illusion, and shape-changing within range.
Heaven's Edge

blocks.text: Mana: 22 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack Range: Far Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You compress a single cut into a wave of severance and send it across the battlefield. The triggering attack instead strikes a target at Far range. The wave travels as a thin line of pale Aura, visible only as a moment of shimmer in the air between you and the target. On a hit, the target takes the attack's weapon damage plus +3 Bonus Dice plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage. The wave ignores cover other than full obstruction.

Heaven's Edge is the working that lets a swordsman strike a target a hundred feet away. The wave is a single coherent slice of cultivated Aura; it does not arc, does not curve, does not adjust its course. A cultivator with Heaven's Edge prepared and a clear line of sight has effectively ranged weapon parity with an archer at Far range. The technique is one of the signature workings of the Master swordsman traditions, and the moment a target sees the cultivator draw and swing at empty air a hundred feet away, they have a fraction of a second to react.
Aura Star Scaling. Add 50 feet to the maximum range and +1 Bonus Die per Aura Star. At Aura Star 5+, the wave may strike up to two adjacent targets at the destination point.
Aura Forge

blocks.text: Mana: 20 Trigger: 10 minutes (Ritual) Range: Touch Duration: 1 encounter Resistance: None

blocks.html: You imbue a weapon you touch with persistent cultivated Aura for the next encounter (or until the next Long Rest, whichever comes first). The weapon gains a permanent damage increase of 1d8 plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice in Aura damage on every successful hit, automatically, with no Mana cost per swing. The weapon also gains Threat 2 against armored opponents (Armor Rank 4+) and counts as magical for the purpose of bypassing damage reduction or resistance based on magical weapon requirement.

Aura Forge is the working a cultivator performs the night before a battle they expect to be long. The imbued weapon does not require Mana to deliver its enhanced strikes during the encounter; the Mana was invested in the imbuement itself. A Knight who has Aura-forged their sword the night before a siege walks into the siege with effectively unlimited applications of Aura Strike against ordinary opponents — though more powerful workings still cost Mana.
Aura Star Scaling. Add +1d8 Aura damage to the weapon's strikes per 2 Aura Stars. At Aura Star 5+, the weapon also gains the effect of Aura Edge (+5 feet of reach) at no Mana cost.

Five workings. At Master rank, cultivated Aura becomes a force that bends the rules of the world locally. A Star-Embracing Strike cleaves the sky between the cultivator and a target a quarter mile away. A Sword Saint's Domain compresses two hundred feet of battlefield into ground that obeys the cultivator's will. The Heart of Stars is not a stance — it is a state in which the cultivator briefly stops being a person and becomes a phenomenon. Few warriors ever reach this tier. Those who do are not warriors anymore in any common sense.
Star-Embracing Strike

blocks.text: Mana: 35 Trigger: Rider on basic melee attack with bladed weapon Range: 1,000 feet Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You raise your weapon to the sky and gather your cultivated Aura into a single coherent strike. The triggering attack instead targets a single creature within 1,000 feet whose precise location you can sense (line of sight or Aura Sense both qualify). The strike crosses the intervening space as a luminous arc of compressed cutting force, visible to any observer along its path as a streak of pale fire. On a hit, the target takes the attack's weapon damage plus +6 Bonus Dice plus double your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage. The strike ignores all armor and all cover other than full magical barriers. Creatures other than the target in a line between you and the impact point must succeed Resistance (Speed) VS your Aura skill roll or take half damage (no Bonus Dice) as the strike passes through their space.

Star-Embracing Strike is one of the named signatures of the highest cultivation traditions. A cultivator who can deliver one is among the most dangerous individuals in their region. The strike is not subtle — the gathering takes a full Action, the weapon is raised visibly, the cut leaves the cultivator's body as a column of light that briefly illuminates the night for half a mile. After the strike resolves, the cultivator is Winded (lose all Vigor); the working is taxing even at Master rank.
Aura Annihilation

blocks.text: Mana: 40 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (60-foot radius) Duration: Instant Resistance: Stamina

blocks.html: You release the totality of your cultivated Aura outward in a single devastating pulse. Every other creature within 60 feet of you takes 6d12 plus double your Aura Star Bonus Dice in Aura damage. Creatures who succeed Resistance (Stamina) VS your Aura skill roll take half damage. The pulse does not discriminate between friend and foe; allies in the radius are affected normally. After the working resolves, all your Aura Stars are dimmed — for the next 24 hours, you cannot use any Aura Technique that costs more than 5 Mana, and your Aura Star Bonus Dice are halved for any purpose.

Aura Annihilation is the working a cultivator does once — sometimes twice, in a very long career. It is the working that ends impossible engagements, that breaks sieges, that turns the moment of certain death into the moment when seven enemies fall and the cultivator walks away. The dimmed-Aura aftermath is real and consequential; the working spends most of what made the cultivator a cultivator, and the recovery is slow. Most Master cultivators carry the working in their repertoire and most never use it.
Sword Saint's Domain

blocks.text: Mana: 45 Trigger: 2 Actions Range: Self (60-foot radius) Duration: Concentration up to 5 minutes Resistance: Resolve (initial)

blocks.html: You declare a Domain of overwhelming scale and depth. The 60-foot radius around you becomes ground that obeys you. All hostile creatures who begin their turn within the Domain take 2d10 Aura damage and must succeed Resistance (Resolve) VS your Aura skill roll or suffer 2 Challenges on all rolls within the area. Creatures who succeed take half damage and suffer only 1 Challenge. You and designated allies are unaffected. Within the Domain, you gain 1 additional Action per turn, your Aura Technique costs are halved, and any basic melee attack you make is treated as if Aura Severance had been applied (ignores armor entirely).

A Sword Saint's Domain is the working that ends battles. The cultivator at its center is briefly the most dangerous person in their world; the geometry of the fight collapses inward toward them. The working is taxing to maintain — at the start of each turn after the first, you must succeed Resistance (Stamina) VS your own Aura skill roll at 1 Challenge or the Domain ends. After the Domain ends, regardless of cause, you are Fatigued (2) until you complete a Long Rest.
Empyrean Sweep

blocks.text: Mana: 38 Trigger: 1 Action Range: Self (300-foot line, 10 feet wide) Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You raise your weapon (or extend your hand, for unarmed cultivators) and sweep it in a horizontal arc, releasing a wave of cultivated Aura that travels 300 feet in a straight line, 10 feet wide. Every creature in the line must succeed Resistance (Speed) VS your Aura skill roll or take 8d10 plus your Aura Star Bonus Dice as Aura damage. Creatures who succeed take half damage. The wave ignores cover other than full magical barriers; ordinary terrain (walls, doors, trees) within the wave's path takes weapon damage and is destroyed or pushed aside as the wave passes.

Empyrean Sweep is the formation-erasing working. A cavalry charge crossing open ground becomes a line of fallen riders. A defensive line in a corridor becomes a corridor of casualties. The wave is visible to any observer not in its path — a pale horizontal line of light flicking across the battlefield in the time it takes for the eye to track it. The cultivator who delivers an Empyrean Sweep is committing to the engagement; the working ends the cultivator's turn and the wave's casualties define the next several rounds of combat.
Heart of Stars

blocks.text: Mana: 60 Trigger: Stance: 3 Actions to enter Range: Self Duration: Concentration up to 1 minute

blocks.html: You enter a state of transcendent cultivation. While in the Heart of Stars, you double your Aura Star Bonus Dice for all calculations, gain 2 additional Actions per turn, may use any Aura Technique you know without paying its Mana cost, and your basic attacks gain the combined effect of Aura Strike, Aura Edge, and Aura Severance automatically. Your body is wreathed in visible Aura that illuminates a 30-foot radius. You may freely move through air at your normal walking Speed (as if Aura Wings were active).

The state is taxing beyond ordinary limits. At the start of each turn after the first, you take 1d10 damage that cannot be reduced or reflected. The Heart of Stars ends at the conclusion of its duration, when you choose to end it, or when you fall to 0 Health (the working will not kill you outright — it ends as your Health reaches 0, leaving you unconscious rather than dead from the working itself). After the Heart of Stars ends, you are Fatigued (3) until you complete two full Long Rests. The working is the closest a mortal cultivator comes to godhood. Most who reach it use it once a decade. Some use it once and never again.
75 words introducing magic and spell craft in the setting.

Any character with Novice Occult or better is able to learn and use Basic Spells. These may pale in comparison to ancient sorceries, but are still powerful abilities. Advanced Spells are typically learned from Grimoires.

Unlike techniques which use 1 action, casting spells requires a number of actions based on the spell’s casting time, consumes an amount of Mana, and has a set of effects, including a range and duration.

Most spells will take 1 or more standard Actions to cast on your turn — this is the casting time. Some spells can be cast as your reaction as well. When cast on your turn, these spells have a casting time of 1 Action. If you do not have enough actions on your turn to cast a spell, the casting time will bleed over into the next round on your turn and create an opening that others can exploit.

When you cast a Spell you make an assault roll with Occult. Usually you add Soul or Blood bonus dice, depending on the school of magic you are casting. If the Spell has a secondary effect that calls for a Resistance check, the VS of this Resistance check is normally your Casting roll.
When a spell is activated and fully cast, an amount of your Mana is consumed.
All spells have a range, as outlined below. Unless specified otherwise, targeting a spell requires line of sight, and magic will be blocked by physical obstacles and cover.
Personal. When cast, the spell only affects your person.
Touch. This spell directly affects the target you touch.
Close. The spell reaches as far as 25 feet away from you. The maximum range increases by 10 feet for every level of the caster.
Medium. The spell reaches as far as 100 feet + 25 feet per level of the caster.
Far. The spell reaches as far as 1000 feet + 100 feet per level of the caster.
Special. A special range usually implies the spell has no target or its effects originate from the caster and affect a specific area.

Most spells create destructive energies that only last a few moments, but many of the more powerful and ancient spells create a lingering effect. A spell’s duration will be listed in minutes, turns or rounds if it has a duration beyond instant.
Instant. This spell creates a onetime effect so that, though it may cause some permanently lasting impact, the magic is gone in an instant.
Concentration. You may maintain this magic indefinitely so long as you are conscious by spending 1 Action per round. You cannot maintain this magic during a break or while resting.

Your Arcane Arms skill is your Casting check skill for Arcane Arms. These magics bridge between weapon and incantation: the wielder draws their Mana through the focus of the arm and shapes it into an effect that is also, in some real sense, a strike. Most Arcane Arms spells produce damage in addition to their other effects, and most use the arm's basic weapon damage as the floor of that damage. Where techniques consume Vigor, spells consume Mana.

Casting a spell uses Actions on your turn equal to the spell's Casting Time, and consumes the listed Mana. The Mage class's Novice Spellcraft talent and certain class abilities can reduce Casting Time; the Lesser Ritual mechanic extends it. Spells require the wielder to hold an Arcane Arm and meet its minimum Mana threshold (20 for a wand, 40 for a rod, 60 for a staff).

Arcane Arms spells take their element from the armament used to cast them. A Fire Wand casts the elemental damage of Fire; a Lightning Rod casts Lightning; an Ice Staff casts Cold. The spell's effects — barrier, dart, ram, cannon — are the same regardless of element; only the damage type and certain conditional effects shift. This is the central conceit of the Arcane Arms discipline: the spell is the form; the arm is the substance.

The standard elements of Radiant Age Arcane Arms are Fire, Lightning, and Ice. These are the common arms found in any sorcerous outfitter, dueling school, or wizard's tower. Exotic elements exist but are rare, expensive, and frequently restricted to specific schools or sects. The table below summarizes the damage type and signature condition each element imparts. Conditions only apply when triggered by the spell or by an effect such as Elemental Grip; mere damage of a given type does not automatically apply the condition.

The foundation. These are the spells any apprentice with a wand or rod begins with — the dart that pierces, the burst that scatters, the shockwave that clears a doorway, the volley that fills the corridor with bolts. None require a Mage's Circle to grasp; any character with the Arcane Arms skill and the Mana to fuel them can learn and cast them. They are not the most powerful spells in the catalog. They are, however, the spells that veterans of a hundred fights still rely on when the moment requires a working that will not fail.
Elemental Dart

blocks.text: Mana: 6 Casting Time: 1 Action Range: Medium Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You conjure and loose a swift bolt of elemental energy at a single target. On a hit, the target takes weapon damage plus 2d6 damage of the arm's element. The dart is fast, precise, and the most reliable single-target opening any Arcane Arms wielder learns.
Elemental Burst

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Casting Time: 2 Actions Range: Close Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You release a concentrated explosion of elemental energy at a point within Close range. The target of the explosion takes weapon damage unless they succeed a Defense (Dodge) reaction. On a hit, Large-size and smaller targets are knocked into the air and land prone at the end of your turn. While airborne, they cannot take reactions.
Elemental Shockwave

blocks.text: Mana: 8 Casting Time: 2 Actions Range: Self (15-foot radius) Duration: Instant Resistance: Strength

blocks.html: You slam your Arcane Arm into the ground, the floor, or any solid surface, releasing a radial shockwave of elemental energy in a 15-foot radius around you. All other creatures in the area must succeed a Resistance (Strength) check VS your casting roll or take weapon damage. Large-size and smaller creatures who fail are also pushed 6 feet directly away from you.
Elemental Volley

blocks.text: Mana: 9 Casting Time: 1 Action Range: Close Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You fire four consecutive bolts of elemental energy at a target within range. Make four Arcane Arms attack rolls against the target's Defense. Each bolt that hits deals base weapon damage (no Bonus Dice). Bolts may be split between multiple targets within range, but each target may only be the recipient of one bolt unless otherwise specified.
Elemental Step

blocks.text: Mana: 4 Casting Time: 1 Action Range: Self (30 feet) Duration: Instant Resistance: None

blocks.html: You dissolve momentarily into your arm's element and re-form up to 30 feet away in a space you can see. You must have line of sight to your destination. The movement does not provoke Openings. You may pass through Difficult Terrain, mundane barriers thinner than 1 foot, and crowds of creatures during this transit, but you cannot end the movement inside solid matter or a creature's space.

Element Variants. Fire: you streak as a line of flame between origin and destination, and any creature whose space the line passes through takes 1d6 Fire damage on a failed Resistance (Speed) check. Lightning: you arc as a bolt, and any creature in the line is briefly Dazed (one Challenge on their next attack). Ice: you shimmer and reform with a corona of frost; the destination square becomes Difficult Terrain (rime) for one turn. Earth: you sink through the ground and emerge, leaving a small pothole at origin and destination. Wind: a gust of displacing air at origin and destination pushes Tiny creatures aside. Aether: you phase, and you are briefly out of phase upon arrival (+1 BD Defense until the end of your next turn).

The working catalog of the trained Arcane Arms wielder. These spells require some refinement — typically the talent investment of a second skill rank or the dedicated study of a real practitioner. They cover the working needs of a magical combatant: the barrier you put between yourself and the line of fire, the ram that ends a stubborn duel, the cloak that turns the enemy's element back upon them, the lash that drags a target into range. These are the spells the journeyman casts daily, and the master still keeps in their rotation.
Barrier

blocks.text: Mana: 10 Casting Time: 2 Actions Range: Self Duration: Special Resistance: None

blocks.html: You raise a hemispherical barrier of elemental energy 20 feet in diameter and 10 feet tall, centered on you. The barrier is impassable by creatures and objects, has 25 Durability, and lasts until the start of your next turn. On your turn you may sustain the barrier with 1 Action and 6 Mana; this recovers 10 Durability and extends the barrier through your next turn. If the barrier's Durability is reduced to 0, it shatters and you are Dazed until the end of your next turn.
Bolt Rain

blocks.text: Mana: 10 Casting Time: 2 Actions Range: Close Duration: Instant Resistance: Speed

blocks.html: You fire a condensed blast of elemental magic into the air above an area you can see within Close range, and the magic rains down as elemental force. Choose an area up to 8 feet in diameter; all creatures in the area must succeed a Defense (Dodge) reaction VS your casting roll or suffer full weapon damage. The rain ignores ordinary cover from above (overhead branches, awnings) but is blocked by complete cover such as solid ceilings.
Elemental Cloak

blocks.text: Mana: 12 Casting Time: 2 Actions Range: Self Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute Resistance: None

blocks.html: Your arm's element wraps you in a protective shroud. While the spell is active, you take half damage from attacks of the same elemental type as your arm. Additionally, any creature that hits you with a melee attack takes 1d6 damage of your arm's element as the cloak flares against them.
