Condition. Complete the full cultivation of a Heroic Class.
Step 1. Purchase all 11 talents within a single Heroic Class's Elite Tree.
Step 2. Use your Rank 5 Capstone ability to accomplish something genuinely transformative — a moment witnesses describe as legendary.
Step 3. Achieve 3 Bonus Dice Burst on a single roll using your Heroic Class's signature mechanic.
Perk. Improve an existing Heroic-tier Talent's effect by 1 rank. Additionally, you gain a permanent +1 Bonus Die to a chosen ability score (must align thematically with your Heroic Class).
Epic Deed
Condition. Cross the Epic threshold — the boundary between Heroic cultivation and the legendary capacity of the Epic tier.
Step 1. Complete the full cultivation of a Heroic Class (all 11 talents).
Step 2. Meet the prerequisites for an Epic Class (specific ability scores, skills, engines, and class-tier-specific requirements).
Step 3. Accomplish a Unique Challenge that the GM judges worthy of Epic-tier recognition (often the resolution of a multi-session character arc or a defining moment that reshapes the campaign world).
Step 4. Receive formal recognition of your Epic crossing from at least three established witnesses — sects, divine patrons, named beings, or major political authorities.
Perk. Unlock entry to an Epic Class. Gain one additional Action per turn. Permanently increase Durability by 4d6, and gain Immunity to one specific Condition of your choosing.
Epic Deed
Condition. Cultivate a single cultivation engine to its full Epic-tier expression.
Step 1. Acquire your 5th or 6th engine grant of the same type (Aura Stars, Mana Circles, etc.) — typically achievable only through Epic Class talent investment.
Step 2. Use the engine's full apex expression to substantially shape the outcome of a major narrative event or boss encounter.
Step 3. Achieve 3 Bonus Dice Burst on a single attack, casting, or skill check that primarily relies on your apex engine.
Step 4. Be formally recognized as a master of the apex engine by at least two established peer practitioners (Heroic-tier or higher Spiritualists, Mages, Knights, etc.).

Perk. Gain a repeatable Epic benefit when fighting beings of equal-tier or higher: your apex-engine-powered attacks gain +1 Charge against such opponents, and you may once per long rest treat your engine as having one additional grant for a single scene's duration.
Epic Deed
Condition. Establish a new sect, school, tradition, or named line of cultivation that survives your active leadership.
Step 1. Complete the cultivation of a unique signature ability or technique that no other practitioner has produced.
Step 2. Recruit at least three students or successors who have meaningfully advanced under your teaching.
Step 3. Establish a physical headquarters, shrine, library, or named seat for your tradition.
Step 4. Defend your tradition's right to exist against a meaningful threat — political, religious, or cultivation-based — and prevail.

Perk. Add 1 universal Charge to all skill checks that align with your tradition's core teaching (e.g., a sword-tradition Founder gains +1 Charge to all Light Weapons or Heavy Weapons checks; an arcane-tradition Founder gains +1 Charge to all Occult checks). Additionally, your tradition becomes a recognized faction in the campaign world, with which the GM may have NPCs, sects, and political entities interact.
Epic Deed
Condition. Defeat a being of Legendary-tier in genuinely contested combat.
Step 1. Confront and survive an encounter with a being of named Legendary tier (an ancient dragon, a divine archon, an apex eldritch incursion, a Legendary cultivator of a rival tradition).
Step 2. Land a meaningful blow against the Legendary being — reducing them to a state where they recognize they may not survive.
Step 3. Force the Legendary being to retreat, surrender, or be slain through your specific direct action.
Step 4. Survive the encounter and its aftermath (Legendary beings rarely die quietly; allies, students, or related cosmic powers may pursue you afterward).

Perk. Gain a repeatable Epic benefit: when you encounter a being of Legendary tier in subsequent combat, you gain +1 Action and +1 Charge on attack rolls against that being for the duration of the encounter. Additionally, your reputation as a Slayer of Legends precedes you — major NPCs of significance recognize your name, and the GM may bring in cosmic enemies whose attention you have earned.
Epic Deed
Condition. Accumulate or recover knowledge of forbidden tier without succumbing entirely to corruption.
Step 1. Accumulate a Corruption Score of at least 30 through committed forbidden cultivation.
Step 2. Successfully use a Forbidden-tier ability or Mystery to accomplish a major narrative objective.
Step 3. Survive a direct contact with an eldritch entity, infernal court, or Legendary corrupt being without being subsumed or destroyed.
Step 4. Maintain at least one significant relational or moral bond against the pull of your accumulated corruption (an ally, an oath, a person you refuse to harm).

Perk. Gain access to one Forbidden-tier Mystery, Corruption Track, or eldritch Talent that your current class does not otherwise grant. Additionally, you gain Heroic Resistance against effects of your forbidden cultivation's primary domain (Umbral, Entropic, Toxic, or as appropriate).
Epic Deed
Condition. Accomplish a singular feat that establishes your name in the historical record of Radiant Age.
Step 1. Complete a Unique Challenge that the GM judges to be of major historical significance — saving a city from a Legendary threat, unifying a fractured kingdom, killing a god, or similar.
Step 2. Receive formal recognition of your feat from at least three major political, religious, or cultural authorities.
Step 3. Have a named monument, holiday, sect, or major artifact established in honor of your accomplishment.
Step 4. Continue to act in alignment with the legend you have established for at least one full narrative arc after the foundational event.

Perk. Gain the equivalent benefit of two Ordinary Perks of your choice, or one Ordinary Perk and two Title Perks. Additionally, you may once per session add 1 Bonus Die to any roll directly tied to your established legend's themes (defending the people you saved, opposing the threats you have already defeated, etc.).

In Radiant Age, the Game Master must steward a luminous world of cultivated power and earned legend, where heroes becoming sword saints, archmages, and sovereigns. You are the narrator, the storyteller, and the director. Yours is the most rewarding and challenging of roles to play in the game. You will set the stage, describe the world, and control the outcome of the events as they transpire. This section will discuss taking on the role of GM, creating the universe and adventures you and your party will explore, and mediating the rules of the game.

As the GM you will play the role of every person that the player characters meet and every adversary they face. You must plan out the adventures that the players embark on and be prepared for the unexpected. You will be the arbiter of the rules, meaning if the outcome or definition of the rules is not clear, it is up to you to make the final call and determine the precedents by which the rules should be followed in your game.

Above all: Role-Play! That is the point of the game, taking the opportunity to get into each non-player character and fleshing them out. From simple store clerks, to recurring villains, role-play each of these unique characters! Doing so creates a living world, and soon you will realize that the setting has taken on a life of its own, with memorable and unique personalities that the players are talking about after each chapter, and characters that fill up your own living legend.

When you and your party gather around the table, it’s up to you to take the lead and drive the game. The rules are an engine for the imagination of you and your players. Remember that the goal of any sessions should be to have fun and share in an adventure. This means at times you will need to mediate between players in and out of the game, as well as set the expectations for the table.

Radiant Age is a game of cultivated power and earned identity, owing as much to console JRPGs and progression manhwa as to classic fantasy. Lean into that ancestry. When a Warrior unfurls an Aura Core, let onlookers gasp; when a Mage’s heart-Circle ignites, let nearby candles bend toward it. Cultivation should be visible, performative, and dramatic, because the world notices heroes becoming something more. Class titles are not labels in Radiant Age; they are stations. A Knight is recognized at the gates. A Sword Saint is sung about in distant taverns. A Spirit Sovereign is feared in three kingdoms.

Keep heritage and history present without letting them flatten the character. A Dwergar Guild Thief who one day becomes a Master Thief should still feel shaped by the guilds, not reduced to a class build. Reward players who weave their identity stack into their play.

Escalate threats alongside ascent. The Realms of Ouroboros are vast, ancient, and they answer to legends. When the heroes grow, so should the names that whisper their growth: rival schools take interest, sealed enemies begin to stir, and the great courts of the world send envoys to measure them. Above all, make becoming greater feel like the central, joyful promise of the game.

The rules of the table are for you to decide; when you start, how long you play, whether you use miniatures or not, and how you judge out-of-game situations is up to you. There are numerous approaches to running the table, and none can be said to be better than another. When players come to your table, outline what to expect and what the rules are. Some things you should make clear to your players include:

items: Be respectful
Let others speak and act
Be honest with dice rolls
Engage as your character
Above all: Have fun

blocks.html: As the GM, you will need to make determinations regarding the rules in the books. While the game rules are clear and can be applied to most situations, there are not mechanical rules for every possible event, and this is a good thing. It is up to you to set the precedents in your games.

When you make a judgment regarding the rules, you have set a standard. You as the Game Master have said that under these circumstances, the rules should be interpreted in a certain way. This precedent should be consistent. Once the interpretation of the rules is set, it becomes the baseline by which your players will operate. You’ll find the rules you establish can go on to become the standards by which your players operate with their own groups when they become GMs. Be fair, fun, and consistent, and you will have a lasting impact on your players, and your games will take on a style all their own.

Dice serve as a way for chaos to play a hand in the outcome of events. This means that dice are both neutral and unpredictable. When you let players roll, there needs to be some chance of both failure and success. Demanding players roll for every menial task, however, can diminish the value of good role-playing and having meaningful exchanges. Similarly, if players are never given the opportunity to roll to overcome challenges, they may feel like their destiny is on rails and they are not part of the story.

Rolling dice should be a part of the action and the drama, not to dictate exactly what a character can and cannot do. Given enough time, a character with a bonus in a skill should be able to accomplish that task with no meaningful chance of failure.

You don’t need to let the players know why you are rolling. If the party is being stalked by a trained assassin, roll a Stealth check while the party sets up camp for the night and watch the tensions rise. After all, they have no idea why you are rolling. This is a great way to build suspense and keep the players engaged.

Because the dice add Chance, you also need to know when to ignore the dice. If the players can derail your story with a critical success, take that option off the table unless you can recover from the event. As the GM, you need to be able to adapt and change when things go in a direction you did not anticipate, but you also need to know when to keep a firm hand on the direction of the adventure.

When a player wishes to take an action that is not completely impossible given their character’s capability or knowledge and has some challenge or chance of failure, a roll should be called for. What they roll should depend on what they hope to accomplish, their knowledge, and their resources.
No matter what the task or action the players wish to accomplish is, it can fall under one of the core skills and ability scores, even if no skill directly calls out the act in question.

With the Charges and Challenges system, you have great ability to influence the outcome of any action or effect. Adding a Challenge to a roll can significantly reduce the chance of success, while adding a Charge to a damage roll can cause devastating damage.

A character should receive a Charge to an action roll when the circumstances are contributing to their success. This could be a morale boost or environmental factors, or superior resources and tools. As GM, give a Charge to players on action rolls that are imaginative, well role-played, or creatively problem-solved. Charges to effect rolls can also add an element of wonder and power when players receive a significant boost from some source.

When an action is attempted in the most unusual and extreme situations, a Challenge is called for. Challenges are a way to balance and show the gravity or enormity of a situation, not to punish players. Adding a Challenge to a player’s action because they do not describe their action well or because they do not fully understand the situation is not a good approach. Instead, try rewarding good behavior and encouraging players that are struggling with coaching and feedback.

Destined is a narrative and mechanical tool that rewards exceptional rolls, bold choices, and character-defining actions. Players have several ways to become destined without you as the GM, but you should feel empowered to grant the Destined condition when a player:

items: Takes a dramatic risk or pushes their character’s story forward.
Performs a clutch action that changes the flow of the encounter.
Makes the table go quiet, or erupt in cheers, that’s your cue.

blocks.html: It’s not about handing it out often, but about making moments matter. Players can only hold one Destined state at a time, and it shouldn’t stack or reset arbitrarily.

For narrative pacing, consider refreshing a character’s Destined status after a major rest, story milestone, or emotional character scene. This ties the system directly to story arcs, keeping it special.
Use Destined to spotlight heroism and amplify the emotion behind your group’s biggest moments.

As the GM, it is your responsibility to reward the players for their accomplishments and achievements. You have several mechanisms to reward players for their exceptional acts. The first is EXP, which are awarded to characters for achieving objectives and defeating enemies. EXP are the primary method to drive advancement and growth. The next is Deeds, specific incredible accomplishments for characters who achieve the extraordinary.

Defeating adversaries in an encounter usually rewards a set amount of EXP, usually found in the adversary’s stat block. When the party overcomes an encounter, the EXP are evenly distributed among each character, including non-players, who participated. Encounters need not always end in violence. Players who are able to defuse or complete an encounter through nonviolent means, especially if there is significant risk or danger, should still be awarded some or all of the EXP depending on the difficulty of the situation.

For example, an encounter with a group of raiders that ends with the party trading resources after a tense negotiation with many Social checks should count as a success, but if the party avoids an encounter entirely with one or two Stealth checks, they should receive little or no EXP, as there was no challenge and no victory.

Eventually, you may find that one of your players cannot be at the table for any number of reasons. Players who miss a session should generally not receive EXP for encounters in that chapter and have no opportunities to accomplish Deeds. However, if the party has been working toward a larger goal or objective that is finalized or concluded during a session the player missed, consider giving them some of the EXP for that objective, based on their contribution.

Awarding experience for completing long-term objectives is a good way to balance out sessions that have few encounters. You should set out some or all these objectives in advance, and the difficulty of the objectives should determine the EXP reward. The EXP award for objectives is laid out below.

Minor objectives are small steps, such as completing a job for a handler or broker, finding a crucial piece of a larger puzzle, or completing a mission with an added parameter, such as take no damage, don’t kill any hostiles, or don’t be seen. When players succeed in a task or tasks set forth by contacts or connections, these are minor objectives.

Major objectives are the culmination of multiple actions and encounters by the party; such as capturing a dangerous, wanted villain alive; retrieving dangerous intelligence that rests deep inside enemy territory; or completing an encounter or encounters without the opportunity to take rests.

Some Game Masters find that awarding and tracking individual experience points adds more bookkeeping than value. In those campaigns, you may use milestone-based advancement instead. Under this model, characters gain levels when they complete meaningful sessions, resolve major conflicts, overcome important challenges, or reach significant turning points in the story.

As a general rule, characters should gain one level after each meaningful session of play. A meaningful session is one in which the characters make real progress, face consequential choices, survive danger, discover something important, change their position in the world, or advance the campaign in a clear way. The characters do not need to win every fight, complete every objective, or achieve a perfect outcome. What matters is that the session moved the story forward and gave the characters opportunities to act, struggle, and grow.

If a session includes a major battle, climactic confrontation, dangerous dungeon, decisive social conflict, boss encounter, or other unusually significant event, the Game Master may award two levels at the end of that session. This should be reserved for sessions that feel like major chapters in the campaign, not for every difficult encounter. A hard fight against wandering monsters probably does not justify an extra level. Defeating the tyrant’s champion, surviving the collapse of an ancient ruin, breaking a curse on a city, or resolving a long-running rivalry might.

This approach assumes a fast heroic growth curve. Radiant Age is a game about characters rising from ordinary adventurers into legendary figures. If you imagine a campaign played once per week for a full year, approximately fifty-two sessions, the characters should likely end that year somewhere between level 50 and level 60. This means advancement is expected to be steady, visible, and dramatic. Characters should regularly gain new talents, improve their skills, awaken new powers, and move toward higher heroic identities.

Game Masters who want a slower campaign can award levels every two sessions, or only after completing major adventures. Game Masters who want a high-escalation campaign can award levels every session and grant additional levels after major victories. The important principle is consistency. Before the campaign begins, tell the players what advancement pace to expect so they understand the rhythm of growth.

Milestone advancement works best when levels reflect the shape of the story. A level should feel like the result of hardship, discovery, triumph, sacrifice, or transformation. The characters should not level up simply because time passed, but because the events of the session mattered.
Use the following benchmarks when deciding whether to award one level, two levels, or no level. Award one level when the characters:

items: Complete a meaningful session of adventure.
Survive a dangerous journey, dungeon, or expedition.
Defeat a notable enemy or dangerous group of foes.
Uncover important information, secrets, or lost history.
Resolve a significant social, political, or personal conflict.
Make a consequential choice that changes the direction of the campaign.
Achieve a major objective, even if the outcome is imperfect.
Suffer a meaningful setback that teaches them something or changes their situation.

blocks.html: Award two levels when the characters:

items: Defeat a major boss, rival, tyrant, monster, or campaign villain.
Complete a major adventure arc.
Survive a climactic battle or large-scale conflict.
Resolve a major mystery, curse, siege, warfront, or magical disaster.
Awaken a major power, claim a legendary relic, or change their heroic path.
Win a battle that significantly changes the world, faction map, or campaign stakes.
Complete a session that functioned as the finale of a chapter, act, or major storyline.

blocks.html: Award no level when the session:

items: Was mostly administrative, shopping, bookkeeping, or travel without consequence.
Did not involve meaningful choices, danger, discovery, conflict, or progress.
Was unusually short or interrupted before the characters could engage the adventure.
Focused entirely on planning without the plan being tested or advanced.

blocks.html: Even in these cases, you should be cautious. If the players spent the session making difficult decisions, negotiating with important NPCs, preparing for danger, or meaningfully shaping the campaign, that may still count as a meaningful session. Milestone advancement should reward play that matters, not only combat.

Deeds can be used to provide a mechanical bonus to characters when they have accomplished something truly incredible. As the GM, it is your responsibility to award Deeds when your players and villains manage to accomplish one.

The criteria to achieve many Deeds is well defined, as are the benefits or the perk that is gained for earning that Deed. The example Deeds provided are only the beginning. Deeds are the perfect way for you as the GM to award your player special powers or abilities based on the things they have accomplished in your game. These world-specific Deeds become part of the legend and history of your campaign and world.

Deeds represent the special sense of accomplishment and skill that comes from doing something extraordinary. Deeds should represent significant effort on the part of the character, and in doing so should also represent what the character is capable of.

Deeds can also play a crucial role in opening special skills and abilities for your players. Magical training that requires you to have consumed the hearts of four ancient dragons slain in single combat, or a sword that can only be wielded by a virgin who has killed a score of men, are examples of things that are only available to characters who have completed some great feat.

Finally, Deeds might represent a path to atonement or damnation for a character. Like the Twelve Labors of Heracles, a character might be called upon to undertake the accomplishment of some Deed to remove a curse or Corruption, or rewrite some failure of theirs. And yet Deeds are not necessarily all good things, just incredible and extraordinary. Undertaking some great plan to accomplish a most heretical sin or terrible crime might qualify as a Deed, earning the character increased corruption and a unique corruption portfolio, or simply a reputation as a horrible villain.

Milestone leveling does not replace Deeds. Deeds are extraordinary accomplishments, personal goals, or moments of exceptional character expression. If levels represent the party’s overall progress, Deeds represent specific heroic acts.

For example, the whole party might gain a level for completing the haunted tower, while one character earns a Deed for dueling the tower guardian alone, another earns a Deed for deciphering the original warding circle, and another earns a Deed for sparing the bound spirit rather than destroying it.
This keeps advancement simple without flattening individual achievement. The party rises together, but each hero still has moments that define their personal legend.

All Deeds are exceptional accomplishments, but not all Deeds are equal. Deeds can be broken down into Titles, Ordinary Deeds, Heroic Deeds, and Epic Deeds. Each requires a number of steps necessary to accomplish, depending on the scope of the perk the character received for the accomplishment.

A Step is a discrete, recordable achievement that represents a moment of exceptional effort, risk, mastery, or narrative significance. The number of steps in a deed sets the tier of deed as well as defines the power of its Perk.
Steps are intentionally rare and difficult; most characters will complete only a handful per tier of play.
A Step is always one clear event, resolved at the table, and marked immediately when it occurs.
A single step for a Deed can be any one of the following:
These Steps are resolved through the dice system and are objective and unambiguous.
1. Accomplish a Check While Suffering 3 Challenges
The character succeeds on a check where their bonus dice are reduced by three ranks due to stacked Challenges.
The GM must explicitly confirm the Challenges before the roll. Failure does not count, regardless of narrative outcome.
2. Accomplish a Check After Taking 3 Chances
The character voluntarily takes three Chances related to the same task, scene, or objective.
After the third Chance is taken, the qualifying check must be attempted and succeeded.
The Chances must meaningfully increase risk; cosmetic or trivial Chances do not qualify.
3. Have 3 Bonus Dice Burst on a Single Check
During one roll, three separate bonus dice explode (roll max, reroll, add).
This may occur on an Action roll, Effect roll, or Crafting roll.
Only one Step may be earned per roll, regardless of how many additional dice burst.
These Steps represent long-term investment and are deterministic.
4. Skill Mastery
A specific skill line is increased to Rank 5.
The skill must be relevant to the Deed being pursued.
Each skill may only generate Step credit once per Deed.
5. Talent Suite
A selection of 3 seemingly unrelated talents are learned.
These must connect to the deed’s final outcome or meaning.
These Steps are granted by the GM but must be concrete and earned.
6. Major Objective
Completion of a major story objective, dungeon, boss encounter, expedition, or crisis.
Typically the culmination of multiple scenes or sessions.
The objective must be clearly relevant to the Deed’s theme.
7. Unique Challenge
A singular, high-impact action tailored to the character. A unique challenge is often personal, symbolic, or unprecedented.
This may overlap with a Major Objective but must highlight the individual character’s role.
Some Examples include:

items: Holding a collapsing bridge alone so others may escape
Parleying with an entity that cannot normally be reasoned with
Using an untested artifact with catastrophic risk

blocks.html: To prevent inflation and confusion, the following never qualify as Steps:

items: Ordinary successes, even if impressive
Multiple similar actions in the same scene (only one Step max per scene)
Routine combat victories
Passive bonuses, buffs, or preparation alone
GM fiat without a clear triggering event

blocks.html: If it cannot be clearly pointed to, written down, and agreed upon at the table, it is not a Step.
Once each step is completed, the Deed is accomplished. This event is much like gaining a level; the character has a kind of epiphany and finds they have suddenly developed the perk.
Deed Perks are the mechanical rewards granted when a character completes a Deed. The tier of the Deed determines both the number of Steps required and the maximum power of the Perk it may grant.
Titles represent a character becoming known for something specific or learning a narrow optimization through experience. Choose one:

items: Reduce the resource cost of one specific Reaction by 1
Reduce the resource cost of one specific Technique or one Spell School by 1
Reduce the resource cost of one Talent by 2
Add 1 Charge to attack rolls with one specific spell or technique
Add 1 Charge to all rolls with one Academic or Practical skill
Learn one new spell or technique the character otherwise qualifies for

blocks.html: Ordinary Deeds reflect consistent heroics and earned competence. They should noticeably improve performance without redefining the character’s build or progression. Choose one:

items: Reduce the cost of all techniques or all spells by 1
Add 1 Charge to attack rolls with all techniques or spells from one school
Add 1 Charge to all rolls with one Combat or Elite skill
Add 1 Charge to effect rolls or crafting results once per turn
Open access to one Elite Skill Tree
Open access to one Unique Talent List
Increase Guard by 2
Increase Ward by 2
Increase Combat Movement Speed by 5
Improve Initiative by +1 Bonus Die
Increase Mana or Vigor recovery by 1d4 during the recovery phase
Increase elemental magic damage by 1 rank (single element)
Add 1 Charge to damage rolls against one specific enemy type

blocks.html: Heroic Deeds define who the character is in the world. These perks are designed to shape play patterns and reinforce character identity. Choose one:

items: Add 1 Charge to all checks under one Ability Score
Increase one Ability Score by 1d4
Increase Health recovery by 1d6 during rest
Increase Mana recovery by 1d10 during rest
Increase one specific weapon type’s damage by 1 rank
Add 1 Charge to attack rolls with weapons and techniques against one enemy type
Gain Heroic Durability against one damage type
Improve an existing Talent’s effect by 1 rank

blocks.html: Epic Deeds represent moments that reshape legend, fate, or the campaign itself. An Epic Perk may bend or override normal assumptions of play. Choose one:

items: Gain Immunity to one specific Condition (fear, sickened, petrified, etc.)
Gain one additional Action per turn
Permanently increase Health recovery beyond normal limits
Permanently increase Durability by a large fixed amount (e.g., +4d6)
Add 1 universal Charge to a core skill that applies unconditionally
Gain a repeatable Epic benefit tied to defeating extreme-risk entities
Gain the benefits of two Ordinary perks, or 1 Ordinary and two title perks.

blocks.html: Deeds are not “extra talents.” They are earned transformations that reflect what the character has survived, mastered, or changed in the world. If a Title or Deed does not feel like a story worth retelling, it should not grant a powerful perk.

One of the most rewarding and challenging aspects of taking on the role of the GM is crafting adventures for the players at your table. Every session they will expect to prepare for, participate in, or complete some adventure for wealth, glory, or experience.

An adventure can range in length from simple encounters like an episode of a television series to multi-part dramas that play out like a novel. The length and scope of the adventure you create is up to you. Each will be a mixture of action, exploration, interaction, and drama. The most important part of any mission, whether you are running one printed in a book or one of your own design, is to create a fun experience for you and your players.

Believe it or not, there is a kind of formula for a great adventure plot, which you can use to help bring the missions you run with your players to life. Most adventures can be broken down into this core structure.

The goal should be outlined at the beginning; players should know what they think they are getting into. During each adventure, you may find that the goal can, and often will, change. That is all right. A simple escort operation may suddenly become a rescue mission after the client’s family is kidnapped by hostile forces. A day off at the beach might suddenly see the players in the center of an attack. However, at each step the players should have a clear idea of what their goals are.

If adventures were not dangerous, why would anyone involve people like the players? There needs to be a real danger to the players, even if that danger is not a villain or hostile force. Time, environment, even disease or poison could be the danger that the players are facing and must overcome. Regardless, the threat needs to be clear and present.

Most adventures are time sensitive. There is only so much time until the Sword of Damocles falls on the players, or the chance of victory is snatched away, or perhaps, if the party does nothing many others will suffer for it. A sense of pressure and urgency keeps the focus of the adventure on the action and in the present, and these will keep players engaged.

The universe is vast and filled with powers both great and terrible, but for the purpose of the adventure, the players must matter. Either they are the only ones with the knowledge, tools, or ability to make a difference or they are simply the only ones around who can even try. The choices they make, and the consequences of those choices, need to matter to the outcome of the adventure, even if that means that the players can fail with disastrous results.

Adventures should have an element of surprise in them. This can be the sudden revelation that the players are on the wrong side, or it could be a sudden change of pace, such as an intercepted communication that shows there is less time than originally thought. At some point in the adventure, one or two surprises complicate or change the original goal and force the players to think of new ways to accomplish what they are after.

It does not matter if an adventure takes one session or one hundred, when things are finally settled, there needs to be resolution. The players either succeeded or failed, and they now reap the rewards or face the consequences. While an adventure need not neatly end every plot aspect, it should resolve the goals laid out in the beginning and the threats faced along the way.

The heart of any conflict is the Encounter, when the players meet a force, friendly or hostile, and must overcome some kind of challenge or combat. Remember, all combat are encounters, but not all encounters are combat.

Radiant Age is a long ascent. Characters begin as apprentices in broad Ordinary Classes, sharpen into iconic Heroic identities, deepen into doctrinal Epic specializations, and may one day rise into singular Legendary stations remembered by the world. A campaign that runs the full ladder will feel like three different games stacked together: a coming-of-age, a saga of named champions, and a myth told in the heavens.

These three Tiers of Play describe what the world should look like, what threats should feel like, and what your job at the table changes into as the heroes climb. They are not hard walls. A campaign may dwell entirely within one tier, or sprint through two, or run all the way to the gates of legend. Use the tier guidance to set expectation and pacing, not to constrain your story.
A useful instinct: Tier I is about the heroes finding their station. Tier II is about earning it. Tier III is about deciding what the world will become because they hold it.

This is the first life of a hero, when the world is still much larger than they are. The characters have chosen a broad Ordinary Class, perhaps branched once into a Heroic identity around level 5, and may glimpse a second specialization on the horizon. A Warrior is lighting their first Aura Stars. A Mage has carved one or two Circles into the heart. A Rogue has only just learned that their Edges are a school of their own. A Spiritualist has touched their first Mystery and discovered that it touches back.

Tone. This tier should feel like the start of every great JRPG: a wide-open road, a humble village, an older sword in a quiet shrine, a master at the edge of town who will train the worthy. Hope is plentiful, and so is danger. The world is full of small wonders that loom larger than they should, because the heroes have not yet learned the scale of things.

Adventure Scope. Local and regional. A monster hunting in the woods. A baronial intrigue. A ruin uncovered by storm. A bandit captain styling himself a warlord. A travelling rival school passing through and looking to test the locals. Adventures should resolve in one to a handful of sessions, and the world should be small enough that the heroes are recognized at the inn by the end of an arc.

Threats. Adversaries of Risk 1 through 10. Bandit captains, monster lords of the regional wilds, low-tier rivals from named schools, lesser Eldritch intrusions, restless ancestors, ambitious nobles. A named villain at this tier should be at most Heroic class, and even then a stretch — most named threats are Ordinary class with one signature Heroic talent that the party must answer with cleverness.

Cultivation Visibility. Aura is a bright shimmer, not a tower of light. A Mage’s spells crackle and singe; they do not yet reshape weather. A Rogue’s Edges are quiet, practical, and survival-shaped. A Spiritualist’s Mysteries hum like overheard prayers. Make these manifestations real and observable at the table. Townsfolk should see the change in characters from session one to session ten.

GM Focus. Your work here is establishing identity. Push players to lean into Heritage, History, and Class together. Reward role-play that reinforces the identity stack. Hand out Titles freely; hand out Ordinary Deeds when earned. The first branching at level 5 is a major moment — give it a scene. The second at level 10 should feel like a rite of passage. By the end of Tier I, every character should know what kind of hero they are becoming.

By Tier II the characters are named. Couriers know them. Tournaments invite them. Rival schools watch them. They have settled into Heroic identity and are sharpening toward Epic Classes, at this stage characters have several rank 3 and four talents, maybe a rank 5. Around level 20 most character have opened a third class option; by Tier II’s end most characters carry one or two Epic features.

Tone. This is the saga middle. The map widens. Politics intrudes. Old masters reach out as patrons, as rivals, as warnings. A sword cut at the start of the tier is a wound; by its end the same cut barely registers. The heroes are no longer learning the rules of the world; they are choosing which rules they intend to keep, and which they will break.

Adventure Scope. Regional becoming continental. Crises that cross borders. Faction wars between sects, schools, and noble houses. Ancient seals weakening because the world has noticed the heroes are old enough to break them. Adventures often run across multiple sessions and stack into arcs that interlock. Recurring villains should appear here and survive, returning more dangerous than before. Players should sense larger shapes moving behind the curtain.

Threats. Adversaries of Risk 11 through 30. Named Epic-class villains. Awakened relics. Lesser dragons and primal beasts. Heretical cult-masters with hidden patrons. Doctrinal rivals who fight by the same rules the heroes do, but better. At this tier a single combat encounter can be the climax of a session, and a named villain’s defeat should feel earned in three or four scenes.

Cultivation Visibility. Aura now casts light across a room. A Mage’s Circle, when fully ignited, can be seen through walls. A Rogue’s Edge becomes recognizable as a style — students of the craft can identify a Stalker by their footwork. A Spiritualist’s Mysteries draw attention from forces that have not spoken to mortals in centuries. The cost of being seen rises with the spectacle.

GM Focus. Your work shifts toward consequence. Pull threads planted in Tier I forward — the bandit captain from level three is now a marshal in someone’s army; the cursed shrine that was a side quest is the keystone of a regional crisis. Heroic Deeds become available and should be hard-won. Begin to seed Legendary names — let players hear of a Sword Saint, a Lich, a Chronarch, three kingdoms away. The world is preparing to ask what kind of legend these characters will become.

In Tier III the characters cease to be participants in the world and become forces within it. They have crossed into Legendary class identity — Sword Saint, Phoenix Magus, Spirit Sovereign, Master Thief, Demon Hunter, Storm Sovereign — or are on the cusp. Their Aura Cores blaze with five or more Stars. Their Mana Circles are temples in the chest. Their Edges have hardened into doctrines. Their Mysteries have become covenants written in the bones of reality.

Tone. Mythic. Every scene matters. The Akashic Record is open to them; what they do will be remembered in celestial ink. Old gods take notice. New gods become possible. This tier should not feel like a louder version of Tier II — it should feel like a different kind of game, where the question is no longer whether the heroes can change the world, but what world they intend to leave behind.

Adventure Scope. World-shaping and cosmic. Breaking primordial seals. Confronting the sleeping things beneath the Realms of Ouroboros. Settling dynastic questions older than nations. Negotiating with divine beings or being inscribed among them. An arc may span entire campaigns and rewrite the map. Side quests at this tier alter the calendar.

Threats. Adversaries of Risk 31 and beyond. Legendary-class antagonists, fellow ascendants who have chosen a darker path. Primordial entities. Hidden Mythic-class horrors the players were never supposed to meet. Divine intrusions. Other peoples’ Legendaries with their own causes and their own pasts. A single encounter at this tier may be the climax of a campaign year.

Cultivation Visibility. Power at this tier is akin to a force of nature, the players will have abilities that could level an army of lesser foes, shatter castle walls, and change the landscape. Restraint becomes a major theme: when characters can level a city, the story shifts to why they do not.

GM Focus. Your work becomes curation. Let the heroes drive setting change. Let them inherit responsibilities. Honor their arcs by making the world remember them. Epic Deeds should be available, and a few characters may pursue unique campaign-defining Legendary Deeds whose Steps are written across the whole campaign. Most importantly: do not flinch from giving these characters real stakes. Legends die. Legends fail. Legends are remembered for both.

Even the most seasoned GM hits a session in which inspiration arrives slowly. These tables are designed to spark adventures quickly, either between scheduled arcs or as the seed of a brand-new campaign. They follow the Anatomy of Adventure described earlier — an Objective, a Threat, an Opportunity, and a Twist, all framed by a chosen Objective Type to set the structure.
Roll once on each of the five tables, in order: Objective Type, Objective, Threat, Opportunity, and Twist. Read the results together and let them suggest the adventure. Then refine.

items: The Objective Type sets the verb of the adventure — the shape of the play it will demand.
The Objective sets the noun — what the heroes are after.
The Threat is the present danger that must be overcome.
The Opportunity is the carrot, the unique chance the world is offering during this adventure.
The Twist is the surprise that complicates everything in the middle act.

blocks.html: If a roll does not match the campaign you are running, re-roll, or pick from the table directly. These are seeds, not commandments. The best results often come from forcing two unlikely entries to fit each other — that is where good stories are written.

rows: Retrieval — recover a person, object, or knowledge
Escort or Protection — keep something safe through danger
Hunt or Slay — bring down a defined target
Heist or Intrusion — enter where you are forbidden
Diplomacy or Parley — settle a matter without bloodshed
Exploration or Discovery — go where no one has
Survival or Endurance — outlast a hostile situation
Trial of Cultivation — prove worth in a sanctioned test, tournament, or rite
Recover a stolen relic from a rival school’s sanctum
Hunt a monster whose name has begun to be whispered
Escort a noble heir through hostile country to a coronation or oath-taking
Investigate a string of magical disappearances along a trade road
Break a curse afflicting a lineage, a town, or a beloved place
Negotiate a settlement between feuding houses, schools, or cults
Map a newly opened ruin from a fallen age before others can
Sabotage a ritual before it completes — or fail and clean up after it
Win a regional cultivation tournament without dishonor
Protect a binding seal from being broken by a determined enemy
Liberate a settlement from a tyrant, occupier, or possessor
Retrieve a master’s lost technique, scroll, or formula
Survive a sacred trial in a wilderness that tests the soul
Deliver a sealed message to a recipient nobody will name aloud
Awaken a sleeping protector, ally, or weapon long dormant
Steal back an heirloom taken by raiders, occupiers, or a victor
Resolve a haunting that has overtaken a holy site or sanctified ground
Avenge a wronged ancestor, fallen mentor, or murdered comrade
Witness a divine sign and discern what it asks of the worthy
Ascend a path forbidden to all but those who pass its trials
A rival school’s enforcers, cultivated to the same tier as the party
A monster lord and its brood, Risk five greater than party level
A corrupt official with the law and the city watch on their side
A sealed evil seeping into the world through a weakening binding
A jealous deity, angry spirit, or slighted ancestor reaching out
A criminal syndicate with reach across the whole region
A natural disaster shaped or sharpened by magic, curse, or omen
A heretical cult led by patrons whose names are never said
A noble house pursuing a vendetta the heroes did not start
An ancient construct or guardian relic, waking after centuries
A plague — mundane, magical, or spiritual — already spreading
A revenant or restless Legendary dead, returned with grievance
A traitor placed deep within the party’s allies or contacts
A summoned outsider drawn to the smell of mortal cultivation
A famine, blight, or failing harvest with a hidden, intentional cause
A duel-challenger who cannot be refused without unforgivable shame
An invading army marching from beyond the known map
A doppelganger or impostor who has replaced a key NPC
A binding broken in real time — minutes, not days
A Legendary-class antagonist who has begun to take personal notice
A master is finally taking on an apprentice — and watching
A rare cultivation reagent has surfaced briefly at a regional market
An ancient discipline scroll has come up for auction at a court
A spirit offers a covenant in exchange for service well rendered
A noble’s favor would clear long-standing debts, oaths, or grudges
A hidden vault has briefly opened, and will not stay open for long
A rival has unexpectedly offered an alliance against a greater foe
A sacred site reveals a teaching to those who arrive at the right hour
A travelling sage will trade rare knowledge for the completion of a deed
A relic-bearer wishes to pass their burden on before they die
A holy order seeks a new champion and has heard the heroes’ names
A bounty has been raised on a known mutual enemy of the party
A guild seat, council chair, or sect office is open and contested
A passage between Realms briefly stabilizes — for one night only
A retired legend will mentor those willing to do something foolish
A monstrous bloodline can be ended permanently if pursued at once
An heirloom of a player character’s heritage surfaces in the area
A duel offered can settle the matter without further bloodshed
A cursed item can be reforged into something blessed by deed
A new Title or honorific can be claimed by those who finish this work
The client is the actual villain, and the heroes have been used
The villain is not the villain — they were a tool of someone hidden
A trusted ally is bound by an oath the party did not know about
The treasure or relic is itself the threat, awake and hungry
The threat is a former hero of renown, fallen from their grace
The objective is already complete — but someone is hiding the fact
Two factions both want the party to win, for opposing reasons
The target is a child, sibling, innocent, or genuinely repentant
A heritage secret of one player character is at the heart of all of it
The reinforcement, aid, or rescue the party was promised never comes
The villain’s grievance is just; only their methods deserve answer
A god, demon, or spirit has been watching the party from session one
There is a witness to all of this who cannot be silenced or bought
The setting itself is haunted, alive, or aware of the heroes by name
Time is shorter than the party was told — by half, or by more
The relic at the center is awakening, and it is choosing a hero
A rival is also pursuing this objective, given a deliberate head start
Completing this objective fulfills a prophecy the party did not know
Failure is not an option — and neither is success without lasting cost
The world is watching; this deed will be remembered in every age

blocks.html: A few example pulls to show how the tables interact:

Pull A. Objective Type: Retrieval. Objective: Recover a stolen relic from a rival school’s sanctum. Threat: A doppelganger has replaced a key NPC. Opportunity: A duel offered can settle the matter without further bloodshed. Twist: The relic at the center is awakening, and it is choosing a hero.

The party is hired by their own school to recover a stolen blade-relic from a rival sanctum. As they prepare, they realize one of the school’s elders has been replaced by an impostor. The rival sanctum, when reached, offers a duel of champions in lieu of a raid. And the blade-relic itself begins to whisper a name — the name of one of the player characters, whose heritage it remembers.

Pull B. Objective Type: Investigation. Objective: Investigate a string of magical disappearances along a trade road. Threat: A famine has a hidden, intentional cause. Opportunity: A spirit offers a covenant in exchange for service well rendered. Twist: The villain’s grievance is just; only their methods deserve answer.

Travellers vanish on a road that ought to be safe. The investigation leads to a starving district whose famine is no accident — and to a spirit of the road who has begun taking lives because someone is taking the offerings due to her shrine. She will swear covenant with the heroes if they help her, but her methods have already killed innocents. The party must decide whether to bind a justified killer, depose her quietly, or find the third path neither she nor the famine-makers will like.

blocks.text: Creating Your Own Settings in Radiant Age

blocks.html: Radiant Age is a fantasy chassis, not a single world. Its mechanical engines — Aura, Circles, Edges, Mysteries — its identity stack of Heritage, History, and Class, and its ladder from Ordinary to Legendary will all serve any number of campaign worlds. The Realms of Ouroboros are the default canvas, broad and deep enough to host most stories, but nothing in these rules requires you to remain within them. The system was designed to be carried into your own settings, and to feel at home there.
This section is a guide to doing exactly that. Treat it as a checklist of decisions rather than a recipe, and answer each in the voice and flavor of the world you wish to build.

Before you set out, recognize what you are keeping and what you are reshaping. The chassis under all Promethium games — the d20 Action Roll, Bonus Dice, Charges and Challenges, Skills tied to Ability Scores, the Risk scale for adversaries, and the cultivation engines of the four Ordinary Classes — should remain intact. These are what make a Radiant Age character recognizable as a Radiant Age character across worlds.

What flexes is everything around the chassis: the look of the heritages, the names of the schools, the meaning of the gods, the shape of the map, the calendar, the tone of magic, the social weight of class titles. Radiant Age trusts you to dress its bones however your campaign needs.

Begin by naming your pillars. Radiant Age’s default pillars are JRPG-style job ascent, knightly aura fantasy, the formal cultivation of mage circles, occult spiritual contracts, and luminous heroic adventure. Your setting needs three to five pillars of its own — its touchstones, its inspirations, its mood board in plain language.

If your setting leans toward Wuxia and martial sects, write that down. If it is a sun-baked dynasty of desert magi, write that down. If it is a luminous archipelago of beast-blooded clans hunting drowned gods, write that down. The pillars are the question your players will be answering when they make characters: what kind of fantasy is this?

Every Radiant Age setting needs a wound. Not all wounds are war. The wound might be a long peace under a god whose price is going unpaid, a magical exhaustion thinning the world’s mana, a vanished generation of masters, a sundering between heritages, or a Legendary act in living memory whose consequences are still arriving.

A clear central crisis grounds every adventure. It tells you why the heroes are needed, why power is being cultivated, and why someone keeps trying to break the binding under the city. Without a crisis, the world is scenery. With one, the world is a story waiting to be answered.

This is the most important worldbuilding step in Radiant Age, and the one most often overlooked. Decide how cultivation works in your setting — not just mechanically, which is set, but culturally, visually, and morally.
Ask of each engine:

items: Aura. Who teaches it? What does it look like in your setting — pale fire, golden script, bands of color, an audible hum, a smell of ozone? Is it a noble art, a peasant tradition, a heresy? What does a fully ignited Aura Core mean to a witness?
Circles. Where are the schools? Are Mana Circles a guild discipline, a royal monopoly, a sacred rite, a banned heresy, a private tutor’s gift? Do circles glow through robes? Can they be inherited?

blocks.html: The answers to these questions are the texture of your setting more than any map or family tree. Cultivation visible at the table is the heartbeat of Radiant Age.

Before you lay out your geography, think of the map as a list of wonders rather than political boundaries. What are the singular places of the world? The towers, the chasms, the sealed ruins, the holy forests, the moon-blessed peaks, the burning sea? Five to ten of these will give your world a skyline.

Then arrange the realms, nations, or regions around the wonders. A nation explains itself in relation to the wonder nearest it. The kingdom that lives beneath a falling star will not be the same as the one that lives in its shadow. The trade league that controls the only safe passage through a haunted strait will have different politics from the one that does not.

Radiant Age comes with twelve heritages organized into four peoples: Men, Ceith, Dwerin, and Beastrals. You can use them as written, rename them, drop some, or design new ones using the heritage rules in Chapter Six. Whatever you do, keep three principles in mind.
First, heritage answers what you are, not how you were raised. History does that. Resist the urge to tie a single culture to a single heritage; let your world be more complicated than that.
Second, heritages should remain visually and biologically distinct. Players choose them for the silhouette as much as for the mechanics. Do not blur the lines.
Third, do not overcrowd. Twelve heritages is a generous count. Your setting may use fewer. A world with three heritages can be just as full as one with twenty.

Time in Radiant Age is best measured in legends. Decide what the great ages of your setting are, and what each is named for. Was there a War of the First Circle? A Long Silence? A King Beneath the Hill? A Year the Stars Stopped? Set out three to seven such ages and let them root your characters in time.

The Calendar of Legends gives players a quick way to place their histories. My grandfather fought in the Second Aurum Crusade, says one. I was raised in the shrines of the Quiet Years, says another. Without much elaboration, both players now share a sense of when their characters live, and what came before.

In Radiant Age, class is not just a build. It is a station the world recognizes. Decide what each Heroic, Epic, and Legendary class means in your setting. Are Knights a sworn order, a noble convention, or a wandering tradition? Are Spellblades feared, courted, or banned? What does it take to be called a Sword Saint in your kingdoms — official recognition, popular acclaim, or both? Does the title carry legal authority?
A class that the world recognizes can be claimed wrongly, granted falsely, lost in disgrace, and won back through deed. This is rich material. Use it.

Decide what Legendary looks like in your setting, and what lies beyond it. The Legendary tier in Radiant Age represents the highest publicly recognized stations. But the design brief acknowledges Mythic Classes as hidden, GM-side identities — the strange and singular forms reserved for the storyteller’s greatest figures.

Ask yourself: what does a Mythic class look like in your world? The last surviving Heaven-Walker of an extinct order? The avatar of a slumbering god? The Empress whose name was eaten by the dragons she made? You do not need to use these in every campaign. But knowing they exist sets the ceiling of your setting’s imagination, and gives Tier III adventures something to reach toward.
A new setting lives or dies in its first session. Some practical advice before you run:

items: Hold a Session Zero. Walk your players through pillars, crisis, wonders, calendar, and heritages. Ask them what their characters know.
Write a one-page setting primer. Geography, peoples, calendar, and the central crisis in plain prose. Hand it out.
Decide where Tier I will be set. Pick one region, one wonder, one local crisis, and run there until characters reach Heroic identity. Open the wider map at Tier II.
Let the players write into the world. A player character with a homeland that does not yet exist is a gift, not a problem. Build it together.
Use the random adventure tables freely. They were designed for the chassis, not for a single setting. Reskin freely.

blocks.html: Radiant Age is a game of cultivated power and earned identity, and it is at its best when both the heroes and the world they inhabit are shaped by deed. Your setting is part of that shaping. Build it with the same care you ask of your players, and it will rise to meet them.
